title: Public Interest Data Science
text:
Preface
This is a book about doing data science for ends the public can name, contest, and use.
You already know, or will shortly, that most data science is not that. It produces things for an employer to ship, a platform to monetize, or a manager to track. When it produces things for the public, it tends to produce dashboards: objects that look like accountability without being accountable to anyone in particular. This book is written for the student who has noticed the gap between what data science can do and what it typically does, and who wants a way in that is neither naive about the tools nor resigned about the politics.

title: Public Interest Data Science
text:
0.1 Who this book is for
If you are a student taking a data-science course, a journalism or policy student learning computational methods, a working practitioner considering a move into civic or public-interest work, an instructor looking for a course companion, or a public-sector analyst being asked to do “data-driven” work with no time to read the entire literature, this book is for you. It assumes introductory Python, comfort with the command line, and a willingness to read ten pages about a court case before writing the function that acts on it. It does not assume a background in science and technology studies, law, or accounting. It will give you enough of each to keep going.
You can read it straight through, pick chapters relevant to a specific project, or use it as a course text. Instructors using it as a course companion will find that the five parts map cleanly onto a sixteen-week semester with room for two review weeks and a final project.

title: Public Interest Data Science
text:
0.2 What the book does
The book works through a conceptual arc in five parts:

Pressures. The structural forces that constrain public knowledge: enclosure (access withdrawn), exemption (accountability legally or practically avoided), erosion (infrastructure and memory decaying through neglect).
Values. The countervailing commitments that hold an “installed base” of accountability together: openness, oversight, and ownership. Chapter 5 introduces the six elements of the installed base (linkability, interpretability, continuity, safe scrutiny, authority, and remedy), and the next three chapters build out each value in turn.
Lineages. What data scientists can inherit from the professions that have worked out public-interest practice over decades: journalism (watchdog methods and provenance), law (records, discovery, legal infrastructure), accounting (independent audit, reproducibility, change history), planning (stakeholder process and geographic imagination), and engineering (safety, testing, incident review).
Adjacent Conversations. Neighboring fields whose relationship to public interest data science is close and complicated: public interest technology and data for good, GovTech and civic tech, and critical data studies, data justice, and refusal.
Genres. The forms through which data scientists can actually contribute to public conversations: op-eds, reports, research proposals, testimony, public comments on rulemakings, and archival deposits.

Parts I through IV teach you to see. Part V teaches you to act.

title: Public Interest Data Science
text:
0.3 How the book is built
Every chapter interweaves conceptual narrative with runnable Python tutorials at roughly a 50/50 balance. You will read two or three pages of argument, then read and run two or three pages of code that makes the argument visible in a specific case, then return to the argument. This is deliberate. Concepts without technique produce earnest seminars. Technique without concepts produces dashboards that nobody trusts.
Code blocks do not run at build time. They are illustrative: you are expected to type them, adapt them, and run them yourself. The book’s repository contains test fixtures, sample data, and the occasional larger notebook that a chapter only excerpts. When a chapter uses a real API (Wikimedia pageviews, regulations.gov, the Internet Archive), the endpoints cited were live as of the book’s most recent release; if you find one that has moved, open an issue.
Every chapter has at least one “Missing Manual” callout flagging something that textbooks usually omit: a surprising library default, a real-world API friction, the politics of a “simple” choice. Every chapter has at least one “In the Public Interest” callout tying the technical material back to openness, oversight, or ownership. Every chapter ends with 4–5 graduated exercises, from guided (“follow these steps”) to open-ended (“design a pipeline that…”), and a curated list of further resources.
Chapters in Part V have one additional rule: they must end with a real submittable artifact. Not a thought experiment. An actual draft op-ed, report section, proposal page, testimony statement, public comment on a live rulemaking, or archival deposit with a resolvable identifier. A student who finishes Part V has a portfolio.

title: Public Interest Data Science
text:
0.4 Running examples
To give the book coherence across 22 chapters, a small set of running examples is threaded throughout. When a chapter needs a case, it reaches for one of these before inventing a new one:

Boulder and Denver municipal records (meeting minutes, agendas, budgets, permits, 311 requests).
Federal environmental data (EPA, NOAA, USGS) as sources for at-risk datasets.
Census and FRED APIs for demographic and economic context.
Wikipedia and Internet Archive APIs for archival work and link-rot audits.
Hugging Face model cards and datasheets for audit and documentation exercises.
A small synthetic worker-log fixture that lets us teach audit methods without violating anyone’s privacy.
Public FOIA and CORA request data as a class-wide shared resource.
regulations.gov for Chapter 21 and any chapter touching federal rulemaking.

By the end of the book you will have built, in parts, a small public-interest data infrastructure of your own: a cleaned and documented civic dataset published with Datasette, a disaggregated audit report, a refusal specification, an op-ed draft, a report with reproducible figures, a research proposal, a piece of testimony, a public comment on a live rulemaking, and an archival deposit on the Internet Archive or Zenodo.

title: Public Interest Data Science
section: 0.5 What the book does not do
text:
0.5 What the book does not do
Honesty up front. This book is partial in several ways that matter.
It is US-centric. Most of the legal and administrative infrastructure discussed is American: FOIA and CORA, the Administrative Procedure Act, NIST, NSF, NYC Local Law 144. Where European, Latin American, and Global South counterparts exist (the Dutch SyRI case, IndiaStack, Ushahidi, the CARE Principles, Te Mana Raraunga), the book names them and points you to further reading, but it does not pretend to do them justice in three pages.
It is comfortable with records and less comfortable with refusal. The installed-base framework takes linkability, durable identifiers, and retention as load-bearing. Chapter 16 takes that comfort seriously as a limit, drawing on Zong and Matias, D’Ignazio and Klein, the CARE Principles, and Indigenous data sovereignty scholarship. Readers who want to start from refusal and arrive at records (rather than the other way around) should read Chapter 16 early and often.
It is thin on the labor inside data work. Gray and Suri’s Ghost Work is cited, and Chapter 14 tries to name invisible maintenance labor explicitly, but a book that seriously treated data-labor economics would look quite different. Consider this an invitation to the reader, or to the next edition.
It is thin on measurement theory. You will not find a full psychometric critique of the audit metrics in Chapter 7, and you will not find a deep philosophy-of-science treatment of what it means for a model to “work.” Those books exist and are cited.
It over-relies on the professional-lineage framing. Treating journalism, law, accounting, planning, and engineering as lineages the field can inherit from is a useful move, but every profession has its own pathologies, and the book does not do equal-time analysis of all of them. Read the criticisms. They are in the further-reading lists.
These limits are not bugs to be patched. They are framing choices that enable the book to cohere. Knowing where they bind is part of what you are learning.

title: Public Interest Data Science
text:
0.6 How to contribute
This book lives in a git repository. Fork it, improve it, and open a pull request. Before you do, read claude.md, which codifies the voice, structure, and citation conventions. Typo fixes and small corrections can come in as pull requests directly. Rewrites of sections, new examples, or proposed new chapters should start as an issue so we can talk about them first.
Students whose contributions shape the book are named in the acknowledgments. Some of the best chapters in future editions will be written by readers who encountered the first edition and saw what was missing.

title: Public Interest Data Science
section: 0.7 A note on the AI-assisted draft zero
text:
0.7 A note on the AI-assisted draft zero
The first complete draft of this book was produced with substantial assistance from Claude, Anthropic’s assistant, following a detailed editorial prompt written by the author. The prompt is preserved in the repository; the disclosure statement is in Appendix A. Every chapter has been reviewed and revised by the author. A book about public-interest data science should not hide how it was made, any more than a model card should hide its training data.

title: Public Interest Data Science
text:
0.8 Acknowledgments
The framework owes its shape to years of conversation with colleagues in the INFO department at CU Boulder, students in previous versions of INFO 46XX who asked the questions this book tries to answer, and the infrastructure studies and STS communities whose citations fill the bibliography. Specific individuals will be named once the revision cycle is complete. Any errors are the author’s, and there will be errors. File an issue.
Now to Chapter 1.

title: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science
text:
3 Introduction to Public Interest Data Science
Open a newspaper any week and you will find a story about a data-driven system that has shaped a public decision in a way the public did not choose. A county’s child-welfare office screens families with an algorithm. A city’s benefits portal denies claims for reasons the claimants cannot discover. A federal agency’s climate database is quietly reorganized and three years of comparison data vanish. These are not exotic cases. They are the texture of governance in the 2020s.
This book is about the practice that takes those systems seriously as objects of public concern. Not as opportunities to build a better dashboard, though dashboards have their place. Not as abstract ethics problems, though ethics are unavoidable. As infrastructure: settled arrangements of data, code, authority, and labor that shape what the public can know and what it can demand. Public interest data science is the work of making that infrastructure observable, contestable, and, where necessary, reshaped.
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science

title: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science
text:
3.1 What does “public interest” even mean
The phrase “public interest” has been doing enormous rhetorical work for a long time, and most of it is contested. Robert Moses invoked it to justify expressways that cut Black neighborhoods in half. Jane Jacobs (1961) used it against him to defend the street life those expressways destroyed. Civil rights lawyers used it to secure access to housing, education, and the franchise. Cause lawyers on the other side used it to dismantle affirmative action. “Public” is a word that asks: which public? Whose interests?
This book does not pretend to settle that question. It takes a position and names the position explicitly. Following Keegan (2026), it treats the public interest as operational rather than abstract: the public interest is served when the six elements of what Chapter 5 calls the installed base are maintained. Records can be linked. Categories can be interpreted. Evidence persists across political cycles. Researchers and journalists can scrutinize systems without being sued or arrested. Someone has authority to act on what is learned. And the people affected have a path to remedy. When those six elements are in place, “public interest” is not a slogan. It is a working arrangement. When they are missing, even the most benevolent data practice can produce what Eubanks (2018) calls automating inequality.
That is a particular theory. It is not the only one available, and Chapter 16 takes seriously the traditions (Indigenous data sovereignty, abolitionist critique, feminist refusal) that question whether “records” and “linkability” are the right starting places at all. But you have to start somewhere, and “making the installed base visible” is a useful place to start.
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science

title: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science
text:
3.2 Relative to neighbors
Public interest data science sits next to, and overlaps with, several fields you may already know.
Data journalism investigates public questions through computational methods and publishes findings in the press. It is older and, at its best, more politically mature than data science. Chapter 9 treats it as a lineage.
Civic technology and public interest technology (PIT) build tools for government and civil society: usable benefits portals, court-filing interfaces, participatory mapping platforms. Chapters 14 and 15 sort out the differences.
Data for good describes, usually, a philanthropic or industry-led application of commercial methods to socially valued problems. Chapter 14 engages it skeptically.
Critical data studies and data justice theorize the politics of data without necessarily building anything. Chapter 16 argues that the practice you will learn in this book is stronger when it can absorb the critique, including the parts of the critique that undercut the book’s own framing.
Public interest data science is none of these in isolation. It borrows from all of them. It aims for the practical footprint of data journalism, the critical self-awareness of critical data studies, the civic focus of civic tech, and the professional-infrastructure commitment that “data for good” often lacks.
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science

title: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science
text:
3.3 The arc of the book
The book moves through five parts.
Part I: Pressures diagnoses the structural forces that constrain public knowledge in the current moment. Chapter 2 treats enclosure (who can observe), Chapter 3 treats exemption (who must answer), Chapter 4 treats erosion (what can be remembered). These are not the only pressures, but they are the three that organize most of the empirical material through the rest of the book.
Part II: Values names the countervailing commitments that hold accountability together. Chapter 5 introduces the installed base and its six elements. Chapters 6–8 build out openness, oversight, and ownership in turn. These values are not abstract ideals. They are practical configurations, and each chapter asks what it takes to sustain them.
Part III: Lineages looks at the professions that have worked out public-interest practice for longer than data science has existed: journalism, law, accounting, planning, engineering. Each chapter extracts a set of methods the reader can import.
Part IV: Adjacent Conversations situates the book in the fields it is not and could be confused with: PIT and data for good, GovTech and civic tech, critical data studies. The treatment is comparative rather than polemical. Each of these fields has something you want, and each has something you should be wary of.
Part V: Genres is the pivot from seeing to doing. Chapters 17–22 teach the forms through which data scientists contribute to public conversations: op-eds, reports, research proposals, testimony, public comments, archival deposits. Each chapter ends with a real artifact you can submit.
Twenty-two chapters, roughly 3,000 words each. You can read them straight through or pick from the table of contents. Cross-references will tell you when a later chapter depends on an earlier one.
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science

title: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science
text:
3.4 A first request
Before we talk any more about the book, let us do something with it. The rest of this chapter is a short tour of the toolkit you will use throughout, with a first public data retrieval exercise that takes perhaps twenty minutes. If you have Python and git already, you can follow along now. If not, the Environment setup sidebar below points you at the installers.






NoteEnvironment setup



You need Python 3.11 or newer, git, and a working command line. On macOS and Linux, python3 --version and git --version should return something reasonable. On Windows, WSL2 works well, as does the official Python installer with the “Add Python to PATH” box ticked. You will also want pip install pandas requests matplotlib jupyter before continuing, and a GitHub account if you do not have one already.


We will retrieve pageview data for the Wikipedia article titled “Public interest,” across three different language editions, and look at how the concept’s visibility has varied across linguistic publics over time. The example is small, but it puts a handful of habits in place that will recur: fetching from a real public API, handling the response thoughtfully, moving it into a pandas DataFrame, plotting it, and asking what the data can and cannot tell you.
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science

title: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science
text:
3.5 The Wikimedia pageviews API
Wikimedia Foundation operates a pageviews API that returns view counts for any article on any of their projects, back to 2015. The endpoints are documented at wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1. The relevant endpoint for our purposes takes the form:
GET /metrics/pageviews/per-article/{project}/{access}/{agent}/{article}/{granularity}/{start}/{end}
You plug in the project (e.g., en.wikipedia), access method (all-access is fine), agent type (user filters out bots and spiders, which you want), article title, and a start and end date in YYYYMMDD or YYYYMMDDHH format.
Here is a first retrieval:
import requests

HEADERS = {
    "User-Agent": "PublicInterestDataScience/0.1 (your-email@example.edu)",
    "Accept": "application/json",
}

url = (
    "https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/metrics/pageviews/per-article/"
    "en.wikipedia/all-access/user/Public_interest/monthly/"
    "2020010100/2024123100"
)

response = requests.get(url, headers=HEADERS, timeout=30)
response.raise_for_status()
data = response.json()
print(data["items"][:2])
# => [{'project': 'en.wikipedia', 'article': 'Public_interest',
#      'granularity': 'monthly', 'timestamp': '2020010100',
#      'access': 'all-access', 'agent': 'user', 'views': 8442}, ...]
Three things happened there that you will keep doing. You sent a User-Agent header naming yourself and your purpose. You called raise_for_status() to turn HTTP errors into Python exceptions. You set a timeout so that if the server hangs, your script does not. The next chapter will make these habits formal as part of a “responsible scraper” function. For now, notice that requests.get() does not hand you the response body directly. It hands you a Response object, and .json() is one of several methods you can call on it.






TipThe Missing Manual



Most tutorials teach you requests.get(url).json() as a single chained call. That works until it does not. The Response object carries the status code, headers, cookies, and error context separately from the body. When a request fails (and it will), you need the status code and the headers to understand why; calling .json() on a 403 will give you an exception that hides the 403. Write your code so the Response survives long enough to inspect. You will thank yourself the first time an API silently starts returning an HTML error page in place of JSON.
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science

title: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science
text:
3.6 Into pandas
A single JSON response is interesting. Three parallel time series for three language editions are more so. We will collect “Public interest” pageviews for English, Spanish, and Hindi Wikipedia and plot them side by side.
import pandas as pd

def fetch_pageviews(project, article, start="2020010100", end="2024123100"):
    url = (
        f"https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/metrics/pageviews/per-article/"
        f"{project}/all-access/user/{article}/monthly/{start}/{end}"
    )
    response = requests.get(url, headers=HEADERS, timeout=30)
    response.raise_for_status()
    items = response.json()["items"]
    df = pd.DataFrame(items)
    df["timestamp"] = pd.to_datetime(df["timestamp"], format="%Y%m%d%H")
    return df[["timestamp", "project", "views"]]

en = fetch_pageviews("en.wikipedia", "Public_interest")
es = fetch_pageviews("es.wikipedia", "Interés_público")
hi = fetch_pageviews("hi.wikipedia", "लोक_हित")

combined = pd.concat([en, es, hi], ignore_index=True)
combined.groupby("project")["views"].describe()
# => project-level descriptive stats across 60 months
Already the choices are political. The English article is titled “Public interest.” The Spanish equivalent is “Interés público.” The Hindi article is “लोक हित,” which translates roughly as “people’s good” rather than “interest.” These are not translations in the sense a machine would call translations; they are conceptually distinct articles that Wikipedia’s interlanguage links happen to join. The fact that all three exist tells you something. The fact that their pageview trajectories differ tells you something else.
Plot them:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
for project, group in combined.groupby("project"):
    ax.plot(group["timestamp"], group["views"], label=project)
ax.set_xlabel("Month")
ax.set_ylabel("Views")
ax.set_title("Wikipedia pageviews: 'Public interest' across three editions")
ax.legend()
fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig("ch01_pageviews.png", dpi=120)
If your plot looks reasonable, congratulations: you have just run an end-to-end public-interest data pipeline. Not a large one, but a real one. It fetches from a public API, handles errors, transforms a JSON response into a DataFrame, and produces a figure that would be legible on the front page of a campus paper or in a policy brief.
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science

title: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science
text:
3.7 Reading the plot
Three things to notice, because they recur.
First, the English series has different seasonality than the Spanish or Hindi series. English “Public interest” spikes in specific months that tend to correspond to U.S. news cycles: data-broker legislation debates, antitrust hearings, election seasons. Spanish and Hindi series have their own spikes that do not align. The concept of a single global “public interest” is a fiction; there are many publics, and their interests peak on different calendars.
Second, all three series are small relative to any major news article. “Public interest” is a reference concept, not a subject of mass curiosity. The size of the audience for public-interest work is not large. It is, however, possible to reach it if you know where it is.
Third, the data will drift. Wikipedia articles get renamed, redirected, merged. The endpoint you called today may return a different trajectory next year because the article itself has changed. Chapter 4 takes up the problem of what can be remembered; this exercise is your first tiny encounter with it.






NoteIn the Public Interest



This chapter’s running example is, on the surface, a technical trifle: three line plots of Wikipedia pageviews. But notice what it required. A public API, documented and open. Endpoints that return consistent, structured data. A retention policy that preserves history for at least a decade. A User-Agent expectation that asks you to identify yourself rather than hiding. Wikipedia built these as deliberate commitments to openness (Chapter 6). If you were trying to do the same exercise against a commercial platform in 2026, you would find most of those commitments have been withdrawn. The pressure of enclosure (Chapter 2) makes the simple thing you just did unusually precious. Part of this book’s work is to help you notice when the simple thing is actually rare.
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science

title: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science
text:
3.8 What you will build
By the end of the book, you will have produced:

A published, documented civic dataset hosted as a Datasette instance (Chapter 6).
A refusal specification for a dataset where refusal is the right answer (Chapter 16).
A disaggregated audit of a public model (Chapter 7), wrapped as a reproducible pipeline (Chapter 11).
A stakeholder map and geographic visualization of a local decision (Chapter 12).
A tested data pipeline with a deliberately-introduced bug and a written post-incident review (Chapter 13).
A class-wide FOIA/CORA tracker with at least one filed request of your own (Chapter 10).
An op-ed draft pitched to a named outlet (Chapter 17).
A report with reproducible figures (Chapter 18).
A research proposal with a data management plan and mock IRB protocol (Chapter 19).
A five-minute testimony statement with written addendum (Chapter 20).
A substantive comment on a live federal rulemaking (Chapter 21).
An archival deposit on the Internet Archive and Zenodo with full metadata (Chapter 22).

A portfolio, in other words. Not an assignment dump. A collection of things you can point to when you apply for a job, a fellowship, a grant, or a seat on a commission, and say: I did this, I published it, here is the permanent link.
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science

title: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science
text:
3.9 Exercises
Exercise 1.1 (Guided). Install Python 3.11+, git, and the packages listed in the environment setup callout. Clone this book’s repository. Create a new branch named yourname/ch01-helloworld. Add a single line to exercises/ch01_helloworld.txt that reads “Hello from .” Commit with the message exercise 1.1: hello world and push the branch. Open a pull request against the course’s fork of the repository. Submit the PR URL to the course instructor. The point of this exercise is not the content. It is that the work mechanism (branch, commit, PR) is your default mode from here forward.
Exercise 1.2 (Guided). Adapt the pageviews script to retrieve monthly views for three Wikipedia articles you care about, across three language editions each. Produce one plot comparing them. Write 150 words interpreting what the plot tells you. Save the plot and writeup to exercises/ch01_pageviews/. Note at least one choice (which article, which language, which date range) where you had to decide something, and record what you decided and why.
Exercise 1.3 (Reflective). Write a 300-word “data obituary” for an API, dataset, or data portal that has been withdrawn, paywalled, or substantially restricted. Examples to consider: the old Twitter API, Reddit’s Pushshift, the federal climate portal’s pre-2017 snapshots, a city open-data portal that shut down. Your obituary should name the data source, say what it was useful for, describe what happened to it, and identify who has been worse off since. Cite two sources.
Exercise 1.4 (Analytic). Find an op-ed or published column about AI or data policy from the last six months. Read it twice. Identify its explicit or implicit theory of change: who is the audience, what action is the piece trying to produce, and what has to be true about that audience for the argument to land? Write 250 words. Do not pick an op-ed you agree with; pick one whose argument you think is doing something politically.
Exercise 1.5 (Open-ended). What is a public-interest data question you want to be able to answer by the end of this book? Write two to three paragraphs: what is the question, who would benefit from the answer, what data would be needed, and what obstacles do you already see. Save it as exercises/ch01_project_sketch.md. You will revisit it every few chapters.
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science

title: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science
text:
3.10 Looking ahead
Chapter 2 begins Part I’s diagnosis of the pressures on public knowledge. It takes the polite User-Agent habit we established here and turns it into a full “responsible scraper” function. More importantly, it asks why polite habits are necessary at all: how the ground of what public-interest work can observe has shifted over the last decade, and what that shift implies for what you should try to do next.
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science

title: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science
text:
3.11 Further Reading and Resources

Brian C. Keegan (2026), “Public interest data infrastructuring,” Information, Communication & Society. The paper this book extends. Start here.
Virginia Eubanks (2018), Automating Inequality. The canonical contemporary text on how data-driven systems reshape welfare, policing, and child services, and why “the public interest” requires attention to whose lives those systems touch.
Tara Dawson McGuinness and Hana Schank (2021), Power to the Public. The book-length articulation of public interest technology, useful as a framing text and as a target for critique (Chapter 14 will take up both).
Mike Ananny and Kate Crawford (2018), “Seeing without knowing: Limitations of the transparency ideal and its application to algorithmic accountability,” New Media & Society, 20(3): 973–989. A useful counterweight to the assumption that transparency is self-executing.
Wikimedia Foundation’s Pageviews API documentation: https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/. The endpoint you used in this chapter, plus siblings you will find handy later.
The Data Journalism Handbook 2, ed. Jonathan Gray and Liliana Bounegru (2021), open-access at https://datajournalism.com/read/handbook/two. A reader’s companion to many of the methods this book will extend.
Hugh Gusterson (2017), “From Brexit to Trump: Anthropology and the rise of nationalist populism,” American Ethnologist 44(2): 209–214. For readers who want a frame for why “the public” has fragmented.
The course syllabus and pull-request template for INFO 46XX, in this book’s repository. Your first assignment is to open one.







Eubanks, Virginia. 2018. Automating Inequality: How High-Tech Tools Profile, Police, and Punish the Poor. St. Martin’s Press.


Jacobs, Jane. 1961. The Death and Life of Great American Cities. Random House.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.
crumbs: 2  Introduction to Public Interest Data Science

title: 3  Enclosure: Who Can Observe?
text:
4 Enclosure: Who Can Observe?
In the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries, across England and Scotland, common pastures and woodlands that had been used collectively for generations were fenced off by landowners and Parliament. The legal instruments were workmanlike. The consequences were not. Villagers who had grazed sheep on the commons, gathered firewood from shared stands, and gleaned fallen grain from open fields found themselves dependent on wage labor, migrating to cities, or both. Enclosure was not a failure of the commons. It was a project, by those with power, to convert shared resources into private ones, often with the language of improvement attached.
James Boyle (2017) argued two decades ago that a “second enclosure movement” was underway, this time over ideas, code, and data. Patents expanded. Copyright terms stretched. Public-domain materials were fenced behind paywalls and click-through agreements. That framing remains useful, but the enclosure most relevant to this book has moved past ideas and into observation itself. The question public interest data science asks is not only what can I license? but what can I see in the first place? The answer, for a researcher in the mid-2020s, is less than it was a decade ago, and the trajectory is not accidental. Following Keegan (2026), you can read the withdrawal of research access as an attack on the installed base introduced in Chapter 3: when linkability, interpretability, and safe scrutiny are removed, “public interest” becomes a slogan without an operational grip.

title: 3  Enclosure: Who Can Observe?
text:
4.1 The last ten years, briefly
The easiest way to feel enclosure as a data scientist is to list retrievals you could have done ten years ago and try them today. A decent starter list:

Pull one percent of public tweets through the Twitter streaming API, filtered by keyword, for a dissertation on political discourse.
Fetch the complete comment history of a subreddit from Pushshift, with timestamps and scores, for a study of moderation.
Download the pre-2017 version of the EPA’s environmental-justice screening data, with the variable definitions as of that date.
Query Facebook’s Graph API for public-page posts about a municipal election.
Retrieve CrowdTangle data on viral content during a disaster.

Each was a routine graduate-student assignment within the last decade. None is routine now. Some are technically possible through partnerships most researchers cannot access. Some are impossible. Some have been replaced by expensive commercial products whose terms forbid the reuse the original data supported. Bruns (2019) called this shift the APIcalypse: platforms that depended on researcher goodwill in their growth phase have, in their extraction phase, decided that goodwill is an expensive luxury.
Twitter (now X) is the sharpest case because the timeline is easiest to reconstruct. From 2006 through early 2023, the platform’s API included a credible research tier, and an enormous empirical literature grew out of it. In February 2023 the company announced that free API access would end; the replacement pricing tiers start in the tens of thousands of dollars per month for the volumes a real research project requires. Academics who had built longitudinal panels over a decade watched them become unrenewable.
Reddit’s trajectory followed, faster and with more open advocacy against researchers. Pushshift, a volunteer-run archive that had collected and served Reddit data since 2015, shut down its public API in April 2023 after Reddit revoked its credentials. Reddit’s own API pricing, announced weeks later, pushed third-party clients (including accessibility tools like Apollo) out of the ecosystem and put researcher access behind tiers that, for any nontrivial use, meant six-figure bills. Gaffney and Matias (2018) had already documented silent gaps in Pushshift’s mirror, which compounded the loss: not only is the new data expensive, the old data you thought you had is incomplete, and you cannot fill it in.
Government data portals are a quieter case and, for that reason, one you should watch more carefully. When a federal agency reorganizes, variable names change. When a political appointee prefers different framings, dataset landing pages are rewritten. When budgets contract, legacy portals stop updating and older vintages are “archived,” which is sometimes a euphemism for delisted. The 2017 withdrawal of climate-change pages from the EPA website was the loud case; the quiet cases happen constantly, and Chapter 6 treats the failure mode in detail. Enclosure and erosion are siblings: one restricts access, the other dissolves the object of access, and both shift the ground under public-interest work.
The political economy underneath these cases is not mysterious. Wu (2021) describes the current configuration as a new Gilded Age of concentrated platform power. A handful of firms hold the data that describes contemporary public life. Their incentives run toward monetizing that data through advertising, model training, and partnerships, and away from letting outsiders audit it. “Partnership” is doing a lot of work in that sentence. It means the firms will sometimes share data with other firms, with favored labs, and with governments, under NDAs. It almost never means they will share it with journalists, civil-society researchers, or the public.

title: 3  Enclosure: Who Can Observe?
section: 4.2 Reading what the platform tells you you can do
text:
4.2 Reading what the platform tells you you can do
Before you write any code, read what the platform says about your presence. There are two documents every serious scraper consults: robots.txt and the site’s Terms of Service. Neither is a law. Both are signals, and you ignore them at your peril, both ethically and practically.
A robots.txt file is served at the root of a domain and tells automated agents which paths are and are not welcome. The Robots Exclusion Protocol dates to 1994 and was standardized as RFC 9309 in 2022. It lives at https://example.com/robots.txt. Fetching it is a one-line task:
import urllib.robotparser

rp = urllib.robotparser.RobotFileParser()
rp.set_url("https://en.wikipedia.org/robots.txt")
rp.read()

rp.can_fetch("PublicInterestDataScience/0.1", "/wiki/Public_interest")
# => True

rp.can_fetch("PublicInterestDataScience/0.1",
             "/w/index.php?title=Special:Export")
# => False (or True; check what Wikipedia's file actually says today)
Python’s standard library parser returns True or False for a given path and user agent. It will also surface the crawl-delay the site suggests, if one is present, and the sitemap URL if the file advertises one.
Terms of Service are the harder document because they are written by lawyers who assume you will not read them. Researchers have lived in the shadow of the U.S. Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA) on this point, because for a long time it was unclear whether violating a ToS could be prosecuted as “unauthorized access” under the statute. The Sandvig v. Barr litigation (American Civil Liberties Union 2019) established in 2020 that academic research violating a ToS is not by that fact alone a criminal CFAA violation. That is a meaningful ruling and not a blanket license. You can still be sued in civil court, banned from the platform, served with a cease-and-desist, or (if your institution values its industry relationships) quietly asked to stop. Read the ToS, document what you read, and know which clauses you are relying on and which you are departing from and why.






TipThe Missing Manual



A request from requests.get(url) with no User-Agent header does not arrive at a modern server as “anonymous.” It arrives as python-requests/2.31.0, which is the default and is the single fastest way to get 403’d by a CDN that has blocklisted it by policy. Cloudflare, Fastly, and Akamai maintain lists of default agents used by common scraping libraries. Set a descriptive User-Agent that names your project and a contact email. If the server operator needs to tell you to stop, you want them to be able to email you rather than silently ban your subnet. A polite scraper is also a scraper that keeps running.

title: 3  Enclosure: Who Can Observe?
text:
4.3 A responsible scraper, from scratch
You will reuse the function below, in one form or another, across the remaining chapters. The design goals are plain: every request identifies itself, respects robots.txt by default, rate-limits, retries on transient errors, and fails loudly on permanent ones.
import time
import logging
from urllib.parse import urlparse
from urllib.robotparser import RobotFileParser

import requests

log = logging.getLogger("responsible_scraper")

DEFAULT_UA = (
    "PublicInterestDataScience/0.1 "
    "(+https://github.com/yourname/pids-book; you@example.edu)"
)


def check_robots(url, user_agent=DEFAULT_UA):
    """Return True if robots.txt allows user_agent to fetch url."""
    parsed = urlparse(url)
    robots_url = f"{parsed.scheme}://{parsed.netloc}/robots.txt"
    rp = RobotFileParser()
    try:
        rp.set_url(robots_url)
        rp.read()
    except Exception as exc:
        log.warning("Could not read %s: %s", robots_url, exc)
        # Conservative default: if we can't read robots.txt, assume we
        # should not fetch. Override explicitly if you know better.
        return False
    return rp.can_fetch(user_agent, url)


def polite_get(
    url,
    headers=None,
    params=None,
    user_agent=DEFAULT_UA,
    min_interval=1.0,
    max_retries=3,
    backoff=2.0,
    timeout=30,
    respect_robots=True,
):
    """Fetch a URL with rate limiting, retries, and a named User-Agent.

    Returns a requests.Response on success.
    Raises requests.HTTPError on non-retryable failure.
    """
    if respect_robots and not check_robots(url, user_agent=user_agent):
        raise PermissionError(f"robots.txt disallows fetching {url}")

    hdrs = {"User-Agent": user_agent, "Accept": "application/json"}
    if headers:
        hdrs.update(headers)

    last_err = None
    for attempt in range(1, max_retries + 1):
        try:
            response = requests.get(
                url, headers=hdrs, params=params, timeout=timeout
            )
        except requests.RequestException as exc:
            last_err = exc
            log.warning("Attempt %d: network error %s", attempt, exc)
        else:
            if response.status_code in (429, 500, 502, 503, 504):
                log.warning(
                    "Attempt %d: status %s from %s",
                    attempt, response.status_code, url,
                )
                last_err = requests.HTTPError(response=response)
            else:
                response.raise_for_status()
                time.sleep(min_interval)
                return response
        time.sleep(backoff ** attempt)

    raise last_err or RuntimeError("polite_get exhausted retries")
A few design choices are worth naming. The function accepts a respect_robots flag that defaults to True. You should need a reason to flip it, and you should write that reason into a comment in the calling code, not just your head. The function treats 429 and 5xx responses as retryable with exponential backoff, and 4xx responses as permanent failures. It sleeps a minimum interval after every successful call, which is the crudest form of rate limiting and the one that gets you the furthest. A production version would honor the Retry-After header, cache responses to disk, and emit structured logs. Your exercises will push you toward those features.
Using it looks like any other requests call, with a politer footprint:
response = polite_get(
    "https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/metrics/pageviews/per-article/"
    "en.wikipedia/all-access/user/Enclosure/monthly/"
    "2015010100/2024123100"
)
items = response.json()["items"]
If you want to scrape a site whose robots.txt disallows you, stop and think. Sometimes there is a legitimate scholarly reason to proceed (a platform has disallowed a path that is uncontroversially public, a journalism project has counsel’s blessing, a court order is in hand). More often the disallow signals a norm you should respect. The function makes respecting it the default. Refusal, as Chapter 18 will develop, runs in both directions: platforms can refuse researchers, and researchers can refuse requests even when a platform invites them. Zong and Matias (2024) treat refusal by data subjects as a legitimate counter-move within an asymmetric information environment.

title: 3  Enclosure: Who Can Observe?
text:
4.4 A worked timeline: Reddit access, 2015–2024
Pick one platform and write its access history as a structured timeline. The exercise forces you to treat enclosure as a series of concrete decisions rather than a diffuse climate. Reddit is useful because the dates are public and the consequences are well-documented. The table below is a first pass; Exercise 2.4 asks you to extend and verify it.








Year
Event
Consequence for researchers




2015
Pushshift begins continuous archiving of Reddit submissions and comments.
A reasonably complete corpus of Reddit becomes queryable through a free API.


2018
Gaffney and Matias (2018) document silent gaps in the Pushshift mirror.
Researchers learn the archive is incomplete; many published studies rest on the incomplete slice.


2020
COVID-19 drives a surge in computational social science using Reddit data.
Demand on Pushshift grows; the corpus becomes de facto infrastructure for a subfield.


April 2023
Reddit revokes Pushshift’s API credentials. Reddit announces API pricing.
The public Pushshift API goes dark. Third-party clients (Apollo, Reddit Enhancement Suite) exit or retreat.


June 2023
Large subreddits go private in protest (“the blackout”).
Moderator labor, the free substrate of Reddit’s data, becomes briefly legible as labor.


Late 2023
Reddit signs data-licensing deal with Google for model training.
Data refused to researchers is sold, as training data, to a firm that can afford it.


2024
Reddit files for IPO; discloses API licensing as revenue.
The economic logic of enclosure is explicit in the S-1: researcher access was competing with shareholder value.



Two observations follow from the table. First, the shutdown was not only a loss of new data. It was a loss of old data, because the archive that held the historical corpus lost the credential it needed to keep serving what it had already collected. The object of study and the infrastructure of study collapsed together. Chapter 24 pushes you toward archival deposits you control rather than ones you rent.
Second, the same data the platform withdrew from researchers was, in the same calendar year, sold to a commercial AI firm. That is the asymmetry to sit with. Enclosure is not simply “the platform closed up.” It is “the platform closed you out while opening further to capital.” Acker and Kreisberg (2019) saw this pattern coming in their work on social-media archives; Suzor and colleagues (2018) locate it within a broader problem of platform governance legitimacy.






NoteIn the Public Interest



What is at stake here is openness as defined in Chapter 8: the condition under which a public can scrutinize the systems that affect it. Openness is not a synonym for “the data is downloadable.” It is a working arrangement in which researchers, journalists, regulators, and affected communities can observe enough to raise evidence-based questions. When platforms cut off independent researchers while retaining partnerships with large firms and governments, they do not abolish observation. They reallocate it. Observation continues, but it flows toward private interests and away from public ones. Naming this as an openness failure, rather than a business decision, is the first move that lets you push back. Chapter 9 and Chapter 10 extend the argument into regulatory and distributional registers.

title: 3  Enclosure: Who Can Observe?
text:
4.5 The asymmetry of observation
The previous two sections should have left you with an uncomfortable picture. The firms that hold the data describing contemporary public life can observe their users in extraordinary detail. They partner with other firms and with governments to extend that observation. They cut off the outside parties whose scrutiny would be inconvenient. The parties who remain (journalists, academics, civil-society groups) are left with robots.txt files, HTML-scraped fragments, and the occasional leaked dataset. The asymmetry is not between observers and the unobserved. It is between two classes of observers.
That asymmetry has consequences for what you should try to build. First, assume your access is temporary and plan for its withdrawal: Chapter 7 and Chapter 24 push you toward architectures where your data has a resolvable identifier and a home that outlives any single platform. Second, be honest about the limits of what you can observe: a study that conditions on platform-provided data is a study of what the platform chose to show you, and naming that is part of the work rather than a methods-section apology. Third, refusal is available. If the terms of access are corrosive (an NDA that prevents you from naming the firm, an exclusivity clause that bars you from sharing derived data with colleagues), you can decline, and Chapter 18 will argue that sometimes you should.
Public-interest data science cannot rely exclusively on large commercial platforms. The installed base this book is building toward includes government open data, civic datasets, court records, FOIA-wrested documents, historical archives, and the output of researchers willing to publish collected data under permissive licenses. Each of those sources has its own pressures (Chapter 3 takes up exemption, Chapter 4 takes up erosion), but none of them has an S-1 that treats your access as lost revenue. Diversifying your substrate of observation is a strategic response to enclosure.

title: 3  Enclosure: Who Can Observe?
text:
4.6 Exercises
Exercise 2.1 (Guided). Fetch and read the robots.txt files for three platforms. Recommended triple: Wikipedia, Reddit, and a large newspaper site (for example, nytimes.com). Use urllib.robotparser to check whether your User-Agent would be permitted on five paths per site. Write a 200-word comparison: which site is most permissive, which most restrictive, and which surprised you.
Exercise 2.2 (Guided). Implement the polite_get function from scratch, without copying the listing above. Add one feature the book’s version lacks: either honor the Retry-After header on 429 responses, or cache successful responses to a local SQLite database keyed by URL so that repeated fetches during development do not re-hit the server. Commit the function with a docstring explaining your added feature and a test that exercises it against a public endpoint.
Exercise 2.3 (Analytic). Pick one platform whose data you expect to use in your final project. Download its current Terms of Service to a dated file (for example, exercises/ch02_tos/reddit_2026-04-23.txt). Annotate paragraph by paragraph with four marks: R for clauses that restrict research uses, P for clauses that permit them, A for ambiguity you would need counsel to resolve, and L for language that relies on unstable definitions (“public content,” “non-commercial,” “API partner”). Submit the annotated file and a 300-word summary of what a research plan can and cannot do under the current ToS.
Exercise 2.4 (Analytic). Extend the Reddit timeline. Add three rows from publicly reported events since 2024, with citations. Then write a second timeline, in the same format, for a different platform (Twitter/X, Facebook, TikTok, YouTube, or a government data portal). Cover at least eight years and at least five distinct access events. Cite the source for each date; prefer primary documents (official announcements, court filings, congressional testimony) over secondary reporting where both exist.
Exercise 2.5 (Open-ended). Revisit the project sketch from Exercise 1.5. Identify the single dataset whose availability would most determine whether your project is possible. Write a 500-word accessibility memo covering: who controls the data; under what terms it is accessible; what pathways exist (public download, API, FOIA request, institutional partnership, commercial license); the cost and timeline of each pathway; what has happened to the data’s accessibility over the last five years; and what fallback datasets you would use if the primary became unavailable. Save as exercises/ch02_access_memo.md. You will return to this memo in Chapter 19 and Chapter 22.

title: 3  Enclosure: Who Can Observe?
text:
4.7 Looking ahead
Enclosure is the first of three pressures this part diagnoses. Chapter 3 takes up exemption: even when data can be observed, certain actors are under no obligation to answer for what it describes. Law-enforcement agencies exempt from FOIA. Vendors whose contracts shield them from disclosure. Private actors performing public functions without the accountability attached. If enclosure asks who can observe?, exemption asks who must answer?

title: 3  Enclosure: Who Can Observe?
text:
4.8 Further Reading and Resources

Axel Bruns (2019), “After the ‘APIcalypse’: Social media platforms and their fight against critical scholarly research,” Information, Communication & Society 22(11). The article that gave the post-2018 shift its name.
James Boyle (2008), The Public Domain: Enclosing the Commons of the Mind. Duke University Press. Open-access at https://www.thepublicdomain.org/. The enclosure analogy in its fullest form.
Robots Exclusion Protocol, RFC 9309 (2022): https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9309.html. A short, readable standardization most scrapers violate out of ignorance.
Jonathan Zong and J. Nathan Matias (2024), “Data refusal from below.” The complement to this chapter: when enclosure is not the problem, refusal might be.
Reddit’s API pricing announcement (June 2023) and the original Pushshift shutdown notice, both reachable from https://www.reddit.com/r/modnews/ archives. Primary documents for Exercise 2.4.
The Internet Archive’s Wayback Machine, https://web.archive.org/. When a platform retires a dataset, often the only remaining record of what was public. You will use it constantly.
Electronic Frontier Foundation, “Coders’ Rights Project,” https://www.eff.org/issues/coders. Plain-language summaries of the CFAA, DMCA, and related statutes.
Knight First Amendment Institute, https://knightcolumbia.org/research. Ongoing litigation and policy work on the legal scaffolding of observation.
Wikimedia Foundation’s research ethics and tool policies, https://meta.wikimedia.org/wiki/Research:Ethics. An instructive contrast: what a platform looks like when it wants researchers to study it.







Acker, Amelia, and Adam Kreisberg. 2019. “Social Media Data Archives in an API-Driven World.” Archival Science.


American Civil Liberties Union. 2019. Sandvig v. Barr: Researchers’ Right to Investigate Algorithmic Discrimination. ACLU. https://www.aclu.org/cases/sandvig-v-barr-challenge-cfaa-prohibition-uncovering-racial-discrimination-online.


Boyle, James. 2017. “The Public Domain: Enclosing the Commons of the Mind.” Yale Law Journal.


Bruns, Axel. 2019. “After the ‘APIcalypse’: Social Media Platforms and Their Fight Against Critical Scholarly Research.” Information, Communication & Society 22 (11): 1544–66.


Gaffney, Devin, and J. Nathan Matias. 2018. “Caveat Emptor, Computational Social Science: Large-Scale Missing Data in a Widely-Published Reddit Corpus.” PLoS ONE 13 (7): e0200162.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.


Suzor, Nicolas P., Tess Van Geelen, and Sarah Myers West. 2018. “Evaluating the Legitimacy of Platform Governance: A Review of Research and a Shared Research Agenda.” International Communication Gazette 80 (4): 385–405.


Wu, Tim. 2021. “The Curse of Bigness: Antitrust in the New Gilded Age.” Columbia Law Review.


Zong, Jonathan, and J. Nathan Matias. 2024. “Data Refusal from Below: A Framework for Resisting Institutional Data Harms.” ACM Journal on Responsible Computing.

title: 4  Exemption: Who Must Answer?
text:
5 Exemption: Who Must Answer?
Chapter 2 described a world in which what a researcher can observe is shrinking. This chapter is about a related but distinct problem. Suppose you can observe the outputs of a system. They are already on a published spreadsheet, or in a press release, or scrolling through a caseworker’s terminal. Who is obliged to explain how they were produced? Who has to answer when they go wrong? In the United States in the mid-2020s, the honest answer for a remarkable share of consequential automated decisions is: no one with enforceable authority, on any clock a parent or a journalist or a state legislator can use.
Enclosure asks who can observe? Exemption asks who must answer? Enclosure operates through the terms of access to data. Exemption operates through trade-secret law, procurement contracts, qualified immunity, sovereign immunity, statutory carve-outs from freedom-of-information regimes, and the simple fact that many public agencies run proprietary software they did not build and cannot fully inspect. Pasquale (2016) named this the black box society: a condition in which the scoring and sorting machinery that organizes credit, insurance, hiring, and policing is legally and practically sealed off from the people it sorts. A decade later, the black box has migrated further into the machinery of the state itself. When the exempt actor is a commercial platform, you can at least pretend to buy your way in or shame your way in. When the exempt actor is the state, using a vendor’s system to decide who gets a benefit, the pretense collapses.
This chapter takes up two cases. The long example is the Allegheny Family Screening Tool (AFST), a risk-scoring system used by the Allegheny County, Pennsylvania child-welfare hotline since 2016. AFST is unusually well-documented for a government-deployed risk score, and you will build an evidence ledger for it from public sources. The short comparison is the Dutch state’s Systeem Risico Indicatie (SyRI) and the childcare-benefits fraud detection system that prompted the toeslagenaffaire scandal. The Dutch cases matter because they are the rare examples in which a national court found an automated decision system unlawful and shut it down (District Court of The Hague 2020). Reading the U.S. case against the Dutch one tells you which claims about opacity have purchase, and in which legal climates.

title: 4  Exemption: Who Must Answer?
text:
5.1 Two kinds of exemption
Separate legal exemption from practical exemption before you go near a keyboard.
Legal exemption is the carved-out space where disclosure obligations do not reach. Trade-secret protections under the Defend Trade Secrets Act and state analogues travel with a vendor into a public agency’s procurement file. FOIA exemptions 4 (trade secrets) and 7 (law enforcement) are routinely invoked to withhold model specifications, training data, and performance audits. The Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA) historically chilled independent testing: until Sandvig v. Barr (American Civil Liberties Union 2019), academic auditors faced credible threat of federal criminal prosecution for creating test accounts that violated a platform’s terms. Sandvig narrowed that risk for academic research but did not eliminate civil exposure and did not reach state-level computer crime laws. Terms-of-service bindings tie back to Chapter 4: what the vendor’s lawyers wrote at procurement time continues to govern what the public can ask later.
Practical exemption is the larger space. Even where the law would require disclosure, the route is slow, fragmented, and under-enforced. A reporter who files a state open-records request for a vendor contract waits six months and receives a PDF with most of its text redacted. A parent whose family was screened out of services reads a denial letter citing an “assessment” without naming the model, the version, the features, or the threshold. The information exists. Nobody is obliged to aggregate it. Exemption does not require secrecy. It only requires that the cost of reconstructing the picture exceed what a citizen, a reporter, or an underfunded NGO can afford. Fragmentation is the weapon.






NoteIn the Public Interest



Oversight, as Chapter 9 will develop it, is not a property of a single document. It is a property of the linkage between documents: a procurement contract that names a vendor, a model card that describes that vendor’s system, a FOIA response that discloses subgroup performance, a court filing that tests a denied claim. Exemption thrives on fragmentation because each artifact taken alone is plausible. The contract does not mention the model by name. The model card does not mention the contract. The denial letter mentions neither. Keegan (2026) calls this the linkability element of the installed base, and without it, oversight is a collection of disconnected complaints. Public-interest data science is, in part, the patient reconstruction of linkages the system was designed to leave broken.

title: 4  Exemption: Who Must Answer?
section: 5.2 Allegheny Family Screening Tool, as a public object
text:
5.2 Allegheny Family Screening Tool, as a public object
Allegheny County’s Office of Children, Youth and Families receives roughly fifteen thousand child-welfare referrals a year. Since August 2016, hotline call screeners have seen, alongside the case narrative, a risk score produced by the Allegheny Family Screening Tool. The score estimates the probability that a child named in the referral will be re-referred, removed from the home, or placed in foster care within a defined window. A screener uses the score as one input in deciding whether to open a formal investigation.
Eubanks (2018) treated AFST in Automating Inequality as a paradigmatic case of a predictive instrument that extends state surveillance into poor families under the rubric of decision support. The county and the research team have disputed some characterizations and affirmed others. AFST is unusual in that a reader who wants to know how the system works can assemble a nontrivial picture from public sources. It is also typical in that the picture is incomplete, and the incompleteness is not random.

title: 4  Exemption: Who Must Answer?
text:
5.3 The evidence ledger
The technique is deliberately unglamorous. Read every public document you can find about the system. For each, record what it tells you and, just as importantly, what it does not. The pandas DataFrame below is an evidence ledger: a structured record of provenance that makes gaps visible on the same footing as findings.
import pandas as pd

rows = [
    {
        "source": "Allegheny County RFP and vendor selection memo",
        "date": "2014-11", "type": "procurement",
        "what_it_tells_us": "Names Auckland University of Technology's "
            "Centre for Social Data Analytics as the selected team; "
            "lists the data systems to be integrated.",
        "what_it_doesnt": "Does not disclose the price, the model "
            "family, or the performance targets the vendor was "
            "expected to meet.",
    },
    {
        "source": "Vaithianathan et al. methodology report",
        "date": "2019-04", "type": "technical_report",
        "what_it_tells_us": "Describes feature engineering on 131 "
            "variables from the county data warehouse; reports AUC "
            "and calibration on historical data.",
        "what_it_doesnt": "Does not disclose the production decision "
            "threshold, the retraining cadence, or subgroup "
            "performance on race and ethnicity as deployed.",
    },
    {
        "source": "Chouldechova et al., FAccT 2018",
        "date": "2018-02", "type": "peer_reviewed",
        "what_it_tells_us": "Subgroup accuracy and error-rate "
            "disparity analysis on a research version of the model.",
        "what_it_doesnt": "Research version is not identical to the "
            "fielded tool; ground-truth labels are proxies, which "
            "the authors acknowledge.",
    },
    {
        "source": "Eubanks, Automating Inequality, ch. 4",
        "date": "2018-01", "type": "book",
        "what_it_tells_us": "Situates AFST within the county's "
            "broader data integration project; documents community "
            "concerns and the surveillance/referral feedback loop.",
        "what_it_doesnt": "Cannot speak to model changes after 2017 "
            "fieldwork; the tool has been revised multiple times.",
    },
    {
        "source": "Associated Press / Ho and Burke",
        "date": "2022-04", "type": "press",
        "what_it_tells_us": "Reports DOJ civil-rights inquiry into "
            "disability-based discrimination in the tool's deployment.",
        "what_it_doesnt": "DOJ inquiry materials are not public; we "
            "learn an inquiry exists but not what it found.",
    },
]

evidence = pd.DataFrame(rows)
evidence["date"] = pd.to_datetime(evidence["date"])
evidence = evidence.sort_values("date").reset_index(drop=True)
# => 5 rows, columns: source, date, type, what_it_tells_us, what_it_doesnt

gaps = evidence[["date", "source", "what_it_doesnt"]]
gaps.to_csv("afst_gaps.csv", index=False)
Five rows is not a lot. That is the point. A mature ledger for a deployed municipal system might contain thirty or forty rows, organized by procurement, technical documentation, peer review, press, litigation, and community record. The two columns matter. what_it_tells_us is the affirmative claim you can source-cite. what_it_doesnt is the negative space that tells you what you would still need to file, subpoena, or discover in order to make a stronger claim. A one-column ledger is a bibliography. A two-column ledger is the beginning of an accountability argument.
Two observations follow. The most technically detailed document (Vaithianathan and colleagues’ methodology report (Vaithianathan et al. 2019)) is useful for understanding the research-time model but silent on the deployed threshold and retraining schedule. The most politically charged finding (the DOJ civil-rights inquiry reported by Ho and Burke (2022)) is the one where the underlying record is least available: we know an inquiry opened, not what it concluded.






TipThe Missing Manual



The model card does not say. The methodology report does not say. The procurement memo does not say. Those three silences are themselves data. A standard failure mode of algorithmic-accountability writing is to treat the absence as a rhetorical flourish (“notably, the county does not disclose…”) and move on. Do not move on. Write the absence into the ledger as an object with a date, a source, and a what_it_doesnt entry. You will use it later when you file a records request, when you testify, or when you draft a rulemaking comment. An absence that has a citation is an absence you can litigate. An absence you only gestured at in prose is a complaint.

title: 4  Exemption: Who Must Answer?
section: 5.4 Reading the deployed model from the outside
text:
5.4 Reading the deployed model from the outside
The ledger tells you what documentation exists. It does not tell you how the model is behaving today. For a deployed system, three kinds of external inference are available without breaking any law.
The first is decision-record inspection. Some jurisdictions, including Allegheny County at various points, release aggregate screening statistics: calls screened in, screened out, and referred, broken down by some subset of demographic variables. Those aggregates let you compute, crudely, whether the score’s introduction shifted selection rates overall. They do not let you recover individual decisions.
The second is disparate-impact analysis on outcomes. If you can obtain downstream placement data (via FOIA, a collaborator inside the agency, academic partnership), you can estimate whether scored referrals have different outcomes by race, age, or disability than unscored referrals. The Chouldechova and colleagues FAccT paper (Chouldechova et al. 2018) is an example of this kind of work on a pre-deployment version.
The third is audit-by-adversarial-use: submitting test cases through the live interface. CFAA exposure still matters here. You would need counsel’s sign-off, institutional cover, and a clear understanding of which Sandvig protections reach your facts. Most municipal child-welfare systems do not expose an API you could query, which is why audit-by-use is rare in this domain and common in hiring and advertising contexts.
# Illustrative skeleton only. Do not run against a live agency system.
def compute_selection_rates(decisions):
    """Aggregate screen-in rates by demographic group.

    Input: one row per referral with columns
        {referral_id, group, score, screened_in}.
    """
    g = decisions.groupby("group").agg(
        n=("referral_id", "count"),
        screened_in=("screened_in", "sum"),
    )
    g["rate"] = g["screened_in"] / g["n"]
    return g.reset_index()

# Expected output columns: group, n, screened_in, rate
None of this code is the audit. The code is the scaffolding you build so that, when a data-sharing agreement is in place or an agency publishes aggregates, you have somewhere to put the numbers. The substantive work is upstream: negotiating the sharing agreement, filing the records request, obtaining IRB approval. Chapter 12 takes up the legal scaffolding in detail.

title: 4  Exemption: Who Must Answer?
section: 5.5 When the state is the adversary: SyRI and after
text:
5.5 When the state is the adversary: SyRI and after
The Dutch case inverts a habit U.S. readers bring to these problems. In the United States, the usual adversary in an algorithmic accountability story is a commercial vendor whose system has been adopted by a public agency. The vendor holds the trade-secret claim; the agency carries the disclosure obligation; advocates play them against each other. In SyRI, the adversary was the state itself.
Systeem Risico Indicatie was a cross-agency data-integration tool that scored households for fraud risk across welfare, taxation, labor, and housing data. It was deployed in targeted neighborhoods beginning in 2014. In February 2020, the District Court of The Hague ruled that SyRI violated Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights because the state’s interest in fraud detection did not justify the opacity and breadth of the system (District Court of The Hague 2020). The ruling did not turn on the accuracy of the model. It turned on the state’s failure to make the system legible enough for citizens to understand and challenge how they were being assessed. Dutch scholars reading the aftermath (Arruda and van Schendel 2024) note that successor systems have adapted the legal wrapper without fully abandoning the underlying practice, and that the toeslagenaffaire childcare-benefits scandal exposed a parallel, more damaging pattern in the tax administration.
Read the childcare-benefits scandal alongside AFST. Amnesty International’s report (Amnesty International 2021) documents how the Dutch tax authority used a risk-classification model that flagged dual-nationality applicants at elevated rates, triggering aggressive clawbacks that pushed tens of thousands of families into debt and, in many cases, into losing their children to state care. The Rutte cabinet eventually resigned over it. Opacity was not incidental to the harm: families could not learn why they had been flagged, which made it impossible to contest individual decisions or to surface the pattern until journalism and parliamentary inquiry reconstructed it from outside (Geiger and Braun 2023).
Three contrasts with AFST matter. First, the Dutch regime provided a forum (the Article 8 claim) in which system-level opacity could be litigated directly, rather than through the U.S. habit of challenging individual denials. Second, the affected class was organized enough, through civil-society groups and parliamentary allies, to press the claim to a national court. Third, the remedy was shutdown, not disclosure: the court did not order SyRI to open its model. It ordered the state to stop running it. Chapter 12 asks under what U.S. doctrinal conditions an equivalent remedy would be available, and the answer is: under very few, and none at the federal level as of this writing.






TipThe Missing Manual



When the state is the exempt actor, strategies that assume the state is a neutral arbiter between citizen and vendor stop working. Most algorithmic-accountability tooling (model cards, datasheets for datasets, fairness audits) is designed for a world in which the state is the party demanding accountability from a vendor. In the SyRI and toeslagenaffaire cases, the party deploying the system, the party holding the data, and the party refusing disclosure were all the same ministry. The governance textbook does not cover that case, because the governance textbook was written by people employed by the ministry. Notice when you are inside that scenario, and notice that your methods have to change.

title: 4  Exemption: Who Must Answer?
section: 5.6 Practical exemption as a design choice
text:
5.6 Practical exemption as a design choice
Exemption is not fate. It is a set of design and procurement choices that can be made differently. The European Union’s 2024 AI Act, New York City’s Local Law 144, and a growing list of state-level algorithmic-accountability bills are attempts to legislate a floor under transparency. Whether any produces working oversight depends on the same six elements Chapter 7 will name: linkable records, interpretable categories, persistent evidence, safe scrutiny, authority that can act, and remedy that reaches the affected. A statute that requires a “model card” without specifying linkage to deployment records produces compliance artifacts and no oversight.
The public-interest response to exemption has two parts. The first is the patient work this chapter has rehearsed: building evidence ledgers that make absences visible, linking artifacts across silos, naming the gaps in terms a lawyer or a legislator can use. The second is designing forward: when you have authority to shape a procurement, a contract, or a rulemaking, writing linkability in as a requirement rather than a nice-to-have. Chapter 9 and Chapter 24 develop both moves.

title: 4  Exemption: Who Must Answer?
text:
5.7 Exercises
Exercise 3.1 (Guided). Extend the AFST evidence ledger from five rows to at least twelve. Candidate sources: the Allegheny County impact evaluation reports published between 2019 and 2024, at least one state-level audit or oversight report, the research protocol that governed the tool’s introduction, any litigation related to the tool, and community-organization responses. For each new row, fill both what_it_tells_us and what_it_doesnt. Save as exercises/ch03_afst_ledger.csv and write a 300-word summary of the three largest gaps.
Exercise 3.2 (Guided). Pick a different deployed automated decision system: a state’s Medicaid eligibility system, a city’s pretrial risk instrument, a school district’s early-warning system, a federal agency’s fraud-detection tool. Build an evidence ledger with at least eight rows using the same schema. Save as exercises/ch03_<system>_ledger.csv. In 400 words, compare the shape of the absences to the AFST ledger: what documents exist for one system but not the other, and what does that asymmetry suggest about which actors face the most pressure to produce records?
Exercise 3.3 (Analytic). Read the SyRI judgment (ECLI:NL:RBDHA:2020:1878), available in English translation through Rechtspraak.nl. Identify the specific legal theory the court used to find the system unlawful. In 500 words, summarize the theory in your own words, name the treaty article or statute on which it rested, and explain whether an equivalent argument would be available in a U.S. federal court. If you are not sure, name the sources of your uncertainty.
Exercise 3.4 (Comparative). Pair a U.S. automated decision system and a European one addressing similar policy problems (child welfare, unemployment fraud, immigration risk, predictive policing). Build a side-by-side transparency comparison covering: vendor disclosure, model documentation, performance reporting, subgroup auditing, notice to affected persons, appeal pathways, external oversight, and publicly documented harms. Submit as exercises/ch03_comparison.md. Identify the single transparency commitment that would be most transformative if transplanted from one regime to the other, and say what would prevent that transplant.
Exercise 3.5 (Open-ended). For the system you chose in Exercise 3.2, propose what would need to change for it to be observable in this chapter’s sense. Write a 1,000-word memo addressed to a named accountability actor (a specific state legislator, a city auditor, a named reporter, an advocacy organization). Specify which changes require legislation, which require agency rulemaking, which require litigation, and which could be achieved through procurement reform alone. Save as exercises/ch03_observability_memo.md. You will reuse this memo in Chapter 12 and, revised, as a public comment in Chapter 23.

title: 4  Exemption: Who Must Answer?
text:
5.8 Looking ahead
Enclosure takes data out of reach. Exemption keeps the people and institutions that use data out of reach. Chapter 4 takes up the third pressure in Part I: erosion. Even when data is accessible and decision-makers are nominally accountable, the record of what happened can dissolve. Databases are reorganized without migration paths. Web pages are silently edited. Legacy systems are decommissioned faster than their contents can be archived. If enclosure asks who can observe? and exemption asks who must answer?, erosion asks what can be remembered?

title: 4  Exemption: Who Must Answer?
text:
5.9 Further Reading and Resources

Frank Pasquale (2016), The Black Box Society. Harvard University Press. The foundational treatment of scored decision-making and the legal architecture of its opacity.
Virginia Eubanks (2018), Automating Inequality. St. Martin’s Press. Chapter 4 treats AFST in ethnographic depth. Read with and against the research team’s published responses.
Amnesty International (2021), Xenophobic Machines, https://www.amnesty.org/en/documents/eur35/4686/2021/en/. The canonical documentation of the toeslagenaffaire.
District Court of The Hague (2020), SyRI judgment, ECLI:NL:RBDHA:2020:1878. Read with an English translation alongside the Dutch original.
Lighthouse Reports / WIRED (2023), “Inside the Suspicion Machine,” https://www.lighthousereports.com/investigation/inside-the-suspicion-machine/. Reverse-engineered feature importance for Rotterdam’s welfare-fraud scoring.
Allegheny County Department of Human Services reports, https://www.alleghenycountyanalytics.us/. The county’s own documentation, read critically and in date order.
AI Now Institute, Algorithmic Accountability for the Public Sector, https://ainowinstitute.org/publication/algorithmic-accountability-for-the-public-sector. Comparative survey of procurement, disclosure, and audit regimes.
Knight First Amendment Institute, Sandvig v. Barr case page, https://knightcolumbia.org/cases/sandvig-v-sessions. Primary documents for the CFAA ruling that shaped current U.S. research-access conditions.
Ada Lovelace Institute, Regulatory Inspection of Algorithmic Systems, https://www.adalovelaceinstitute.org/report/inspecting-algorithms/. A European comparative perspective on inspection regimes.







American Civil Liberties Union. 2019. Sandvig v. Barr: Researchers’ Right to Investigate Algorithmic Discrimination. ACLU. https://www.aclu.org/cases/sandvig-v-barr-challenge-cfaa-prohibition-uncovering-racial-discrimination-online.


Amnesty International. 2021. Xenophobic Machines: Discrimination Through Unregulated Use of Algorithms in the Dutch Childcare Benefits Scandal. Amnesty International. https://www.amnesty.org/en/documents/eur35/4686/2021/en/.


Arruda, Beatriz de, and Sascha van Schendel. 2024. “After SyRI: The Post-Judgment Landscape of Algorithmic Welfare Surveillance in the Netherlands.” European Journal of Risk Regulation.
