Chouldechova, Alexandra, Diana Benavides-Prado, Oleksandr Fialko, and Rhema Vaithianathan. 2018. A Case Study of Algorithm-Assisted Decision Making in Child Maltreatment Hotline Screening. Proceedings of the 1st Conference on Fairness, Accountability,; Transparency.


District Court of The Hague. 2020. NJCM et al. v. The State of the Netherlands (SyRI Judgment), ECLI:NL:RBDHA:2020:1878. Rechtspraak.nl. https://uitspraken.rechtspraak.nl/details?id=ECLI:NL:RBDHA:2020:1878.


Eubanks, Virginia. 2018. Automating Inequality: How High-Tech Tools Profile, Police, and Punish the Poor. St. Martin’s Press.


Geiger, Gabriel, and Justin-Casimir Braun. 2023. Inside the Suspicion Machine. Lighthouse Reports / WIRED. https://www.lighthousereports.com/investigation/inside-the-suspicion-machine/.


Ho, Sally, and Garance Burke. 2022. An Algorithm That Screens for Child Neglect Raises Concerns. Associated Press. https://apnews.com/article/child-welfare-algorithm-investigation-9497ee937e0053ad4144a86c68241ef1.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.


Pasquale, Frank. 2016. The Black Box Society: The Secret Algorithms That Control Money and Information. Harvard University Press.


Vaithianathan, Rhema, Emily Putnam-Hornstein, Nan Jiang, Parma Nand, and Tim Maloney. 2019. Developing Predictive Models to Support Child Maltreatment Hotline Screening Decisions: Allegheny County Methodology and Implementation. Centre for Social Data Analytics, Auckland University of Technology.

title: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?
text:
6 Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?
A public record that cannot be found is not a public record. A footnote whose URL returns a 404 is a claim without a witness. A training corpus that has been quietly withdrawn is a model whose inputs the public can no longer inspect. This chapter is about the third pressure in Part I, the one that works by subtraction. Chapter 4 asked who can observe? Chapter 5 asked who must answer? This chapter asks what can be remembered? and describes the slow, less visible process by which the evidence needed for any answer simply dissolves.
Erosion is rarely dramatic. An agency reorganizes a website during a transition and the old paths return 404s. A journal publisher migrates and the DOIs still resolve but the supplementary materials do not. A civic-tech nonprofit runs out of funding and its dashboard goes dark. A machine-learning dataset that trained a generation of models is withdrawn after researchers document abusive content inside it, and the weights of every model trained on it remain in circulation. Any one event looks like an operational hiccup. Taken together, they are the texture of a public record thinning as fast as anyone can thicken it.
Plantin, Lagoze, Edwards, and Sandvig (2016) argue that the platforms the public has learned to depend on operate on a different clock than public infrastructures do. A municipal water system is designed to last decades and is maintained by a workforce whose job is to keep it running. A cloud storage service or a dataset release is designed to last a product cycle and is maintained by whoever still has a quarterly incentive to care. Karasti and Blomberg (2018) call this the mismatch between project time and infrastructure time: projects end; infrastructures are supposed not to. When the public record lives inside projects, it ends with them.
crumbs: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?

title: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?
text:
6.1 The mundane politics of retention
There is a temptation to tell erosion stories as conspiracies. Sometimes they are. Much more often they are what happens when nobody’s job description includes long-term stewardship. A sysadmin retires. A contract lapses. A platform closes its API and thirty years of researcher-accumulated corpora become unciteable overnight. Gaffney and Matias (2018) documented this for a single, widely-used Reddit corpus, showing that silent data loss had produced an entire body of published work built on samples missing, in predictable ways, parts of the thing they claimed to describe. The missingness was not announced. It was visible only when someone looked.
Acker and Kreisberg (2019) describe this as the problem of archiving in an API-driven world. Classical archival theory assumes a record exists somewhere, that custody can be established, and that preservation is a matter of migration. The contemporary record does not sit still long enough to be taken into custody. Denton, Hanna, and colleagues (2021) trace the same problem for machine-learning datasets: ImageNet, the canonical benchmark for a decade of computer-vision research, was assembled from a pipeline of choices (scraping Flickr, filtering through WordNet, paying crowdworkers to label) that is undocumented in any single place. When a dataset is questioned, the record of how it was made has often evaporated faster than the dataset itself.
The model-era instances are bigger and not meaningfully different in kind. LAION-5B, the image-text dataset used to train Stable Diffusion and many of its descendants, was taken offline in late 2023 after researchers at Stanford’s Internet Observatory documented verified child sexual abuse material inside it (Thiel 2023). The withdrawal was the right call. It was also, for research purposes, a form of erosion: every paper that trained on LAION-5B now cites a dataset the reader cannot audit. Common Crawl, which underlies much of the open web’s representation in large language model training, retains its crawls but its long-term archival status is a question nobody has answered on the record.
Keegan (2026) names continuity as one of the six elements of the installed base. The point is not that every artifact should live forever. The point is that when the public loses the ability to return to a source, the ability to audit what was built on that source goes with it. Erosion is not a failure of tools. It is a failure of incentives.
crumbs: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?

title: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?
section: 6.2 A link-rot audit you can run this afternoon
text:
6.2 A link-rot audit you can run this afternoon
You need to see this happen in a corpus you can touch. The corpus to start with is the one you are reading: this book’s own bibliography plus the “Further Reading” URLs at the end of each chapter. If you do not have that list handy, the EPA’s climate-change press-release archive and NOAA’s hurricane-research page are excellent substitutes, because both agencies have been reorganized enough times since 2016 that you will find real rot within minutes.
Walk a list of URLs, classify each response, record the result in a DataFrame. A 404 is obvious rot. A 301 redirect to a generic landing page is a worse and more common failure: the link resolves, returns a 200, and gives you nothing.
import pandas as pd
import requests
from urllib.parse import urlparse

HEADERS = {
    "User-Agent": "PublicInterestDataScience/0.1 (your-email@example.edu)",
    "Accept": "text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,*/*;q=0.8",
}

def classify_url(url, timeout=15):
    """Fetch a URL and classify the response.

    Returns a dict with url, status_code, final_url, category, notes.
    Categories: ok, redirect_same_host, redirect_offsite,
                client_error, server_error, timeout, dns_fail, other.
    """
    try:
        r = requests.get(url, headers=HEADERS, timeout=timeout,
                         allow_redirects=True)
    except requests.exceptions.Timeout:
        return {"url": url, "status_code": None,
                "final_url": None, "category": "timeout", "notes": ""}
    except requests.exceptions.ConnectionError as e:
        return {"url": url, "status_code": None,
                "final_url": None, "category": "dns_fail",
                "notes": str(e)[:120]}

    start_host = urlparse(url).netloc
    end_host = urlparse(r.url).netloc
    if r.status_code == 200 and r.url == url:
        cat = "ok"
    elif r.status_code == 200 and start_host == end_host:
        cat = "redirect_same_host"
    elif r.status_code == 200:
        cat = "redirect_offsite"
    elif 400 <= r.status_code < 500:
        cat = "client_error"
    elif 500 <= r.status_code < 600:
        cat = "server_error"
    else:
        cat = "other"

    return {"url": url, "status_code": r.status_code,
            "final_url": r.url, "category": cat,
            "notes": f"bytes={len(r.content)}"}


def audit(urls):
    rows = [classify_url(u) for u in urls]
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
    return df
Point this at a real list of URLs and you will get something like the distribution below. The shape is representative, not exact; rerun it and the numbers will drift.
urls = [
    "https://www.epa.gov/climate-change",
    "https://www.noaa.gov/climate",
    "https://www.propublica.org/article/machine-bias-risk-assessments-in-criminal-sentencing",
    "https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/",
    # ... twenty more from your syllabus or bibliography
]

report = audit(urls)
report.groupby("category").size()
# => ok                     11
#    redirect_same_host      4
#    redirect_offsite        3
#    client_error            2
#    server_error            0
#    timeout                 1
#    dns_fail                0
#    other                   0
The redirect_offsite row is the one to look at first. A federal agency URL that now redirects to a generic “archive” subdomain, or to a White House press page on a different topic, is a link that technically resolves but practically does not. Whenever you cite a government source in a paper, a report, or a public comment, assume the URL will be reorganized at the next administration transition. Plan for it.






TipThe Missing Manual



Status codes lie. A page that returns HTTP 200 may still be useless: a content management system that issues a “soft 404,” a WAF page saying “request blocked,” a single-page application that loads a JavaScript shell and fetches its real content from an API you are not calling. You can partly guard against this by checking response size and content against a known baseline, but there is no substitute for opening a sample of the “ok” rows in a browser. The Wayback Machine has the same problem from the other direction: its Availability API will cheerfully return a capture that was itself a 404 page archived at the moment of rot. If the service your audit uses does not let you inspect the content of a “successful” response, your audit is not done. Always verify a subset by eye.
crumbs: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?

title: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?
section: 6.3 Recovering the rot with the Wayback Machine
crumbs: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?

title: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?
text:
6.4 Datasheets as a retention practice
A link-rot audit tells you what is missing from the outside. A datasheet tells the next reader what the thing you produced actually was, so that when pieces of it erode, there is a record of what to look for. Gebru and colleagues (2021) proposed datasheets for datasets as a deliberate import from electronics engineering: every component ships with a spec sheet documenting what it is, how it was made, what it is for, and what it is not. Machine-learning datasets routinely ship with nothing, and the absence produces exactly the retroactive reconstructions Denton and colleagues (2021) had to do for ImageNet.
A datasheet is not a README. It is a structured document with seven sections, and the discipline of filling each one out is what makes it useful:

Motivation. Who made this, for what purpose, funded by whom? What gap did it fill?
Composition. What does an instance look like? How many are there? Are there labels, and how were they produced? Are there known subgroup imbalances?
Collection process. Where did the data come from? When? Through what mechanism? Were the subjects informed? Was consent obtained? What ethical review, if any?
Preprocessing, cleaning, labeling. What transformations were applied between raw and released? Is the raw version available? What filtering removed what?
Uses. What has this been used for? What should it not be used for? Are there tasks for which it is actively unsuitable?
Distribution. How is it distributed? Under what license? With what restrictions? Through what channel, and what is the expected lifetime of that channel?
Maintenance. Who maintains it? How will errata be communicated? Is there a versioning scheme? When is the next review?

The maintenance section will almost always be blank on the first pass, which is the point: if you cannot name the maintainer or the review cadence, you are publishing a dataset with no plan for its erosion. That is worth knowing before you publish.
DATASHEET_TEMPLATE = {
    "motivation": {
        "purpose": "",
        "creators": "",
        "funding": "",
        "gap_addressed": "",
    },
    "composition": {
        "instance_type": "",
        "n_instances": None,
        "labels": "",
        "subgroup_imbalances": "",
        "sensitive_attributes": "",
    },
    "collection_process": {
        "source": "",
        "period": "",
        "mechanism": "",
        "consent": "",
        "ethical_review": "",
    },
    "preprocessing": {
        "transformations": "",
        "raw_available": False,
        "filtering": "",
    },
    "uses": {
        "prior_uses": "",
        "inappropriate_uses": "",
    },
    "distribution": {
        "license": "",
        "channel": "",
        "restrictions": "",
        "expected_lifetime": "",
    },
    "maintenance": {
        "maintainer": "",
        "errata_channel": "",
        "versioning": "",
        "next_review": "",
    },
}
Fill this in once, by hand, for a dataset you have actually used. The first datasheet will take several hours and will be worse than you expected. By the third, you will notice yourself asking questions at dataset-selection time that you did not ask before, which is the actual point of the practice.
crumbs: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?

title: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?
section: 6.5 Why “just put it on GitHub” is not a preservation strategy
text:
6.5 Why “just put it on GitHub” is not a preservation strategy
Students and, increasingly, agencies treat GitHub as a preservation layer. It is not one. GitHub is a hosting service owned by Microsoft, with no long-term commitment to any particular repository and a history of taking down content for reasons ranging from DMCA claims to policy disputes. Repositories can be made private, renamed, force-pushed over, or deleted by their owner. The commit hash is stable; the URL that resolves it is not. Software Heritage mirrors much of public GitHub, which is good, but public awareness of Software Heritage is limited.
The same logic applies to Google Drive links in syllabi, Dropbox links in footnotes, and Notion pages in institutional documentation. Each is a commercial service on a project-time clock. For anything you need to cite, publish through a channel designed for infrastructure time: a Zenodo deposit with a DOI, an Internet Archive item, an institutional repository your university has committed to maintaining. Chapter 24 walks through the mechanics.
crumbs: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?

title: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?
text:
6.6 Closing the triptych
Chapter 2 asked who can observe; Chapter 3 asked who must answer; this chapter asks what can be remembered. Together they are Part I’s working diagnosis. Access has narrowed, accountability is exempt, and the record that would let us reason about either is eroding faster than the institutions charged with preserving it can match.
Part II turns to the values that make a response coherent. Chapter 7 names the installed base explicitly, the six-element framework Keegan (2026) proposes and that this book has been gesturing at since Chapter 3. The link-rot audits and datasheets this chapter asks you to write are the first practical instances of what the next part will call continuity.
crumbs: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?

title: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?
text:
6.7 Exercises
Exercise 4.1 (Guided). Pick a document with at least twenty external URLs: a syllabus, a paper’s reference list, a policy brief, a government report. Run the link-rot audit from this chapter over its URLs and save the DataFrame as exercises/ch04_audit.csv. In 200 words, describe the distribution of outcomes and name the single most consequential broken link.
Exercise 4.2 (Guided). For three of the broken or offsite-redirecting URLs from Exercise 4.1, use the Availability API to locate captures. Record the original URL, the Wayback URL, the timestamp, and a one-sentence note on whether the capture actually contains what the original cited. Submit as exercises/ch04_recoveries.md. Where no capture exists, use the web.archive.org/save/ endpoint to request one of the current state for future reference.
Exercise 4.3 (Analytic). Using the Gebru and colleagues template, write a full datasheet for a dataset you have used. Fill every section. Where you do not know the answer, say so explicitly rather than leaving the field blank. Save as exercises/ch04_datasheet_<dataset>.md. Minimum 1,500 words; the maintenance section must name a human or an institution.
Exercise 4.4 (Comparative). Pick a government webpage whose content you care about (EPA climate-change, a CDC surveillance page, a BLS methodology page). Use the Wayback Machine to locate its earliest capture and its most recent capture before an administration transition (January 2017 or January 2021 are useful inflection points). Compare the two and, in 500 words, describe what changed: content, framing, linked resources. Do not editorialize; describe. Save as exercises/ch04_agency_comparison.md.
Exercise 4.5 (Open-ended). Identify one dataset, corpus, or data portal that you believe will not survive the next decade in publicly accessible form. Write a 600-to-800-word memo naming the resource, the mechanisms you expect to erode it, and a single concrete intervention a student or small nonprofit could take now. The intervention must be something doable in a semester. Save as exercises/ch04_endangered.md. You will revisit it in Chapter 24.
crumbs: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?

title: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?
text:
6.8 Looking ahead
Part I has now laid out its three pressures. Part II begins with Chapter 5, The Installed Base, which names the six-element framework that the rest of the book uses as a working definition of public interest. The link-rot function, the Wayback recovery helper, and the datasheet template you built in this chapter are the first instances of a pattern Chapter 5 will generalize: public-interest data science is less a set of analyses than a set of maintenance practices, carried out in public, by people who are willing to do them when nobody’s quarterly review depends on it.
crumbs: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?

title: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?
text:
6.9 Further Reading and Resources

Timnit Gebru, Jamie Morgenstern, Briana Vecchione, Jennifer Wortman Vaughan, Hanna Wallach, Hal Daumé III, and Kate Crawford (2021), “Datasheets for datasets,” Communications of the ACM 64(12): 86–92. The canonical template. Read it before you write your first datasheet; refer back to it every time you write the next.
Emily Denton, Alex Hanna, Razvan Amironesei, Andrew Smart, and Hilary Nicole (2021), “On the genealogy of machine learning datasets: A critical history of ImageNet,” Big Data & Society 8(2). Essential counterweight to treating datasheets as a solved problem.
Jean-Christophe Plantin, Carl Lagoze, Paul N. Edwards, and Christian Sandvig (2016), “Infrastructure studies meet platform studies in the age of Google and Facebook,” New Media & Society 20(1): 293–310. The paper that names the mismatch between platform time and infrastructure time most clearly.
Helena Karasti and Jeanette Blomberg (2018), “Studying infrastructuring ethnographically,” Computer Supported Cooperative Work 27: 233–265. The source for project time versus infrastructure time as analytical categories.
The Internet Archive Wayback Machine Availability API documentation: https://archive.org/help/wayback_api.php. Short, readable, and the endpoint your audits will lean on.
David S. H. Rosenthal, LOCKSS: Lots of Copies Keep Stuff Safe: https://www.lockss.org/. The library-community preservation infrastructure that most of the web could learn from.
Software Heritage: https://www.softwareheritage.org/. The mirror-of-record for public code repositories, including substantial GitHub coverage. Useful when you need to cite a commit whose origin repository has disappeared.
David S. H. Rosenthal’s blog at https://blog.dshr.org/. Two decades of patient, unglamorous writing on the economics and mechanics of digital preservation. Start with any post whose title mentions “bit rot.”
Environmental Data and Governance Initiative (EDGI), Website Monitoring Reports: https://envirodatagov.org/website-monitoring/. The civil-society project that tracked federal environmental website changes across the 2017 and 2021 administration transitions. An existence proof that the audit practice in this chapter scales.
Stanford Internet Observatory (2023), “Identifying and eliminating CSAM in generative ML training data and models”: https://purl.stanford.edu/kh752sm9123. The technical report that led to the withdrawal of LAION-5B and a case study in what dataset deprecation actually involves.







Acker, Amelia, and Adam Kreisberg. 2019. “Social Media Data Archives in an API-Driven World.” Archival Science.


Denton, Emily, Alex Hanna, Razvan Amironesei, Andrew Smart, and Hilary Nicole. 2021. “On the Genealogy of Machine Learning Datasets: A Critical History of ImageNet.” Big Data & Society 8 (2).


Gaffney, Devin, and J. Nathan Matias. 2018. “Caveat Emptor, Computational Social Science: Large-Scale Missing Data in a Widely-Published Reddit Corpus.” PLoS ONE 13 (7): e0200162.


Gebru, Timnit, Jamie Morgenstern, Briana Vecchione, et al. 2021. “Datasheets for Datasets.” Communications of the ACM 64 (12): 86–92.


Karasti, Helena, and Jeanette Blomberg. 2018. “Studying Infrastructuring Ethnographically.” Computer Supported Cooperative Work 27: 233–65.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.


Plantin, Jean-Christophe, Carl Lagoze, Paul N. Edwards, and Christian Sandvig. 2016. “Infrastructure Studies Meet Platform Studies in the Age of Google and Facebook.” New Media & Society 20 (1): 293–310.


Thiel, David. 2023. Identifying and Eliminating CSAM in Generative ML Training Data and Models. Stanford Internet Observatory. https://purl.stanford.edu/kh752sm9123.
crumbs: 5  Erosion: What Can Be Remembered?

title: 6  The Installed Base
text:
7 The Installed Base
Part I described three pressures on public knowledge: enclosure, exemption, erosion. Each names something that goes wrong. Part II turns to the arrangements that go right when accountability works, or that have to be repaired when it fails. Chapter 6 develops openness. Chapter 7 develops oversight. Chapter 8 develops ownership. Before any of those chapters, you need a shared vocabulary for what those values operate on, and why “add more dashboards” is not a sufficient answer.
That vocabulary is the installed base. Star and Ruhleder (1994) used the phrase to describe the way a new piece of infrastructure never lands on a blank site. It lands on top of prior commitments, prior wiring, prior data formats, prior labor. A new system inherits its predecessors’ choices the way a neighborhood inherits its streets: you can pave them, but you cannot move them without moving everything connected to them. Karasti and Blomberg (2018) pick up the idea to show that keeping infrastructure running (“infrastructuring,” in their phrasing) is ongoing alignment between what the current system promises and what the installed base will tolerate. Aanestad and colleagues (2017) show the same dynamic in healthcare: national digital-health programs that ignore the installed base of paper forms, clinician workflows, and regional labs fail in the same way, every time.
Keegan (2026) extends the concept. For a public-interest data practice, the installed base is not just the technical stack. It is the socio-technical settlement that makes accountability possible: the records, the access rules, the interpretive conventions, the legal protections, the delegated authorities, and the remedies available to the people affected. When any of those is missing, the visible parts of the system (the dashboard, the API, the model card) are working on borrowed time. When they are in place, a dashboard is a tool. When they are absent, a dashboard is a performance.

title: 6  The Installed Base
text:
7.1 Six elements
Keegan’s framework names six elements. Together they are the minimum conditions under which “public interest” can be said to be served by a data system, rather than invoked on its behalf.
Linkability. Each record the system depends on has a stable identifier that allows it to be referenced, compared, and joined across time and across systems. A case number that changes when the case is moved between databases is not a case number. A dataset version that cannot be pinned is not a version. Linkability is what makes a record a record rather than an observation.
Interpretability. The categories, units, and transformations the data encode are documented well enough that a qualified outsider can reason about what the numbers mean. Bowker and Star (2000) is the canonical argument that classification schemes are never neutral; interpretability is the installed-base requirement that whoever uses the data can see the classification and argue with it. Without it, audits produce numbers without findings.
Continuity. Evidence persists across political cycles, vendor changes, funding gaps, and administrative reorganizations. Chapter 4 gave you the technical shape: link-rot audits, datasheets, Wayback captures. Continuity is the property Karasti and Blomberg call “infrastructure time,” maintained against the pull of project time.
Safe scrutiny. Researchers, journalists, auditors, and affected communities can examine the system without being sued, arrested, deplatformed, or retaliated against. Safe scrutiny is the installed-base counterpart to the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act cases Chapter 7 will treat in detail. In its absence, openness is a trap: the data are technically available, but looking too hard is legally or institutionally punished.
Authority. Someone, a named person or a named office, has the responsibility to act on what scrutiny reveals. An audit that lands on no one’s desk is a training exercise. Authority turns a finding into a decision, a decision into a policy change, a policy change into an enforcement action. It is also the element public-interest technologists most often forget to design for, because the technologist’s habit is to build the artifact and assume the institution will catch it.
Remedy. The people affected have a path to contest decisions, correct the record, and receive reparation when the system has harmed them. Remedy is the element that most often has no software layer at all. It lives in ombuds offices, administrative appeals, class-action complaints, and community organizing. Any data system that offers no remedy is, for the people at the rough end of it, a system of unilateral power.
Linkability, interpretability, continuity, safe scrutiny, authority, remedy. The order matters: later elements presuppose earlier ones. You cannot offer remedy over records that cannot be linked, and you cannot wield authority over findings nobody can interpret. The list also bakes in a theory of what the next three chapters are for. Openness (Chapter 8) operationalizes linkability, interpretability, and parts of continuity. Oversight (Chapter 9) operationalizes safe scrutiny and authority. Ownership (Chapter 10) operationalizes remedy, and the stewardship that keeps continuity from collapsing into somebody’s weekend hobby.

title: 6  The Installed Base
section: 7.2 What goes wrong when an element is missing
text:
7.2 What goes wrong when an element is missing
The framework earns its keep diagnostically. Consider the Allegheny County Family Screening Tool, introduced in Chapter 5 and picked up in Chapter 9. Linkability is good: each hotline call is identified and joined to administrative records. Interpretability is partial: features are documented; weights are not always easy to reason about. Continuity is maintained by a named team at the county and at Auckland University of Technology. The system is weakest at the tail. Safe scrutiny is contingent on the county’s willingness to cooperate with outside researchers, which has varied. Authority lives with call-screeners who can override the score but report social pressure not to. Remedy for a family flagged and investigated is almost nonexistent: you cannot unring a caseworker visit, and the administrative record persists. This is an unusually documented system by the standards of public-sector ML. The framework lets you name precisely where the weakness lives.
Consider a contrasting case. A city publishes a real-time “open data” portal with dozens of datasets updated nightly. Linkability and interpretability are strong within each dataset. Continuity is weak: nobody has committed to preserving pre-publication raw data, the portal’s vendor contract is up for rebid every three years, and every URL path will change the next time the vendor changes. Safe scrutiny is strong in the abstract and untested in practice. Authority is diffuse: the datasets are “owned” by a half-dozen departments, none of whom see responding to outside findings as their primary job. Remedy is absent. A glance at the portal would give you a five-star “open data” rating. The installed base underneath would give you a shrug.
The framework does not tell you what to do about either failure. It tells you where to look, so that the intervention lands somewhere that will hold it.






NoteIn the Public Interest



The installed base is where openness, oversight, and ownership stop being rhetorical and become operational. Openness (Chapter 8) is not a slogan about transparency; it is the working combination of linkability, interpretability, and the portions of continuity that let outsiders join and compare records over time. Oversight (Chapter 9) is not a regulator’s aspiration; it is the working combination of safe scrutiny and authority. Ownership (Chapter 10) is not private property over data; it is the stewardship that sustains continuity and the remedy paths that connect records to people. When you see the three values named abstractly elsewhere, translate back to the six elements. That translation is the discipline this chapter teaches.

title: 6  The Installed Base
text:
7.3 A minimum viable installed base
Enough conceptual work. The rest of the chapter builds a concrete installed base for a small civic dataset. You cannot talk seriously about any of the six elements without doing a version of this work at least once.
The dataset is a procurement log for a fictional small city the book provides: 500 rows of vendor, amount, award date, and award type. The book ships it with two deliberate failures. Some rows are missing identifiers. One transformation step, a mid-pipeline currency conversion, is undocumented. Your job is to fix both and, in doing so, touch every one of the six elements.

7.3.1 Linkability: persistent identifiers
First, look at what you have.
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("data/smallcity_procurement.csv")
df.head()
# => columns: record_id, vendor, amount, award_date, award_type
#    record_id is populated for 472 of 500 rows; 28 rows are blank.
df["record_id"].isna().sum()
# => 28
The 28 blank identifiers are the first gap. Two reasonable choices: a random UUID, or a hash derived from the record’s own content. A UUID is opaque and will not change if the record is corrected; a content hash is deterministic and detects silent changes, but it shifts the moment anyone fixes a typo.
For civic records, where correction is expected and auditability matters, a compromise works. Assign a UUID as the canonical identifier. Also compute a content hash, store it in a separate column, and keep a history of hash values as a change log.
import hashlib
import uuid

def content_hash(row):
    payload = f"{row['vendor']}|{row['amount']}|{row['award_date']}|{row['award_type']}"
    return hashlib.sha256(payload.encode("utf-8")).hexdigest()[:16]

missing = df["record_id"].isna()
df.loc[missing, "record_id"] = [str(uuid.uuid4()) for _ in range(missing.sum())]
df["content_hash"] = df.apply(content_hash, axis=1)
df.to_csv("data/smallcity_procurement_v2.csv", index=False)
You now have a stable reference for every record and a side channel that flags when a record’s substance has changed. That is linkability in minimum viable form. The social work of linkability, keeping the ID stable across vendor migrations and administrative reorganizations, is not solved by the code.






TipThe Missing Manual



UUIDs are the easy part. Every language ships a generator. The hard part is durable reference, and durable reference is social work. Somebody has to maintain the resolver (the service that turns an identifier into a record), negotiate the naming authority (who is allowed to mint identifiers), and make the migration commitment (when we move systems, we carry these identifiers forward). DOIs work because Crossref and DataCite exist as institutions, not because the string 10.1234/... is clever. ORCIDs work because the ORCID consortium has a funding model. Your procurement log’s identifiers will work if, and only if, some office commits to carrying them across the next three vendor contracts. If nobody has signed up, you have a UUID, not an identifier. The difference is everything.




7.3.2 Interpretability: a provenance document
Now the undocumented transformation. Inspect the amount column and you will notice something:
df["amount"].describe()
# => min is suspiciously close to zero, max is an order of magnitude above
#    reasonable small-city contracts. The column mixes USD and some EUR rows
#    from a pass-through transformation nobody wrote down.
An outsider cannot know what the numbers mean. An auditor who assumes USD will misreport. The fix is not code; it is a provenance document committed alongside the data, in a format a human and a machine can both read.
# data/provenance.yaml
dataset: smallcity_procurement
version: "2.0"
upstream_source:
  name: Small City Department of Finance, SAP export
  accessed: 2026-03-12
  contact: finance@smallcity.gov
transformations:
  - step: 01_extract
    description: CSV export from SAP AP module, fields mapped per schema v4.
  - step: 02_currency_normalize
    description: >
      Rows with vendor country code in {DE, FR, IE, NL} carry EUR amounts.
      Converted to USD using ECB monthly reference rate for award_date month.
    rate_source: https://www.ecb.europa.eu/stats/policy_and_exchange_rates/
    applied_by: bkeegan
    applied_on: 2026-03-13
  - step: 03_identifier_assignment
    description: UUID minted for 28 rows with missing record_id; content_hash added.
  - step: 04_publication
    channels:
      - public_portal
      - authenticated_api
      - restricted_view
known_issues:
  - Pre-2022 rows inherited from legacy ERP; vendor spelling unnormalized.
  - EUR-to-USD conversion uses monthly averages; daily rates not preserved.
This is the interpretability layer. Without it, the dataset is a column of numbers that behaves badly. With it, the dataset carries its own explanation. Commit the YAML to the same repository as the data.


7.3.3 Continuity: a retention policy
Continuity without a retention policy is an act of faith. Write the policy down, in a format that would survive the person who wrote it leaving the job.
# data/retention.yaml
dataset: smallcity_procurement
schedule:
  raw_extracts:
    retain_for: 10_years
    storage: city_records_archive
    medium: encrypted_s3_with_offline_tape_copy
  published_versions:
    retain_for: indefinite
    storage: zenodo_deposit_plus_ia_mirror
    minimum_review: annual
  derivation_scripts:
    retain_for: indefinite
    storage: city_gitlab
  access_logs:
    retain_for: 2_years
    storage: city_siem
    purpose: audit_of_restricted_queries_only
responsible_office: Department of Finance, Data Stewardship Lead
review_cadence: annual
escalation_contact: clerk@smallcity.gov
The values chapters that follow will press on each field. What matters here is that the document exists, names a responsible office, and survives independently of the person who set it up.


7.3.4 Safe scrutiny and authority: tiered access
Not every field in a public dataset should be equally public. Vendor names and contract totals are public records in almost every jurisdiction. Bank account numbers and unredacted home addresses of sole proprietors often are not. A small, honest access tier lets you publish openly without publishing recklessly.
Use SQLite views to express the tiers. The underlying table is the full record; each view is a lens on it, and access is governed by an application-layer credential.
import sqlite3

con = sqlite3.connect("data/smallcity_procurement.db")
con.executescript("""
DROP VIEW IF EXISTS v_public;
DROP VIEW IF EXISTS v_authenticated;
DROP VIEW IF EXISTS v_restricted;

CREATE VIEW v_public AS
SELECT record_id, vendor, amount, award_date, award_type
FROM procurement;

CREATE VIEW v_authenticated AS
SELECT record_id, vendor, amount, award_date, award_type,
       contact_email, contract_reference
FROM procurement;

CREATE VIEW v_restricted AS
SELECT *
FROM procurement;
""")
con.commit()
In production you would enforce the tiering with row-level security, OAuth scopes, or an API gateway. For a pedagogical installed base, the view definitions make the commitments legible. Anyone reading the schema can see what a public user, an authenticated user, and a restricted internal user are authorized to see. The access tier is the instrument that makes safe scrutiny possible without collapsing into either “publish everything” or “publish nothing.”


7.3.5 Remedy: an escalation log
Access without escalation is a suggestion box with no one watching it. The last piece is a record of who gets notified when which kind of query arrives. Keep it boring.
# data/escalation.yaml
routes:
  - trigger: public view query volume > 10k/hour
    notify: [data_stewardship_lead, comms]
    purpose: anticipate press or scraping
  - trigger: restricted view accessed
    notify: [records_officer, audit_log]
    purpose: per-access review
  - trigger: correction request received
    notify: [records_officer, affected_department]
    sla: 10_business_days
    remedy: issue corrected row, log in change_history
  - trigger: removal request received (privacy or safety)
    notify: [city_attorney, records_officer]
    sla: 5_business_days
    remedy: redaction + tombstone record retained
The point of the escalation log is to make explicit, in one file under version control, what happens after a query runs. Without it, the installed base routes everything to nobody. With it, the path from observation to remedy has at least been drawn.

title: 6  The Installed Base
text:
7.4 What this demonstrates
You have just built, in roughly a hundred lines of code and markup, a minimum viable installed base. Linkability: UUIDs and content hashes. Interpretability: a provenance document. Continuity: a retention policy. Safe scrutiny: tiered views. Authority and remedy: an escalation log with named offices and SLAs. Each artifact is a stand-in for a much larger professional practice you will meet again. None of them does its work without the others.
Notice what this is not. It is not a dashboard. It is not a model. It is not an “AI governance” framework. It is the boring, legible plumbing under any of those things, and the book’s argument is that without the plumbing, the dashboards and models and frameworks collapse the moment anyone tries to use them for what they claim to do. Keegan (2026) calls this the infrastructuring move: treating what looks like a platform problem as an installed-base problem.
Chapter 6 begins the values half of Part II by asking what openness actually requires, starting from linkability and interpretability as its operational components. The procurement dataset you have just instrumented will reappear there, extended into a Datasette-published artifact with a public URL. Chapter 9 will come back to the same dataset through the lens of oversight, asking what it takes for a researcher or journalist to scrutinize it without legal risk. Chapter 10 will return once more, asking who owns it, who stewards it, and how a person harmed by a procurement decision reaches a remedy.
The installed base is how those three chapters connect. Carry the vocabulary forward.

title: 6  The Installed Base
text:
7.5 Exercises
Exercise 5.1 (Guided). Download the book’s smallcity_procurement.csv dataset. Identify the 28 rows missing identifiers and assign a UUID to each, following the pattern in this chapter. Add a content_hash column for every row. Save the result as exercises/ch05_procurement_v2.csv. In 150 words, explain why you chose UUID rather than content hash as the canonical record_id.
Exercise 5.2 (Guided). Write the provenance.yaml for the procurement dataset. Include at least the five transformation steps shown in this chapter, and add one additional step of your own (for example, a vendor name normalization pass, or a deduplication). Save as exercises/ch05_provenance.yaml. The document must name a responsible office and at least one known issue.
Exercise 5.3 (Guided). Draft a retention policy for the same dataset. Use the template in this chapter as a starting point and adapt at least three values (retention periods, storage locations, responsible office) to a city or organization you know something about. Save as exercises/ch05_retention.yaml. In 200 words, justify the retention periods you chose, citing at least one real records-retention schedule (your city, your state, or a federal agency).
Exercise 5.4 (Applied). Implement the three-tier access scheme as SQLite views in a real database built from the procurement CSV. For each of three example queries (a total by vendor, a lookup by contract reference, a full row export) say which tier can run it and why. Save the database as exercises/ch05_procurement.db and the writeup as exercises/ch05_access.md.
Exercise 5.5 (Open). Design an escalation pathway and a threat model for a small civic dataset of your own choosing (a school board meeting minutes scrape, a local housing-permit feed, a campus crime log). Your pathway must name at least three triggers, the offices or individuals notified, and a service-level agreement for each. Your threat model must name at least three adversaries (not all of them malicious; an underfunded vendor is an adversary in this sense) and one failure mode for each. Save as exercises/ch05_escalation.md, 800 to 1,200 words. You will revisit this artifact in Chapter 9.

title: 6  The Installed Base
text:
7.6 Looking ahead
Part II continues in Chapter 6 with openness, the first of the three values. The chapter takes the linkability and interpretability pieces you have just built and asks what it takes to publish them in a form that an interested outsider can actually use. The procurement dataset will move from a local SQLite file to a Datasette instance with a resolvable URL, a license, and a documented schema, and the discussion will press on what “open” has to mean to count as open for public-interest purposes.

title: 6  The Installed Base
text:
7.7 Further Reading and Resources

Brian C. Keegan (2026), “Public interest data infrastructuring,” Information, Communication & Society. The paper that names the six-element framework. The rest of Part II leans on it constantly.
Susan Leigh Star and Karen Ruhleder (1994), “Steps toward an ecology of infrastructure,” CSCW 1994, 253–264. The original “installed base” argument.
Helena Karasti and Jeanette Blomberg (2018), “Studying infrastructuring ethnographically,” CSCW 27: 233–265. The clearest articulation of infrastructuring as alignment work.
Margunn Aanestad, Miria Grisot, Ole Hanseth, and Polyxeni Vassilakopoulou (2017), “Information infrastructures and the challenge of the installed base.” The healthcare-systems reading that makes clear why ignoring the installed base is not a niche failure.
Geoffrey C. Bowker and Susan Leigh Star (2000), Sorting Things Out. Indispensable for the interpretability element.
Ben Hutchinson and colleagues (2021), “Towards accountability for machine learning datasets,” FAccT 2021. A complementary framework for ML dataset governance.
The DataCite schema documentation: https://schema.datacite.org. A production-grade persistent-identifier standard backed by an institutional community.
UK Government Digital Service, “Service Manual,” https://www.gov.uk/service-manual. An institution treating the installed base as a design problem.
The 18F “Before You Ship” checklist, https://before-you-ship.18f.gov/. A reference for what a minimal production installed base looks like.







Aanestad, Margunn, Miria Grisot, Ole Hanseth, and Polyxeni Vassilakopoulou. 2017. “Information Infrastructures and the Challenge of the Installed Base.” Health Informatics Meets eHealth.


Bowker, Geoffrey C., and Susan Leigh Star. 2000. Sorting Things Out: Classification and Its Consequences. MIT Press.


Karasti, Helena, and Jeanette Blomberg. 2018. “Studying Infrastructuring Ethnographically.” Computer Supported Cooperative Work 27: 233–65.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.


Star, Susan Leigh, and Karen Ruhleder. 1994. “Steps Toward an Ecology of Infrastructure: Complex Problems in Design and Access for Large-Scale Collaborative Systems.” In Proceedings of the 1994 ACM Conference on Computer-Supported Cooperative Work.

title: 7  Openness
text:
8 Openness
Chapter 5 argued that the six elements of the installed base are the conditions under which “public interest” can be said to be served by a data system rather than invoked on its behalf. This chapter takes the first three of those elements (linkability, interpretability, and the openness-facing portion of continuity) and turns them into a practice. The practice has a name most readers will already have heard. It is called open data, and the name is doing too much rhetorical work.
Open data as a programmatic idea is roughly twenty years old. It has produced real goods: the federal data.gov portal, municipal 311 feeds, FEC disbursement extracts, the census ACS API. It has also produced a great deal of what practitioners now call, with a mixture of resignation and contempt, the data dump. A data dump is a civic file dropped onto a portal with a permissive license, no documentation, inconsistent columns, encoding errors, and a filename like final_final_v3.xlsx. It is technically open. It satisfies the ordinance. For any public that does not already work inside the agency, it is functionally unusable.
The distinction this chapter insists on is between formal openness and durable legibility. Formal openness is what a compliance officer can certify: the file exists at a URL, the license permits redistribution, the API returns a 200. Durable legibility is what a non-expert can actually do with the thing six months from now: load it, understand its columns, cite it, compare it to last year’s version. Formal openness without legibility reproduces exemption under a different name. The agency has technically satisfied disclosure while ensuring that nobody outside can use the disclosure to act.

title: 7  Openness
text:
8.1 Posting is not publishing
An analogy with scholarly publication is useful. A preprint PDF on a researcher’s hard drive is not a publication. A PDF uploaded to a webserver with no metadata is not a publication either, though it meets some naive definition of “posted.” A publication is the combination of a document, a record about the document (title, author, date, version, license), a citable handle (a DOI, an ISBN, a versioned URL), and a commitment from some institution that the handle will continue to resolve. Publication is a social act, not a file-system operation.
The same distinction applies to data. df.to_csv("out.csv") is a file-system operation. It produces a file. It does not produce a publication. A published dataset requires, at minimum, provenance (where did this come from, what was done to it), documentation (what the columns are and what they mean), a license, a citation, and a hosting commitment (where it will live, and for how long). The FAIR principles (findable, accessible, interoperable, reusable) name four of these without quite naming the last. This chapter adds legibility-to-publics as the extension FAIR understates: a dataset that is machine-interoperable and human-inscrutable is FAIR and useless. Hutchinson and colleagues (2021) make the adjacent argument for machine-learning datasets, borrowing from software engineering the habit of treating a dataset as an artifact with a lifecycle, not a file.
Zuiderwijk and Janssen (2014) reviewed national open-data policies and found the same pattern across countries: the policies are clearest on what agencies must post and weakest on what they must sustain. You can write an open-data ordinance in an afternoon. You cannot write, in any afternoon, the ongoing labor that keeps a civic dataset findable, legible, and citable three budget cycles from now. That labor is what Star and Ruhleder (1994) named as the installed base, and what Karasti and Blomberg (2018) insisted had to be studied as work rather than assumed as context. Openness, in this chapter’s operational sense, is the configuration of artifacts and commitments that makes that labor repeatable by someone other than its originator.

title: 7  Openness
text:
8.2 Licensing as a governance choice
One short detour before the tutorial. An open license is not a technical checkbox. It is a small act of governance. The license you attach to a civic dataset tells downstream users what they may do, what they must do in return, and what you expect when your dataset is recombined with others.
Three license families come up repeatedly. CC-BY 4.0 (Creative Commons Attribution) permits any reuse as long as attribution is given. It is appropriate for most municipal datasets where the agency wants credit trails but does not want to constrain reuse. CC0 waives copyright entirely, placing the dataset as close to the public domain as a contract can reach. It is appropriate when the dataset is already the product of public work and should have no friction at all. PDDL (the Open Data Commons Public Domain Dedication and License) is CC0’s database-specific cousin, designed for jurisdictions where databases have a separate copyright status from their contents. For U.S. civic data, CC-BY 4.0 or CC0 will cover almost every case.
What to avoid: “non-commercial” restrictions (CC-BY-NC). Journalists and small newsrooms sell their work. A non-commercial clause tells the local paper it cannot use the dataset without a lawyer’s letter. That is not openness. It is a defensive crouch dressed up in a compliance costume.

title: 7  Openness
section: 8.3 The running example: Boulder campaign finance
text:
8.3 The running example: Boulder campaign finance
The tutorial uses a campaign finance extract from a city open-data portal. Campaign finance is the most politically interesting of the common civic datasets because it sits at the intersection of accountability and messiness. The file tends to be a CSV with predictable problems: dates as strings, currency with dollar signs and commas, donor names inconsistently capitalized (“SMITH, JOHN” on one row, “John Smith” on the next, “j smith” on a third), employer and occupation fields sometimes blank and sometimes filled with “SELF” in several creative spellings, and an amount column that occasionally contains negative values for refunds without documenting that convention.
You start from the raw file, boulder_campaign_finance_raw.csv, which the book provides in its data folder. In practice you would download the equivalent from the city’s portal. The portal will offer a filename like Campaign_Finance_Filings.csv with no versioning and no changelog; that is the first problem you will document. Treat the raw file as immutable. Do not edit it in Excel. Excel will silently coerce columns and you will not find out until a reader writes to tell you the dates are off by a month.

8.3.1 Ingest and inspect
import pandas as pd

raw = pd.read_csv(
    "data/boulder_campaign_finance_raw.csv",
    dtype=str,            # read everything as string first
    keep_default_na=False # do not turn empty strings into NaN yet
)
raw.shape
# => (3421, 11)

raw.columns.tolist()
# => ['Filing Id', 'Contributor', 'Address', 'Employer',
#     'Occupation', 'Amount', 'Date', 'Election',
#     'Candidate', 'Committee', 'Filing Type']

raw.head(3)
Reading everything as string on first pass is a habit worth keeping. Pandas’s default type inference is confident and frequently wrong: it will read a zip code with a leading zero as an integer, or a dollar amount as a string if one row has a stray character. Taking inference off the table lets you see what is actually there.


8.3.2 Clean
import re

df = raw.copy()

# Lowercase column names, snake_case
df.columns = [c.strip().lower().replace(" ", "_") for c in df.columns]

# Dates: the portal gives mm/dd/yyyy; parse and keep ISO 8601
df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"], format="%m/%d/%Y", errors="coerce")
df["date"].isna().sum()
# => 4   four rows had a blank or malformed date; keep them, flag them

# Currency: strip $ and , then cast to float
df["amount_raw"] = df["amount"]
df["amount"] = (
    df["amount"]
    .str.replace(r"[\$,]", "", regex=True)
    .replace("", pd.NA)
    .astype("Float64")
)

# Donor names: title-case, collapse whitespace
def normalize_name(name):
    if not isinstance(name, str):
        return pd.NA
    name = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", name).strip()
    # "SMITH, JOHN" -> "John Smith"
    if "," in name:
        last, first = [p.strip() for p in name.split(",", 1)]
        return f"{first.title()} {last.title()}"
    return name.title()

df["contributor_clean"] = df["contributor"].apply(normalize_name)

# Preserve the original; do not destroy evidence
assert "contributor" in df.columns
Three habits are worth naming. First, every destructive transformation keeps the original column. amount_raw survives next to amount; contributor survives next to contributor_clean. If a reader disputes your normalization, they can recover what was in the source. Second, the date parse uses errors="coerce" rather than silently dropping failures. The four malformed dates stay visible in the frame so a datasheet can mention them. Third, the normalization is documented inline, because “we title-cased the donor names” is a choice future you will have forgotten in six weeks.


8.3.3 Normalize into an analysis-ready frame
keep = [
    "filing_id", "date", "contributor_clean", "contributor",
    "address", "employer", "occupation",
    "amount", "amount_raw",
    "election", "candidate", "committee", "filing_type",
]
clean = df[keep].sort_values("date").reset_index(drop=True)
clean.to_csv("data/boulder_campaign_finance_clean.csv", index=False)
clean.to_parquet("data/boulder_campaign_finance_clean.parquet")
Two formats, intentionally. CSV is the compatibility format. Parquet preserves the types you just fought to recover, so whoever reads it six months from now does not repeat your work. Publish both.

title: 7  Openness
text:
8.4 Writing the README
A dataset’s README is the first thing a non-specialist will read and often the only thing. Write it as prose, not as bullet points of field names. At a minimum, it needs six sections:

What this is. One paragraph, plain language, for a reader who has never heard of the dataset.
Provenance. Where the raw data came from, with a URL and a retrieval date. What laws or ordinances require its publication.
What was done to it. A short narrative of the cleaning steps, with a pointer to the script that produced the cleaned file.
Column documentation. Every column, one line each, in file order. Units. Missing-value conventions.
License. Named (e.g., CC-BY 4.0) with a link to the canonical text.
Canonical citation. How the dataset should be cited. This is where a DOI goes once you have one; see Chapter 24 for the Zenodo workflow. Until then, a versioned URL and a date.

A README that fits on a single page and covers those six sections outperforms, in practice, an 80-page PDF “data dictionary” that nobody reads.

title: 7  Openness
text:
8.5 The datasheet
A README is for users. A datasheet is for auditors, downstream reusers, and future maintainers who need to know how the dataset came to exist and what it is and is not fit for. Gebru and colleagues (2021) proposed the form, adapting it from hardware datasheets. Chapter 6 explained why every dataset worth preserving needs one. This chapter is where you write one.
A compact datasheet for the campaign finance file has seven sections:

Motivation. Why was this dataset created? Who funded it?
Composition. What do the rows represent? What are the columns? Are there sensitive fields (addresses, employer names) and what is their status?
Collection process. How was the data gathered? By whom? Under what legal authority?
Preprocessing. What cleaning, filtering, or normalization has been applied between the raw source and this release? (Point to the script.)
Uses. What has this dataset been used for? What uses are appropriate? What uses should be approached with caution?
Distribution. Under what license? At what URL? With what permanence commitment?
Maintenance. Who is responsible for updates? How should errors be reported?

Two pages is plenty. The point is not comprehensiveness. The point is that a reader who will never meet you can, from the datasheet alone, decide whether to trust the file for their purpose.






NoteIn the Public Interest



Openness without interpretive legibility reproduces exemption under a different name. Chapter 5 names six installed-base elements; this chapter’s work touches three. Interpretability is what the README and datasheet produce: the file’s categories, units, and transformations become legible to a reader who did not build it. Continuity is what versioned, citable hosting produces: the dataset exists under a handle that will still resolve after the next vendor contract or administrative reorganization. Linkability is what the stable filing identifiers and ISO-8601 dates preserve: a row in this release can be joined to the same row in next year’s release. A civic dataset that skips the README, the datasheet, the versioned citation, and the long-run hosting commitment has satisfied the ordinance and failed the public. That failure is precisely what the six elements were designed to name.

title: 7  Openness
text:
8.6 Publishing with Datasette
Datasette is Simon Willison’s open-source tool for publishing SQLite databases as browsable, queryable websites. It is the smallest plausible piece of infrastructure that turns a cleaned CSV into something a non-expert can explore. You point it at a SQLite file; it gives you a web UI with faceted search, a read-only SQL console, a JSON API, and a handful of export formats. For civic datasets in the single-gigabyte range, it is close to ideal.
First, convert the cleaned file to SQLite and serve it locally.
pip install datasette sqlite-utils datasette-publish-fly

# Build a SQLite database from the cleaned CSV
sqlite-utils insert boulder.db contributions \
  data/boulder_campaign_finance_clean.csv --csv --detect-types

# Add metadata: title, license, source, column descriptions
# (metadata.yml author writes this once; datasette reads it)
datasette serve boulder.db \
  --metadata metadata.yml \
  --port 8001
Open http://localhost:8001/ and click around. You should see the table, the column facets, and a search box. If you typed a column description into metadata.yml, it will show up as hover text on the column header. If you did not, you will see why you should: the UI will read “column employer, no description provided” and your reader will learn nothing.
The metadata.yml is where your README’s column documentation lives inside Datasette. A minimum version:
title: "Boulder campaign finance filings"
description_html: |
  <p>Campaign finance disclosures for Boulder municipal elections.
  See <a href="README.md">README.md</a> and
  <a href="datasheet.md">datasheet.md</a> for provenance.</p>
license: CC-BY 4.0
license_url: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/
source: Boulder Open Data Portal
source_url: https://open-data.bouldercolorado.gov/
databases:
  boulder:
    tables:
      contributions:
        description: "One row per reported contribution, post-cleaning."
        columns:
          filing_id: "Unique identifier assigned by the city filing system."
          date: "ISO 8601 date of the contribution."
          contributor_clean: "Donor name, normalized to Title Case."
          amount: "Contribution amount in USD. Negative values are refunds."

8.6.1 Deploying to free hosting
Local Datasette is useful for development. For the class, you want a URL someone else can visit. datasette publish wraps a handful of cloud providers; the Fly.io path is the cheapest currently viable.
# One-time setup
fly auth signup           # or: fly auth login
# Datasette wraps Dockerfile creation and deploy
datasette publish fly boulder.db \
  --app boulder-campaign-finance \
  --metadata metadata.yml \
  --install datasette-vega \
  --install datasette-cluster-map
The --install flags pull in two plugins worth knowing. datasette-vega gives quick in-browser charts. datasette-cluster-map geocodes rows that include latitude and longitude. Neither is essential; both turn a queryable table into something a city council staffer will actually browse.






TipThe Missing Manual



df.to_csv("out.csv") is not publication. Publication requires metadata, a citation, a license, and a place the file is committed to live. Until you have all four, you have a file. Two traps in the Datasette workflow are worth flagging before you hit them. First, datasette publish fly will cheerfully try to ship a SQLite file larger than the free tier’s 512 MB container limit and silently fail with an error that reads, to the uninitiated, like a network problem. If your database is big, filter it down, move to a paid tier, or split it into a separate “archive” database you publish less often. Second, the free tier’s machine sleeps after inactivity, so the first visitor each morning waits ten seconds for a cold start. None of this is in the quickstart. All of it will happen to you.




8.6.2 Registering a citation
A live Datasette URL is a useful demo. It is not, by itself, a citation. URLs drift, organizations shut down, Fly.io apps get renamed. For a dataset you want to cite in a report or a peer-reviewed paper, you want a DOI, the citation infrastructure the research world has settled on. The forward reference is Chapter 24, which walks through depositing on Zenodo and getting back a DOI you can include in the README’s “canonical citation” section. The layered model is: Datasette is the interactive front door, a versioned Zenodo deposit is the permanent record, and the README ties them together with a clear “cite this” block. Compare with Chapter 10 on access tiers when some of the underlying data cannot be fully open.
Before you publish, one last sanity pass. Chapter 18 makes the case for a category audit before release: read the columns again with the question “is there anyone whose interests are harmed by the way this is named, categorized, or made joinable?” Campaign finance is less fraught than, say, 311 calls or housing-code violations, but even it exposes addresses and employers. The public-interest practice is not to stop publishing. It is to publish with the audit documented.

title: 7  Openness
text:
8.7 Exercises
Exercise 6.1 (Guided). Download the provided raw campaign finance CSV (or pull an equivalent file from your own city’s open-data portal). Reproduce the ingest, clean, and normalize steps in this chapter. Commit the cleaning script and both output formats (CSV and Parquet). Write 150 words describing one decision you made during cleaning that a reasonable person could have made differently.
Exercise 6.2 (Guided). Write a README and a datasheet for the dataset you produced in 6.1, using the section templates in this chapter. Target length: one page of README, two pages of datasheet. Save both as Markdown in exercises/ch06_openness/. Include a license choice (CC-BY 4.0 or CC0) and justify it in one sentence.
Exercise 6.3 (Technical). Install Datasette locally. Build a SQLite database from your cleaned file. Write metadata.yml with title, license, source, and column descriptions. Run datasette serve and browse the result. Submit a screenshot of the column-description hover text working.
Exercise 6.4 (Deploys to the internet). Deploy your Datasette instance to a free hosting tier (Fly.io with datasette publish fly, or an equivalent you document). Share the URL with the class. In 200 words, describe one thing you changed between the local and deployed versions, and one thing that broke at deploy time.
Exercise 6.5 (Diagnostic). Find a published civic dataset in the wild: a municipal portal, data.gov, a state disclosure site. Diagnose how it fails at least one of Chapter 5’s six installed-base elements. Write 400 words. Name the dataset, the agency, and the specific element; describe what a reader trying to use the data would encounter; and propose one concrete change that would close the gap. Good candidates tend to involve continuity (the dataset updates in place with no versioning) or interpretability (columns are documented by field code and nothing else).

title: 7  Openness
text:
8.8 Looking ahead
This chapter built the openness side of the installed base. Chapter 7 takes up oversight: what happens when openness has done its job and somebody starts asking inconvenient questions of the data. Oversight depends on openness having been done well (you cannot oversee what you cannot read) but is not reducible to it. Safe scrutiny and authority are distinct problems with their own tools. Chapter 7 treats disaggregated audits, model cards, and the legal environment in which outside scrutiny is conducted. Bring the published Datasette with you. We will put it under an auditor’s gaze.

title: 7  Openness
text:
8.9 Further Reading and Resources

Simon Willison’s Datasette documentation: https://docs.datasette.io/. The canonical reference. Read “getting started” and “metadata” before the deploy section.
Datasette Cloud: https://www.datasette.cloud/. Willison’s hosted option when you do not want to run Fly.io yourself.
Simon Willison’s weblog: https://simonwillison.net/. Running commentary on Datasette, sqlite-utils, and the surrounding ecosystem.
The FAIR Guiding Principles: https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/. The four-letter scaffold this chapter extends.
Creative Commons license chooser: https://creativecommons.org/choose/. A three-question form that produces an appropriate license and boilerplate.
Open Knowledge Foundation, The Open Data Handbook: https://opendatahandbook.org/. The movement’s original playbook; still the clearest short treatment of what “open” means.
Open Data Commons: https://opendatacommons.org/. PDDL and ODbL, for cases where Creative Commons does not quite fit.
Sunlight Foundation archives: https://sunlightfoundation.com/. Ongoing critique of data.gov’s drift and the U.S. open-government movement.
Gebru et al. (2021), “Datasheets for datasets,” Communications of the ACM 64(12): 86–92. The source for this chapter’s datasheet template.







Gebru, Timnit, Jamie Morgenstern, Briana Vecchione, et al. 2021. “Datasheets for Datasets.” Communications of the ACM 64 (12): 86–92.


Hutchinson, Ben, Andrew Smart, Alex Hanna, et al. 2021. “Towards Accountability for Machine Learning Datasets: Practices from Software Engineering and Infrastructure.” Proceedings of FAccT 2021.


Karasti, Helena, and Jeanette Blomberg. 2018. “Studying Infrastructuring Ethnographically.” Computer Supported Cooperative Work 27: 233–65.


Star, Susan Leigh, and Karen Ruhleder. 1994. “Steps Toward an Ecology of Infrastructure: Complex Problems in Design and Access for Large-Scale Collaborative Systems.” In Proceedings of the 1994 ACM Conference on Computer-Supported Cooperative Work.


Zuiderwijk, Anneke, and Marijn Janssen. 2014. “Open Data Policies, Their Implementation and Impact: A Framework for Comparison.” Government Information Quarterly 31 (1): 17–29.

title: 8  Oversight
text:
9 Oversight
In 2018, Joy Buolamwini and Timnit Gebru published “Gender Shades,” a disaggregated audit of three commercial gender-classification systems (Buolamwini and Gebru 2018). The headline finding is familiar: the systems misclassified darker-skinned women at rates up to 34 percentage points higher than lighter-skinned men. The methodological finding is less famous and more important. Aggregate accuracy had hidden the disparity. Every vendor’s marketing materials had reported a single accuracy number, and every single-number report was technically true and substantively misleading. The audit did not discover a new failure mode. It discovered a disclosure regime designed not to show it.
That is what oversight is for. It is the work of making a system’s behavior visible to parties other than its builders, in a form those parties can use. “Visible” is a demanding word. A datasheet on a vendor website is not visibility if nobody with authority to act on it ever reads it. A one-off academic audit is not visibility if the vendor is free to change the model the next day. Buolamwini and Gebru’s paper mattered not only because of what it found, but because it was followed by sustained pressure, by IBM’s and Microsoft’s decisions to pause face-recognition contracts, by the organizing that Raji and colleagues (2020) later formalized into an end-to-end internal auditing framework, and by legislation that cited the work by name. An audit is a technique. Oversight is an arrangement.
This chapter is about the difference. You will learn to produce a disaggregated audit using fairlearn, and you will learn what kinds of audit regimes hold up under political pressure versus collapse into what Goodman and Trehu (2023) call audit-washing: the performance of accountability in place of its substance. The technical skill transfers. The political judgment about where the skill is worth deploying does not transfer automatically, and that is the harder half of the chapter.

title: 8  Oversight
text:
9.1 Routine auditability versus episodic audits
Two arrangements get called “audits” and they are not the same thing.
Routine auditability is built into a system. Logs are kept in a form an outsider can parse. Model versions are signed and archived. Performance metrics are recomputed and published on a schedule. When a disaggregated regression is run, the inputs it needs already exist, and the person running it does not need anyone’s permission. This is the regime that public accounting aspires to in principle and sometimes achieves in practice. Baker’s (2005) history of “the public interest” in American accounting traces how this aspiration has been narrowed and co-opted over a century, and his warning is worth keeping in view: audit professions do not stay public-interested on their own.
Episodic audits are one-off events. A regulator commissions a report. A researcher publishes a paper. A journalist files a records request and runs numbers. These can be powerful, but their power is bounded by the moment and by the willingness of the audited party to cooperate a second time. Gender Shades was episodic. The follow-up work that turned it into sustained pressure was closer to routine.
Most of what the 2020s legislative wave has produced sits uncomfortably between the two. New York City’s Local Law 144, which requires “bias audits” for automated employment decision tools, is the instructive case. The law took effect in 2023. It mandates annual disaggregated impact ratios for employers using covered tools. It does not specify what counts as an adequate auditor, how data must be collected, or what happens when the audit finds a problem. Goodman and Trehu (2023) and a wave of empirical studies have documented the result: a compliance rate estimated at under 20 percent in the first year, audits of wildly varying rigor, and a cottage industry of vendors offering “LL144 bias audits” in formats that would not survive scrutiny by anyone outside the vendor ecosystem. The law created the appearance of routine auditability without the authority to enforce it. What you get is episodic audits pretending to be routine.
The European Union’s AI Act, which entered staged application in 2024 and 2025, is a more ambitious attempt. It requires conformity assessments for high-risk systems, post-market monitoring, and documentation that must survive into the market-surveillance phase. Whether it holds up is a question the rest of this decade will answer. Early signs, from the machinery directive and GDPR enforcement histories, are that conformity assessments without vigorous market-surveillance authority become paperwork factories. Openness without oversight (Chapter 8) publishes information nobody acts on. Oversight without remedy (Chapter 7) produces reports nobody can use.






NoteIn the Public Interest



Oversight, in the installed-base framework, depends on two elements that the audit itself cannot supply: authority and remedy. A perfect audit nobody acts on is theater. When you read an “audit report” for a public-sector system, your first two questions should not be about method. They should be: who has the authority to act on this finding, and what remedy is available to the people harmed by the behavior it documents? If the answers are “nobody” and “none,” you are reading audit-washing. The technical work in this chapter is a precondition for oversight. It is not oversight.

title: 8  Oversight
text:
9.2 A disaggregated audit in fairlearn
The rest of the chapter works through a concrete audit. You will use folktables, which wraps the American Community Survey (ACS) into tasks with documented protected attributes, and fairlearn, which provides disaggregated metric computation. The task is income prediction: given a respondent’s demographic and employment features, predict whether their annual income exceeds $50,000. A deliberately banal task. The point is to practice the audit on a problem where you already have intuitions about where disparities are likely to appear.
Install the packages if you have not:
# pip install folktables fairlearn scikit-learn pandas
Fetch a state-level slice and define the task:
from folktables import ACSDataSource, ACSIncome
import pandas as pd

data_source = ACSDataSource(survey_year="2022", horizon="1-Year", survey="person")
ca_data = data_source.get_data(states=["CO"], download=True)

features, label, group = ACSIncome.df_to_pandas(ca_data)
# features: demographic/employment columns
# label:    Series of booleans, income > $50k
# group:    Series with racial category codes (RAC1P in the ACS schema)

print(features.shape, label.mean())
# => (roughly 30000 rows, 10 columns), positive rate ~0.45
folktables returns the RAC1P race code as its default group. You will want a second protected attribute for cross-tabulation. Sex (SEX, 1 = male, 2 = female in ACS coding) is already in the feature matrix; extract it alongside.
sex = features["SEX"].map({1: "male", 2: "female"})
race_map = {
    1: "White",
    2: "Black",
    3: "AIAN",
    4: "Asian",
    5: "NHPI",
    6: "Other",
    7: "Two or more",
    8: "Hispanic (recoded)",
    9: "Other (recoded)",
}
race = group.map(race_map).fillna("Unknown")
A few notes on what you just did. You flattened a complex racial classification into a small number of buckets for display. The ACS RAC1P variable itself already did a lot of flattening upstream. Hispanic origin in the ACS is coded separately from race (HISP), and whether you treat “Hispanic” as a race-level category depends on what you are trying to show. There is no technically correct answer, only an answer you can defend to a skeptical reader. Write down which one you made and why, in the audit output.
Fit the simplest possible model and get predictions:
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test, race_train, race_test, sex_train, sex_test = (
    train_test_split(
        features, label, race, sex,
        test_size=0.3, random_state=42, stratify=label
    )
)

pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("scaler", StandardScaler()),
    ("lr", LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000)),
])
pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred = pipeline.predict(X_test)
A logistic regression is not the state of the art. It is deliberately chosen. If your audit only works on the model you trained, it is not an audit. It is a commentary on your own pipeline. The audit should be method-agnostic.
Now the disaggregated performance analysis:
from fairlearn.metrics import (
    MetricFrame,
    selection_rate,
    false_positive_rate,
    false_negative_rate,
    demographic_parity_difference,
    equalized_odds_difference,
)
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score

metrics = {
    "accuracy": accuracy_score,
    "precision": precision_score,
    "recall": recall_score,
    "selection_rate": selection_rate,
    "fpr": false_positive_rate,
    "fnr": false_negative_rate,
    "count": lambda y_true, y_pred: len(y_true),
}

sensitive = pd.DataFrame({"race": race_test, "sex": sex_test})

mf = MetricFrame(
    metrics=metrics,
    y_true=y_test,
    y_pred=y_pred,
    sensitive_features=sensitive,
)

print(mf.overall)
# => accuracy, precision, recall, etc. over the full test set

print(mf.by_group)
# => the same metrics, broken out by (race, sex) cells
MetricFrame.by_group returns a DataFrame indexed by the cross-product of your sensitive features. This is the centerpiece of the audit. The count metric is not optional. It is the column that tells your reader how many people are actually in each cell, which is the number that tells them whether any of the other numbers mean anything.






TipThe Missing Manual



fairlearn’s defaults will not tell you which parity metric is politically defensible. That choice is yours, and you have to justify it. Demographic parity asks whether positive predictions are allocated at the same rate across groups. Equalized odds asks whether error rates are balanced across groups, conditional on the true label. These metrics can disagree. They often do. And they cannot both be satisfied in the presence of unequal base rates, which is a mathematical result, not a political one. Worse: most audit reports bury the per-subgroup sample size in a footnote, and the FPR you are reporting for the smallest cell is computed on thirty people. That number is noise.
