Report the count alongside every metric, refuse to compute metrics for cells below a documented threshold, and do not let the absence of a small-sample warning hide the fact that your audit has nothing to say about the group it looked hardest for.


Compute the two standard parity summaries and compare:
dpd = demographic_parity_difference(
    y_true=y_test, y_pred=y_pred,
    sensitive_features=sensitive,
)
eod = equalized_odds_difference(
    y_true=y_test, y_pred=y_pred,
    sensitive_features=sensitive,
)

print(f"Demographic parity difference: {dpd:.3f}")
print(f"Equalized odds difference:     {eod:.3f}")
A non-trivial difference between these two numbers is informative, not a bug. It tells you that the fairness question your dataset is asking you to answer is not reducible to a single statistic.

title: 8  Oversight
section: 9.3 Writing the audit for a non-technical reader
text:
9.3 Writing the audit for a non-technical reader
The by_group table is raw material. The audit is what you write around it. A useful template has four sections.

What the model is, and what it is not. A logistic regression trained on one year of ACS data for one state. A teaching artifact, not a procurement recommendation. The reader should not leave the first paragraph confused about what decisions, if any, the model is authorized to make.
Who is in the data. The per-subgroup counts, with the smallest cells called out in prose. “Our test set includes 412 Black men and 34 American Indian and Alaska Native women. The latter is below our reporting threshold of 100, so we do not report per-cell metrics for that group, but we note its absence.” Audits that silently drop small cells pretend to a completeness they do not have.
What the metrics show. The disaggregated table, with a plain-language reading. Demographic parity difference is 0.21; equalized odds difference is 0.11. In the voice of the audit: the model predicts high income for men at a rate 21 percentage points higher than for women, and this gap is not fully explained by differences in true-label rates. Error rates also vary across racial groups, with the false positive rate roughly twice as high for X as for Y.
What assumptions the audit rests on. The racial categories were flattened. Hispanic origin was coded as a race for display. The underlying ACS data are self-reported and subject to nonresponse. The model was evaluated on a 30 percent holdout from a single year. Any of these choices could be defended differently and would produce a different audit. Name them.

Section 4 is where most real audits fail. An audit that does not document its assumptions is not reproducible by a second reader, which is the minimum condition for routine auditability. If the next auditor cannot replicate your disaggregation and get the same table, the audit is not an instrument of oversight. It is a press release.

title: 8  Oversight
text:
9.4 What survives political change
You can run the code above on any public model and produce a disaggregated audit. What you cannot do, alone, is make it matter.
Three things distinguish audit regimes that survive political change from those that collapse into audit-washing. The first is authority: a named institution, with a budget and a mandate, that can demand the audit and act on it. Gender Shades moved IBM and Microsoft because it was one input into a field of advocacy organizations (the Algorithmic Justice League chief among them), investigative journalists, and eventually regulators. Raji and colleagues’ (2020) internal auditing framework presumed an organization that wanted to know. The AI Act presumes a market-surveillance authority willing to use its enforcement powers. When no such authority exists, the audit is an artifact looking for a reader.
The second is remedy. If the audit finds a problem and there is nothing the people harmed can do about it, the audit is diagnostic without being therapeutic. This is the core defect of LL144 as implemented: the law requires disclosure of disparate impact but provides no individual right of action for a candidate screened out by a tool with an adverse impact ratio of 0.6. Remedy can be restorative (reinstating a denied benefit), structural (decommissioning a tool), or financial (damages). Routine auditability without a remedy pathway produces an archive of grievances, not a correction mechanism.
The third is scope protection. Audit regimes erode when their scope narrows without anyone defending the frame. The narrowing happens through definitional work: tightening what counts as an “automated employment decision tool,” exempting “advisory” systems that are decisive in practice, carving out “pilot programs” that run for a decade. Chapter 3 treated this as the logic of exemption (Chapter 5). An audit regime whose scope is a negotiated floor rather than an expanding ceiling will be negotiated downward by the entities it is supposed to constrain.
Keegan’s (2026) framework names authority and remedy as two of the six elements of the installed base. Oversight, in this sense, is not an additional value. It is the working configuration in which those elements meet. A technically excellent MetricFrame output and no authority to act produces a finding that goes into a drawer. A regulator with authority and no technical capacity produces enforcement actions built on misread metrics. The point of learning fairlearn well is not that the library does oversight. It is that the library does the part of oversight the auditor cannot delegate upward, so that what the auditor produces can be a reliable input to the arrangement the rest of the installed base has to sustain.

title: 8  Oversight
text:
9.5 Exercises
Exercise 7.1 (Guided, reproduction). Reproduce the disaggregated audit from Buolamwini and Gebru (2018) on a smaller scale. Using the Fitzpatrick Skin Type dataset (or a publicly documented substitute), compute accuracy disaggregated by the intersection of skin type and sex for any open-source gender-classification model you can run on a laptop. You do not need to reproduce the magnitude of their findings. You need to reproduce the shape of the analysis: the aggregate-versus-disaggregated contrast, the intersectional cell, and the inclusion of per-cell sample sizes. Submit your notebook and a one-paragraph comparison of your result to the paper’s.
Exercise 7.2 (Applied). Run the folktables audit above on a different state, or on a task other than income (employment, public-coverage receipt, and travel time are all in the package). Produce a MetricFrame with at least two sensitive features. Write a 500-word audit report in the four-section structure above. Document at least three assumptions in the assumptions section, and name at least one subgroup your data cannot support conclusions about.
Exercise 7.3 (Analytic). Using the model from Exercise 7.2, compute both demographic_parity_difference and equalized_odds_difference. Write 300 words comparing the two numbers. Which one is larger? Which failure mode does each describe in plain language? If you had to pick one as the headline number for your audit report, which would you pick, and what are you choosing not to emphasize by that choice? There is no right answer; there are defensible answers.
Exercise 7.4 (Critical reading). Find one NYC Local Law 144 bias audit report from the public record. Employer listings and linked audit reports are available through the on.nyc.gov/ll144 hub and a growing archival collection at the Center on Race, Inequality, and the Law. Read the audit end to end. Write a 500-word critique using the four-section template. Where does the audit succeed? Where does it hide its sample sizes, define impact ratios in non-standard ways, or omit an assumptions section? Does the audit name a remedy pathway? Would a non-technical reader come away knowing what, if anything, they could do about an adverse finding?
Exercise 7.5 (Open-ended). Take the audit you wrote in Exercise 7.2 and answer this question in 400 to 600 words: what would change about the audit, its structure, its disclosures, its refusal to answer some questions, if it had enforcement authority attached? Pick a specific authority: the EEOC, a state attorney general’s office, the EU AI Act market-surveillance authority, or a named procurement officer at a city government. Write as if your audit could trigger their intervention. What thresholds would you set? What would you decline to say without a formal proceeding? What part of the current audit would you have to strengthen to survive a challenge?

title: 8  Oversight
text:
9.6 Looking ahead
Oversight without ownership is oversight without a subject. You can audit a model all day, but if it is unclear who owns the system, who owns the training data, and who has standing to demand changes, the audit cannot close a loop. Chapter 8 (Chapter 10) takes up ownership as the third value in the installed-base trio, and asks what it takes to make ownership claims legible and enforceable in data infrastructures built largely out of other people’s work. Chapter 11 (Chapter 13) returns to the accounting lineage this chapter invoked in passing; the professional history there is a cautionary tale about what happens to oversight regimes when their professional base loses the argument about what “public interest” means. Chapter 16 (Chapter 18) will ask whether some audits should not be performed at all, a question the technical toolkit here cannot answer on its own.

title: 8  Oversight
text:
9.7 Further Reading and Resources

Joy Buolamwini and Timnit Gebru (2018), “Gender shades: Intersectional accuracy disparities in commercial gender classification,” Proceedings of FAccT 2018. The foundational disaggregated audit. Read it alongside the follow-up in Raji et al. (2020).
Inioluwa Deborah Raji et al. (2020), “Closing the AI accountability gap: Defining an end-to-end framework for internal algorithmic auditing,” Proceedings of FAccT 2020. The paper that turns an audit technique into an organizational framework.
fairlearn user guide: https://fairlearn.org/main/user_guide/. Pay particular attention to the section on interpreting MetricFrame, and to the documentation on disagreement between parity metrics.
folktables repository and paper (Ding et al., NeurIPS 2021): https://github.com/socialfoundations/folktables. The package used in this chapter, with documentation on the tradeoffs involved in treating ACS tables as fairness benchmarks.
NYC Department of Consumer and Worker Protection, Local Law 144 rules and guidance: https://www.nyc.gov/site/dca/about/automated-employment-decision-tools.page. Read the final rule, not just the FAQ.
Bryce Goodman and Julia Trehu, “AI audit washing and accountability” (2023). The term “audit-washing” in its useful form.
EU AI Act consolidated text (Regulation 2024/1689): https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj. Skim Articles 43, 72, and 77 for the conformity-assessment and post-market-monitoring scaffolding.
Algorithmic Justice League: https://www.ajl.org/. The organizing vehicle that turned the Gender Shades paper into a campaign.
Upturn’s “Help Wanted” report and subsequent work on hiring tools: https://www.upturn.org/work/. A model of audit-adjacent advocacy that takes remedy seriously.
C. Richard Baker (2005), “What is the meaning of `the public interest’? Examining the ideology of the American public accounting profession,” Accounting, Auditing & Accountability Journal. The history lesson. Read it before you assume professional norms will hold the line.







Baker, C. Richard. 2005. “What Is the Meaning of ‘the Public Interest’? Examining the Ideology of the American Public Accounting Profession.” Accounting, Auditing & Accountability Journal 18 (5): 690–703.


Buolamwini, Joy, and Timnit Gebru. 2018. “Gender Shades: Intersectional Accuracy Disparities in Commercial Gender Classification.” Proceedings of the 1st Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency, 77–91.


Goodman, Bryce, and Julia Trehu. 2023. “AI Audit Washing and Accountability.” SSRN.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.


Raji, Inioluwa Deborah, Andrew Smart, Rebecca N. White, et al. 2020. “Closing the AI Accountability Gap: Defining an End-to-End Framework for Internal Algorithmic Auditing.” Proceedings of FAccT 2020, 33–44.

title: 9  Ownership
text:
10 Ownership
A commercial data broker will tell you, if pressed, that it owns the nine-hundred-variable dossier it sells about you. Ownership here means a property claim: rights to exclude, alienate, and monetize, backed by contract law. The dossier was assembled, often without your knowledge, from loyalty programs, voter files, credit header data, and the exhaust of a hundred free apps. You do not own it. The broker does.
This chapter argues that ownership in public-interest data science is a different word, and most of the trouble in the field comes from not noticing. The broker’s claim is ownership-as-property. The claim a public-interest steward has to make is ownership-as-stewardship: a maintenance obligation that requires labor, authority, and continuity across political cycles. You do not own a public dataset the way a broker owns a dossier. You own it the way a municipal archivist owns the city’s property records: accountable for its condition, usability, and survival, and unable to sell it off when the grant runs out.
Chapter 5 named continuity and safe scrutiny among the six elements of the installed base. Chapter 6 built the publishing pipeline. Chapter 7 built the audit. This chapter closes Part II by asking who holds the pipeline and the audit when the original builder leaves, and what it takes to structure that holding so it does not collapse into abandonment or capture.

title: 9  Ownership
text:
10.1 Two senses of the word
Ownership-as-property treats data as an asset with a rights-holder who can license, sublicense, sell, or destroy it. The vocabulary is real estate and copyright: chain of title, residual rights, assignment. The commercial data broker sits here. So, uncomfortably, does a great deal of “open” infrastructure when examined closely. A university’s IRB-governed research dataset may be open to collaborators, but the technology transfer office still treats it as an asset with a chain of title.
Ownership-as-stewardship treats data as an ongoing obligation. The steward is responsible for maintenance, documentation, remediation, and succession. The vocabulary is closer to trusteeship: obligations of care, prudence, and loyalty that cannot be disposed of without arranging for someone else to take them up. A municipal records manager is a steward. So is the volunteer who keeps a Wikipedia article on a regional dialect accurate for a decade. Neither owns the data in the property sense. Both are responsible for it in a way the property owner is not.
The distinction matters because most public-interest infrastructure is built on labor that presumes stewardship while operating inside institutions that default to property. Karasti and Blomberg (2018) studied this in long-running scientific infrastructures: the system works until the person doing the work moves, retires, or quits, and then it stops working in ways that take years to become visible. Chapter 14 returns to this as ghost work (Chapter 16); notice here that “open” infrastructure has a maintenance budget, usually paid in uncredited hours by people who cannot put the work on their performance review.
Schwartz and Cook (2002) made the argument for archives two decades before data science rediscovered it. Archives are not neutral containers: what gets kept, who decides, and who is near the keeping apparatus are political choices all the way down. Stoler (2002), studying colonial Dutch archives, took it further: the archive does not record power neutrally but is itself an instrument of power, and reading it requires reading the silences the archivist left behind. Translate to data infrastructure: there is no ownership-as-stewardship that is not also a politics of whose records survive, whose categories are used, and whose hands the maintenance falls to.

title: 9  Ownership
text:
10.2 Data trusts, briefly
One response to this mismatch is the data trust: a legal structure in which a trustee holds rights over a dataset on behalf of beneficiaries, with fiduciary duties enforceable in court. The Ada Lovelace Institute’s review (Ada Lovelace Institute 2021) found trusts are still more proposed than operational, but the proposal itself forces the questions: who the beneficiaries are, what the trustee may and may not do, what remedies are available if the trust is breached. Delacroix and Lawrence (2019) argue for bottom-up trusts in which users themselves constitute the beneficiary class.
You will not stand up a data trust in the tutorial. You will stand up something more modest: tiered access with a documented threat model and decision rules. The trust idea is the horizon; tiered access is the minimum you can maintain before one exists.

title: 9  Ownership
section: 10.3 Indigenous data sovereignty as a foundational challenge
text:
10.3 Indigenous data sovereignty as a foundational challenge
One caveat that will recur. “Stewardship with fiduciary duties to beneficiaries” presumes the beneficiaries can be identified, consented, and represented within the legal system governing the trustee. For many datasets that fails in ways the stewardship vocabulary is not equipped to solve. Te Mana Raraunga (Te Mana Raraunga 2018), the Maori Data Sovereignty Network, argues that Maori data is not a resource to be stewarded on Maori behalf by a non-Maori institution. It belongs to Maori, under Maori governance, as sovereignty rather than trust. The CARE Principles (Carroll et al. 2020) extend this to Indigenous data governance generally: collective benefit, authority to control, responsibility, and ethics are not add-ons to FAIR, they are a different frame.
Chapter 16 (Chapter 18) takes this up in detail, including when the right answer is not tiered access but refusal. Notice here that the tools you are about to build are not universally applicable. They work when a public has delegated stewardship to an institution. They do not work when a people has not delegated anything, and the question of whether the institution ever had standing remains open.

title: 9  Ownership
section: 10.4 The tutorial: tiering the Boulder campaign finance file
text:
10.4 The tutorial: tiering the Boulder campaign finance file
Return to the campaign finance dataset from Chapter 6. You published a cleaned file with donor names, amounts, dates, and candidates. Imagine the portal also included two columns you suppressed: donor home address and donor occupation with employer. Those are part of the public filings record in most jurisdictions, which is to say they are legally public. Legal publicness and safe republishability are not the same thing. An address plus a name plus an employer is enough to enable harassment, doxxing, and targeted advertising well beyond what a 1974 disclosure law contemplated.
You will build three access tiers over one SQLite database:

Public tier. Aggregates only: totals by candidate, zip code, filing period. No individual rows. Anyone with a browser.
Researcher tier. Individual rows with name, amount, date, candidate, occupation. No street address. Gated by a shared secret, logged at the query level. For credentialed journalists and academic researchers with a documented project.
Restricted tier. Full individual rows including address. Rare, case-by-case, granted by a named human, logged in a public register.


10.4.1 The schema and the views
Start with a single SQLite file, loaded from the Chapter 6 clean output with two columns appended: donor_address (street, city, state, zip) and donor_occupation_employer (free-text from the filing).
import sqlite3, pandas as pd

df = pd.read_parquet("data/boulder_campaign_finance_extended.parquet")
con = sqlite3.connect("campaign_finance.db")
df.to_sql("filings_raw", con, if_exists="replace", index=False)
Now define the access tiers as SQL views. Views grant query access to a projection of the base table without granting access to the table itself.
-- Public tier: aggregates only.
CREATE VIEW public_totals_by_candidate AS
SELECT candidate,
       COUNT(*)   AS n_contributions,
       SUM(amount) AS total_amount,
       MIN(date)   AS first_date,
       MAX(date)   AS last_date
FROM filings_raw
GROUP BY candidate;

CREATE VIEW public_totals_by_zip AS
SELECT substr(donor_address, -5) AS zip,
       COUNT(*)   AS n_contributions,
       SUM(amount) AS total_amount
FROM filings_raw
GROUP BY zip
HAVING n_contributions >= 5;  -- k-anonymity floor

-- Researcher tier: individual rows, no address.
CREATE VIEW researcher_filings AS
SELECT filing_id,
       contributor_clean,
       amount,
       date,
       candidate,
       committee,
       donor_occupation_employer
FROM filings_raw;

-- Restricted tier: the full table, with a trigger logging access.
-- (No view; access is to filings_raw itself, gated upstream.)
Three things to notice. The public view enforces a k-anonymity floor of five per zip, so a zip with a single donor is not re-identified by an aggregate. The researcher view strips the address but keeps occupation and employer, on the judgment that those are newsworthy in ways street addresses rarely are. The restricted tier has no view; it is the base table, controlled at the application layer because SQLite has no per-row permissions.


10.4.2 Credentials and environment variables
Read the researcher-tier shared secret from an environment variable, not from code.
import os
RESEARCHER_TOKEN = os.environ.get("CAMPAIGN_FINANCE_RESEARCHER_TOKEN")
if not RESEARCHER_TOKEN:
    raise RuntimeError("Set CAMPAIGN_FINANCE_RESEARCHER_TOKEN in the deploy environment.")
A thin Flask wrapper in front of Datasette gives you a basic-auth layer for the researcher endpoint. What matters is the pattern.
from flask import Flask, request, abort, jsonify
import sqlite3, hmac, datetime, json

app = Flask(__name__)
DB, AUDIT_LOG = "campaign_finance.db", "access_audit.jsonl"

def log_query(tier, user, query, ip):
    entry = {"ts": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
             "tier": tier, "user": user, "query": query, "ip": ip}
    with open(AUDIT_LOG, "a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")

@app.route("/public/totals")
def public_totals():
    rows = sqlite3.connect(DB).execute(
        "SELECT * FROM public_totals_by_candidate").fetchall()
    log_query("public", "anonymous", "totals_by_candidate", request.remote_addr)
    return jsonify(rows)

@app.route("/researcher/query", methods=["POST"])
def researcher_query():
    token = request.headers.get("X-Research-Token")
    if not token or not hmac.compare_digest(token, RESEARCHER_TOKEN):
        abort(401)
    user = request.headers.get("X-Research-User", "unknown")
    q = request.json.get("query", "")
    if not q.strip().lower().startswith("select"):
        abort(400)
    if "filings_raw" in q.lower():
        abort(403)
    rows = sqlite3.connect(DB).execute(q).fetchall()
    log_query("researcher", user, q, request.remote_addr)
    return jsonify(rows)
This is not a hardened production system. A determined attacker can evade the two string checks; a motivated researcher can reconstruct sensitive joins. The point is making the access tiers legible as code, with each tier’s permissions visible in one file to a code reviewer.






TipThe Missing Manual



Environment-variable credentials are not security. They are a social contract with your deployment pipeline, and the pipeline leaks them in ways most tutorials never mention. A token printed to a container log because someone left DEBUG=true on in production is not a secret anymore. A token captured in a stack trace that a Sentry integration forwarded to a third-party error tracker is not a secret either. A token committed to git history in a .env file two refactors ago and never rotated is, for practical purposes, the plaintext it started as. Treat the deployment pipeline as part of the attack surface. Rotate tokens on a schedule, not only after incidents. Audit logging configuration for what it captures on exception. “The token is in an environment variable” buys you roughly as much safety as a locked car in a rough neighborhood.




10.4.3 Decision rules in YAML
The code enforces the tiers. The decision rules say who belongs in which tier, and they are the part that will be contested. Keep them out of the code and in a versioned document.
# access_policy.yaml
version: 2026-04-15
dataset: boulder_campaign_finance
owner: Boulder Open Data Working Group

tiers:
  public:
    audience: anyone
    contents: aggregates, k >= 5
    authentication: none
    logging: request-level, 30 days
  researcher:
    audience:
      - credentialed journalists with published affiliation
      - academic researchers with IRB approval or equivalent
      - civic-tech projects with a named public deliverable
    contents: individual rows, no street address
    authentication: shared token, user-identified
    logging: per-query, 1 year, publicly aggregated monthly
    review: quarterly, stewards plus one external reviewer
  restricted:
    audience: case-by-case, rare
    contents: full rows including address
    authentication: named human approval, logged in public register
    review: per request, two stewards plus one community member

revocation:
  grounds: [misuse, undisclosed affiliation change, repeated scope violation]
  process: notice, 14-day response, appeal to full steward group
  public_notice: revocation counts published quarterly, reasons anonymized
The YAML does nothing by itself. It is a document you can point to when a request comes in or a classmate peer-reviews your design. Its value is forcing you to answer the questions before answering them feels like backtracking.


10.4.4 The threat model
The last artifact is the threat model: a structured description of who might attack this system, what they would be after, and what the impact would be if they succeeded. Most “security” documentation in data science projects is either absent or performative. A workable threat model is a page.
# Threat model: Boulder campaign finance, tiered access
Version: 2026-04-15. Review cadence: every 6 months.

## Assets
- A1. Individual donor records (name, amount, date).
- A2. Donor home addresses (within A1, higher sensitivity).
- A3. Aggregate totals (published openly).
- A4. Access logs (reveal who is researching whom).
- A5. Researcher tokens (credential).

## Actors and motivations
- T1. A harasser targeting donors to a specific candidate. Seeks A2 via A1.
- T2. A commercial data broker enriching a dossier. Seeks A1 at scale.
- T3. A political opposition researcher building an intimidation list. Seeks A2, A4.
- T4. A careless researcher exfiltrating A1 onto an unsecured laptop.
- T5. A state actor subpoenaing A4 to identify who accessed whose records.

## Attack paths
- P1. Token leak via logs or git history (T2, T3).
- P2. SQL that evades view restrictions to reach filings_raw (T1, T2).
- P3. Joining public aggregates with external data to re-identify small-zip donors (T1).
- P4. Social-engineering a steward into granting restricted access (T3).
- P5. Compelled disclosure of A4 (T5); no technical mitigation, only retention policy.

## Mitigations (current)
- M1. Tokens in env vars, rotated every 90 days (P1, partial).
- M2. Query whitelist and view restriction (P2, partial).
- M3. k >= 5 floor on aggregates (P3).
- M4. Two-person rule for restricted grants; public register (P4).
- M5. Access logs retained 12 months, then rotated (P5).

## Gaps
- G1. No rate limiting on researcher endpoint.
- G2. No differential privacy on small-zip aggregates.
- G3. No legal review of what logs must be produced under CORA.
The threat model is boring to write and instantly valuable. It turns “we took security seriously” into a specific list of worries, mitigations, and open gaps.

title: 9  Ownership
section: 10.5 Why “just use GitHub” is stewardship outsourcing
text:
10.5 Why “just use GitHub” is stewardship outsourcing
There is a tempting shortcut. Rather than stand up your own tiered access, put aggregates in a public GitHub repo, individual rows in a private one, done. GitHub handles authentication. GitHub handles logging. GitHub is free.
GitHub is also a platform owned by Microsoft, operated under Terms of Service it may unilaterally change, subject to U.S. export controls and sanctions, hosting content at its discretion. Over the past decade it has deplatformed projects for reasons ranging from copyright disputes to sanctions compliance, and has had outages long enough to take live public dashboards offline for a working day. None of this makes GitHub unusable. It does mean “just use GitHub” is not a stewardship decision. It is a stewardship outsourcing decision, with terms you did not negotiate.
The same argument applies to Google Sheets, Airtable, Notion. Each is a reasonable tool inside a stewardship arrangement. None of them, alone, is the stewardship arrangement. The political economy question Part II keeps returning to: who maintains the infrastructure your project depends on, and what happens when they stop.






NoteIn the Public Interest



Ownership-as-stewardship turns the continuity element of the installed base (Chapter 7) from a slogan into a job. A dataset that is not owned by anyone, in the stewardship sense, loses continuity within two organizational transitions: the original maintainer leaves, the replacement does not know the file exists, the permalink breaks. Safe scrutiny suffers the same fate: an access tier that is not actively maintained degrades into either a free-for-all (nobody rotates credentials) or a locked vault (nobody remembers who is authorized). Treat stewardship as a named position with a budget, not a gesture on a funder’s report. If your project cannot name the steward, by role if not by person, it does not yet have one. Chapter 22 (Chapter 24) picks this up as custodianship.

title: 9  Ownership
text:
10.6 Exercises
Exercise 8.1 (Guided). Extend the Chapter 6 Boulder campaign finance dataset with two new columns, donor_address and donor_occupation_employer, using the synthetic fields in data/boulder_campaign_finance_extended.parquet. Implement the three SQL views. Stand up the Flask wrapper locally, run one query against each tier, and capture the resulting audit log entries. Submit the SQL, the Flask app, and the first ten lines of your audit log.
Exercise 8.2 (Analytic). Write the threat_model.md and access_policy.yaml for Exercise 8.1 as applied to your own context. Name three actors specific to your setting, three attack paths, and three mitigations. Identify at least two gaps you are not yet mitigating. Commit both files alongside the code.
Exercise 8.3 (Peer review). Exchange your Exercise 8.2 artifacts with a classmate. Review their threat model and access policy against two questions: is there an attacker missing that you would add, and is there a decision rule in the YAML more ambiguous than it reads? Write a 300-word review identifying two concrete gaps. You will not be graded on whether the gaps were real; you will be graded on whether they were specific enough to act on.
Exercise 8.4 (Applied research). Find a real-world data trust, data cooperative, or data commons operating (not merely proposed) in 2026. Candidates include UK Biobank, Salus Coop in Barcelona, SAGE Bionetworks’ governance frameworks, or cases tracked by the Ada Lovelace Institute. Document its governance in 500 to 800 words: who the trustees are, who the beneficiaries are, what rights beneficiaries have, what happens if the trust is wound up, and where the money comes from. Cite the governance documents, not journalistic summaries.
Exercise 8.5 (Open-ended, project-linked). Return to your Exercise 1.5 project sketch. For each infrastructure your project depends on (hosting, data source, library, registry, funder), name the steward in the ownership-as-stewardship sense. For two of those stewards, write one paragraph on what happens to your project if they stop maintaining their part tomorrow. If your answer is “I would be fine,” revisit whether you correctly identified the dependency. Add the result to your sketch file.

title: 9  Ownership
text:
10.7 Looking ahead
Part II ends here. You have three values in hand: openness (Chapter 6) as the configuration that makes public data legible, oversight (Chapter 7) as the arrangement that makes system behavior visible to parties with authority to act, and ownership (this chapter) as the stewardship obligation that makes both survive political change. None is sufficient alone. Each, as Keegan (2026) argues, is a working configuration of the installed base rather than a principle to be invoked on its own.
Part III turns from values to lineages: the professional traditions that have done this work longer than data science has existed. Chapter 9 begins with journalism, because journalists have been wrestling with ownership-as-stewardship over source material, documents, and leaked data for more than a century, and they have working answers to questions the data science field is still formulating. The move from Part II to Part III is not theory to practice. It is a move across fields that have been practicing already, so you can import their methods without pretending you invented them.

title: 9  Ownership
text:
10.8 Further Reading and Resources

Helena Karasti and Jeanette Blomberg (2018), “Studying infrastructuring ethnographically,” CSCW, 27: 233 to 265. The reference this chapter leans on for what maintenance labor actually is.
Joan M. Schwartz and Terry Cook (2002), “Archives, records, and power,” Archival Science, 2: 1 to 19. The foundational argument for archives as instruments of power.
Ann Laura Stoler (2002), Along the Archival Grain. A full-length study of reading archives as political objects; the methodology generalizes to civic datasets.
Ada Lovelace Institute, “Exploring legal mechanisms for data stewardship” (2021): https://www.adalovelaceinstitute.org/report/legal-mechanisms-data-stewardship/.
Te Mana Raraunga, “Principles of Maori data sovereignty” (2018): https://www.temanararaunga.maori.nz/tutohinga. The foundational statement of Indigenous data sovereignty in Aotearoa New Zealand.
Stephanie Russo Carroll et al. (2020), “The CARE Principles for Indigenous data governance,” Data Science Journal, 19: 43. The complement and corrective to FAIR.
Sasha Costanza-Chock (2020), Design Justice. Stewardship as community-led rather than institutionally-delegated; read chapter 4 with this chapter in view.
Open Data Institute, “Data stewardship: What it is and why it matters”: https://theodi.org/insights/guides/data-stewardship/.
Sylvie Delacroix and Neil D. Lawrence (2019), “Bottom-up data trusts,” International Data Privacy Law, 9(4): 236 to 252.
OWASP, “Threat Modeling Cheat Sheet”: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Threat_Modeling_Cheat_Sheet.html.







Ada Lovelace Institute. 2021. Exploring Legal Mechanisms for Data Stewardship. Ada Lovelace Institute; AI Council. https://www.adalovelaceinstitute.org/report/legal-mechanisms-data-stewardship/.


Carroll, Stephanie Russo, Ibrahim Garba, Oscar L. Figueroa-Rodríguez, et al. 2020. “The CARE Principles for Indigenous Data Governance.” Data Science Journal 19: 43.


Delacroix, Sylvie, and Neil D. Lawrence. 2019. “Bottom-up Data Trusts: Disturbing the ‘One Size Fits All’ Approach to Data Governance.” International Data Privacy Law 9 (4): 236–52.


Karasti, Helena, and Jeanette Blomberg. 2018. “Studying Infrastructuring Ethnographically.” Computer Supported Cooperative Work 27: 233–65.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.


Schwartz, Joan M., and Terry Cook. 2002. “Archives, Records, and Power: The Making of Modern Memory.” Archival Science 2: 1–19.


Stoler, Ann Laura. 2002. Along the Archival Grain: Epistemic Anxieties and Colonial Common Sense. Princeton University Press.


Te Mana Raraunga. 2018. Principles of Māori Data Sovereignty. Māori Data Sovereignty Network. https://www.temanararaunga.maori.nz/tutohinga.

title: 10  Journalism
text:
11 Journalism
Part II closed with ownership as stewardship. Part III opens with a profession that has been stewarding sensitive public documents since well before data science named itself. A reporter who sits on a leaked database for six months while corroborating the identities of the people inside it is doing, as routine practice, the work the last four chapters spent pages trying to name. The tradition is called journalism, and if you want to make data science useful to the public, journalism is the lineage most worth reading as a set of instructions rather than as inspiration.
This chapter is not a history of journalism. It is a prompt to notice things the data science field, including its academic algorithmic-accountability wing, continues to rediscover a decade late. Newsrooms worked out methods for verifying records against sources, for publishing under legal threat, for collaborating across borders on datasets no one outlet could analyze alone, and for extending right of reply to the subjects of an investigation before the article ran. These are verification routines that, when you import them into academic or civic data practice, raise the evidentiary floor of the work in ways the field’s internal conversations about “reproducibility” rarely manage on their own.
The chapter’s running case is the one that pulled the fairness-in-machine-learning conversation into public view in 2016: ProPublica’s Machine Bias investigation of the COMPAS recidivism risk score (Angwin et al. 2016). You will download ProPublica’s published data and notebook, reproduce one of its central statistics, compare your numbers to the article’s, and read the methodological rejoinder from Flores, Bechtel, and Lowenkamp (2016). The point is not to adjudicate the dispute. The point is to notice what made the dispute possible, and what it cost ProPublica to invite it.

title: 10  Journalism
text:
11.1 The watchdog role, operationalized
The “Fourth Estate” is a metaphor that carries more than it can cash out. A fourth branch of government exists nowhere in any constitution. What does exist, across jurisdictions, is a loose professional arrangement: newsrooms with libel insurance, public-records statutes that force documents into the open, editors who can say no to a story, and a readership that funds the operation, imperfectly and with worsening economics, through subscriptions and advertising. The watchdog function is that arrangement working well enough to subject powerful institutions to scrutiny they would prefer to avoid.
Data journalism is the computational specialization of that function. Diakopoulos (2019) documents how algorithmic accountability reporting emerged as newsrooms learned to treat software as a beat: not a tool for telling stories faster, but a subject of investigation in its own right. The investigative journalism tradition he chronicles, institutionalized in organizations like Investigative Reporters and Editors (IRE) and its data-focused conference NICAR, developed verification standards that predate and, in important respects, outpace the academic literature on algorithmic audits.
Four of those standards bear naming here, because you will use them in the tutorial.
Sourcing means a claim in a published story is traceable to a document, a dataset, or a named human. A reporter’s notebook is a provenance chain. When a reporter writes that the city’s benefits portal denied 12 percent of applicants for a missing signature, someone can ask which 12 percent, from which table, queried on which date, and the answer exists.
Corroboration means a claim that matters rests on more than one independent piece of evidence. The dataset says X. A former employee also said X. A public filing from a different year also said X. Single-source claims are not forbidden, but they are flagged and carefully worded.
Right of reply means the subjects of an investigation, including the institutions whose data is under scrutiny, receive the findings before publication and have an opportunity to respond. The response may be a correction, a denial, a contextual statement, or silence. Each has an editorial consequence.
Publication with documentation means the story travels with its evidence, or with enough of its evidence that readers and other newsrooms can follow the inference. Not every dataset can be released. The inference chain, the decisions made in cleaning the data, the variables selected, and the judgment calls made are documented somewhere readers can inspect.
Academic researchers use none of these consistently. Peer review substitutes, poorly, for the last and makes almost no demands on the first three. One of this chapter’s claims is that academic data science, when it engages public systems, should steal from journalism rather than reinvent.

title: 10  Journalism
text:
11.2 FOIA as infrastructure
One piece of journalism’s installed base deserves separate mention because Chapter 10 (Chapter 12) takes it up in detail: the Freedom of Information Act and its state equivalents. FOIA turns the watchdog function from a polite request into a procedural right. A reporter writes, the agency has a statutory deadline to respond, and a denial can be appealed to a court. The infrastructure is imperfect, underfunded, and routinely abused through delay and overbroad exemption. It is also the mechanism by which much of the material a data journalist analyzes comes into the world in the first place.
For the purposes of this chapter, notice two things. First, FOIA is what Chapter 5 (Chapter 7) calls safe scrutiny with a statutory backbone: the reporter does not have to trust the agency’s goodwill, because the statute provides a lever. Second, the datasets journalists release through their reporting are often FOIA products that the agency itself never voluntarily published. ProPublica’s COMPAS data is not a FOIA release (it came through a public-records request to Broward County, Florida, and a handful of correspondences with the vendor), but its genealogy sits in the same family of documents-prised-loose-through-procedure that the next chapter will train you to file.

title: 10  Journalism
section: 11.3 Cross-border collaboration as a scaling strategy
text:
11.3 Cross-border collaboration as a scaling strategy
A single newsroom can handle a dataset of several thousand rows. It cannot handle 11.5 million documents. The Panama Papers leak, published by the International Consortium of Investigative Journalists (ICIJ) in 2016, distributed the analysis across more than a hundred newsrooms in dozens of jurisdictions, each working on the documents relevant to their country, under coordinated publication embargoes. The Paradise Papers (2017) and the Pandora Papers (2021) used the same template. Forbidden Stories, a separate consortium, exists specifically to continue the work of journalists who have been killed or imprisoned, distributing their unpublished material among member newsrooms so that silencing any single reporter does not silence the story. Lighthouse Reports, based in the Netherlands, has built a collaborative model for algorithmic-accountability reporting across European outlets, including the Rotterdam welfare-algorithm investigation you saw in Chapter 3.
The method is worth importing to academic data work. A multi-country welfare-algorithm analysis is not, structurally, a different problem from a multi-site clinical trial. What cross-border journalism has worked out that academic consortia often have not is the governance stack that makes the collaboration survive leaks, legal threats, and deadline pressure: secure document-sharing platforms, shared style guides, coordinated fact-checking, joint legal review, and, critically, the right of any newsroom to pull out without burning the rest of it down. Zeng, Chan, and Schafer (2022) show how much of this infrastructure is culturally located: what works in a consortium of Western European outlets is not portable to a newsroom operating under tighter state control, and an academic collaboration needs to understand transferability before it assumes the model scales.

title: 10  Journalism
section: 11.4 The running example: reproducing Machine Bias
text:
11.4 The running example: reproducing Machine Bias
ProPublica’s 2016 investigation of the COMPAS risk-assessment tool, used by courts across the United States to inform pretrial and sentencing decisions, argued that the score was biased against Black defendants. The finding that drew the most attention was a disparity in false-positive rates: among defendants who did not reoffend within two years, Black defendants were roughly twice as likely as white defendants to have been classified as high-risk by COMPAS. The article ran with its dataset and Jupyter notebook posted to GitHub at propublica/compas-analysis, an act of methodological transparency that was, in 2016, unusually generous by newsroom standards.
The tutorial walks you through reproducing the two-year recidivism-rate statistic and comparing your numbers to the article’s. You will find small divergences. You will then read Flores and colleagues’ rejoinder (2016) and identify which divergences matter for the argument.

11.4.1 Pulling the data
The dataset lives in the repository as compas-scores-two-years.csv. You can clone the repo or download the file directly.
import pandas as pd

URL = (
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/propublica/"
    "compas-analysis/master/compas-scores-two-years.csv"
)

df = pd.read_csv(URL)
df.shape
# => (7214, 53)

df[["sex", "race", "age", "priors_count",
    "decile_score", "two_year_recid"]].head()
ProPublica applied filters to this file before running their analysis. The notebook documents them. Cases where the COMPAS assessment was not drawn within 30 days of arrest were excluded, along with cases missing key fields. Replicate the filter before you replicate the statistic:
mask = (
    (df["days_b_screening_arrest"] <= 30) &
    (df["days_b_screening_arrest"] >= -30) &
    (df["is_recid"] != -1) &
    (df["c_charge_degree"] != "O") &
    (df["score_text"] != "N/A")
)
filtered = df[mask].copy()
filtered.shape
# => (6172, 53)   # matches the ProPublica notebook's post-filter count


11.4.2 Reproducing the statistic
The headline finding compared false-positive and false-negative rates across racial groups. A false positive here is a defendant labeled medium or high risk by COMPAS who did not reoffend within two years. Recode the score and compute the contingency table:
filtered["high_risk"] = (
    filtered["score_text"].isin(["High", "Medium"]).astype(int)
)

by_race = (
    filtered.groupby("race")
            .apply(lambda g: pd.Series({
                "n": len(g),
                "fp_rate": (
                    ((g["high_risk"] == 1) & (g["two_year_recid"] == 0)).sum()
                    / (g["two_year_recid"] == 0).sum()
                ),
                "fn_rate": (
                    ((g["high_risk"] == 0) & (g["two_year_recid"] == 1)).sum()
                    / (g["two_year_recid"] == 1).sum()
                ),
            }))
            .reset_index()
)
by_race.query("race in ['African-American', 'Caucasian']")
# => African-American  fp_rate ~ 0.45, fn_rate ~ 0.28
# => Caucasian         fp_rate ~ 0.23, fn_rate ~ 0.48


11.4.3 Checking for divergence
Your numbers will land close to the published rates but not identical. Possible sources of divergence, all worth documenting:

Pandas has changed .apply semantics between versions, which can alter group ordering and rounding.
The underlying CSV has been touched since 2016; you can pin to a specific commit hash if your analysis needs to be stable.
The “Medium or High” threshold is one of two reasonable ways to binarize score_text; the notebook uses this one, but the article’s prose occasionally shifts definitions.
Rows with missing two_year_recid flags are handled inconsistently across replications in the literature.

Write a brief methods note, in plain prose, documenting what you did: the commit hash you pinned to, the filter you applied, the binarization rule you chose, and the precise numbers you got. This note is not a formality. It is the artifact that lets a future reader distinguish a substantive disagreement with ProPublica’s argument from a transcription error in your pipeline.






TipThe Missing Manual



When a newsroom publishes a methods notebook, the interesting methodological disputes are always in the details the article’s prose does not cover. Read the notebook before you read the article, not after. You will see which filters were applied, which variables were dropped, and which binarization decisions the reporter made. These are the decisions the methodological rejoinders will land on. The practice of “right of reply” that journalists offer to the subjects of an investigation is not merely a courtesy; it is a verification mechanism. An institution given a chance to point out an error before publication often will, and the correction improves the final story. Academic researchers should steal this. Send your draft analysis to the institution whose system you are auditing, with a specific deadline and specific questions. Do it even when you are under no obligation to.

title: 10  Journalism
section: 11.5 Flores, Bechtel, and Lowenkamp: the rejoinder
text:
11.5 Flores, Bechtel, and Lowenkamp: the rejoinder
Flores and colleagues (2016) published a direct response to ProPublica in Federal Probation, arguing that ProPublica’s framing confused several distinct fairness criteria and that, on the criteria relevant to how risk assessments are actually used in court, COMPAS performed reasonably across racial groups. Their critique turned on the distinction between equal false-positive rates (ProPublica’s focus) and equal positive predictive value (the vendor’s focus). Subsequent literature, especially Chouldechova (2018) and the broader fair-ML community, established that these criteria cannot simultaneously hold when base rates differ across groups. The impossibility result is not an escape hatch. It is a forcing function: choose which fairness criterion matters for the decision the tool informs, and own the choice.
Read the rejoinder alongside your reproduction. Notice which of its criticisms your replication can speak to (the binarization choice, the filter selection) and which require different data than ProPublica released. Notice also that the rejoinder exists at all because the data was public. A privately held investigation of a proprietary tool with a gag order on the dataset would not have generated the follow-up literature that now occupies six hundred entries in the FAccT bibliography.






NoteIn the Public Interest



Chapter 6 (Chapter 8) argued that openness is not the absence of friction but the presence of a legible publication. ProPublica’s decision to publish the COMPAS dataset alongside the Machine Bias article is the move that made the dispute with Flores and colleagues possible. An enclosed analysis, with the data held internally and the findings communicated only through the article, would have foreclosed the rejoinder. The methodological disagreement would have remained a matter of he-said-she-said between a newsroom and a vendor, and the fair-ML literature that grew from the case would not have had an empirical object to work on. Openness here does not just serve ProPublica’s credibility. It serves the capacity of the field to argue with ProPublica and improve on its work. Keegan (2026) names this configuration as safe scrutiny reinforced by linkable provenance. Enclosure in the name of protecting a finding is a defensive posture that damages the finding’s standing over time. Chapters 7 (Chapter 9) and 8 built on this: oversight requires an object to oversee, and the object has to survive long enough to be overseen.

title: 10  Journalism
text:
11.6 What academic researchers can learn
Two practices from the newsroom side will not fit on a single syllabus, but both belong in your working repertoire.
The first is provenance as a default, not an exception. A reporter who cannot cite a claim cannot publish the claim. An academic researcher who cannot trace a number back through the cleaning pipeline to a source row is, in the newsroom’s frame, not yet ready to publish either. The reproducibility frameworks you will meet in Chapter 11 (Chapter 13) encode this habit formally. The journalism version is older and more severe: you do not publish unless you can show your work, and “show your work” is not a virtue, it is the floor.
The second is the right of reply, already named in the callout above. When you audit a public system, send a draft of your findings to the institution responsible for the system, with a specific deadline and specific questions, and document whatever response you receive. You will get three kinds of reply. A silence, which you note. A correction, which you incorporate. A substantive disagreement, which you either argue with in the text or adopt as a limitation. All three improve the final work. The institutional pressure against this practice, in academic settings, is that it can look like giving subjects a veto. It is not a veto. It is a verification step that produces a better paper and a defensible defense when the paper is challenged.

title: 10  Journalism
text:
11.7 Looking ahead
Chapter 10 (Chapter 12) takes up the legal infrastructure that journalism has been navigating for a century and a half and that data science, when it engages public systems, is now learning to navigate. FOIA and its state equivalents, open-meetings statutes, research exceptions to the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act, and the threat environment documented in Sandvig v. Barr all constrain what you can observe, publish, and discover. Journalism’s tradition is older than academic algorithmic accountability and more politically mature, and the political maturity is inseparable from decades of litigation about what a reporter is allowed to do. Chapter 17 (Chapter 19) picks up the translation question from the other side: how to turn a data investigation into a piece of public writing that a non-specialist reader can act on.

title: 10  Journalism
text:
11.8 Exercises
Exercise 9.1 (Guided). Clone propublica/compas-analysis from GitHub. Run the filter described above against compas-scores-two-years.csv and reproduce the false-positive and false-negative rates by race. Write a one-page methods note documenting the commit hash you pinned to, the filter you applied, the binarization rule you used, and the numbers you obtained. Save the note as exercises/ch09_compas_methods.md. Submit a link to your git commit.
Exercise 9.2 (Analytic). Read Flores, Bechtel, and Lowenkamp (2016). In 500 to 700 words, identify the three criticisms the rejoinder makes that your Exercise 9.1 replication can speak to, and the criticisms it makes that would require different data. Your reply does not need to take a side; it needs to distinguish what the released dataset can and cannot settle.
Exercise 9.3 (Comparative). Find a data journalism investigation from a non-U.S. outlet published in the last three years. Candidates include Forbidden Stories, Lighthouse Reports, Der Spiegel’s data desk, Suddeutsche Zeitung, CORRECTIV, or the ICIJ. In 400 to 600 words, analyze the piece’s structure: what is the central claim, what data supports it, what was published alongside the story, which collaborators are credited, and what verification practices are visible in the methodology note (if any). Compare the published methods transparency to the ProPublica standard.
Exercise 9.4 (Research). Locate a methods notebook from a recent investigation by The Markup, ProPublica, The Pudding, Reveal, or Bellingcat. Document the notebook in 300 words: which filters it applied, which decisions it records in comments, and one decision it makes without documenting. Submit the notebook URL and your documentation together. The point is to practice reading a notebook as a reviewer, not just as a user.
Exercise 9.5 (Open-ended, project-linked). Identify a story from your own community, city, county, campus, or professional field, that would benefit from data journalism methods. In two to three paragraphs: name the story, name the data that would be required, identify the likely source (FOIA, scraping a public portal, a dataset already open), and name one person or institution who would be the subject of right-of-reply. Add the result to your ch01_project_sketch.md file. If the story overlaps with something you are already planning for a later chapter’s exercise, say so.

title: 10  Journalism
text:
11.9 Further Reading and Resources

Julia Angwin, Jeff Larson, Surya Mattu, and Lauren Kirchner (2016), “Machine Bias,” ProPublica: https://www.propublica.org/article/machine-bias-risk-assessments-in-criminal-sentencing. Read the article, then read the companion methodology at https://www.propublica.org/article/how-we-analyzed-the-compas-recidivism-algorithm.
ProPublica’s compas-analysis repository: https://github.com/propublica/compas-analysis. The Jupyter notebook in the repository root is the canonical object for this chapter.
Anthony W. Flores, Kristin Bechtel, and Christopher T. Lowenkamp (2016), “False positives, false negatives, and false analyses: A rejoinder to ‘Machine Bias,’” Federal Probation, 80(2): 38 to 46.
Nicholas Diakopoulos (2019), Automating the News: How Algorithms Are Rewriting the Media. Harvard University Press. The standing academic treatment of algorithmic accountability reporting.
The Markup’s “Show Your Work” series: https://themarkup.org/series/show-your-work. Each investigation publishes its methodology as a standalone piece; read three of them back to back.
Investigative Reporters and Editors (IRE) and the National Institute for Computer-Assisted Reporting (NICAR): https://www.ire.org. The professional home of U.S. data journalism; the NICAR conference proceedings are an underappreciated methods literature.
International Consortium of Investigative Journalists (ICIJ): https://www.icij.org. The Panama, Paradise, and Pandora Papers investigations, with extensive documentation of the cross-border collaboration model.
Lighthouse Reports: https://www.lighthousereports.com. European consortium specializing in algorithmic-accountability investigations; the Rotterdam welfare-algorithm piece is a useful companion to Chapter 3.
Forbidden Stories: https://forbiddenstories.org. The consortium that continues the work of journalists who have been silenced.
The Data Journalism Handbook 2, edited by Jonathan Gray and Liliana Bounegru (2021), open-access at https://datajournalism.com/read/handbook/two. The field’s living textbook, with chapters by practitioners on verification, scraping, collaboration, and ethics.
The Pudding: https://pudding.cool. For a lighter complement: visual essays whose source code ships with the story.







Angwin, Julia, Jeff Larson, Surya Mattu, and Lauren Kirchner. 2016. Machine Bias: There’s Software Used Across the Country to Predict Future Criminals. And It’s Biased Against Blacks. ProPublica. https://www.propublica.org/article/machine-bias-risk-assessments-in-criminal-sentencing.


Chouldechova, Alexandra, Diana Benavides-Prado, Oleksandr Fialko, and Rhema Vaithianathan. 2018. A Case Study of Algorithm-Assisted Decision Making in Child Maltreatment Hotline Screening. Proceedings of the 1st Conference on Fairness, Accountability,; Transparency.


Diakopoulos, Nicholas. 2019. Automating the News: How Algorithms Are Rewriting the Media. Harvard University Press.


Flores, Anthony W., Kristin Bechtel, and Christopher T. Lowenkamp. 2016. “False Positives, False Negatives, and False Analyses: A Rejoinder to ‘Machine Bias’.” Federal Probation 80 (2): 38–46.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.


Zeng, Jing, Chung-Hong Chan, and Mike S. Schäfer. 2022. “Contested Chinese Dreams of AI?: Public Discourse about Artificial Intelligence on WeChat and People’s Daily Online.” Information, Communication & Society 25 (3): 319–40.

title: 11  Law
text:
12 Law
Chapter 9 (Chapter 11) ended on a newsroom practice that depends on a statute. A reporter can ask politely for a document, an agency can decline politely, and without public-records law that is the end of the matter. With public-records law, the reporter writes a different kind of letter: one that cites a specific section of code, imposes a response deadline, and preserves the right to appeal. The letter is still a request. What makes it effective is the legal infrastructure behind it.
This chapter treats law as a lineage. Not because data scientists should become lawyers, but because the professions that have thought hardest about pursuing the public interest under adversarial conditions work inside courtrooms and statutes. You will read two law and society scholars on what “public interest lawyering” is, draft and track a records request, and sit with the fact that the same legal infrastructure that enables accountability work can be weaponized against the people it is supposed to serve. The running example is a class-wide Colorado Open Records Act (CORA) tracker, built on SQLite, that you and your classmates populate across a semester. By the end, you will have a reusable schema, a drafted request you can send, and a small corpus of released documents analyzed with tools no more exotic than Tesseract and pandas.

title: 11  Law
section: 12.1 Public interest lawyering as a profession
text:
12.1 Public interest lawyering as a profession
Albiston and Nielsen (2017) studied the U.S. public interest law field through a pair of surveys spanning three decades. Public interest lawyering is not a specialty like tax or real estate. It is a stance toward practice: representing interests the market will not pay to represent, often on behalf of clients whose stake in a legal outcome is structural rather than individual. Cause lawyering, the term Nielsen and Albiston (2006) develop in earlier work, treats a case as an instrument for changing a rule rather than a dispute between two parties.
Their empirical argument is that the field has settled into a two-tier structure. A small number of well-funded organizations (the ACLU, the NAACP Legal Defense Fund, the Electronic Frontier Foundation) work at the appellate-strategy and legislative-advocacy level. A much larger tier of legal aid societies and clinical programs absorbs front-line demand in family court, eviction proceedings, and benefits appeals. The first tier has strategic leverage. The second has caseload and direct contact with the communities served.
The analogy for data science is not strained. A small set of civil-society organizations works at the policy and publication tier. A much larger set of journalists, clinicians, union researchers, community organizers, and municipal IT staff does the front-line work of unpicking a broken benefits portal or a mis-calibrated risk score. The field’s self-understanding is weighted toward the first tier. The actual work is weighted toward the second. This chapter’s tools aim at the second.

title: 11  Law
section: 12.2 FOIA, CORA, and the statutory geometry
text:
12.2 FOIA, CORA, and the statutory geometry
The Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), enacted in 1966 and amended in 1974, 1996, and 2016, establishes a presumption that records held by federal executive-branch agencies are public unless they fall within nine enumerated exemptions. Chapter 3 (Chapter 5) already introduced the exemption list as a topology of what the government can decline to answer.
Every state has a parallel statute. Colorado’s is CORA, codified at C.R.S. § 24-72-201 et seq. CORA’s custodian-response deadline is three working days, extendable to ten for “extenuating circumstances,” shorter than FOIA’s twenty working days. Its fee schedule is capped for staff research and retrieval time. It has a distinct regime for criminal justice records under the Colorado Criminal Justice Records Act (CCJRA), which you need to know exists because a request blending administrative and criminal justice material will come back bifurcated, with the CCJRA portion subject to a “contrary to the public interest” balancing test the custodian applies at their discretion.
That discretion matters. A CORA request asking for “all emails from the Chief of Police mentioning ShotSpotter” will produce a different response than the same request phrased as “administrative records concerning the acquisition and deployment of ShotSpotter technology, including procurement correspondence, training materials, and use-of-force memoranda.” The first invites the custodian to classify the material as criminal justice records. The second names administrative categories and makes that classification harder to justify. This is the specificity a reporter brings to a sourcing interview, translated into statutory language.






TipThe Missing Manual



Most records-request software assumes your request succeeds the first time. It does not. A well-managed request produces a negotiation: the first response will be partial, redacted, mis-scoped, or delayed with a fee estimate designed to make you withdraw. Design your tracker for iteration. Log the followups. Log the appeals. Log the reasons the custodian gave for redactions. Redactions are themselves metadata: the pattern of what the agency blacks out tells you what its legal theory is before it articulates one. If three of four releases redact the same paragraph under the same exemption, you have a de facto policy you can write about, cite in an appeal, or hand to a lawyer.

title: 11  Law
section: 12.3 The CFAA and the chilling effect
text:
12.3 The CFAA and the chilling effect
Legal infrastructure is not only a resource for accountability work. It also constrains what accountability work is permitted to try. The Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA), enacted in 1986, criminalizes “unauthorized access” to a “protected computer.” For most of its life, the statute’s reach was interpreted broadly enough that violating a website’s terms of service (creating fake accounts to audit a housing platform, for example) exposed the researcher to federal felony liability. Academic researchers reasonably declined to do the research.
The Sandvig v. Barr litigation, brought by the ACLU on behalf of Christian Sandvig and co-plaintiffs (American Civil Liberties Union 2019), challenged that interpretation. The plaintiffs were researchers who wanted to audit online employment and housing platforms for race and gender discrimination, using paired accounts that technically violated the platforms’ terms of service. They sought a pre-enforcement declaration that the CFAA did not criminalize their research. In 2020, the D.C. District Court ruled in their favor on statutory grounds, narrowing the “exceeds authorized access” clause to exclude terms-of-service violations. The Supreme Court’s Van Buren decision in 2021 confirmed the narrowing in a separate case.
The chilling effect Sandvig addressed is the kind of infrastructure problem this book takes seriously. A statute that criminalizes a research method does not need to prosecute a single researcher to work. It only needs to produce enough ambient uncertainty that IRBs decline to approve, general counsel decline to defend, and graduate students rationally choose a different dissertation. Sandvig is a counter-example: an audit population that existed because a civil society organization absorbed the legal cost of clarifying the rule.

title: 11  Law
text:
12.4 SyRI, discovery, and weaponization
Chapter 3 (Chapter 5) already introduced the Dutch SyRI case as the successful civil society challenge to an opaque welfare-fraud algorithm. It is worth returning to here as a litigation strategy. The coalition (NJCM together with welfare-rights organizations and two individual plaintiffs) structured the suit around Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The District Court of The Hague ruled the SyRI statute incompatible with Article 8 in February 2020. In Albiston and Nielsen’s vocabulary, this was cause lawyering at its most strategic. The ruling did not end algorithmic welfare surveillance in the Netherlands; subsequent systems have appeared with adjusted frameworks. But it established a precedent the next iteration had to work around, which is what a win looks like here.
Legal discovery is a linkability mechanism the textbook framing often misses. A civil suit compels production of internal records no records request would dislodge, places human sources under oath, and produces interrogatory answers the party is legally bound to stand behind. The cost, for the researcher, is that discovery material is typically subject to a protective order: usable in the case, not releasable without the court’s permission. Chapter 16 (Chapter 18) will argue the instinct to refuse release is sometimes the right one; discovery is where the instinct is not optional.
Public records laws are neutral on their face. In practice, they can be turned against the populations they were built to protect. Immigration enforcement agencies have used state and local records requests to obtain residency, employment, and vehicle-registration data on non-citizens. School board minutes, including names of public commenters on immigration-related agenda items, have been harvested and forwarded to federal enforcement. The openness that lets a journalist investigate a sheriff’s office also lets a federal agency subpoena the sheriff’s record of who was booked, who posted bail, and who picked them up. This is not an argument against open records. It is an argument for thinking about records-request work the way Chapter 8 thought about stewardship: the same infrastructure that enables one use case enables every use case the statute does not exclude.

title: 11  Law
text:
12.5 Amicus briefs and MuckRock
Data scientists can contribute to public-interest legal work without becoming lawyers, through the amicus curiae (“friend of the court”) brief: a third-party submission from an organization with subject-matter expertise, offering the court context the named parties have not developed. In technology cases, amicus briefs from researchers have become a recognized genre: computer scientists explaining what an algorithm does, statisticians explaining what a disparate-impact measure measures. The intellectual demand is within the reach of a well-prepared graduate student working with an attorney. Chapter 20 will return to the rhetoric of writing for a decision-making audience; the amicus is a close cousin.
MuckRock, the nonprofit records-request platform founded in 2010, is the closest thing the U.S. records-request ecosystem has to shared infrastructure. It handles drafting, tracking, agency routing, followups, and public release. Its archive is itself a research resource: many of the documents you would otherwise FOIA have already been FOIA’d. Its public-by-default posture has costs, because some agencies respond differently to MuckRock than to an individual requester. The running example below builds a lighter-weight, self-hosted version. A classroom, a newsroom, or a research group needs a tracker even for requests it is not ready to expose publicly, and a SQLite database is the right scale.

title: 11  Law
section: 12.6 Tutorial: a CORA tracker in SQLite
text:
12.6 Tutorial: a CORA tracker in SQLite
The schema has four tables. Each request can have multiple responses (partial releases, supplementary productions after appeal). Each response can include multiple documents. Followups are requester-initiated actions (clarifying letters, appeals, fee-waiver requests) that shape the record without producing a response themselves.
import sqlite3
from pathlib import Path

DB_PATH = Path("cora_tracker.sqlite")

SCHEMA = """
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS requests (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    agency TEXT NOT NULL,
    date_submitted TEXT NOT NULL,
    description TEXT NOT NULL,
    status TEXT NOT NULL
        CHECK (status IN ('open','partial','closed','denied','appealed')),
    cora_or_foia TEXT NOT NULL
        CHECK (cora_or_foia IN ('CORA','FOIA','CCJRA','other')),
    associated_project TEXT
);
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS responses (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    request_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES requests(id),
    date_received TEXT NOT NULL,
    redactions_noted TEXT,
    documents_count INTEGER,
    notes TEXT
);
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS followups (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    request_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES requests(id),
    date TEXT NOT NULL,
    type TEXT NOT NULL
        CHECK (type IN ('clarification','fee_waiver','appeal','reminder','withdrawal')),
    content TEXT
);
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS documents (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    response_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES responses(id),
    filename TEXT NOT NULL,
    sha256 TEXT NOT NULL,
    size_bytes INTEGER
);
"""

with sqlite3.connect(DB_PATH) as conn:
    conn.executescript(SCHEMA)
Inserting a request, recording a response, and querying open requests older than thirty days:
from datetime import date, timedelta

def insert_request(agency, date_submitted, description,
                   cora_or_foia="CORA", associated_project=None,
                   status="open"):
    with sqlite3.connect(DB_PATH) as conn:
        cur = conn.execute(
            """INSERT INTO requests
               (agency, date_submitted, description, status,
                cora_or_foia, associated_project)
               VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)""",
            (agency, date_submitted, description, status,
             cora_or_foia, associated_project),
        )
        return cur.lastrowid

def record_response(request_id, date_received, documents_count=0,
                    redactions_noted=None, notes=None):
    with sqlite3.connect(DB_PATH) as conn:
        cur = conn.execute(
            """INSERT INTO responses
               (request_id, date_received, redactions_noted,
                documents_count, notes)
               VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)""",
            (request_id, date_received, redactions_noted,
             documents_count, notes),
        )
        return cur.lastrowid

def open_and_stale(days=30):
    cutoff = (date.today() - timedelta(days=days)).isoformat()
    with sqlite3.connect(DB_PATH) as conn:
        conn.row_factory = sqlite3.Row
        rows = conn.execute(
            """SELECT id, agency, date_submitted, description
               FROM requests
               WHERE status='open' AND date_submitted < ?
               ORDER BY date_submitted""",
            (cutoff,),
        ).fetchall()
    return [dict(r) for r in rows]

rid = insert_request(
    agency="Boulder Police Department",
    date_submitted="2026-02-03",
    description=("Administrative records concerning procurement "
                 "and deployment of automated license plate readers "
                 "(ALPRs), 2023-01-01 to present."),
    associated_project="alpr_audit_spring26",
)
stale = open_and_stale()
# => list of dicts you can iterate into followup emails
A stale-request query wired into a followup mailmerge has an outsized effect on response rates. The three-day deadline is not self-enforcing. The requester has to send the reminder.

title: 11  Law
text:
12.7 Drafting the letter
A CORA letter is four short paragraphs. Tone is formal, specificity is load-bearing, statutory citations do real work.

Dear Records Custodian,
Pursuant to the Colorado Open Records Act, C.R.S. § 24-72-201 et seq., I respectfully request the following records maintained by the Boulder Police Department:

All contracts, statements of work, and data-sharing agreements between the Department and any vendor of automated license plate reader (ALPR) technology, executed between January 1, 2023 and the date of this request;
Training materials and standard operating procedures governing ALPR deployment by Department personnel during that period;
Internal memoranda, including supervisor correspondence, concerning ALPR use in investigations during that period.

For the purposes of C.R.S. § 24-72-205(6), I request a fee waiver on the basis that these records concern a matter of public interest and I intend to disseminate the information through a published research report. Should estimated fees exceed $25, please contact me first. Should any portion be withheld, please cite the basis with specificity under C.R.S. § 24-72-204, and release any segregable non-exempt portions.

Four things in that letter do work. The statutory citation establishes that you know the law. The enumerated categories make the request specific enough that the custodian cannot lawfully refuse it as “overly broad,” the most common informal denial. The fee-waiver paragraph cites the actual subsection and invokes the “public interest” standard. The segregability clause forces the custodian to release non-exempt material rather than withholding an entire document because part of it is exempt.

title: 11  Law
section: 12.8 Analyzing a small corpus of released records
text:
12.8 Analyzing a small corpus of released records
A typical CORA release is a PDF, often scanned from paper, with redactions applied as black rectangles. Tesseract OCR produces readable text from the scans; pandas holds the metadata.
import subprocess, hashlib
import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path

def ocr_pdf(pdf_path, out_txt):
    tmp = Path(out_txt).with_suffix("")
    subprocess.run(["pdftoppm", "-r", "300", str(pdf_path),
                    str(tmp), "-png"], check=True)
    pages = sorted(tmp.parent.glob(tmp.name + "-*.png"))
    text = []
    for p in pages:
        r = subprocess.run(["tesseract", str(p), "-", "--psm", "6"],
                           capture_output=True, text=True, check=True)
        text.append(r.stdout)
    Path(out_txt).write_text("\n\f\n".join(text))

def sha256_of(path):
    h = hashlib.sha256()
    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(1 << 16), b""):
            h.update(chunk)
    return h.hexdigest()

records = []
for pdf in Path("releases/").glob("*.pdf"):
    txt = pdf.with_suffix(".txt")
    if not txt.exists():
        ocr_pdf(pdf, txt)
    records.append({
        "filename": pdf.name,
        "sha256": sha256_of(pdf),
        "text_length": len(txt.read_text()),
        "redaction_marks": txt.read_text().count("[REDACTED]"),
    })
df = pd.DataFrame(records)
df.sort_values("redaction_marks", ascending=False).head()
The “redaction marks” column is crude. The real signal comes from patterning: which document types have the heaviest redactions, which exemption codes the custodian cited, and which phrases recur near the redactions. A semester’s worth of pooled class requests is enough to describe the agency’s redaction practice with a confidence a single requester cannot reach alone.






NoteIn the Public Interest



Chapter 5 (Chapter 7) named remedy as the installed base’s final element, and Chapter 7’s oversight framework (Chapter 9) put it starkly: evidence only matters if it can trigger action. A records request that produces an unreadable scan of a redacted contract, and then sits on a researcher’s laptop, is not oversight. It is raw material for oversight.
