The tracker, letter template, and OCR pipeline above are the minimum scaffolding that turns that material into something a journalist can publish, an advocate can cite in a council meeting, an attorney can attach to a complaint, or a commissioner can reference in a rulemaking (Keegan 2026). The tools here advance oversight when they close the loop to remedy. They advance openness in a weaker sense even when they do not: a well-tracked request produces a record of what the agency said.

title: 11  Law
text:
12.9 Looking ahead
Public records work is slow. The typical timeline from a first CORA letter to a usable dataset is measured in months, and an appeal can extend it by a year. Reporters and lawyers budget for this. Data scientists, trained on the responsiveness of a public API, often do not, and the gap between what a researcher expects to take a week and what actually takes a semester is the biggest reason accountability work stalls. File requests you will not use for months. Chapter 21 (Chapter 23) takes up a different records-based genre, the public comment: similar infrastructure, opposite direction, you adding to the record rather than extracting from it.
Chapter 11 (Chapter 13) turns to accounting and auditing. Auditors, like public-interest lawyers, have spent a century working out methods for producing credible claims about institutions under adversarial conditions. What journalism contributes through sourcing and right of reply, and what law contributes through discovery and statutory procedure, accounting contributes through the audit opinion and its scaffolding: workpapers, materiality thresholds, and the reviewer who signs off on the signoff.

title: 11  Law
text:
12.10 Further Reading and Resources

Colorado Freedom of Information Coalition: https://coloradofoic.org/. The clearest practitioner guide to CORA, with sample letters and records of notable appeals (Colorado Freedom of Information Coalition 2024).
MuckRock: https://www.muckrock.com/. File requests, track them, and search prior releases before reinventing (MuckRock Foundation 2024).
Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press, Open Government Guide: https://www.rcfp.org/open-government-guide/. State-by-state comparative reference (Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press 2024).
Electronic Frontier Foundation, CFAA reform page: https://www.eff.org/issues/cfaa (Electronic Frontier Foundation 2024).
Sandvig v. Barr filings and briefs at ACLU: https://www.aclu.org/cases/sandvig-v-barr-challenge-cfaa-prohibition-uncovering-racial-discrimination-online (American Civil Liberties Union 2019).
Text of the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552: https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/5/552 (US Congress 1966). Reading the nine exemptions in order is thirty minutes well spent.
Colorado Open Records Act, C.R.S. § 24-72-201 et seq.: https://leg.colorado.gov/colorado-revised-statutes (Colorado General Assembly 1968).
Sunshine Week: https://sunshineweek.org/. The annual March observance that catalyzes most of the open-records coverage worth reading (News Leaders Association 2024).
Albiston and Nielsen (Albiston and Nielsen 2017) on the two-tier structure of U.S. public interest law.
Samantha Warren, “Where public records meet the public interest: A reporter’s field guide” (Warren 2025).

title: 11  Law
text:
12.11 Exercises
Exercise 10.1 (Guided). Draft a CORA or FOIA request on a topic you care about. Boulder, Denver, and Colorado state agencies are all reasonable targets; if you are not in Colorado, use your state’s equivalent statute. Your instructor will help scope the topic. Submit the request. Paste the confirmation into your class tracker.
Exercise 10.2 (Guided). Implement the SQLite schema. Insert your Exercise 10.1 request. Write a function that emits a formatted reminder email to any agency whose open request is older than the statutory deadline plus a grace period you justify in a comment. Commit the code to the class repository.
Exercise 10.3 (Analytic). Obtain a small corpus (five to ten documents) of released records, from MuckRock’s archive if your own request has not returned yet. OCR them. For each document, record the exemption codes cited, the types of content redacted, and the date. Produce a one-page analysis describing the redaction pattern. Do not over-interpret: five documents is not a population.
Exercise 10.4 (Reading). Read the Sandvig v. Barr complaint, the government’s motion to dismiss, and the March 2020 opinion. In 400 words, summarize the plaintiffs’ legal theory, the government’s theory, and the narrow statutory ground on which the court ruled. Identify which plaintiffs’ arguments the court did not reach, and speculate briefly about why.
Exercise 10.5 (Open-ended). Identify a public-interest question you could answer with a records request but cannot answer from public datasets or scraping alone. Write two pages: the question, the records you would request, the agencies, the anticipated exemption battles, and the theory of change for what you would do with the records. Save it as exercises/ch10_foia_plan.md. The best of these will become Exercise 10.1 filings for next year’s class.






Albiston, Catherine R., and Laura Beth Nielsen. 2017. “Public Interest Law Organizations and the Two-Tier System of Access to Justice in the United States.” Law & Social Inquiry 42 (4): 990–1023.


American Civil Liberties Union. 2019. Sandvig v. Barr: Researchers’ Right to Investigate Algorithmic Discrimination. ACLU. https://www.aclu.org/cases/sandvig-v-barr-challenge-cfaa-prohibition-uncovering-racial-discrimination-online.


Colorado Freedom of Information Coalition. 2024. CORA Resources and Sample-Letter Library. Colorado Freedom of Information Coalition. https://coloradofoic.org/.


Colorado General Assembly. 1968. Colorado Open Records Act, c.r.s. § 24-72-201 Et Seq. Colorado Revised Statutes. https://leg.colorado.gov/colorado-revised-statutes.


Electronic Frontier Foundation. 2024. Computer Fraud and Abuse Act Reform. Electronic Frontier Foundation. https://www.eff.org/issues/cfaa.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.


MuckRock Foundation. 2024. MuckRock: Public Records Requests, Tracked and Published. MuckRock Foundation. https://www.muckrock.com/.


News Leaders Association. 2024. Sunshine Week. News Leaders Association. https://sunshineweek.org/.


Nielsen, Laura Beth, and Catherine R. Albiston. 2006. The Organization of Public Interest Practice, 1975–2004. University of Chicago Law School.


Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press. 2024. Open Government Guide. Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press. https://www.rcfp.org/open-government-guide/.


US Congress. 1966. Freedom of Information Act, 5 u.s.c. § 552. United States Code. https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/5/552.


Warren, Samantha. 2025. Where Public Records Meet the Public Interest: A Reporter’s Field Guide. Nieman Lab.

title: 12  Accounting and Auditing
text:
13 Accounting and Auditing
In October 1929, the New York Stock Exchange lost roughly a quarter of its value in a week. Congress spent the next four years arguing about why, and by 1934 it had produced two statutes (the Securities Act of 1933 and the Securities Exchange Act of 1934) that reshaped the American professional landscape. The new law required publicly traded companies to file financial statements, and those statements to be examined by an independent auditor. Overnight, the external audit went from a discretionary service a firm might commission to reassure its banker, to a legally mandated act of public verification. The profession that conducted those audits acquired, as part of the bargain, something it had not previously had: a public interest mandate. A certified public accountant’s client is the company paying the invoice. The CPA’s duty of care runs to shareholders, lenders, regulators, and everyone downstream of the company’s claims about itself.
That bargain has been renegotiated several times since. Arthur Andersen, one of the “Big Five” audit firms of the 1990s, signed off on Enron’s financial statements for more than a decade before Enron collapsed in 2001, taking roughly $63 billion of market capitalization and twenty thousand jobs with it. Andersen did not survive the fallout. Its consulting practice had been earning more from Enron than its audit practice, and its own workpapers, shredded in a Houston office as federal investigators approached, became the emblematic image of audit failure. Congress responded with the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002, which tightened auditor independence rules, required public companies to certify the accuracy of their filings, and established the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board to audit the auditors. The profession’s public interest mandate was not abolished. It was recertified under closer supervision.
This chapter treats the audit tradition as a central lineage public interest data science should claim. Carnegie and Napier (1996) argue that accounting is not a neutral record-keeping technology but a practice whose conventions and disclosure rules are the sediment of specific historical bargains. Baker (2005), examining the American public accounting profession’s rhetoric, asks a pointed question: when the profession invokes “the public interest,” whose interest is it actually defending? His answer, drawn from close reading of AICPA pronouncements, is that the profession has tended to serve the paying client while invoking the public as a legitimating abstraction. That gap matters here: algorithmic audit is about to repeat every mistake the accounting profession has already made, unless it studies the history and does otherwise.

title: 12  Accounting and Auditing
section: 13.1 From financial audit to algorithmic audit
text:
13.1 From financial audit to algorithmic audit
Raji and colleagues (2020) propose a framework for internal algorithmic auditing that borrows from accounting, aerospace, and medicine: three professions where audit of some form has been institutionalized for decades. Their framework names five stages (scoping, mapping, artifact collection, testing, reflection) and insists on durable, dated workpapers. A good algorithmic audit produces documents that a reasonable outsider could read a year later and reconstruct what was examined, what was found, and what was decided. A bad one produces a one-paragraph attestation, or a dashboard with fairness metrics but no record of which metrics were chosen, by whom, against which thresholds.
The distinction matters because the regulatory environment is hardening around audit. New York City’s Local Law 144, effective July 2023, requires employers using automated employment decision tools to commission an independent “bias audit” and publish a summary on the deploying company’s public website before any covered hiring decision. The law is narrow, the enforcement modest, and the early compliance data unflattering. Goodman and Trehu (2023) examine LL144 as a case of what they call “audit washing”: audits whose institutional form looks like oversight but whose content is shallow enough to credential any system as compliant. The European Union’s AI Act, in force since 2024, introduces a more ambitious conformity assessment regime for high-risk AI systems. It will also, if past is prologue, generate a cottage industry of audits whose depth varies enormously. Your job as a public interest data scientist is to know the difference, and to be able to defend the difference in writing.






NoteIn the Public Interest



Baker (2005) reads the American accounting profession’s own pronouncements and finds a persistent conflation: “the public interest” names, in the profession’s literature, sometimes the paying client, sometimes “the market,” sometimes an abstract audience. Baker argues the defensible definition is narrower and harder: the public interest is served when audit practice protects “all those affected by a corporation’s activities,” not the entity signing the check. The oversight value from Chapter 7 (Chapter 9) asks what independent verification requires. Accounting answers: dated workpapers, separate audit and consulting functions, enforceable standards, and a credential that can be revoked. If your algorithmic audit lacks all four, it performs oversight rather than providing it. The continuity element of the installed base from Chapter 5 (Chapter 7) matters for the same reason: an audit that cannot be reproduced next quarter because the analyst’s laptop has been wiped is, for public-interest purposes, an audit that did not happen.

title: 12  Accounting and Auditing
section: 13.2 The running example: wrapping the Chapter 7 audit
text:
13.2 The running example: wrapping the Chapter 7 audit
Chapter 7 walked you through a disaggregated fairlearn audit on a public dataset, with metrics broken out by protected group. That exercise produced a set of numbers. This chapter produces something different: a pipeline that produces those numbers on demand, with a record of every decision made along the way and a log of what changed between runs. The difference is roughly the difference between a paragraph and a workpaper.
Here is the directory layout you will build:
audit-pipeline/
├── CHANGELOG.md
├── Makefile
├── README.md
├── manifest.yaml
├── audit.py
├── thresholds.yaml
├── data/
│   └── .gitkeep
├── models/
│   └── .gitkeep
└── reports/
    └── .gitkeep
Nine top-level items, most of them placeholders. The whole project is perhaps three hundred lines of code. What makes it an audit is not the code volume. It is the manifest, the changelog, and the discipline of running one before touching the other.

13.2.1 The manifest
manifest.yaml is the input contract. It says, for a given audit run, exactly what is being audited, at what version, with what decisions frozen in advance. A reviewer reading the manifest a year later should be able to reconstruct the run without guessing.
# manifest.yaml
audit:
  name: "COMPAS recidivism disaggregated audit"
  run_id: "2026-04-23-001"
  date: "2026-04-23"
  analyst: "your.name@colorado.edu"
  purpose: "Quarterly oversight audit per org policy OV-07."

dataset:
  source: "ProPublica COMPAS two-year dataset"
  url: "https://github.com/propublica/compas-analysis"
  version: "commit bf4ce2"
  local_path: "data/compas-scores-two-years.csv"
  sha256: "b1946ac92492d2347c6235b4d2611184..."
  retrieved_on: "2026-04-20"

model:
  name: "logistic-baseline"
  version: "v0.3.1"
  path: "models/logistic-baseline-v0.3.1.pkl"
  sha256: "3a7bd3e2360a3d29eea436fcfb7e44c7..."
  hyperparameters:
    C: 1.0
    penalty: "l2"
    random_state: 42

protected_attributes:
  - race
  - sex

metrics:
  - selection_rate
  - false_positive_rate
  - false_negative_rate
  - demographic_parity_difference
  - equalized_odds_difference

thresholds_file: "thresholds.yaml"
Every field is load-bearing. The dataset sha256 is how a later reader verifies the same bytes. The model hash and hyperparameters verify the same model. The run_id is what the changelog references when numbers change. Nothing here is optional.
thresholds.yaml carries the decision rules separately, because they change on a different cadence than the data.
# thresholds.yaml
demographic_parity_difference:
  warn: 0.05
  fail: 0.10
equalized_odds_difference:
  warn: 0.05
  fail: 0.10
false_positive_rate_gap:
  warn: 0.08
  fail: 0.15
notes: |
  Thresholds set by Policy OV-07, adopted 2026-02-15.
  "warn" triggers a note in the report; "fail" triggers an
  escalation to the Oversight Committee per Section 4.2.
Two files. Both dated, both in version control, both citing the policy that authorized them. Financial auditors call this authority documentation. Most algorithmic audits skip it.


13.2.2 The audit script
audit.py reads the manifest, verifies the hashes, runs the disaggregated audit from Chapter 7, compares results against the thresholds, and writes a structured report.
# audit.py
import hashlib
import json
import pathlib
import sys
from datetime import datetime, timezone

import pandas as pd
import yaml
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix
from fairlearn.metrics import (
    MetricFrame,
    selection_rate,
    false_positive_rate,
    false_negative_rate,
    demographic_parity_difference,
    equalized_odds_difference,
)
import joblib


def sha256_of(path):
    h = hashlib.sha256()
    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(8192), b""):
            h.update(chunk)
    return h.hexdigest()


def load_manifest(path="manifest.yaml"):
    with open(path) as f:
        return yaml.safe_load(f)


def verify_hashes(manifest):
    problems = []
    for kind in ("dataset", "model"):
        declared = manifest[kind]["sha256"]
        actual = sha256_of(manifest[kind]["path" if kind == "model" else "local_path"])
        if declared != actual:
            problems.append(f"{kind}: declared {declared[:12]}, got {actual[:12]}")
    return problems


def run_audit(manifest, thresholds):
    df = pd.read_csv(manifest["dataset"]["local_path"])
    model = joblib.load(manifest["model"]["path"])
    X = df.drop(columns=["two_year_recid", "race", "sex"])
    y_true = df["two_year_recid"]
    y_pred = model.predict(X)
    results = {}
    for attr in manifest["protected_attributes"]:
        frame = MetricFrame(
            metrics={
                "selection_rate": selection_rate,
                "fpr": false_positive_rate,
                "fnr": false_negative_rate,
            },
            y_true=y_true,
            y_pred=y_pred,
            sensitive_features=df[attr],
        )
        results[attr] = {
            "by_group": frame.by_group.to_dict(),
            "dpd": float(demographic_parity_difference(
                y_true, y_pred, sensitive_features=df[attr])),
            "eod": float(equalized_odds_difference(
                y_true, y_pred, sensitive_features=df[attr])),
        }
    return results


def evaluate_thresholds(results, thresholds):
    findings = []
    for attr, res in results.items():
        for metric_name, key in [
            ("demographic_parity_difference", "dpd"),
            ("equalized_odds_difference", "eod"),
        ]:
            value = abs(res[key])
            rule = thresholds[metric_name]
            if value >= rule["fail"]:
                findings.append(("FAIL", attr, metric_name, value))
            elif value >= rule["warn"]:
                findings.append(("WARN", attr, metric_name, value))
    return findings


def write_report(manifest, results, findings, out_dir="reports"):
    out_dir = pathlib.Path(out_dir)
    out_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
    run_id = manifest["audit"]["run_id"]
    timestamp = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    report = {
        "run_id": run_id,
        "generated_at": timestamp,
        "manifest": manifest,
        "results": results,
        "findings": findings,
    }
    json_path = out_dir / f"{run_id}.json"
    md_path = out_dir / f"{run_id}.md"
    with open(json_path, "w") as f:
        json.dump(report, f, indent=2, default=str)
    # Human-readable companion
    with open(md_path, "w") as f:
        f.write(f"# Audit report {run_id}\n\n")
        f.write(f"- Generated: {timestamp}\n")
        f.write(f"- Analyst: {manifest['audit']['analyst']}\n")
        f.write(f"- Dataset: {manifest['dataset']['source']} "
                f"(sha256 {manifest['dataset']['sha256'][:12]})\n")
        f.write(f"- Model: {manifest['model']['name']} "
                f"{manifest['model']['version']}\n\n")
        f.write("## Findings\n\n")
        if not findings:
            f.write("No threshold violations.\n")
        for level, attr, metric, value in findings:
            f.write(f"- **{level}** on `{attr}` / {metric}: {value:.4f}\n")


def main():
    manifest = load_manifest()
    with open(manifest["thresholds_file"]) as f:
        thresholds = yaml.safe_load(f)
    problems = verify_hashes(manifest)
    if problems:
        print("Hash verification failed:", problems, file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    results = run_audit(manifest, thresholds)
    findings = evaluate_thresholds(results, thresholds)
    write_report(manifest, results, findings)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Notice what the script does that a notebook would not. It verifies hashes before running anything. It writes both a machine-readable JSON report and a human-readable Markdown companion. It exits non-zero when the inputs do not match the manifest, because a run against unverified inputs is not an audit. It records the UTC timestamp of the run. Every one of these behaviors is stolen from financial audit practice, where a workpaper exists to be legible to someone who was not in the room.


13.2.3 The Makefile
Makefile is the single-command interface. If a reviewer cannot run make audit and get a report, the pipeline is not reproducible.
# Makefile
.PHONY: audit verify clean

audit: verify
    python audit.py

verify:
    @python -c "import yaml; yaml.safe_load(open('manifest.yaml'))"
    @test -f $$(python -c "import yaml; print(yaml.safe_load(open('manifest.yaml'))['dataset']['local_path'])")
    @test -f $$(python -c "import yaml; print(yaml.safe_load(open('manifest.yaml'))['model']['path'])")

clean:
    rm -f reports/*.json reports/*.md
Three targets. verify confirms the manifest parses and its files exist. audit runs the script. clean removes generated reports, not inputs. You do not clean your workpapers. You archive them.


13.2.4 The changelog
CHANGELOG.md is the narrative companion to the numbers. It records, for each run, what changed since the last run and why.
# CHANGELOG

## 2026-04-23-001

- First run under Policy OV-07.
- Dataset: ProPublica COMPAS, commit bf4ce2, retrieved 2026-04-20.
- Model: logistic-baseline v0.3.1, trained 2026-04-15.
- Findings: WARN on race / equalized_odds_difference (0.071).
- No prior run to compare against.

## 2026-04-30-001

- Re-run with no changes to manifest.
- Library update: fairlearn 0.10.0 -> 0.11.0.
- equalized_odds_difference changed from 0.071 to 0.068.
  Root cause: fairlearn 0.11 changed default NaN handling
  in MetricFrame.by_group; see fairlearn CHANGELOG entry
  for issue #1237. No change to audit conclusion.
- Analyst decision: accept new value; document in report.






TipThe Missing Manual



Reproducibility is not pip freeze. A requirements.txt tells you which packages were installed; it does not tell you why a metric changed between runs. A Dockerfile preserves a runtime; it does not preserve a decision. A signed sha256 manifest fixes the inputs; it does not explain why those inputs were chosen. Reproducibility, in the sense an auditor means the word, is decisions, data, and narrative travelling together. Financial auditors date and initial their workpapers for a reason: an undated workpaper is deniable, and deniability is the enemy of accountability. Your audit code, your manifests, and your changelog entries should be dated and attributable. An undated audit script and a results.csv with no run context is not oversight. It is exhibit A in the defense’s motion to dismiss.

title: 12  Accounting and Auditing
text:
13.3 Running the pipeline, twice
Commit the whole directory to git (git init, git add ., git commit -m "ch-11 audit skeleton, run 001"). Run make audit. Inspect reports/2026-04-23-001.md. Read what it says. Read what it does not say. Does the report record which columns were treated as features, and which were dropped? Does it record the split between training and test, or evaluate on the whole dataset? Both choices are defensible. Neither is defensible if it is implicit. The report’s job is to make implicit choices legible.
One week later, run make audit again after updating the run_id in the manifest. With luck, the numbers will be identical, and you will have produced an uninteresting second workpaper confirming the pipeline is deterministic. More often, something will have drifted. A minor version bump of fairlearn or scikit-learn will have changed a default; a transitive dependency will have been yanked from PyPI; your OS will have updated openssl and changed a cryptographic default a download step depended on. The drift is usually small. The discipline is not in preventing it but in noticing it, documenting it in the changelog, and deciding whether it changes the audit conclusion. Nine times out of ten it does not. The tenth time is why you keep a changelog.

title: 12  Accounting and Auditing
section: 13.4 The regulatory landscape: LL144 and the EU AI Act
text:
13.4 The regulatory landscape: LL144 and the EU AI Act
LL144 requires four things of a covered automated employment decision tool: a bias audit conducted by an independent auditor, performed within the last year, measuring selection rates and impact ratios by race/ethnicity and sex, with results published on the deploying company’s public site. Goodman and Trehu (2023) surveyed first-year compliance and found that published audits varied wildly in depth and intellectual defensibility. Some were serious workpapers. Others were one-page PDFs attesting that the tool “did not exhibit bias” without disclosing which metric, which threshold, which population. A legal regime that accepts both as compliant is a regime whose public interest mandate has been captured by the rhetoric of audit without its substance.
The EU AI Act’s conformity assessment regime, applied to high-risk AI systems under Article 43, is structurally more ambitious. It requires either an internal conformity assessment with extensive documentation, or a third-party assessment through a notified body, depending on the system’s risk category and whether a harmonized standard is available. The regime borrows from the EU’s product safety tradition (CE marking, notified bodies, technical files), older than computing itself. The question, again, is whether the form will be filled with substance. The same auditors who wrote one-page LL144 attestations are already selling EU AI Act conformity packages. Buolamwini and Gebru’s Gender Shades study (2018), which opened Chapter 7’s discussion of oversight (Chapter 9), is a useful benchmark: a rigorous public audit of a deployed system, by researchers with no commercial relationship to the vendor, published with full methodology. Most conformity-assessment output will not look like Gender Shades. Some of it should.

title: 12  Accounting and Auditing
text:
13.5 Exercises
Exercise 11.1 (Guided). Build the directory and files above. Use the COMPAS dataset (or another tabular dataset with protected-attribute columns) and any scikit-learn classifier you trained in Chapter 7. Populate manifest.yaml with real hashes, commit the project to git, and run make audit. Submit the repository URL and the generated report. Graded on completeness, not on whether your model passes its thresholds.
Exercise 11.2 (Guided). One week after completing Exercise 11.1, run make audit again with an updated run_id. Compare the new report to the old one. Write a changelog entry describing what changed and why. If nothing changed, write an entry documenting that nothing changed and stating the exact versions of fairlearn and scikit-learn you ran under. Either way, commit the changelog update.
Exercise 11.3 (Analytic). NYC’s Department of Consumer and Worker Protection maintains an enforcement page linking to employer disclosures under LL144. Pick one published bias audit from a real deploying company. Write 400 words analyzing it against the Raji et al. (2020) framework: is the scope named, the artifacts listed, the testing methodology described, the reflection documented? Where it falls short, be specific about which workpaper is missing. Do not pick an audit from a company you hold stock in.
Exercise 11.4 (Comparative). Write a 500-word comparison of LL144 and the EU AI Act’s conformity assessment regime along three dimensions: (1) scope (what systems are covered), (2) who performs the audit (independent third party, internal, notified body, regulator), and (3) what is published (full workpapers, summary report, certification only). Cite the text of both regulations and at least one secondary source for each.
Exercise 11.5 (Open-ended). Pick a data system you interact with weekly: your university’s learning management system, your health insurer’s claims portal, a dating app, your bank’s fraud-detection system. Design an audit for it. Write 800 words covering what outcome variable you would measure, what protected attributes you would disaggregate by, what data you would need and how you would get it (including legal barriers), what thresholds you would set and on what authority, and who would receive the workpapers. You are not required to run the audit. You are required to make the audit thinkable.

title: 12  Accounting and Auditing
text:
13.6 Looking ahead
Chapter 12 turns from accounting to planning, the profession that has spent a century working out what it means to act in a public interest it did not invent. Where accounting answers “did the numbers happen the way the entity claims,” planning answers “whose future is this, and who was in the room when it was decided.” An audit without a plan produces compliance. A plan without an audit produces promises. Data science needs both.

title: 12  Accounting and Auditing
text:
13.7 Further Reading and Resources

C. Richard Baker (2005), “What is the meaning of ‘the public interest’? Examining the ideology of the American public accounting profession,” Accounting, Auditing & Accountability Journal 18(5): 690–703. The essay this chapter leans on most heavily. Read it for the close reading of AICPA language.
Inioluwa Deborah Raji et al. (2020), “Closing the AI accountability gap: Defining an end-to-end framework for internal algorithmic auditing,” Proceedings of FAccT 2020. The foundational paper on audit as a practice rather than a metric.
Garry D. Carnegie and Christopher J. Napier (1996), “Critical and interpretive histories: Insights into accounting’s present and future through its past,” Accounting, Auditing & Accountability Journal 9(3): 7–39. The programmatic call for treating accounting as a historical practice rather than a neutral technique.
NYC Department of Consumer and Worker Protection, Local Law 144 enforcement page: https://www.nyc.gov/site/dca/about/automated-employment-decision-tools.page. Start here to find real published bias audits. The page links to the rule text and to deploying-company disclosures.
European Union (2024), “Artificial Intelligence Act,” Regulation (EU) 2024/1689. Official text: https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj. Conformity assessment is Article 43 and Annex VII. Read both.
Bryce Goodman and Julia Trehu (2023), “AI audit washing and accountability.” The working paper on LL144’s early compliance data and on the structural incentives that produce shallow audits.
PBS Frontline, “Bigger Than Enron” (2002). The canonical documentary on the Andersen-Enron collapse. Watchable in an evening; useful for the texture of how an audit firm loses its mandate.
Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB), inspection reports: https://pcaobus.org/oversight/inspections. Real workpapers of real audit failures, dissected by the regulator. If you want to see what detailed audit criticism looks like, read a PCAOB inspection.
Accounting, Auditing & Accountability Journal (AAAJ), the critical-accounting journal of record. Browse the archive for thirty years of scholarship on the public interest in accounting.
Data & Society, “Algorithmic Impact Assessments” report series. Transitional reading between the accounting tradition and its algorithmic descendants.







Baker, C. Richard. 2005. “What Is the Meaning of ‘the Public Interest’? Examining the Ideology of the American Public Accounting Profession.” Accounting, Auditing & Accountability Journal 18 (5): 690–703.


Buolamwini, Joy, and Timnit Gebru. 2018. “Gender Shades: Intersectional Accuracy Disparities in Commercial Gender Classification.” Proceedings of the 1st Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency, 77–91.


Carnegie, Garry D., and Christopher J. Napier. 1996. “Critical and Interpretive Histories: Insights into Accounting’s Present and Future Through Its Past.” Accounting, Auditing & Accountability Journal 9 (3): 7–39.


Goodman, Bryce, and Julia Trehu. 2023. “AI Audit Washing and Accountability.” SSRN.


Raji, Inioluwa Deborah, Andrew Smart, Rebecca N. White, et al. 2020. “Closing the AI Accountability Gap: Defining an End-to-End Framework for Internal Algorithmic Auditing.” Proceedings of FAccT 2020, 33–44.

title: 13  Planning
text:
14 Planning
Every data project is a planning document in disguise. It proposes that some slice of the world is worth measuring, some category system worth applying, some decision worth making differently once the analysis is done. Planning has spent the last century learning the consequences of that kind of proposal. Data scientists have not. This chapter borrows from the planners.
Part III treats the professions that figured out public-interest practice before data science existed. Chapter 9 handed you the journalist’s habits of verification, Chapter 10 the lawyer’s habits of record access, Chapter 11 the accountant’s habits of audit and disaggregation. Planning, the fourth lineage, contributes the discipline of asking, before any decision is made, who is affected, who was in the room, who was not, and whose knowledge of the terrain counts as evidence.

title: 13  Planning
section: 14.1 A profession that learned from its mistakes
text:
14.1 A profession that learned from its mistakes
Planning as you encounter it today is the downstream result of a public fight it mostly lost and then, slowly, learned from. Robert Moses ran New York’s public works apparatus for four decades on a specific theory: the public interest could be computed by a qualified expert, expressed as a highway alignment, and imposed on neighborhoods whose residents could not contest it. Jane Jacobs (1961), as Chapter 1 noted, took the other side, insisting that people who lived on a block knew things no master plan could capture, and that a plan ignoring that knowledge was not a plan for the public.
The work of codifying what Jacobs had demonstrated fell to a generation of planners who took the problem seriously for planning, not merely against it. Paul Davidoff’s 1965 essay “Advocacy and Pluralism in Planning” (1965) is the founding document. Davidoff argued there is no single neutral public interest a competent planner discovers through analysis. There are many publics, with conflicting interests, and the planner’s job is to make those conflicts legible rather than paper over them with a unitary plan. The profession should supply advocate planners to underserved communities the way the legal profession supplies public defenders.
Advocacy planning did not become the default, but it changed what the default had to argue against. Sherry Arnstein’s “ladder of citizen participation” (1969) followed four years later, giving planners a vocabulary for distinguishing real participation from its counterfeits (manipulation, therapy, information, consultation, placation, partnership, delegated power, citizen control). Marcus Lane’s intellectual history (2005) traces how participation became a procedural requirement in most Western planning regimes, even when its substance was thin. Hashem Dadashpoor and Somayeh Ahani (2021) extend the inheritance in recent work on peri-urban change, insisting the forces reshaping a region are multiple and not reducible to what the master plan says.
You inherit this tradition when you work with geographic data. The choropleth expresses a theory about which boundaries matter; the demographic overlay, a theory about which categories count; the stakeholder list, a theory about whose voice will be sought. Planning’s contribution is to name these theories as theories and insist they be defended.

title: 13  Planning
section: 14.2 Back to the Allegheny Family Screening Tool
text:
14.2 Back to the Allegheny Family Screening Tool
Chapter 3 took up the Allegheny Family Screening Tool as a case of exemption: a risk-scoring system for child-welfare referrals that could not easily be audited from outside, because the administrative records feeding it were sealed. Read the same case through a planning lens and a different failure surfaces. The tool was designed by social scientists, procured by a county, and deployed on families. The families were not in the room. Neither were the front-line caseworkers whose judgment the tool was meant to augment, nor the neighborhoods whose referral rates it would amplify. The county ran a period of public comment. Comments were received. The deployment went forward.
A planner recognizes this as a participation artifact on the lower rungs of Arnstein’s ladder: information transmitted, consultation held, the decision made by the same people who would have made it without consultation. A participation process that does not feed back into who holds authority (Chapter 7) to change the decision, and that produces no path to remedy (Chapter 5) when it goes wrong, is a participation process in name only.

title: 13  Planning
section: 14.3 A running example: the Central 70 project
text:
14.3 A running example: the Central 70 project
You will practice the tools of this chapter on a real Denver decision: the Central 70 project, the reconstruction and widening of Interstate 70 through the Elyria-Swansea and Globeville neighborhoods north of downtown. The Colorado Department of Transportation published a final environmental impact statement and record of decision in 2017 (2017) and began construction shortly after. The project lowered the highway below grade, capped a four-block section with a park, and widened the traveled way. It also displaced residents, triggered lawsuits over environmental review, and intensified concerns about diesel exposure in neighborhoods that had already absorbed decades of pollution from 1960s highway decisions that cut the same neighborhoods in half (2019).
This is a planning case with a documented stakeholder process, contested boundaries, and rich public data. It is also heavy material. Students who find it draining may substitute the Alpine-Balsam redevelopment in Boulder (2019) or another local case; the techniques transfer.

title: 13  Planning
text:
14.4 Stakeholders as a structured document
A stakeholder map lists the parties to a decision, what each wants, can do, and can see. It reflects the mapper’s theory of whose interests count; writing that theory down makes it contestable.
Planners often produce stakeholder maps as tables. For a data science workflow, a structured, version-controlled document serves better. YAML reads like prose, diffs well in git, and loads into Python for downstream analysis. Here is a schema with one filled-in entry:
# stakeholders_i70.yaml
decision:
  name: Central 70 reconstruction and widening
  lead_agency: Colorado Department of Transportation
  record_of_decision: 2017-01
  geography:
    primary_neighborhoods: [Elyria-Swansea, Globeville]
    county: Denver
    state: CO

stakeholders:
  - id: cdot
    name: Colorado Department of Transportation
    role: lead_agency
    interests:
      - completing federally-funded reconstruction on schedule
      - maintaining I-70 freight and commuter capacity
    power:
      formal: high    # NEPA lead; owns the right of way
      informal: high
    access:
      in_the_room: yes
      documents_produced: [DEIS, FEIS, ROD, mitigation plan]
    notes: |
      Procedurally the decision-maker. Subject to FHWA oversight
      and court review under NEPA.

  - id: elyria_swansea_residents
    name: Elyria-Swansea residents
    role: affected_community
    interests:
      - reducing diesel particulate exposure
      - avoiding displacement
      - retaining neighborhood connectivity
    power:
      formal: low     # no veto over alignment
      informal: medium  # organized through neighborhood associations
    access:
      in_the_room: partially
      documents_produced: [public comments, litigation filings]
    notes: |
      Historically split by the 1964 I-70 viaduct. Limited
      Spanish-language outreach in the early EIS phases.
A complete map for Central 70 would list twenty to thirty entries: CDOT, FHWA, the city and county of Denver, Denver Public Schools (whose Swansea Elementary sits within two hundred feet of the widened roadway), the EPA, neighborhood associations on both sides of the highway, the freight industry, commuter constituencies, public health researchers, legal aid organizations, and contractors.
Notice what the schema forces. You cannot fill in power.formal without deciding what “formal” means here, or access.in_the_room without deciding what “the room” is. For Central 70, “the room” is at minimum five rooms: NEPA scoping meetings, technical advisory committees, city council chambers, federal court, and the CDOT executive offices where alignment decisions were actually made. A stakeholder with full access to one may be absent from another.

title: 13  Planning
section: 14.5 Who was missing from the room
text:
14.5 Who was missing from the room
Once the map exists, a companion document lists whose perspectives are absent or under-represented, and says why. This “who was missing” analysis is short, uncomfortable to write, and the point of the exercise.
For Central 70, a partial list would include renters whose landlords received displacement compensation but who received nothing; undocumented residents who did not attend public meetings for reasons unrelated to interest; children whose cumulative diesel exposure was the actual health outcome at stake but who had no standing in NEPA; the ghosts of households displaced by the original 1964 viaduct, whose descendants bore the continuing costs of a decision made before most 2017 stakeholders were born; and residents of neighborhoods not directly touched by I-70 whose regional air quality nonetheless changed.
A “who was missing” analysis is a design input, not a ritual apology. If you can name whose perspective is missing, you can design the analysis to compensate: pulling in demographic data the formal process did not surface, weighting comments from less-represented zip codes, or publishing intermediate findings in formats a missing stakeholder could respond to. That last move is where planning bleeds into journalism (Chapter 9) and the report-writing of Chapter 18.

title: 13  Planning
text:
14.6 The geographic imagination
Planning’s other contribution is the habit of putting decisions on a map. A map is an argument about what counts as “the area,” and every map excludes something the mapmaker did not think was relevant. Part of working with geographic data is learning to notice the exclusions.
The Python library geopandas is the default tool for this work. It extends pandas with a geometry column, so a GeoDataFrame behaves like a DataFrame that knows where each row is on the surface of the earth. Underneath, it is a mixing bowl of shapely (geometry), pyogrio or fiona (file I/O), and pyproj (coordinate reference systems). You will spend more time with the third than you expect.
import geopandas as gpd
import contextily as ctx
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Load a Colorado county shapefile from Census TIGER/Line
counties = gpd.read_file(
    "tl_2023_08_cousub/tl_2023_08_cousub.shp"
)
# => GeoDataFrame with columns including NAME, GEOID, geometry
# => native CRS is typically EPSG:4269 (NAD83, lat/lon)

# Load a block-group demographic overlay (ACS 5-year, 2018-2022)
acs = gpd.read_file("acs_blockgroups_denver.geojson")

# Load a project boundary (traced from the CDOT FEIS project description)
i70 = gpd.read_file("central70_project_area.geojson")
Three layers, three files, three potential failure modes. The most common is a coordinate reference system (CRS) mismatch. A shapefile of Colorado counties in NAD83 (EPSG:4269) and a project boundary in Web Mercator (EPSG:3857) will plot together without raising an exception, because matplotlib does not know what a CRS is. The layers will not line up, because the underlying coordinate numbers mean different things. Before any spatial join, buffer, or distance calculation, project everything to a common CRS:
# Project all three layers to Web Mercator for contextily basemaps
target_crs = "EPSG:3857"
counties = counties.to_crs(target_crs)
acs = acs.to_crs(target_crs)
i70 = i70.to_crs(target_crs)

# For area or distance calculations, use a projected CRS
# appropriate to the region. For the Front Range:
#   EPSG:26913 (NAD83 / UTM zone 13N)
# Distances in meters will be correct; Web Mercator distances will not.
The to_crs() method rewrites the geometry column so every coordinate is expressed in the target system; it does not change the underlying shapes, only how they are addressed. If you skip this step, your choropleth will still render, but it will be wrong in ways you cannot see.
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 10))

# Choropleth: percent of population below poverty line, by block group
acs.plot(
    column="pct_poverty",
    cmap="YlOrRd",
    linewidth=0.1,
    edgecolor="gray",
    legend=True,
    ax=ax,
)

# Project boundary on top
i70.boundary.plot(ax=ax, color="black", linewidth=2)

# Contextily basemap (OpenStreetMap tiles)
ctx.add_basemap(ax, source=ctx.providers.OpenStreetMap.Mapnik)

ax.set_axis_off()
ax.set_title(
    "Central 70 project area and block-group poverty rate\n"
    "ACS 5-year 2018-2022; CDOT project boundary from 2017 FEIS"
)
fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig("ch12_i70_choropleth.png", dpi=150)
A choropleth with warmer colors clustering along the project corridor is not a causal claim; it is a descriptive statement about which communities surround the decision. Whether the project caused the poverty rate, was caused by historical decisions that also produced it, or is unrelated, is a separate analytical question. The map’s job is to make the spatial question visible, not to close it.






TipThe Missing Manual



geopandas is a mixing bowl: shapely handles geometry, fiona or pyogrio reads files, pyproj handles CRSs, and matplotlib draws the result. When something breaks, the traceback can come from any of four libraries you did not explicitly import. The most common failure is a CRS mismatch, because matplotlib will cheerfully plot layers in incompatible coordinate systems without complaining. Always call .to_crs() to project every layer to a common CRS before you join, buffer, measure, or plot. The second most common failure: a shapefile arrives without its .prj sidecar about thirty percent of the time, leaving geopandas with no way to know what CRS the coordinates are in. Check for the .prj before you open the .shp; if it is missing, find out what projection the data was prepared in before you plot.

title: 13  Planning
text:
14.7 Community-informed categorization
The demographic columns you drop into the map are themselves planning artifacts. “Percent below poverty line” is a federal category with a specific history. “Hispanic or Latino” was introduced as a Census category in 1970 after organized advocacy. “Two or more races” did not exist as a Census response option before 2000. Every category is a fossil of an earlier argument about who counted and as what.
Bowker and Star (2000), whose work returns in Chapter 16, call this the “torque” of classification: category systems that once fit the world now shape what the world is allowed to look like. Community-informed categorization treats category choice as participatory. If the community you are mapping has its own neighborhood boundaries that do not match Census tract lines, include both, labeled. If a demographic question the community cares about (length of residence, primary language at home) is not in standard ACS tables, say so in the caption rather than pretending those dimensions do not exist.
This connects back to Chapter 8’s treatment of ownership as stewardship. Treating the power to categorize as held in trust, rather than exercised unilaterally, is a planning ethic and an ownership ethic at once.






NoteIn the Public Interest



Ownership, in Chapter 8’s sense, is about who has the standing and means to act as steward for data that describes them. Planning translates this into practice: the categories used to describe a place, and the boundaries used to define “the area of impact,” should themselves be subject to participation. This is where the installed-base framework’s remedy element (Chapter 5) gets geographic teeth. A community that cannot contest the map on which its displacement was planned has no path to remedy for the displacement itself. Audit-washing (Chapter 7) has a geographic cousin: participation-washing, where the map is drawn, the meeting is held, comments are noticed, and the decision proceeds as if none of it occurred. You can tell the difference by asking whether any document you produced could actually change the decision, or whether it was always going to be filed.

title: 13  Planning
text:
14.8 Exercises
Exercise 12.1 (Guided). Pick a local decision in Boulder, Denver, or your home community: a rezoning, a transit line, a school consolidation, a development approval. Build a stakeholder map in YAML using the schema above. Include at least ten entries. Commit the file to exercises/ch12_stakeholders_<slug>.yaml. Include a two-paragraph README.md that states what the decision is, what records you used to identify stakeholders, and what you could not find.
Exercise 12.2 (Analytic). For the decision you mapped in 12.1, write a 400-word “who was missing from the room” analysis. Name at least four stakeholder groups whose perspectives are absent or thinly represented in the documented process, and for each, name one document, dataset, or outreach method that would have surfaced them. Save as exercises/ch12_missing_<slug>.md.
Exercise 12.3 (Technical). Produce a geopandas choropleth of your decision’s geography. Use TIGER/Line shapefiles for the base geometry and an ACS 5-year table for the demographic overlay. Include at least three layers: the administrative base, the demographic variable, and the decision’s boundary or corridor. Project all layers to a common CRS and document the CRS choice in the caption. Save the figure and the notebook to exercises/ch12_map_<slug>/. If a .prj file is missing from any shapefile, document how you determined the correct CRS.
Exercise 12.4 (Reflective). Read one chapter from Eubanks (2018) (the Allegheny chapter or the Indiana eligibility chapter works well for this purpose). Apply her analytic framework to the case you have been working with in 12.1 through 12.3. Where does the decision’s theory of the problem locate the cause of the harm? Where does Eubanks locate it? What would change in your stakeholder map if you adopted her framing? 500 words.
Exercise 12.5 (Open-ended). Design a participatory process that would have produced a materially different outcome for your decision. “Materially different” means: a different alignment, a different boundary, a different set of mitigations, a different timeline, or a decision not to proceed. Specify what rung of Arnstein’s ladder your process would occupy, who would hold what authority, and what the minimum infrastructure (time, money, translation, childcare, data access, legal support) the process would require. Then answer the harder question: what would have had to be true, structurally, about the lead agency, the funding source, or the political moment for this process to actually happen? 750 words. Save as exercises/ch12_counterfactual_<slug>.md. You will revisit this exercise in Chapter 15 when the reading pivots to procurement as planning by contract.

title: 13  Planning
text:
14.9 Looking ahead
Planning gave you the habit of asking who was in the room and whose knowledge of the terrain counts. Chapter 13 turns to engineering, which asks, after a bridge falls down, what combination of design choice, material weakness, and organizational pressure produced the failure. Engineering’s lineage for public interest data science is the post-incident review, the failure taxonomy, and the discipline of treating each deployed system as a potential failure in slow motion. You will build a test suite, introduce a bug on purpose, and write the post-mortem. Chapter 15 returns to planning when it treats government procurement as planning by contract, and Chapter 18 returns to it when it asks you to write a report for a stakeholder audience that is not the one you would have picked.

title: 13  Planning
text:
14.10 Further Reading and Resources

Paul Davidoff (1965), “Advocacy and Pluralism in Planning,” Journal of the American Institute of Planners 31(4): 331–338. The founding essay for advocacy planning. Short, sharp, and still the clearest statement of the position.
Sherry R. Arnstein (1969), “A Ladder of Citizen Participation,” Journal of the American Institute of Planners 35(4): 216–224. The eight-rung ladder you will use to diagnose participation processes for the rest of your career.
Virginia Eubanks (2018), Automating Inequality. The chapter on the Allegheny Family Screening Tool is directly relevant to Exercise 12.4; the Indiana eligibility and Los Angeles homelessness chapters both work as alternative readings.
The GeoPandas user guide: https://geopandas.org/en/stable/docs.html. The CRS handling section is worth reading end-to-end before you plot anything serious.
QGIS: https://qgis.org/. The free and open-source desktop GIS. When geopandas is not enough, or when you need to hand a reproducible map project to a non-Python collaborator, QGIS is the default.
U.S. Census TIGER/Line shapefiles: https://www.census.gov/geographies/mapping-files/time-series/geo/tiger-line-file.html. Most administrative boundaries you will plot in the United States come from here.
The Central 70 project record of decision and supporting environmental review documents, published by CDOT and FHWA. A substantial real-world planning archive, worth reading as an example of what a regulated public process produces and what it does not.
The City of Boulder’s Alpine-Balsam area plan: https://bouldercolorado.gov/projects/alpine-balsam. A smaller-scale planning process with extensive documentation of its public engagement phases. Useful as a second case for students who find the Central 70 material heavy.
Marcus B. Lane (2005), “Public Participation in Planning: An Intellectual History,” Australian Geographer 36(3): 283–299. The longer historical arc from which Arnstein and Davidoff are two nodes.







Arnstein, Sherry R. 1969. “A Ladder of Citizen Participation.” Journal of the American Institute of Planners 35 (4): 216–24.


Bowker, Geoffrey C., and Susan Leigh Star. 2000. Sorting Things Out: Classification and Its Consequences. MIT Press.


City of Boulder. 2019. Alpine-Balsam Area Plan. City of Boulder Planning Department. https://bouldercolorado.gov/projects/alpine-balsam.


Colorado Department of Transportation. 2017. Central 70 Project: Final Environmental Impact Statement and Record of Decision. Colorado Department of Transportation and Federal Highway Administration. https://www.codot.gov/projects/i70east.


Dadashpoor, Hashem, and Somayeh Ahani. 2021. “Explaining Objective Forces, Driving Forces, and Causal Mechanisms Affecting the Formation and Expansion of the Peri-Urban Areas: A Critical Realism Approach.” Land Use Policy 102: 105232.


Davidoff, Paul. 1965. “Advocacy and Pluralism in Planning.” Journal of the American Institute of Planners 31 (4): 331–38.


Eubanks, Virginia. 2018. Automating Inequality: How High-Tech Tools Profile, Police, and Punish the Poor. St. Martin’s Press.


Jacobs, Jane. 1961. The Death and Life of Great American Cities. Random House.


Lane, Marcus B. 2005. “Public Participation in Planning: An Intellectual History.” Australian Geographer 36 (3): 283–99.


Swansea, Ben. 2019. The Highway That Split Denver: Race, Displacement, and the I-70 Rebuild. Denverite / Colorado Public Radio.

title: 14  Engineering
text:
15 Engineering
Most bridges do not fall down. This is unremarkable until you notice how many there are, how heavy the trucks on them are, and how few civil engineers are on site at any given bridge on any given day. The low background rate of collapse is a professional accomplishment. It rests on standards, inspection schedules, conservative design margins, licensure, and the slow accumulation of what Henry Petroski (1992) calls the knowledge of failure. Engineering became a profession in the nineteenth century partly by agreeing to pay close attention to its own disasters. The Tay Bridge. The Tacoma Narrows. The DC-10 cargo door. The hospitality of an engineer’s bookshelf to a failure taxonomy is one of the most important cultural facts about the field.
Data science does not yet have that bookshelf. It has conference papers on bias, a growing body of audit work (Chapter 9), and a scattered practice of post-incident reviews at the sites that run production systems at scale. What it lacks is a professional identity organized around the question “what failed, why, and what will we change so it does not fail again.” This chapter, the last in Part III, proposes that engineering is the discipline most worth borrowing from on that question. From engineering, you take tests, incident reviews, and the cultural insistence that reliability is earned and paid for, not assumed.

title: 14  Engineering
section: 15.1 The engineer’s duty to the public
text:
15.1 The engineer’s duty to the public
Engineering’s claim to be a profession rests on codes of ethics that give the engineer a duty of loyalty to the public that overrides the duty to the client. The canonical formulation, in the National Society of Professional Engineers code, puts “hold paramount the safety, health, and welfare of the public” at the top of the fundamental canons. An engineer who signs off on a bridge they know to be unsafe is not acting unprofessionally because they disappointed a customer. They are acting unprofessionally because they violated a duty to the people who will drive over it. That duty survives the contract.
This matters for public interest data science because the closest analog, the duty of a financial auditor to the public investor (Chapter 13), is the reason accountancy works at all. Baker (2005) argues that the American accounting profession has spent decades rhetorically invoking “the public interest” while operating as a service industry for paying clients; engineering has managed the same tension slightly better, mostly because bridges are more visible than audit opinions. When you write a data pipeline that supports a public decision, you inherit a weaker version of the engineer’s obligation. You cannot licensed-stamp a dataset. You can agree that the client’s deadline does not overrule the public’s exposure to a quietly broken number.

title: 14  Engineering
text:
15.2 Normalization of deviance
The second thing you borrow is a vocabulary for how safe systems go bad. Diane Vaughan’s (1996) study of the Challenger launch decision introduced the phrase normalization of deviance to describe how NASA engineers, over a series of flights, came to treat O-ring erosion as acceptable despite it never being in the original design specification. Each individual decision to fly was defensible on the data NASA had. The drift was collective and gradual. By the night before the launch, the engineers who objected were the ones required to prove it was unsafe, rather than the other way around.
The data-pipeline analog is familiar. A nightly job fails at 2 a.m. once a week. Someone reruns it by hand. Eventually the rerun is automated as a retry loop. Eventually the retry loop masks a schema change upstream for six months. Eventually someone notices that the numbers in a public dashboard are off. Nobody did anything wrong on any individual Tuesday. The deviance normalized. Incident reviews, done well, reverse that drift: they put the question “when did we stop noticing this” on the table and keep asking it.
Karl Weick and Kathleen Sutcliffe’s work on high-reliability organizations (Weick et al. 1999) generalizes the point. Organizations that run complex systems with low failure rates, like aircraft carriers and air traffic control, share cultural traits: preoccupation with failure, reluctance to simplify, sensitivity to operations, commitment to resilience, deference to expertise. No heroic engineer. No superhuman vigilance. A steady institutional refusal to relax. Charles Perrow (1984) pushed back that some systems are so tightly coupled and interactively complex that accidents in them are “normal” rather than preventable. The debate is unresolved and productive. The takeaway for the data scientist is that reliability is organizational before it is technical. Tests do not run themselves.

title: 14  Engineering
text:
15.3 Software engineering as inheritor
Software engineering has sometimes claimed this tradition and sometimes been accused of squatting on the word. The Therac-25 radiation machine, the Ariane 5 rocket, the 737 MAX, the UK Post Office’s Horizon accounting system: each is a software case the older engineering professions would recognize immediately as a safety-and-ethics failure. Data pipelines are a newer cousin, usually less safety-critical individually, often more consequential in aggregate. Raji et al. (2020) make the case that internal algorithmic auditing, done with engineering-style discipline, closes the accountability gap between aspirational ethics and operational practice. Their framework reads, deliberately, like an engineering standards document.
What software and data work usually lack is the labor that sustains reliability in older engineering fields. There is no equivalent of the railway inspector walking the track. The people who maintain production data systems tend to be invisible in the way that Gray and Suri (2019) document ghost workers are invisible: present when something breaks, forgotten when it does not. Chapter 16 will return to this under the heading of sustainability. For now, notice that adding tests to a pipeline is a small, concrete way of making that invisible labor partly visible and transferable.

title: 14  Engineering
section: 15.4 From the Ch 11 audit to a tested pipeline
text:
15.4 From the Ch 11 audit to a tested pipeline
The running example is the reproducible audit pipeline from Chapter 13. That chapter gave you a manifest, a script, a Makefile, and a changelog. Now you add what converts workpapers from a genre into a discipline: a test suite. Add a tests/ directory at the top of the project:
audit-pipeline/
├── manifest.yaml
├── audit.py
├── Makefile
├── tests/
│   ├── fixtures/
│   │   └── tiny_compas.csv
│   ├── test_shape.py
│   ├── test_ranges.py
│   └── test_regression.py
└── .github/
    └── workflows/
        └── ci.yml
The tiny_compas.csv is a fifty-row synthetic file, committed. It looks like the real input but is not. Its job is to let every test run in under a second, on any laptop, with no network access.

15.4.1 Shape tests
The first tests you write catch the failure modes a data pipeline actually has. Not “does this function return 42.” Closer to “does the table have the columns I expect, in roughly the row count I expect, with dtypes that will not silently coerce to nonsense.”
# tests/test_shape.py
import pandas as pd
import pytest

from audit import load_input

EXPECTED_COLUMNS = {
    "person_id": "object",
    "age": "int64",
    "race": "object",
    "sex": "object",
    "score": "float64",
    "assessment_date": "datetime64[ns]",
}

@pytest.fixture
def df():
    return load_input("tests/fixtures/tiny_compas.csv")

def test_columns_present(df):
    missing = set(EXPECTED_COLUMNS) - set(df.columns)
    assert not missing, f"missing columns: {missing}"

def test_column_dtypes(df):
    for col, expected in EXPECTED_COLUMNS.items():
        actual = str(df[col].dtype)
        assert actual == expected, f"{col}: expected {expected}, got {actual}"

def test_row_count_in_range(df):
    # Fixture is a small slice; production run will be larger.
    # For the fixture, we expect 40-60 rows.
    assert 40 <= len(df) <= 60
The row-count assertion is deliberately a range, not an equality. In production, an equality test against a moving dataset is a future flaky test waiting to be disabled.


15.4.2 Range tests
The second kind of test asserts that values fall in the ranges the domain actually permits. Percentages live in [0, 1] or [0, 100], never both. Ages are non-negative and below some sane upper bound. Dates do not claim to be in 1899 or 2099 unless you are doing something unusual.
# tests/test_ranges.py
import pandas as pd
import pytest
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

from audit import load_input

@pytest.fixture
def df():
    return load_input("tests/fixtures/tiny_compas.csv")

def test_score_is_probability(df):
    assert df["score"].between(0, 1).all()

def test_age_is_plausible(df):
    assert df["age"].between(10, 110).all()

def test_dates_within_window(df):
    earliest = pd.Timestamp("2010-01-01")
    latest = pd.Timestamp.now() + pd.Timedelta(days=1)
    assert df["assessment_date"].between(earliest, latest).all()

@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "column,low,high",
    [
        ("score", 0.0, 1.0),
        ("age", 10, 110),
    ],
)
def test_column_bounds(df, column, low, high):
    assert df[column].between(low, high).all(), f"{column} out of [{low}, {high}]"






TipThe Missing Manual



Tests for data pipelines are different from tests for web applications, and most testing tutorials teach the web-app kind. A web-app test asserts exact equality: the login endpoint returns status 200 and the body contains “Welcome.” A data-pipeline test that asserts “there are exactly 18,472 rows” is the same test and almost always wrong, because the data moves. Your test for a historical dataset is closer to “the most recent row’s timestamp is within 48 hours of the run time” or “the monthly row count is within two standard deviations of the trailing twelve-month mean.” Write assertions in the shape of distributions and ranges, not exact values. And reach for pytest.mark.parametrize early: without it you will write fifteen near-identical “each column is in range” tests, and the fifteenth is where the bug lives. Parametrization is the feature that makes shape-and-range testing tractable. Nobody maintains the version written without it.




15.4.3 Regression tests on a fixture
The third test keeps the pipeline’s outputs stable against itself. Run the whole audit on the committed fixture, compute the disaggregated metrics, and compare to a snapshot in the repository. If the snapshot changes, someone has to explain why.
# tests/test_regression.py
import json
from pathlib import Path
import pytest

from audit import run_audit

FIXTURE = "tests/fixtures/tiny_compas.csv"
SNAPSHOT = Path("tests/fixtures/expected_metrics.json")

def test_audit_matches_snapshot(tmp_path):
    results = run_audit_on_file(FIXTURE)
    expected = json.loads(SNAPSHOT.read_text())
    # Compare by group and metric, allow tiny float drift.
    for group, metrics in expected.items():
        for metric, value in metrics.items():
            got = results[group][metric]
            assert abs(got - value) < 1e-6, (
                f"{group}.{metric}: expected {value}, got {got}"
            )

def run_audit_on_file(path):
    # Thin wrapper around the manifest-driven pipeline; omitted for brevity.
    ...
The snapshot file is human-readable JSON, small enough to diff in a pull request. When the numbers change, the diff itself becomes part of the documentation. A reviewer can see at a glance whether the change is a bug fix or a regression.

title: 14  Engineering
text:
15.5 Deliberately introducing a bug
You learn more from breaking the pipeline on purpose than from any amount of testing theory. Imagine the audit dataset includes a column fine_amount_cents and the pipeline converts it to dollars for reporting. Edit audit.py:
def to_dollars(cents):
    # Silent unit conversion error: dropped a zero.
    return cents / 10   # should be / 100
Commit the change on a branch with a message that honestly labels it:
git checkout -b demo/unit-conversion-bug
git commit -am "demo: introduce unit conversion bug (drop a zero)"
Run pytest. If the suite is doing its job, test_audit_matches_snapshot fails because the dollar amounts are ten times what the snapshot says. The failure message points at the specific metric and group. Now you have something concrete to investigate.
Fix the bug (cents / 100), rerun the tests, commit. The two-commit sequence (bug introduced, bug fixed) is the minimum evidence that the test was doing real work.

title: 14  Engineering
text:
15.6 The post-incident review
The fix is not the end of the response. The review is. Engineering’s specific contribution to the documentation genres of public-interest work is the post-incident review, sometimes called a post-mortem or, in aviation, an accident report. Its structure is stable across industries. A useful template has five sections.
Summary. One paragraph. What broke, when it was detected, who was affected, what was done.
Timeline. A minute-by-minute or day-by-day record, in UTC: detection, response start, each diagnostic step, mitigation, all-clear. Timestamps are non-negotiable.
Impact. Quantified where possible. How many users, records, or dollars, over what window. For a public-facing pipeline: how many published numbers were wrong, for how long, and on what downstream decisions they might have been relied on.
Contributing factors. Plural. What combination of design choice, process gap, missing test, insufficient alert, organizational pressure, and plain human error produced the failure. Blameless does not mean vague.
What we are changing. Specific, assigned, dated. New tests. Process changes. Monitoring additions. A change that is not assigned to someone is not a change.
For the unit-conversion bug, a three-page review is sufficient. The timeline shows the bug was introduced at 09:04, caught by pytest in a pre-merge CI run at 09:07, and never reached production. Impact is near zero. Contributing factors include the unit being encoded in the column name but not the type system, the absence of a sanity-check on dollar totals, and the pipeline’s reliance on a reviewer noticing a number looked wrong. Changes include a range test on dollar totals, a refactor to a typed currency helper, and a note in CONTRIBUTING.md about unit discipline.
Publishing, even for a demo bug, is the habit. Cloudflare, GitLab, Google, NASA, and the NTSB all publish. Writing for an external reader forces a different standard of clarity than writing for yourself.

title: 14  Engineering
text:
15.7 Continuous integration
Tests you do not run are decorations. The minimal configuration that makes pytest run on every push is a single YAML file.
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: ci

on:
  push:
  pull_request:

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          pip install -r requirements.txt
          pip install pytest
      - name: Run tests
        run: pytest -v
Push a branch. Open a pull request. GitHub Actions runs the workflow, marks the check green or red next to the commit, and blocks the merge if branch protection is on. The cost is five minutes to set up. The return is that the entire test suite runs on a clean machine, with declared dependencies, every time anyone proposes a change. A test that runs only on the author’s laptop does not, for the purposes of the installed base (Chapter 7), exist.






NoteIn the Public Interest



Oversight, in the sense Chapter 9 developed, is not a heroic intervention. It is routine scrutiny, organized so that the routine itself makes surprises visible. Tests and CI are that routine at the pipeline layer. They are how you earn the right to make a public claim about a number. The workpapers of Chapter 13 and Raji et al.’s (2020) audit framework both presuppose that the numbers were produced by a process someone can vouch for. A tested pipeline with a CI check, a committed fixture, and a published post-incident review is what “vouching for it” looks like in code. The engineer’s duty to public welfare, transposed into data work, is not larger or more dramatic than this; it is exactly this, done routinely, for every claim that leaves the repository with your name on it. Continuity, in the installed-base sense of Chapter 7, is the practice of making sure the next person who inherits the pipeline can tell, from the tests and reviews alone, what it is for and when it is broken.

title: 14  Engineering
text:
15.8 Exercises
Exercise 13.1 (Guided). Clone the audit pipeline from your work for Chapter 13. Add the three test files shown above: a shape test, a range test (with at least one pytest.mark.parametrize block), and a regression test against a fifty-row synthetic fixture. The suite must run in under one second. Commit to exercises/ch13_tests/ with a README.md listing what each test asserts and why.
Exercise 13.2 (Guided). On a branch named demo/bug, introduce a deliberate silent error: a dropped-zero unit conversion, a wrong column in a groupby, a boolean reversed. Commit the introduction with a message that labels it a demo bug. Run the tests and confirm they fail. Commit the fix on a second commit. Push. Do not merge. The two-commit history is the artifact.
Exercise 13.3 (Analytic). Write a post-incident review for the bug from 13.2, using the five-section template. Three to four pages. Name the pipeline, the commit SHAs, the timestamps, the impact (even if zero), the contributing factors, and the changes. Save as exercises/ch13_incident_<date>.md. Write it as though a reporter might read it, because in the public-interest version of this work they might.
Exercise 13.4 (Technical). Add the .github/workflows/ci.yml shown above. Push a branch. Confirm the Actions tab shows the workflow running and that any test failure turns the check red. Screenshot the green check on a passing run and the red check on the bug-introducing commit from 13.2. Save both to exercises/ch13_ci/.
Exercise 13.5 (Open-ended). Find a real-world data-pipeline incident review in the public record. Good sources: Cloudflare’s incident archive, GitLab’s post-mortems, NASA mission failure reports (Mars Climate Orbiter, Genesis), NYC 311 data-system outage reports, CERT vulnerability notes, AWS post-event summaries. In 300 words, summarize the five template sections as they appear in the source (the gaps are informative), identify one contributing factor that is organizational rather than technical, and say what this book’s framework would have added. Save as exercises/ch13_review_summary.md.

title: 14  Engineering
text:
15.9 Looking ahead
Part III set out to show that data science is not the first field to struggle with accountability to a public it cannot see directly. Journalism gave you the habit of publishing and being wrong in public. Law gave you the mechanics of compelling a record. Accounting gave you workpapers and the auditor’s duty to the investor. Planning gave you the stakeholder map and the uncomfortable question of who was not in the room. Engineering closes the part by giving you the failure mode, the test, and the post-incident review: the cultural technology for treating every deployed system as something that can and will break, and for making the breakage legible when it does. The preservation end of that cultural technology, how a failure record and the data it references survive long enough to matter, is taken up again in Chapter 24.
Part IV, Adjacent Conversations, turns from lineages you inherit to fields you sit beside. Chapter 16 opens with the one most likely to be confused with this book’s project: public interest technology and the “data for good” movement. Both claim the public-interest label. Both do work this book respects. Both also carry assumptions about philanthropy, the state, and whose labor maintains the systems once the grant ends that are worth naming rather than absorbing. You will read those fields with the lineages of Part III still in hand, because sustainability is a reliability problem too. The pipeline that is not maintained is a pipeline that will eventually produce the wrong number, quietly, in a decision that matters.

title: 14  Engineering
text:
15.10 Further Reading and Resources

Henry Petroski (1992), To Engineer Is Human: The Role of Failure in Successful Design. The accessible entry point to engineering’s culture of learning from disaster. The Tacoma Narrows chapter pairs well with this one.
Diane Vaughan (1996), The Challenger Launch Decision. The canonical sociology-of-technology study of a high-consequence failure. Worth it for the “normalization of deviance” framing alone.
Charles Perrow (1984), Normal Accidents: Living with High-Risk Technologies. The counterweight to high-reliability optimism. Read alongside Weick and Sutcliffe.
Betsy Beyer et al., eds., Site Reliability Engineering: How Google Runs Production Systems (2016), free at https://sre.google/books/. The chapters on post-mortem culture and error budgets are directly relevant.
Increment magazine’s “On-Call” and “Post-mortem” issues, at https://increment.com/. Short practitioner essays on running and reviewing systems at scale.
NTSB accident investigation reports, at https://www.ntsb.gov/investigations/. The gold standard for the post-incident review as a public document. Pick any completed report and read it end to end.
Cloudflare’s public postmortem archive, at https://blog.cloudflare.com/tag/postmortem/. A corporate example of the genre done publicly.
GitLab’s public incident postmortems, at https://about.gitlab.com/handbook/engineering/infrastructure/incident-management/. A SaaS shop that lost user data in 2017 and wrote about it in detail.
The pytest documentation, at https://docs.pytest.org/. The parametrize, fixture, and tmp_path sections are the ones you will reach for.
NSPE Code of Ethics for Engineers, at https://www.nspe.org/career-growth/ethics/code-ethics. Two pages. Read alongside Baker (2005) on accounting’s parallel code.







Baker, C. Richard. 2005. “What Is the Meaning of ‘the Public Interest’? Examining the Ideology of the American Public Accounting Profession.” Accounting, Auditing & Accountability Journal 18 (5): 690–703.


Gray, Mary L., and Siddharth Suri. 2019. Ghost Work: How to Stop Silicon Valley from Building a New Global Underclass. Houghton Mifflin Harcourt.


Perrow, Charles. 1984. Normal Accidents: Living with High-Risk Technologies. Basic Books.


Petroski, Henry. 1992. To Engineer Is Human: The Role of Failure in Successful Design. Vintage Books.


Raji, Inioluwa Deborah, Andrew Smart, Rebecca N. White, et al. 2020. “Closing the AI Accountability Gap: Defining an End-to-End Framework for Internal Algorithmic Auditing.” Proceedings of FAccT 2020, 33–44.


Vaughan, Diane. 1996. The Challenger Launch Decision: Risky Technology, Culture, and Deviance at NASA. University of Chicago Press.


Weick, Karl E., Kathleen M. Sutcliffe, and David Obstfeld. 1999. “Organizing for High Reliability: Processes of Collective Mindfulness.” Research in Organizational Behavior 21: 81–123.

title: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good
text:
16 Public Interest Technology and Data for Good
