Part IV begins with a problem of neighbors. Public interest data science, as this book develops it, has two immediate cousins whose self-presentation is strong enough that students often arrive assuming the three terms are synonyms. They are not. Public interest technology (PIT) and data-for-good each have their own institutional history, funders, canonical texts, and characteristic blind spots. You should know what those are, because the job listings you will apply for and the foundations that will eventually pay your rent draw their vocabulary from these fields. And because, as Aula and Erkkilä (2023) argue, the philanthropic political economy that sustains PIT and data-for-good is not the same political economy that sustains journalism, law, or the archival sciences you met in Part III. Its rhythms are different. Its pathologies are different.
This chapter is sympathetic and skeptical in equal measure. PIT has built real things. GetCalFresh helped millions of Californians apply for food assistance through a form that did not hate them. Ushahidi mapped post-election violence in Kenya in 2008 and has since been deployed in dozens of countries during crises where official channels failed. The question this chapter asks is not whether these tools worked. It is whether they were built to last, who pays when the launch press cycle ends, and what labor is rendered invisible when a tool is described as “the platform.” The concept that organizes the chapter is sustainability, understood not as a vague aspiration but as a specific configuration of code, people, money, and data that has to be maintained or it will rot. You will conduct a sustainability audit of two real projects, one from the US PIT world and one from the global South data-for-good world, and score them against a rubric. By the end you should be able to look at a project’s launch announcement and tell, within a rough range of error, how likely it is to still be there in five years.
crumbs: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good

title: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good
section: 16.1 The PIT University Network and its framing text
text:
16.1 The PIT University Network and its framing text
Public interest technology as an organized field is young. McGuinness and Schank (2021) trace its emergence to the Obama-era US Digital Service and the parallel founding of Code for America, arguing that the lesson of healthcare.gov’s 2013 failure and its subsequent rescue was that government needed people who could write code, understand policy, and translate between the two. Power to the Public is the book that codified this framing. It is a confident book, written by practitioners, and it does real work: it names a kind of professional who does not fit the conventional civil-service or tech-industry career ladder, and it argues that such people should be trained, hired, and paid.
The Public Interest Technology University Network, coordinated by New America, took up that argument and built around it. By 2024 the network included dozens of member universities, each committing to some combination of new courses, fellowship programs, and hiring pathways (New America 2024). The network’s framing is career-oriented: its promotional material emphasizes the pipeline from student to fellowship to government-or-nonprofit job. That emphasis is one of the field’s genuine strengths. A generation of students now has a defensible answer to the question “what do you do with a computer science degree if you do not want to work for a surveillance advertising company.”
What the PIT-UN framing tends to elide is what happens after the student becomes the fellow, the fellow becomes the hire, and the hire ships the thing. The career pipeline metaphor implies a forward-flowing river; actual projects are more like gardens. Karasti and Blomberg (2018) call this maintenance work infrastructuring and argue that it is the least-narrated, least-funded phase of any sociotechnical system. It is the move Keegan (2026) makes in framing the installed base as something maintained rather than built. You met the concept in Chapter 7; you will meet it in Chapter 15 under the heading of operations. Here it becomes a diagnostic lens for any PIT project.
crumbs: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good

title: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good
text:
16.2 The rhythm of foundation funding
The pathology is structural. Most PIT and data-for-good projects are funded by philanthropic foundations on three-year grant cycles. The first year is design and pilot. The second year is refinement. The third year is “scaling.” The theory implicit in that rhythm is that a pilot, if it works, will either attract a sustaining revenue stream (government contract, earned revenue, follow-on philanthropy) or be absorbed by an institution that already has one. This theory is sometimes correct. It is much more often wrong. Aula and Erkkilä (2023) describe the resulting dynamic as a churn in which foundations perpetually fund new pilots because the old pilots did not scale, with the result that a disproportionate share of field capacity is tied up in pilot projects that will be dead in five years. Chambers (2025) sharpens this critique: the “data-for-good” imaginary treats data-intensive civic projects as discrete interventions that can be completed, rather than as ongoing commitments that require ongoing labor.
You can see the pattern in the shape of project websites. A project in year one has blog posts every two weeks and a Medium series and a podcast. A project in year three has a landing page with three case studies dated from year two and a Slack invitation link that no longer works. A project in year five either has a funding home (a government line item, a membership model, fee-for-service revenue) or a GitHub repo whose last meaningful commit was the grant-close deliverable. The audit below is designed to surface which kind of project you are looking at before you commit time to building on top of it.
crumbs: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good

title: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good
section: 16.3 Two running examples: GetCalFresh and Ushahidi
text:
16.3 Two running examples: GetCalFresh and Ushahidi
Consider two projects that have lasted a long time.
GetCalFresh was launched by Code for America in 2014 to simplify the CalFresh (California’s SNAP) application process (Code for America 2024). The original application was an 18-page PDF. The GetCalFresh version took most applicants under ten minutes, and millions of Californians used it over the next decade. It became the template for a broader argument: that benefits-access technology can measurably move the needle on enrollment, and that civic tech should focus on this kind of service delivery rather than flashier interventions. In 2023 the service transitioned from a Code-for-America-operated platform to one operated by the state of California. That transition is, in sustainability terms, the best possible outcome. A civic-tech pilot became a line item in a state agency’s budget. The thing you should want for any project you build is that you eventually work yourself out of a job because the state has taken over the function.
Ushahidi was launched in Nairobi in 2008 to crowdsource reports of post-election violence in Kenya, after major media outlets stopped publishing details (Ushahidi 2024). The platform was built, initially, over a weekend. It has since been deployed in over 150 countries, during earthquakes, refugee crises, elections, and epidemics. Ushahidi is an unusual case because it emerged from a global-South context and has remained headquartered there, funded through a mix of foundation grants, deployment fees, and in-kind partnerships. Its maintenance problem is also unusually visible: the codebase is open source, deployments are often led by local partners whose technical capacity varies, and the organization has been candid over the years about the difficulty of keeping a 2008-vintage platform current against 2024-era security and mobile-device realities.
Pair these two. One US project that transitioned to a government funding home. One global-South project surviving 16 years on a hybrid funding model. Neither is a failure. Both illustrate that sustainability is possible, looks different in different institutional contexts, and is the exception rather than the rule.
crumbs: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good

title: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good
text:
16.4 A sustainability audit: querying GitHub
The technical component of this chapter is a reproducible audit you can run on any project with a public code repository. The audit asks six questions:

Where does the code live? Repo URL, license, last commit date, contributor count.
Who maintains it? Named maintainers, their employer, and how they are funded.
Who owns the data?
What happens when the current grant ends?
What labor is invisible in the product: translation, annotation, moderation, cleaning?
How does the project score against a simple rubric?

The first question is the one you can mostly answer with the GitHub REST API. The later ones require reading. The code below fetches repo metadata, the license, contributor count, open issues, and open pull requests, and returns a one-page DataFrame that you can render into a report.
import os
import requests
import pandas as pd
from datetime import datetime, timezone

GITHUB_TOKEN = os.environ.get("GITHUB_TOKEN")  # strongly recommended
HEADERS = {
    "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json",
    "User-Agent": "PublicInterestDataScience/0.1 (your-email@example.edu)",
    "X-GitHub-Api-Version": "2022-11-28",
}
if GITHUB_TOKEN:
    HEADERS["Authorization"] = f"Bearer {GITHUB_TOKEN}"


def gh(path, params=None):
    url = f"https://api.github.com{path}"
    response = requests.get(url, headers=HEADERS, params=params, timeout=30)
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response


def audit_repo(owner, repo):
    base = f"/repos/{owner}/{repo}"
    meta = gh(base).json()

    # Contributors: paginate to count, not to list.
    contributors = gh(f"{base}/contributors",
                      params={"per_page": 100, "anon": "true"}).json()
    contributor_count = len(contributors)

    # Open issues and PRs. GitHub counts PRs as issues; split them.
    issues = gh("/search/issues",
                params={"q": f"repo:{owner}/{repo} is:open is:issue"}).json()
    prs = gh("/search/issues",
             params={"q": f"repo:{owner}/{repo} is:open is:pr"}).json()

    # Last commit date on the default branch.
    commits = gh(f"{base}/commits", params={"per_page": 1}).json()
    last_commit = commits[0]["commit"]["committer"]["date"] if commits else None
    if last_commit:
        last_commit_dt = datetime.fromisoformat(last_commit.replace("Z", "+00:00"))
        days_since = (datetime.now(timezone.utc) - last_commit_dt).days
    else:
        days_since = None

    license_name = (meta.get("license") or {}).get("spdx_id") or "NONE"

    row = {
        "repo": f"{owner}/{repo}",
        "stars": meta.get("stargazers_count"),
        "forks": meta.get("forks_count"),
        "license": license_name,
        "last_commit": last_commit,
        "days_since_last_commit": days_since,
        "contributors": contributor_count,
        "open_issues": issues.get("total_count"),
        "open_prs": prs.get("total_count"),
        "default_branch": meta.get("default_branch"),
        "archived": meta.get("archived"),
    }
    return row


projects = [
    ("codeforamerica", "getcalfresh-site"),   # illustrative; confirm exact repo
    ("ushahidi", "platform"),
]

audit = pd.DataFrame([audit_repo(o, r) for o, r in projects])
print(audit.T)
# => one column per project, rows: stars, forks, license, last_commit,
#    days_since_last_commit, contributors, open_issues, open_prs, archived
Two observations. The search/issues endpoint is rate-limited more strictly than most others (30 requests per minute for authenticated users); for a classroom audit across five projects this is fine, for a broader study you will want to page through the issues endpoint directly. And anon: "true" on the contributors endpoint tells GitHub to include commits from email addresses that do not resolve to current GitHub accounts, which matters because in many PIT projects a meaningful share of historical contribution is from now-departed staff whose accounts have lapsed.






TipThe Missing Manual



Two traps. First, a healthy-looking GitHub repo with recent commits is not evidence of sustainability. It might be one person making cosmetic updates before a grant report. Always check the breadth of commits: run git shortlog -sne --since="1 year ago" against a local clone and count how many distinct humans made substantive changes. Fifty commits in the last year from one committer is a solo project that has not yet been told. Twelve commits from seven committers is, in this field, genuinely healthy. Second, the GitHub API rate-limits unauthenticated requests to 60 per hour. If you run the audit without GITHUB_TOKEN set, you will get throttled before the third project and the errors will not be obvious. Create a personal access token with public_repo scope, put it in an environment variable, and confirm your headroom with a quick call to /rate_limit before you start.
crumbs: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good

title: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good
text:
16.5 Interpreting the audit
The numbers are a first cut. They do not tell you who pays the maintainers. For that you have to read the project’s About page, their annual reports, and their IRS Form 990 if they are a US nonprofit. You might find that the repo with fifteen active contributors is supported by a $4M/year philanthropic grant whose cycle ends in eighteen months, while the repo with three contributors sits on a state agency’s operations budget and will quietly keep running for a decade. The first looks healthier on GitHub. The second is more sustainable.
A simple rubric, scored 0 to 3 on each dimension for a 0 to 15 total, does useful disciplinary work:

Code health. License present, recent commits, open issues being triaged, at least one release tag in the past year.
Maintenance breadth. More than one active human contributor; more than one employer represented among contributors.
Labor visibility. The project publicly names the people who do the unglamorous work: translators, annotators, moderators, support staff.
Data ownership. The project has a written data policy, a stated position on whose data it holds, and a plausible answer to “what happens to the data if the project shuts down.”
Funding model. The project has a named current funder, a stated runway, and a plausible non-grant funding path (government contract, fee-for-service, membership, endowment).

A score of 12 or higher suggests the project will probably still exist in five years. A score below 6 suggests you should not build a dependency on it. Scores in between are where most real projects live, and the rubric’s value is less the number and more the specific gaps it surfaces.
crumbs: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good

title: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good
section: 16.6 Ghost work and the invisible labor
text:
16.6 Ghost work and the invisible labor
No audit of a data-for-good project is complete without asking about labor the product does not advertise. Gray and Suri (2019) introduced the term ghost work for the tasks humans perform to make “artificial intelligence” appear to work: content moderation, image labeling, spam filtering, transcript cleanup, edge-case triage. Ghost work is not an implementation detail. It is the labor that sustains the system. It is also, in the PIT and data-for-good worlds, disproportionately performed by people who are paid little, credited never, and employed through layers of subcontracting that make their labor legally separate from the mission-driven organization whose logo is on the product.
Benefits-access projects depend on translation. Someone translated the GetCalFresh intake flow into Spanish, Mandarin, and Vietnamese. That someone’s name is not on the About page. Crisis-mapping projects depend on verification: every Ushahidi deployment involves volunteers or paid staff who triage incoming SMS reports, assess plausibility, and decide what to publish. Those verifiers are the platform, in a way that is not true of the software. When Chambers (2025) critiques the “data-for-good imaginary,” part of the critique is that it treats the technology as the thing and the labor as a regrettable overhead. Your audit should flip that framing. When the rubric asks about “labor visibility,” it is asking whether the people whose work keeps the system alive appear anywhere a reader can find them.






NoteIn the Public Interest



In Chapter 10 you learned to distinguish data ownership as control from data ownership as stewardship. Stewardship is a verb. It implies ongoing labor. The sustainability audit here is one way of asking who is doing that labor for a project you did not build. When a project scores well on “data ownership” in the rubric, that does not mean someone has the data in a legal-custody sense. It means someone is paid to care for it: to migrate it across storage platforms, to answer deletion requests, to maintain access controls, to renew the domain. Openness without stewardship is neglect with extra steps. Oversight without stewardship is performance art. The single most useful question you can ask a PIT or data-for-good project is not “is your code open source?” It is “who pays the person who will answer the phone in 2029?”
crumbs: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good

title: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good
text:
16.7 Exercises
Exercise 14.1 (Guided). Conduct a sustainability audit of one US PIT project. Recommended choices: GetCalFresh, Colorado’s PEAK benefits portal, or the Benefits Data Trust enrollment tools. Apply the GitHub API audit code above if the project has a public repo; if not, note the absence and document what you can find from the project’s website, annual report, and recent news coverage. Produce a filled-in rubric with a paragraph justifying each score. 500 words plus the audit table.
Exercise 14.2 (Guided). Do the same for a non-US project. Recommended choices: Ushahidi, Data4Change, TransparenciaMX, or Code for Pakistan’s Civic Innovation Lab. Use the same rubric, and explicitly note where the rubric fits poorly. Global-South projects often operate under funding, governance, or regulatory conditions the US-centric rubric does not capture. Say so rather than pretending the score is comparable.
Exercise 14.3 (Analytic). Pick one project from the two exercises above and identify three forms of invisible labor it relies on. For each, name the task, describe who is likely to be doing it, estimate how many hours per year the task consumes across the organization, and propose one specific way the project could make that labor visible in its public materials (annual report, website, credits page). Use Gray and Suri (2019) as a reference. 400 words.
Exercise 14.4 (Design). Propose a sustainability intervention for one of the projects you audited. Your proposal must include: one named role (title and fraction of FTE), a dollar figure for twelve months of salary and benefits, a specific funding source that could support it, and a paragraph on what would have to be true for the funding to actually bite. If you cannot estimate the dollar figure within a factor of two, your proposal is not done. Use public salary data (Glassdoor, Form 990 Schedule J, PayScale) to anchor it. 500 words plus a one-page budget.
Exercise 14.5 (Open-ended). Return to the final-project sketch you drafted in Exercise 1.5. What would you need to keep it alive for five years after you leave the class? Write a short sustainability plan addressing: who hosts it, who owns the domain, who has the credentials, who answers when something breaks, who pays for storage, who decides when to retire it. Be honest. “I will maintain it in my free time” is an answer, but it is a weak one, and you should say why it is weak. 400 to 600 words. You will revisit this plan in Chapter 24 when you make an archival deposit of the project.
crumbs: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good

title: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good
text:
16.8 Looking ahead
Chapter 15 turns from PIT and data-for-good to GovTech and civic tech, a neighboring but distinct conversation about building technology inside and around government itself. Where PIT is an academic-philanthropic formation and data-for-good is a corporate-philanthropic one, GovTech is an industry and civic tech is a movement. The sustainability problems you diagnosed here recur there in different forms: procurement cycles replace grant cycles, contractor lock-in replaces foundation churn. But the underlying question, who pays the maintenance cost, is the same. Chapter 18 will then ask whether the right response to a project you cannot sustain is sometimes to refuse to build it at all.
crumbs: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good

title: 15  Public Interest Technology and Data for Good
text:
16.9 Further Reading and Resources

Tara Dawson McGuinness and Hana Schank (2021), Power to the Public (McGuinness and Schank 2021). The framing text for the PIT field. Read it as a manifesto, not a diagnosis.
Mary L. Gray and Siddharth Suri (2019), Ghost Work (Gray and Suri 2019). The canonical account of the invisible labor behind AI systems. The argument generalizes beyond the Mechanical Turk cases they study.
Ville Aula and Tero Erkkilä (2023), “Digital governance and the philanthropic political economy of the data-for-good” (Aula and Erkkilä 2023). The clearest academic statement of the critique this chapter draws on.
Public Interest Technology University Network: https://www.newamerica.org/pit/university-network/. The hub page for the US academic consortium, including member institutions, fellowship listings, and the field’s self-description (New America 2024).
Code for America: https://codeforamerica.org/. The longest-running US civic tech organization. Their annual report is worth reading for its candor about what worked and what did not.
Ushahidi: https://www.ushahidi.com/ (Ushahidi 2024). The long-running global-South counter-example. Their GitHub organization at https://github.com/ushahidi is a good place to run the audit code in this chapter.
Carter Chambers (2025), “The trouble with the ‘data-for-good’ imaginary” (Chambers 2025). The sharpest recent critique of the data-for-good framing.
GitHub REST API documentation: https://docs.github.com/en/rest (GitHub 2024). The reference for the audit code.
Helena Karasti and Jeanette Blomberg (2018), “Studying infrastructuring ethnographically” (Karasti and Blomberg 2018). The theoretical backbone for thinking about maintenance as a first-class concern.
Code for America’s 2023 transition memo on GetCalFresh becoming a state-operated service is a useful case study in succession planning; search the organization’s blog for “CalFresh transition.”







Aula, Ville, and Tero Erkkilä. 2023. “Digital Governance and the Philanthropic Political Economy of the Data-for-Good.” Information Polity.


Chambers, Carter. 2025. “The Trouble with the ‘Data-for-Good’ Imaginary.” Data & Society.


Code for America. 2024. GetCalFresh: A Simpler Way to Apply for CalFresh Food Benefits. Code for America. https://www.getcalfresh.org/.


GitHub. 2024. GitHub REST API Documentation. GitHub Docs. https://docs.github.com/en/rest.


Gray, Mary L., and Siddharth Suri. 2019. Ghost Work: How to Stop Silicon Valley from Building a New Global Underclass. Houghton Mifflin Harcourt.


Karasti, Helena, and Jeanette Blomberg. 2018. “Studying Infrastructuring Ethnographically.” Computer Supported Cooperative Work 27: 233–65.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.


McGuinness, Tara Dawson, and Hana Schank. 2021. Power to the Public: The Promise of Public Interest Technology. Princeton University Press.


New America. 2024. Public Interest Technology University Network. New America. https://www.newamerica.org/pit/university-network/.


Ushahidi. 2024. About Ushahidi: Crowdsourced Crisis Mapping Since 2008. Ushahidi. https://www.ushahidi.com/about.

title: 16  GovTech and Civic Tech
text:
17 GovTech and Civic Tech
On October 1, 2013, HealthCare.gov launched and immediately collapsed. The federal insurance marketplace had been built by a constellation of prime contractors through a procurement process whose incentives rewarded compliance with the Statement of Work rather than delivery of a working system. Six weeks of public humiliation, a rescue squad flown in from the private sector, and a Presidential apology later, two institutions were born: the United States Digital Service, housed in the Executive Office of the President, and 18F, housed in the General Services Administration. Their pitch was simple. Government cannot buy its way out of software problems if the people writing the contracts do not know what software is. Both offices were founded on the conviction that technical competence has to live inside the state, not at the end of a procurement chain.
That conviction has had a rough decade. 18F shipped a catalog of reusable components, a hiring pipeline for civic-minded technologists, and a reputation for finishing what it started. It also spent its entire institutional life as a target. Inspector General reports questioned its cost-recovery model. Successive administrations slashed its budget, restored it, and slashed it again. By 2025 the office was operating under an anti-DEI executive order that made it hard to retain the staff it had spent a decade recruiting, and its institutional future was once again being debated on a rolling basis. The United States Digital Service, rebranded in 2025 by executive order and partially repurposed, was likewise caught in the cycle. The lesson these trajectories offer is not that internal capacity is a bad idea. It is that internal capacity has no natural constituency, whereas vendor capacity has a lobbying association. Chapter 7’s argument about oversight, and Chapter 8’s argument about ownership, both run through the question of where the technical knowledge sits. When it sits with a vendor, the vendor owns what the public can inspect.
This chapter is the second in Part IV. It extends Chapter 16 by asking a sharper question. If public interest technology is the professional identity, what are the organizational forms through which it actually reaches the public? Two candidate answers dominate the field. GovTech refers to firms that sell software and services to the government. Civic tech refers to technologists, often volunteer or philanthropically funded, who build alongside or outside government. The two are sometimes described as complements. They have very different accountability levers. GovTech is accountable, in principle, through procurement law, contract terms, and the audit rights retained by the buyer. Civic tech is accountable, in principle, through the communities it serves and the funders it relies on. In practice, as Bharosa (2022) argues in his research agenda on the rise of GovTech, neither accountability story holds up without intentional construction. Procurement is where governance happens or does not. And civic tech is sustained only as long as someone writes the check.

title: 16  GovTech and Civic Tech
text:
17.1 Five models on one map
It helps to fix five reference points before going further.
The United States Digital Service was founded in 2014, modeled on the UK’s Government Digital Service, and designed to deploy small teams of technologists into agencies in crisis. It is internal capacity, funded through the executive branch, with limited authority to compel. The 18F office, founded the same year inside the General Services Administration, was designed as a fee-for-service consultancy to other agencies. Both are US examples of what Brown et al. (2017) call the “Government as a Platform” (GaaP) ambition, where the state itself provides shared services that reduce duplication across agencies.
The UK Government Digital Service (GDS), founded in 2011, is the elder sibling the US offices tried to copy. Its Service Manual and Service Standard became export goods, adopted in variant form by Canada, Australia, Singapore, and others. Estonia’s X-Road, by contrast, is not primarily about service design. It is an interoperability layer. X-Road is a secure data exchange protocol that lets one Estonian agency query another’s database without either agency building a custom integration, and it sits underneath e-residency, digital signatures, and most of what people mean when they praise “Estonian e-government.” Governance in the Estonian model happens through the interoperability spec. If your system does not speak X-Road, it does not get to play.
The fifth reference point is IndiaStack, the layered set of APIs built around the Aadhaar biometric identity system. IndiaStack is what a GaaP platform looks like at population scale, and it is what Taylor (2021) calls the hard case. Presence-less, paperless, cashless service delivery is a real improvement when it works, and a serious violation when it does not. Biometric enrollment of over a billion people was not, in any meaningful sense, consented to, because the alternative to enrollment was exclusion from subsidized food rations, banking, and in some cases public-sector employment. Rao and Nair (2019) tracks how Aadhaar’s optionality eroded in practice even where it was legally preserved. The IndiaStack case does not discredit the GaaP ambition. It names the governance stakes of building at that scale without the installed base of Chapter 7 actually in place.
These five cases do not share a politics. They share a structural question: where does technical capacity sit, and what does it have to answer to?

title: 16  GovTech and Civic Tech
section: 17.2 Procurement is where the governance is
text:
17.2 Procurement is where the governance is
Most of a government’s relationship with technology is mediated by contracts. A Request for Proposal (RFP) is not, at first glance, a governance document. It is a solicitation. Vendors read it and decide whether to bid. But the RFP is where the buyer declares, in binding language, what the system must do, what data it must produce, what interfaces it must expose, and what happens when the contract ends. If the RFP does not require the vendor to export data in an open format on termination, the vendor owns the database. If the RFP does not require logging, there will be no logs to audit. If the RFP does not reserve the buyer’s right to publish, the public cannot see what it paid for.
Brown et al. (2017) make this point at the level of platform theory: Government as a Platform succeeds or fails at the interface specifications, not at the marketing. The interface specifications live in the contract. You can read a thousand op-eds about digital government without encountering a single clause from an actual contract, which is why the op-eds tend to generalize in ways the contracts do not.
Consider a running example. The State of Colorado, like most US states, maintains a procurement portal where active solicitations are posted. Boulder’s municipal purchasing division does the same at the city level. A plausible RFP type for a public-interest audit is a benefits eligibility modernization, a permit portal, or a data warehouse consolidation. Pick one. For the rest of this chapter, assume you are looking at a Colorado RFP for a permit portal that would replace the existing paper-and-PDF process in a mid-sized county. The vendor will build a web application, a backend database, an API for third-party integrations, and a payment integration. The county will pay in milestone installments over three years and then enter a five-year operations-and-maintenance (O&M) period.
Your first move, reading this RFP, is to map what artifacts the system would produce. A permit application is a record. A decision is a record. A payment is a record. A site inspection generates a record. An appeal generates a record. Every one of these is either stored in the vendor’s database, forwarded to the county’s records system, or both. The question the RFP has to answer, and often fails to answer, is which of these records the county owns, in what format they can be exported, and what happens to them when the contract terminates.






NoteIn the Public Interest



The contract is the governance. This claim is not rhetorical. If the RFP does not require the vendor to export data in a non-proprietary format at no additional cost, on demand, with documented schema, then the vendor owns the database in operational terms even if the county owns it in legal terms. You met this distinction in Chapter 10: legal title is not the same as practical control. Oversight, as Chapter 9 developed it, requires the ability to see. A clause that requires nightly exports to an S3 bucket the county controls, in Parquet or CSV plus a JSON schema file, is worth more than fifty pages of strategic planning documents. The procurement clause is where linkability, interpretability, continuity, and safe scrutiny are either bought or given up. If you take one practice from this chapter, let it be this: when a colleague tells you a public system is “well governed,” ask to see the contract.

title: 16  GovTech and Civic Tech
text:
17.3 Five clauses worth writing
Here are five procurement clauses, expressed as YAML for readability, that any public-interest-oriented buyer should consider boilerplate. They are not legal drafting. They are the specification from which a legal drafter works.
data_export:
  trigger: [on_demand, quarterly, on_termination]
  format: [csv, parquet]
  schema: json_schema_v7
  destination: buyer_controlled_storage
  delivery_sla_hours: 72
  cost: included_in_base_contract

interoperability:
  apis:
    style: REST
    auth: oauth2
    spec: openapi_3_1
    rate_limits_documented: true
  identifiers:
    persistent: true
    resolvable_across_agencies: true
    scheme: [ark, doi, uuid_v4]

auditability:
  immutable_log:
    events: [read, write, delete, access_grant, access_revoke]
    retention_years: 7
    export_format: ndjson
  third_party_inspection:
    allowed: true
    notice_days: 10
    scope: [code, logs, schemas]

source_code:
  ownership: buyer
  delivery: git_repository_with_full_history
  license_on_termination: public_domain_or_cc0

vendor_transition:
  termination_assistance_months: 6
  knowledge_transfer: documented_runbooks
  successor_onboarding: required
  data_migration_tested_quarterly: true
Read those back. Each clause is a specific technical commitment with a specific failure mode if you omit it. Omit data_export and the vendor can charge a five-figure extraction fee on termination. Omit interoperability.identifiers.persistent and two years later no one will be able to link a 2026 permit record to a 2028 inspection record because the IDs reset when the database was migrated. Omit auditability.immutable_log and you will not be able to tell, after the fact, whether a decision was overridden by a human or by a scheduled job. Omit source_code.ownership and you have a vendor lock-in problem that will outlast the people who wrote the contract. Omit vendor_transition and the moment you want to switch vendors you will discover that switching is impossible, which is how vendor lock-in becomes a permanent governance condition rather than a temporary inconvenience.






TipThe Missing Manual



Government APIs have documentation that describes what the endpoint returns. The documentation almost never describes what it does not return. The Census Bureau’s American Community Survey API, for example, returns an array-of-arrays where the first row is the column headers. If you feed the raw response to pd.DataFrame the headers will land as a data row and your first real record will be read as column names. The fix is trivial once you know: pd.DataFrame(data[1:], columns=data[0]). The fix is a small tax. The tax you did not anticipate is that the list of variables an agency publishes in its metadata is not the list of variables it considers reliable, and the list of geographies the endpoint accepts is not the list of geographies for which estimates exist. Document these gaps for yourself. Then document them for the next person.

title: 16  GovTech and Civic Tech
text:
17.4 A small Census API exercise
You can run an actual government API call in a few lines. The Census Bureau’s ACS 5-year endpoint is documented at census.gov/data/developers and returns estimates for any level of geography down to block group. Use it to pull median household income for all Colorado counties for the most recent vintage.
import requests
import pandas as pd

# ACS 5-year, 2022 vintage. Variable B19013_001E is median household income.
url = "https://api.census.gov/data/2022/acs/acs5"
params = {
    "get": "NAME,B19013_001E,B19013_001M",
    "for": "county:*",
    "in": "state:08",  # Colorado FIPS
}

response = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=30)
response.raise_for_status()
payload = response.json()

# The Missing Manual move: first row is headers, the rest is data.
df = pd.DataFrame(payload[1:], columns=payload[0])
df = df.rename(columns={
    "B19013_001E": "median_income",
    "B19013_001M": "moe",
})
df["median_income"] = pd.to_numeric(df["median_income"], errors="coerce")
df["moe"] = pd.to_numeric(df["moe"], errors="coerce")
df = df.sort_values("median_income", ascending=False)
print(df[["NAME", "median_income", "moe"]].head())
# => Douglas County, Broomfield County, Elbert County at the top;
#    margin of error column tells you how much to trust each point estimate.
Plotting is one matplotlib call away.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 10))
top = df.head(20)
ax.barh(top["NAME"], top["median_income"], xerr=top["moe"])
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.set_xlabel("Median household income (USD)")
ax.set_title("Colorado counties: ACS 5-year median income, 2022 vintage")
fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig("ch15_acs_median_income.png", dpi=120)
Two things are worth noticing. First, the margin of error bars are not decorative. ACS is a sample survey; small counties have margins of error so wide that the point estimate is almost meaningless on its own. Any dashboard that publishes the point estimate without the MOE is misreading the data. Second, the API required no authentication for a small request like this one, but the production pattern is to register for an API key and pass it as &key=... so the Bureau can rate-limit you without guessing. The same pattern applies to the Federal Reserve’s FRED API (fred.stlouisfed.org/docs/api), which requires a key, returns JSON with a clearer schema, and is the right endpoint if your question is about macroeconomic rather than demographic series.

title: 16  GovTech and Civic Tech
section: 17.5 Why the distinction between GovTech and civic tech matters
text:
17.5 Why the distinction between GovTech and civic tech matters
A GovTech vendor answers to its client. The client is the government. If the government writes good contracts, GovTech becomes a reasonable way to deliver public services. If the government writes bad contracts, GovTech becomes a mechanism for extracting rents from public budgets while producing systems the public cannot inspect. The accountability lever is procurement, amplified by the audit and inspection rights written into the contract. Bharosa (2022) raises the “Trojan horse” worry specifically: if a state’s internal capacity has atrophied, it no longer has the expertise to write contracts that hold vendors accountable, and GovTech becomes structurally extractive even where individual vendors are well-intentioned.
A civic tech project answers to its funders and, in principle, to the communities it serves. If the funder is a foundation, the accountability lever is the foundation’s theory of change and renewal criteria. If the users are community members, the accountability lever is their continued use, which is easier to observe than to measure. Aragon et al. (2020) make the case, in Human-Centered Data Science, for building with communities rather than for them. That is a methodological commitment, but it does not solve the funding problem. As Chapter 16 developed, civic tech projects are structurally prone to die between grant cycles, and the distinction you are drawing now matters because the failure modes are different. A failed GovTech contract produces a locked-in system that no one can afford to replace. A failed civic tech project produces a dead GitHub repo and a handful of volunteers who do not get paid.
Neither failure mode is inevitable. Both become more likely when the people commissioning the work do not know what they are looking at. Reading a procurement document with the clauses above in mind, or reading a civic tech project’s governance page with Chapter 16’s sustainability rubric in mind, is part of the craft. The craft is not glamorous. Chapter 14 argued that planning’s lineage contributes rigor about who bears costs and who bears benefits. GovTech and civic tech inherit that question. The contract, or the grant agreement, is where the answer gets written down.

title: 16  GovTech and Civic Tech
text:
17.6 Exercises
Exercise 15.1 (Guided). Go to the Colorado state procurement portal or Boulder’s municipal purchasing site and find an active or recently-closed RFP for a software system. Read the Statement of Work in full. Produce a one-page map of the artifacts the winning system would generate: records (what kinds, how many per year), documents (what formats), and processes (which ones would leave a trace in the system). For each artifact, write one sentence saying whether the RFP requires that artifact to be exportable by the buyer, and in what format.
Exercise 15.2 (Applied). Take the RFP from Exercise 15.1. Draft three to five additional procurement clauses, in YAML or markdown, that would close the gaps you found. Each clause should name a specific data type, format, retention period, and access condition. Share your draft with a classmate and defend each clause against a skeptical vendor’s likely objection.
Exercise 15.3 (Technical). Pull a dataset from either the Census ACS API or the FRED API. Choose a question you actually want the answer to. Document the call, the response structure, and at least one gotcha you encountered that was not in the published documentation. Produce one chart. Write 200 words on what the chart shows and what it cannot show.
Exercise 15.4 (Comparative). Write a 600-word comparison of two “government as a platform” initiatives drawn from this list: USDS, 18F, GDS UK, Estonia X-Road, IndiaStack. Your comparison should address funding model, accountability structure, and scope of authority. Cite at least one scholarly source per case, including Bharosa (2022) or Brown et al. (2017). Close with a paragraph on what each case teaches about where internal state capacity should sit.
Exercise 15.5 (Open). What data would you require a vendor to export before you would declare a public-sector contract complete? Write a one-page specification with a title, a scope section, five to ten required exports, a default format for each, and a definition of “complete” that a county attorney could attach to a contract. Treat this as a reusable template. You will use it again in Chapter 20 when you write the report section on public-sector procurement, and again in Chapter 23 if you ever comment on a federal IT rulemaking.

title: 16  GovTech and Civic Tech
text:
17.7 Looking ahead
Chapter 18 closes Part IV by turning the critical lens back on the book’s own framing. If GovTech and civic tech are the organizational forms through which public interest technology reaches the public, critical data studies is the tradition that asks whether “reaching the public” is always the right ambition. Some of the traditions Chapter 16 takes up, including Indigenous data sovereignty, abolitionist critique, and feminist refusal, would read the procurement clauses in this chapter and ask a different question: what data should not be collected at all, and whose consent to the platform was never asked? The contract is where governance happens. It is also where extraction happens. The next chapter holds both truths at once.

title: 16  GovTech and Civic Tech
text:
17.8 Further Reading and Resources

United States Digital Service, https://www.usds.gov/. The founding US internal-capacity office, with a Digital Services Playbook worth reading even if you never intend to work there.
18F, https://18f.gsa.gov/. The GSA-housed sibling, with open-source components and case studies that double as procurement worked examples.
Estonia’s X-Road, https://x-road.global/. The canonical interoperability protocol; the technical documentation is unusually honest about tradeoffs.
Code for America, https://codeforamerica.org/. The US civic-tech anchor institution, with GetCalFresh and the Clear My Record initiative as case material.
GOV.UK Service Manual, https://www.gov.uk/service-manual. The UK’s public playbook for designing government services; still the single best operational reference in the field.
US Census Bureau API User Guide, https://www.census.gov/data/developers/guidance/api-user-guide.html. The endpoint used in this chapter; read the “Known Issues” section before you write any code against it.
FRED API documentation, https://fred.stlouisfed.org/docs/api/fred/. The Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis’s macroeconomic series, with a cleaner schema than Census and a more opinionated rate limiter.
Nesta’s GovTech landscape reports, https://www.nesta.org.uk/. UK-grounded but globally relevant mapping of the GovTech vendor ecosystem.
Linnet Taylor, “The price of certainty” (2021). The IndiaStack critique in its most citable form; read alongside any breathless GaaP advocacy.
Nitesh Bharosa, “The rise of GovTech: Trojan horse or blessing in disguise?” (2022). A research agenda that names the hard questions explicitly and refuses to resolve them prematurely.







Aragon, Cecilia, Shion Guha, Marina Kogan, Michael Muller, and Gina Neff. 2020. “Human-Centered Data Science: An Introduction.” MIT Press.


Bharosa, Nitesh. 2022. “The Rise of GovTech: Trojan Horse or Blessing in Disguise? A Research Agenda.” Government Information Quarterly 39 (3): 101692.


Brown, Alan, Jerry Fishenden, Mark Thompson, and Will Venters. 2017. “Appraising the Impact and Role of Platform Models and Government as a Platform (GaaP) in UK Government Public Service Reform: Towards a Platform Assessment Framework (PAF).” Government Information Quarterly 34 (2): 167–82.


Rao, Ursula, and Vijayanka Nair. 2019. “Aadhaar: Governing with Biometrics.” South Asia: Journal of South Asian Studies 42 (3): 469–81.


Taylor, Linnet. 2021. “The Price of Certainty: How the Politics of Pandemic Data Demand an Ethics of Care.” Big Data & Society 7 (2).

title: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal
text:
18 Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal
This chapter is the hinge of the book. For fifteen chapters you have been building an installed base: records that link, categories that can be interpreted, evidence that persists, researchers who can scrutinize, authorities who can act, and people who can seek remedy (Keegan 2026). The argument of Chapters 2 through 15 is that a working public-interest data practice needs all six. The argument here is that, for some of the people this installed base is supposed to serve, any one of those six can also be the mechanism by which harm arrives. A record that links is a record that surveils. A category that can be interpreted is one that was once imposed. Evidence that persists can be subpoenaed a decade later by an agency that did not exist when the data was collected. Scrutiny can be predatory. Authority can be carceral. Remedy can be a pretext for further data collection.
Critical data studies holds these possibilities in view. Data justice asks who decides, under what conditions, on whose terms. Refusal is what you do when the answer to those questions is that the data should not exist in the form proposed. This chapter introduces all three and asks you to do two things with the same municipal dataset: audit its classification schema, and write a refusal specification for it. The pair is the point. Neither alone does the work.
Part IV has been about neighbors: public interest technology, data-for-good, GovTech, civic tech. This final chapter sits next to a neighbor who is less comfortable. Critical data studies does not build platforms and is not in the career pipeline Chapter 16 describes. But the practice you will learn in Part V is stronger when it can absorb the critique honestly, including the parts that undercut this book’s own starting framework.
crumbs: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal

title: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal
section: 18.1 The starting canon: boyd and Crawford
text:
18.1 The starting canon: boyd and Crawford
Begin with boyd and Crawford (2012), “Critical questions for big data.” Still the most cited essay in the field because it named six provocations every chapter of this book has implicitly answered. Big data changes the definition of knowledge. Claims to objectivity are misleading. Bigger is not always better. Out of context, big data lose meaning. Accessibility is not ethicality. Limited access creates new digital divides.
The provocation that matters most here is the fifth. Accessibility is not consent. The fact that a record exists in a public dataset and is legally available for analysis does not settle whether you should use it. Homeless Management Information System (HMIS) data, the running example for this chapter, is a tight case. HMIS exists because the US Department of Housing and Urban Development requires it as a condition of Continuum of Care funding (US Department of Housing and Urban Development 2024). The records are real, detailed, and, under certain conditions, available to researchers. The people documented in them did not consent in any sense a research ethics board would recognize. They sought shelter, and documentation was the price.
crumbs: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal

title: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal
text:
18.2 The political work of classification
Bowker and Star (2000), Sorting Things Out, is the second book you need. Classification is never neutral. Every category system is a historical artifact produced by some authority, for some purpose, at some moment. The ICD encoded homosexuality as mental illness until 1973. The US Census’s race categories have been rewritten roughly every decade since 1790, each time to serve a different administrative project. Classification does not describe the world. It imposes a shape on it, and the shape tends to serve whoever did the imposing.
The consequence for data science is specific. When you call value_counts() on a column labeled race or gender or veteran_status and see five or six buckets, you are not seeing the distribution of human variation. You are seeing the surviving output of a classification pipeline that, at some earlier stage, had more, fewer, or different categories with boundaries that did not match the ones you now see. The flattening happened upstream, usually at intake, often because a reporting template required a fixed list. Your descriptive statistics inherit the politics of that template whether or not you read it.
Denton et al. (2021) make this concrete for machine learning. Their genealogy of ImageNet traces how a dataset assembled in 2006 to 2009, using WordNet’s 1980s synset hierarchy, carried into the 2010s a taxonomy of human kinds that encoded racial pseudoscience, categories for “failure” and “loser,” ethnic slurs, and an implicit theory that the visual world is a tree of discrete kinds. When ImageNet became the benchmark for a generation of computer vision models, those decisions propagated into production systems that never revisited the WordNet assumptions. The dataset was a historical artifact. The benchmarks trained on it made the artifact load-bearing.
crumbs: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal

title: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal
section: 18.3 Data justice and the question of for whom
text:
18.3 Data justice and the question of for whom
Taylor (2017) puts a different question on the table. The fairness literature that Chapter 7 reviewed asks whether an algorithm treats different groups equivalently. Data justice asks something prior: what counts as justice in this context, for whom, through what mechanisms? An algorithm can satisfy demographic parity across race categories and still be unjust, because the deeper question is whether the decision system itself should exist, whether the people subject to it had any say in its design, and whether the categories used to sort them are categories they recognize. D’Ignazio and Klein (2020), Data Feminism, extends this framing into seven principles, of which “examine power” and “elevate emotion and embodiment” are the ones most likely to make an engineering audience uncomfortable, and therefore the ones most likely to be doing real work.
You met openness in Chapter 8 and ownership in Chapter 10. Data justice says those values are not sufficient. Openness can be the mechanism by which a community’s vulnerability becomes indexable by anyone with a laptop. Ownership can be a contract that extracts data from people under conditions they had no power to negotiate. Neither value is wrong; both are incomplete without the prior question of for whom.
crumbs: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal

title: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal
text:
18.4 Refusal as design
Zong and Matias (2024) and Garcia et al. (2022) develop the concept this chapter is built around. Refusal is not the absence of data practice. It is a design move. To refuse is to specify what would not be collected, what would be collected differently, what would be collected only under conditions the affected community controls, and who gets to make each call. A well-written refusal spec is as detailed as a data management plan and does similar work: it commits authors to particular operations and can be held to account if they are violated. The output is not a dataset. It is an absence that has been designed.
The key move, which students miss the first time, is that refusal is generative. A refusal spec is not a protest document. It says: if we were not collecting this, what would we do to answer the underlying question? That substitution is where the intellectual work lives.
crumbs: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal

title: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal
text:
18.5 The installed base, revisited
This is where the book’s framework binds. The installed base is built around the premise that records should link, persist, be interpretable, and be available for scrutiny. Carroll et al. (2020) and the Te Mana Raraunga network propose something different. The CARE Principles for Indigenous Data Governance (Collective Benefit, Authority to Control, Responsibility, Ethics) were articulated as a companion, and corrective, to FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable), the default standard for scientific data stewardship (Wilkinson et al. 2016). CARE does not reject FAIR. It says FAIR is necessary and insufficient. A dataset can satisfy every letter of FAIR and still be in the wrong relationship with the community it describes.
Māori data sovereignty, as Te Mana Raraunga articulates it (Te Mana Raraunga 2018), begins with the premise that data about Māori communities are a Māori responsibility. Who holds the data, who decides what questions get asked of it, who benefits from the use, are not methodological concerns downstream of the research question. They are the question. The US Indigenous Data Sovereignty Network (United States Indigenous Data Sovereignty Network 2024) has made a parallel argument in the United States context.
This is the honest moment. The installed-base framing assumes the default should be linkable, persistent, interpretable records under conditions of scrutiny. CARE and Indigenous data sovereignty say that default is itself a political choice reflecting the settler colonial state’s interest in legibility more than any universal principle of good governance. You can take the critique seriously without abandoning the framework. You cannot take it seriously while pretending the framework is neutral.
crumbs: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal

title: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal
text:
18.6 Running example: Denver HMIS
The Denver metro region, like every US Continuum of Care, operates an HMIS as a condition of HUD funding (US Department of Housing and Urban Development 2024). HMIS records client-level data for anyone entering homelessness services: intake demographics, shelter stays, case notes, exits, returns. A researcher working with a CoC partner can, under a Data Use Agreement, obtain de-identified extracts.
The tension is stark. Without HMIS, continuums cannot document who they served, cannot qualify for HUD funding, and cannot produce the Point-in-Time counts that drive resource allocation. With HMIS, people experiencing homelessness are among the most heavily documented populations in the United States per capita, and that documentation is potentially available to immigration enforcement, family court, criminal justice agencies, and any other state actor with a sufficiently motivated subpoena (Eubanks 2014). The audit is not academic. The refusal is not rhetorical.






TipThe Missing Manual



The HMIS Data Standards manual names the categories. It does not name the decisions that produced them. The “Race” field allows American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Black or African American, Native Hawaiian or Pacific Islander, White, and Client Doesn’t Know / Client Refused / Data Not Collected. When you run df["race"].value_counts() and see six buckets, there were almost certainly more at intake. A client who identified as Oaxaqueño, Somali Bantu, Rohingya, or Dakota was collapsed upstream into one of the HUD-allowable codes by the intake worker, because the HMIS validation rules reject anything else. The documentation will tell you what the codes mean. It will not tell you what was lost when the intake worker had three seconds to pick one.
crumbs: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal

title: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal
text:
18.7 Technical component 1: the category audit
The first artifact is a notebook that inventories the categorical fields, traces each to an administrative source, and documents what the collapsing does.
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("data/hmis_extract.csv", low_memory=False)

CATEGORICAL_FIELDS = [
    "race", "ethnicity", "gender", "veteran_status",
    "project_type", "destination", "disabling_condition",
    "prior_living_situation",
]

def audit_field(series):
    counts = series.value_counts(dropna=False).to_frame("n")
    counts["pct"] = (counts["n"] / len(series) * 100).round(2)
    counts["field"] = series.name
    return counts.reset_index(names="value")

audit = pd.concat([audit_field(df[f]) for f in CATEGORICAL_FIELDS])
audit.to_csv("out/category_audit.csv", index=False)
# => One row per (field, value) pair with counts and pct.
That gives you the frequencies. It does not give you the provenance. For each field, write a one-paragraph provenance note that answers four questions. Who defined the categories, when, for what administrative need, and what changed from the prior version? The HMIS manual is your source for the first three; HUD’s Federal Register notices from each update cycle are your source for the fourth.
PROVENANCE = {
    "race": {
        "authority": "OMB Directive 15 (1997), implemented in HMIS 2014",
        "known_flattening": (
            "Collapses Indigenous identities (Dine, Dakota, Lumbee) "
            "into 'American Indian or Alaska Native'. Collapses South, "
            "Southeast, and East Asian into 'Asian'. No MENA category."
        ),
        "purpose": "Federal civil-rights reporting, not service-matching.",
    },
    # Repeat for each field.
}
Cross-tabulate categories against missingness. A provenance note is worth more when you can show the rate at which “Data Not Collected” is selected and how that rate varies by intake site.
missing_by_site = (
    df.assign(race_missing=df["race"].isin(
        ["Client doesn't know", "Client refused", "Data not collected"]
    ))
    .groupby("intake_site")["race_missing"]
    .mean()
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
)
# => High "Data Not Collected" rates often track sites where clients
# distrust documentation; the distrust is itself data.
Notice what that last snippet did. It treated absence as a finding. D’Ignazio and Klein (2020) call this counting what is missing, and counting why. A site with 40% missingness on race is not a site with poor data quality. It is a site where 40% of clients, faced with a form, chose not to answer. That refusal is a signal. A competent audit surfaces it.
crumbs: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal

title: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal
text:
18.8 Technical component 2: the refusal specification
The second artifact is a structured refusal specification. The spec is a markdown document with a YAML preamble. The YAML commits the authors to specific operations; the markdown explains each. Below is a skeleton you can adapt.
---
dataset: denver_metro_hmis_2024
governance_body: denver_lived_experience_advisory_board
review_cycle: annual

would_not_collect:
  - field: full_legal_name
    rationale: |
      Legal name enables cross-system linkage with criminal-
      justice and immigration databases. Replace with a
      salted pseudonymous ID generated at intake.
  - field: social_security_number
    rationale: |
      HUD requires SSN only for specific subpopulations.
      Defaulting to SSN collection exceeds the federal
      minimum and creates a subpoena target.

collect_differently:
  - field: race
    current: HUD six-category closed list
    proposed: |
      Free-text self-identification, with HUD codes as an
      administrative mapping applied only when required
      for federal reporting. The raw self-id is retained
      under community control; only the mapping leaves the
      Continuum.

collect_under_community_governance:
  - field: case_management_notes
    governance: |
      Notes are retained by the direct-service provider,
      not uploaded to the shared HMIS instance. Aggregate
      statistics derived from notes are reviewed by the
      Lived Experience Advisory Board before release.

decision_process:
  who_decides: |
    The Lived Experience Advisory Board, which includes at
    least five currently- or recently-unhoused members with
    voting rights equal to agency staff.
  by_what_process: |
    Changes require a majority vote at a noticed public
    meeting. Emergency additions can be enacted by the
    chair pending ratification at the next meeting.
  appeals: |
    A client may request removal of their own record from
    any downstream analytic extract. The request is honored
    within 30 days and logged in aggregate.
---
The structure is load-bearing. Each section answers a specific question: what would you not collect, what would you collect differently, what would you collect only under community-controlled conditions, who makes the call when the spec needs to change, by what process, and with what appeals. A refusal spec that skips the decision-process section is a wish list, not a governance document.
The deliverable is the pair. The audit shows what is in the data and what the categories are doing. The refusal shows what would be different, and under whose authority, if the audit’s findings were taken seriously. Neither on its own forces the question. Together they do.
crumbs: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal

title: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal
section: 18.9 Refusal in the wild: the Algorithmic Ecology
text:
18.9 Refusal in the wild: the Algorithmic Ecology
The Stop LAPD Spying Coalition, in partnership with the Free Radicals collective, published “The Algorithmic Ecology” in 2020 (Stop LAPD Spying Coalition and Free Radicals 2020). Its method is to map an algorithmic system (LAPD’s predictive policing program at the time) as an ecology: community, institutional, and political-economic levels. The refusal it articulates is not “fix the algorithm” but “dismantle the conditions that make the algorithm legible as a solution to anything.” The coalition’s earlier “Before the Bullet Hits the Body” (Stop LAPD Spying Coalition 2018) did the groundwork by showing how LAPD’s data-driven programs routed back to the same neighborhoods regardless of the model’s claims to neutrality. When LAPD discontinued PredPol in 2020 and the LASER program shortly after, the coalition’s organizing was a proximate cause.
That is refusal as generative, in the sense Zong and Matias (2024) intend. It did not settle for a seat at the table. It specified the conditions under which the table should not exist in its current form, and built the political capacity to enforce the specification. Your spec in Exercise 16.2 is modest by comparison. The move is the same.






NoteIn the Public Interest



Chapter 6 argued openness is interpretive legibility: data you can read, re-read, and link. Chapter 8 argued ownership is the authority to determine use, including the authority to not be owned. This chapter asks what happens when those two framings conflict. A dataset can be maximally open, perfectly interpretable, and exhaustively linkable, and still violate CARE because the community it describes never had authority over whether it should exist. Openness as you met it in Chapter 8 breaks down when the community is not the author of the opening. Ownership as you met it in Chapter 10 breaks down when it can only be asserted by entities the installed base recognizes. Indigenous data sovereignty names both breakdowns. Your refusal specification is where you do something about them, not by rejecting openness or ownership, but by rebuilding both on the prior question of authority.
crumbs: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal

title: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal
text:
18.10 Exercises
Exercise 16.1 (Guided). Obtain a public HMIS-derived dataset. Many Continuums of Care publish de-identified aggregate extracts, HUD AHAR reports contain analyzable tables, and the Denver Metro CoC publishes dashboards with extractable tables. If no HMIS analog is accessible, use Denver Police stop data or Boulder’s eviction-filings feed. Produce the category audit: a table of field frequencies, a provenance note for each field, and a missingness cross-tab by at least one stratifying variable. Commit as audit.ipynb.
Exercise 16.2 (Applied). Write a refusal specification for the same dataset, following the YAML skeleton. Include at least two would_not_collect entries, two collect_differently entries, one collect_under_community_governance entry, and a full decision_process section. Each entry needs a rationale you would defend in a public meeting. Commit as refusal.md.
Exercise 16.3 (Reading). Read the CARE Principles (Carroll et al. 2020) and FAIR (Wilkinson et al. 2016). Write 400 words comparing them on three dimensions: who each treats as the data-governance authority, what each says about reuse, and where they conflict in practice. Do not pretend they are compatible where they are not.
Exercise 16.4 (Analytic). Pick a refusal in the wild: the Algorithmic Ecology from Stop LAPD Spying, the Detroit Community Technology Project’s work on Project Green Light (Detroit Community Technology Project 2019), a recent Indigenous data sovereignty initiative tracked by USIDSN, or another case your instructor approves. Write 500 words on what is being refused, who is doing the refusing, what alternative the refusal proposes, and what made it effective or not. Cite at least three primary sources.
Exercise 16.5 (Open-ended). Return to the project sketch from Exercise 1.5. Identify one question where refusal is the right answer. Write two paragraphs: what you would not collect or analyze, and what substitutes for the refused collection. The substitute may be a different research question, a different unit of analysis, a governance structure, or a partnership. Save as ch16_project_refusal.md. Carry this forward into Part V.
crumbs: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal

title: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal
text:
18.11 Looking ahead
Part V begins in Chapter 17 (Chapter 19) with the op-ed, the first of six genres through which data scientists contribute to public conversations. The audit and refusal you produced here are internal artifacts, the kind of document a research team circulates to set its own terms. The op-ed is their public counterpart: a form that addresses an audience that is not a research team, in a language that is not YAML, with a claim that is not hedged. Part V is the pivot from seeing to doing. This chapter is the last moment to settle what you see.
crumbs: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal

title: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal
text:
18.12 Further Reading and Resources

Bowker and Star (2000), Sorting Things Out: Classification and Its Consequences. MIT Press. Start here.
D’Ignazio and Klein (2020), Data Feminism. MIT Press. Open-access at https://data-feminism.mitpress.mit.edu/. Seven principles and the case studies that make them mean something.
The CARE Principles for Indigenous Data Governance: https://www.gida-global.org/care. Short, specific, a direct complement to FAIR.
Te Mana Raraunga, “Principles of Māori Data Sovereignty”: https://www.temanararaunga.maori.nz/tutohinga.
Stop LAPD Spying Coalition, “The Algorithmic Ecology” (2020): https://stoplapdspying.org/the-algorithmic-ecology-an-abolitionist-tool-for-organizing-against-algorithms/. A short essay and a diagram that will change how you read algorithmic systems.
Audra Simpson (2014), Mohawk Interruptus. Duke University Press. The book that made refusal a theoretical term in contemporary Indigenous studies.
Simone Browne (2015), Dark Matters: On the Surveillance of Blackness. Duke University Press. The prehistory of the surveillance systems this chapter’s audit targets.
Safiya Umoja Noble (2018), Algorithms of Oppression. NYU Press. A canonical treatment of search as a site of racial formation.
US Indigenous Data Sovereignty Network: https://usindigenousdata.org/.
HUD HMIS Data Standards manual: https://www.hudexchange.info/resource/3824/hmis-data-dictionary/. Read the introduction and the Race field documentation before Exercise 16.1.







Bowker, Geoffrey C., and Susan Leigh Star. 2000. Sorting Things Out: Classification and Its Consequences. MIT Press.


boyd, danah, and Kate Crawford. 2012. “Critical Questions for Big Data: Provocations for a Cultural, Technological, and Scholarly Phenomenon.” Information, Communication & Society 15 (5): 662–79.


Carroll, Stephanie Russo, Ibrahim Garba, Oscar L. Figueroa-Rodríguez, et al. 2020. “The CARE Principles for Indigenous Data Governance.” Data Science Journal 19: 43.


D’Ignazio, Catherine, and Lauren F. Klein. 2020. Data Feminism. MIT Press.


Denton, Emily, Alex Hanna, Razvan Amironesei, Andrew Smart, and Hilary Nicole. 2021. “On the Genealogy of Machine Learning Datasets: A Critical History of ImageNet.” Big Data & Society 8 (2).


Detroit Community Technology Project. 2019. A Critical Summary of Detroit’s Project Green Light and Its Greater Context. Detroit Community Technology Project. https://detroitcommunitytech.org/?q=content/critical-summary-detroits-project-green-light-and-its-greater-context.


Eubanks, Virginia. 2014. “Want to Predict the Future of Surveillance? Ask Poor Communities.” The American Prospect, January.


Garcia, Patricia, Tonia Sutherland, Marika Cifor, et al. 2022. No: Critical Refusal as Feminist Data Practice. Proceedings of CSCW 2022.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.


Stop LAPD Spying Coalition. 2018. Before the Bullet Hits the Body: Dismantling Predictive Policing in Los Angeles. Stop LAPD Spying Coalition. https://stoplapdspying.org/before-the-bullet-hits-the-body-a-report-on-predictive-policing/.


Stop LAPD Spying Coalition, and Free Radicals. 2020. The Algorithmic Ecology: An Abolitionist Tool for Organizing Against Algorithms. Medium / Stop LAPD Spying Coalition. https://stoplapdspying.org/the-algorithmic-ecology-an-abolitionist-tool-for-organizing-against-algorithms/.


Taylor, Linnet. 2017. “What Is Data Justice? The Case for Connecting Digital Rights and Freedoms Globally.” Big Data & Society 4 (2).


Te Mana Raraunga. 2018. Principles of Māori Data Sovereignty. Māori Data Sovereignty Network. https://www.temanararaunga.maori.nz/tutohinga.


United States Indigenous Data Sovereignty Network. 2024. USIDSN: Promoting Indigenous Data Sovereignty in the United States. Native Nations Institute, University of Arizona. https://usindigenousdata.org/.


US Department of Housing and Urban Development. 2024. HMIS Data Standards Manual. HUD Exchange. https://www.hudexchange.info/resource/3824/hmis-data-dictionary/.


Wilkinson, Mark D., Michel Dumontier, IJsbrand Jan Aalbersberg, et al. 2016. “The FAIR Guiding Principles for Scientific Data Management and Stewardship.” Scientific Data 3: 160018.


Zong, Jonathan, and J. Nathan Matias. 2024. “Data Refusal from Below: A Framework for Resisting Institutional Data Harms.” ACM Journal on Responsible Computing.
crumbs: 17  Critical Data Studies, Data Justice, and Refusal

title: 18  Op-Eds
text:
19 Op-Eds
Parts I through IV have been about learning to see. You have traced the structural pressures that constrain public knowledge (Chapter 4, Chapter 5, Chapter 6), named the countervailing values that hold accountability together (Chapter 8, Chapter 9, Chapter 10), studied the professional lineages that have done public-interest work for longer than data science has existed (Chapter 11 through Chapter 15), and triangulated your practice against the fields it is not (Chapter 16, Chapter 17, Chapter 18). That work has produced a diagnosis and a vocabulary. It has not yet produced a reader.
Part V is the pivot from seeing to doing. Each of the next six chapters takes a distinct genre of public writing or making, traces its history and its audience, and walks you through producing a real artifact in that form. By the end of Part V you will have, on disk and ready to submit, an op-ed, a report, a research proposal, a testimony statement, a public comment on a live rulemaking, and an archival deposit with a resolvable identifier. The chapters are independent enough that you can read them out of order, but the artifact convention is not optional. The chapter is not done when you have read it; the chapter is done when you have sent the draft. This one starts with the shortest form and the widest audience.

title: 18  Op-Eds
section: 19.1 Why the op-ed matters, and why it does not
text:
19.1 Why the op-ed matters, and why it does not
The op-ed is the 800-word argument you write for a stranger. It is not a report (Chapter 20), which assumes a policy audience willing to read 40 pages. It is not testimony (Chapter 22), which assumes a subpoena-strength reason for you to be at the microphone. It is a piece of persuasion, addressed to a general reader who has approximately four minutes and two competing browser tabs, written in the voice of a person who thinks something should happen. Most of what you have built in previous chapters (the audit, the dataset, the scraper) is illegible to that reader. The op-ed is what makes it legible.
It is also the genre public-interest data scientists most frequently skip. The report goes to the funder. The paper goes to the committee. The dashboard goes to the client. None of them reach the person whose benefits were denied, the council member whose zoning vote is next Tuesday, or the reporter who might pick up the thread if they knew the thread existed. An op-ed that is published in a regional paper is read by more members of the affected public than any peer-reviewed article you will write this year. That is an uncomfortable fact, and one the profession has mostly refused to sit with.

title: 18  Op-Eds
section: 19.2 A short history of a strange page
text:
19.2 A short history of a strange page
The op-ed page (literally, the page opposite the editorial) is younger than most of the datasets you work with. The New York Times ran its first recognizable op-ed page on September 21, 1970, under editorial page editor John B. Oakes and op-ed editor Harrison Salisbury (Oakes 1970). The idea was to make room for outside voices on the page facing the paper’s unsigned institutional editorials: voices that did not agree with the Times, that came from outside journalism, and that addressed the day’s questions with a specificity the editorial page was too institutional to risk.
The op-ed page spread from the Times to other broadsheets through the 1970s and 1980s, and with it a set of conventions that still govern most pitches today: roughly 750 words, an argument pegged to the news cycle, a voice the paper’s own columnists cannot reliably produce. In 2021, the Times renamed the form “guest essays” on the grounds that the page-layout metaphor no longer matched the digital product (Kingsbury 2021). Most outlets have quietly followed, though the genre itself is stable. Writers still call them op-eds.
What has changed since 1970 is the ecosystem in which the op-ed sits. The legacy broadsheets still publish op-eds, but their slush piles are brutal and their turnaround on unsolicited pitches is measured in weeks. Around them has grown a second tier that is, for most public-interest data scientists, more realistic and in some ways more useful: The Conversation US, which commissions op-eds from academic authors and republishes them under Creative Commons to hundreds of regional outlets; Inside Higher Ed and The Chronicle of Higher Education, which publish arguments on research policy; MIT Technology Review and Wired, which publish longer technology op-eds with reporting attached; regional dailies like the Boulder Daily Camera and the Colorado Sun, whose readers include the city councils and county commissioners your audit is actually addressed to; and the newsletter tier (Substack, Ghost, individual blogs) where you publish the same 800 words to a self-selected audience with zero gatekeeping.

title: 18  Op-Eds
text:
19.3 Who gets published where
The op-ed page is not a neutral meritocracy. The Op-Ed Project, a nonprofit founded by Catherine Orenstein in 2008, has for fifteen years documented the demographic distribution of bylines in major U.S. op-ed pages.
