Their byline surveys consistently find that roughly 80 percent of opinion writers in the legacy broadsheets are men, and that writers of color are underrepresented at ratios that have improved slowly and unevenly since tracking began (The Op-Ed Project 2022, 2024). The explanation is not that underrepresented writers pitch less often, though they do; it is that the pitch economy runs on weak-tie networks (Rolodexes, conference circuits, editor DMs) and those networks are the ones the profession has always had. If you are reading this book, you have reasons to pitch that are exactly as legitimate as anyone else’s. Pitch anyway.
The political economy also shapes what counts as an “op-ed-able” argument. A piece that says a city’s predictive-policing vendor is overfitting to historical arrests is op-ed-able; a piece that says policing is itself the problem is, at most outlets, not. The constraint is not a conspiracy. It is a product of what editors believe their median reader will finish. You will do better work if you understand the constraint, write around it where you can, and name it when you cannot.

title: 18  Op-Eds
text:
19.4 The anatomy of an 800-word argument
An op-ed is not a short paper. It is an argument with a particular load-bearing structure, and editors can diagnose whether the structure is there within sixty seconds of opening your attachment. Five parts:
The hook is the first sentence, or at most the first two. It is the news peg, the anecdote, the surprising number. Its job is to earn the next paragraph.
The nut graf (from journalism; the paragraph that says “in a nutshell”) is where you tell the reader what your argument is. Not what the piece is about. What you want the reader to believe by the end. If you cannot write your nut graf as a single declarative sentence, the argument is not ready.
The argument is two to four paragraphs that develop the claim. Each paragraph should do one thing. “One paragraph, one move” is the rule you will hear from every op-ed coach, and it is the rule most first drafts violate.
The evidence is where your data lives. One chart, one number, one case, a named source. You are not proving the argument the way a paper would; you are giving the reader enough to trust you. A single well-chosen statistic with a live hyperlink is worth a paragraph of hedged prose.
The call to action is the last paragraph. What should happen, and who should make it happen? “Policymakers should consider” is the call to action of a piece that has no call to action. “The Boulder City Council votes Tuesday on an ordinance that would require X; it should vote yes” is a call to action. Specificity is the whole game.
Hook, nut graf, argument, evidence, call. Print it on an index card. Pin it above your monitor.

title: 18  Op-Eds
text:
19.5 Pitching is half the work
Editors do not read op-eds they have not been pitched. This is the single most common failure mode among first-time authors: they write a brilliant 800 words, attach it to an email that says “Please consider the attached for your opinion page,” and wonder why they never hear back. The attached draft is the easy half. The three sentences in the email body are the hard half.
A working pitch email has a specific shape. The subject line names the genre and the angle: “Op-ed pitch: what Boulder’s eviction data actually shows.” The body opens with the argument in one sentence. The second sentence says why the piece is timely (there is a vote, a ruling, an anniversary, a report). The third sentence says why you are the person to write it: your credential is not your degree, it is the work you have already done on the question. Paste the full draft below a horizontal rule in the email body. Attach it as well, ideally as .docx and .txt. Put your phone number in the signature. If the editor wants the piece, they want to call you today.






TipThe Missing Manual



Op-ed editors want a pitch email and a draft that is one click from publishable. Submitting a Google Doc with Track Changes, or a PDF, or a link to a Notion page, is a rookie mistake that tells the editor you do not know the genre. Paste the full text into the email body. Attach the draft as .docx for the copy desk. Include a one-sentence bio and a phone number. Do not send a 500-word cover letter. Do not apologize for the length of your pitch. Do not ask whether they would be interested before sending the draft: by the time they decide, they have already moved on. The other piece of infrastructure most authors skip: a single embedded chart. Outlets that can publish a chart (and most can, even the online editions of print dailies) report that op-eds with one chart get substantially more social-share traffic than op-eds without one. Make the chart non-optional.

title: 18  Op-Eds
section: 19.6 A worked example: the Boulder audit as an op-ed
text:
19.6 A worked example: the Boulder audit as an op-ed
Take the dataset you have been building since the earlier chapters. It does not matter which one: the HMIS audit from Chapter 9, the campaign-finance scrape from Chapter 11, the Alpine-Balsam housing analysis from Chapter 14, or your own. You have a finding. You have evidence. You have, almost certainly, a moment when a public body is about to decide something your finding is relevant to. An op-ed is the translation.
Suppose your audit found that the Boulder County homelessness response system (as documented in HMIS) routes single adults to shelter beds in a way that systematically disadvantages households with recent non-Colorado addresses. You have the numbers. You have the code. You have Chapter 9’s disaggregated accuracy report. What you need is a 800-word piece for the Boulder Daily Camera or the Colorado Sun, pegged to the next Boulder City Council agenda item on homelessness services funding, that gives a reader who will never open your notebook a reason to call their council member.
Your hook is the upcoming vote, or the specific person turned away. Your nut graf says, in one sentence, that the scoring system is producing a pattern its designers did not intend and the Council should pause the contract renewal to investigate. Your argument is two paragraphs on how the scoring works and one paragraph on what the data shows. Your evidence is the chart below. Your call is: postpone the December vote, commission an external audit, and release the vendor’s scoring logic under CORA (the Colorado Open Records Act; see Chapter 12).
Here is the chart. One chart. You will not get two.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Rates are illustrative; replace with your audit's actual figures.
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "group": ["In-state, 1+ yr", "In-state, <1 yr", "Out-of-state"],
    "referral_rate": [0.58, 0.41, 0.23],
    "n": [812, 294, 187],
})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.5, 3.8))
bars = ax.bar(df["group"], df["referral_rate"],
              color=["#2b6cb0", "#2b6cb0", "#c53030"])
for bar, n in zip(bars, df["n"]):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,
            bar.get_height() + 0.015,
            f"n={n}", ha="center", fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylabel("Share referred to shelter within 7 days")
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.75)
ax.set_title("Boulder County HMIS referral rates by prior-address tenure,\n"
             "January 2023 through June 2025")
ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)
fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig("ch17_hmis_referral.png", dpi=160, bbox_inches="tight")
Two things about that chart are deliberate. The title is a complete sentence, because a newsroom reader looks at the title first and the axes second. The colors code the argument (one group is the outlier), because a newsroom reader will not parse a legend. The sample sizes are on the bars, because a newsroom reader who does not see them will assume the disparity is noise. The chart earns its place in 800 words because it does in one image what a paragraph of prose would fail to do.
A note on rendering. Editors accept .docx, .txt, and pasted-into-email plain text. They do not accept .qmd, .ipynb, or .tex. Render your draft to Word with quarto render draft.qmd --to docx, and keep a plain-text version in the email body so the editor can read it without an attachment. For citations, the web convention is in-text hyperlinks rather than footnotes: link the phrase “the county’s own data” to the dataset or the audit. Do not include a bibliography. If the editor wants a source list, they will ask.

title: 18  Op-Eds
section: 19.7 Why this matters for the book’s argument
text:
19.7 Why this matters for the book’s argument
An op-ed is, among other things, an exercise in openness as argument rather than openness as data release. Chapter 8 taught you to publish a dataset with a data dictionary and a durable identifier. That is necessary and insufficient. A dataset nobody reads, like a report nobody reads, is openness in form without openness in effect. An op-ed is how you make the dataset’s implication publicly legible; it is the reason the data dictionary existed in the first place.






NoteIn the Public Interest



Transparency is not self-executing. Chapter 8 built an installed base of documented, citable data; this chapter is where that base meets a reader. An op-ed that lands in the Daily Camera the week before a council vote does more work for the public interest than the same finding published as a 40-page report the council staff will skim in a briefing (compare Chapter 20). You will write both. The difference between a data release that changes a vote and one that does not is almost always a 750-word argument somebody pitched to an editor who picked up the phone.

title: 18  Op-Eds
text:
19.8 Exercises
Exercise 17.1 (Analytic). Pick three recent op-eds on AI or data policy, at least one from a data scientist writing for a general audience. Start with Emily Bender (Bender 2023) on large language models, Deborah Raji (Raji 2020) on how data encodes racism, and Meredith Broussard (Broussard 2023) on algorithmic bias; substitute equivalents if those links have rotted. For each, identify the hook (first sentence), the nut graf (one sentence that states the argument), and the call to action (what the author wants the reader to do, and who the reader is). Produce a two-page annotated comparison. Note: at least one of your three op-eds will not have a crisp nut graf. Say so.
Exercise 17.2 (Core artifact, part 1). Draft a 750 to 1,000 word op-ed based on the audit, dataset, or analysis you built earlier in the book. Target a specific, named outlet: the Boulder Daily Camera, the Colorado Sun, The Conversation US, Inside Higher Ed, or an instructor-approved equivalent. Your draft must include a hook, a nut graf, a specific call to action, and at least one in-text hyperlink to source data. Save it as exercises/ch17_oped_draft.md and render to exercises/ch17_oped_draft.docx. Word count is enforced. A 1,200-word op-ed will not be published; a 600-word op-ed is not yet finished.
Exercise 17.3 (Core artifact, part 2). Write the pitch email that accompanies your draft. Three sentences in the body: argument, timeliness, credential. Paste the full draft below a horizontal rule. Include an appropriate subject line, a one-sentence bio, and a phone number. Save it as exercises/ch17_pitch_email.md. The email must be one a real editor at your target outlet could read in under ninety seconds and decide to act on.
Exercise 17.4 (Core artifact, part 3). Produce one embedded chart for the op-ed. Build it in matplotlib, seaborn, or plotnine; export at a size that will survive being shrunk to 500 pixels wide. The chart’s title must be a full declarative sentence. The chart must carry its sample sizes. The chart must work in grayscale (test by saving as black-and-white and confirming the argument survives). Save as exercises/ch17_oped_chart.png at 160 dpi.
Exercise 17.5 (Open, moment-of-publication). Op-eds live or die by timing. Watch the local and national news cycle for a week. Identify the moment, real or plausible, when your op-ed would be most likely to land: a council vote, a court ruling, an anniversary, a federal action. Rewrite the lede (the hook plus the first paragraph) for that moment. Save both ledes (the original and the retimed version) and write 200 words on what changed and why. If the moment arrives within the window of the course, send the pitch.
Exercises 17.2, 17.3, and 17.4 together constitute the Part V artifact for this chapter. At the end of the week you will have a file in the course’s submission repository that is, without further work, a complete op-ed package you could send tomorrow. Some of you will send it. Some of yours will be published. The draft that is not published goes into the portfolio alongside the five artifacts from the remaining Part V chapters.

title: 18  Op-Eds
text:
19.9 Looking ahead
The op-ed reaches audiences the report never will. The report, for a different audience, makes a case the op-ed cannot. Chapter 20 takes up the longer form: how to structure a 20-to-40 page argument for a policy reader, how to keep the figures reproducible under a build system, and how to write the two-page executive summary that is, in practice, the only part most readers will read. The skills carry forward. The pitch-mindset (know your reader, make every element earn its place) carries forward most of all. Chapter 22 and Chapter 24 extend it into genres where the audience is a committee chair and a permanent record, respectively, and the stakes of getting the framing right are proportionally higher.

title: 18  Op-Eds
text:
19.10 Further Reading and Resources

The Op-Ed Project, https://www.theopedproject.org/. Handbook, byline survey, and pitch-coaching network. If you read only one external resource from this chapter, read this one.
The Conversation US, pitch guidelines: https://theconversation.com/us/pitches. The most accessible outlet in the United States for academic data scientists. Pieces are published under Creative Commons and routinely picked up by regional papers.
Emily M. Bender, representative guest essay on large language models: (Bender 2023). A model of how to write a data-science op-ed for a general audience without flattening the technical argument.
Inioluwa Deborah Raji, “How our data encodes systematic racism,” MIT Technology Review, December 2020: (Raji 2020). A model of a data-scientist op-ed that leads with a case and a number, not a methodology.
Meredith Broussard, guest essay on algorithmic bias: (Broussard 2023). See also her book (Broussard 2018) for the longer argument.
The Associated Press Stylebook (Associated Press 2024). The house style used by most U.S. newsrooms. Read the entries on numbers, dates, and attribution before your first pitch.
Poynter, op-ed how-to resources: (Poynter Institute 2023). Craft-level advice from the journalism industry’s in-house trainer.
Kathleen Kingsbury, “Why we’re retiring the term op-ed,” The New York Times, April 2021: (Kingsbury 2021). Context on the genre’s current self-understanding at the paper that invented it.
ProPublica’s “Machine Bias” series (Angwin et al. 2016). Not op-eds, but the kind of reporting that the best data-scientist op-eds cite and translate for a general reader.
Keegan (2026), “Public interest data infrastructuring.” The framework this book extends. An op-ed is the smallest unit of public infrastructure you will build in this course; read the paper for the larger unit.







Angwin, Julia, Jeff Larson, Surya Mattu, and Lauren Kirchner. 2016. Machine Bias: There’s Software Used Across the Country to Predict Future Criminals. And It’s Biased Against Blacks. ProPublica. https://www.propublica.org/article/machine-bias-risk-assessments-in-criminal-sentencing.


Associated Press. 2024. The Associated Press Stylebook. Associated Press. https://www.apstylebook.com/.


Bender, Emily M. 2023. Opinion: AI Hype and the Language Model Arms Race. The New York Times / guest essay.


Broussard, Meredith. 2018. Artificial Unintelligence: How Computers Misunderstand the World. MIT Press.


Broussard, Meredith. 2023. Opinion: The Bias Baked into AI. The New York Times / guest essay.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.


Kingsbury, Kathleen. 2021. Why We’re Retiring the Term “Op-Ed”. The New York Times. https://www.nytimes.com/2021/04/26/opinion/nyt-oped-guest-essay.html.


Oakes, John B. 1970. The New York Times Launches the Op-Ed Page. The New York Times.


Poynter Institute. 2023. How to Write an Op-Ed That Gets Published. Poynter. https://www.poynter.org/.


Raji, Inioluwa Deborah. 2020. How Our Data Encodes Systematic Racism. MIT Technology Review. https://www.technologyreview.com/2020/12/10/1013617/racism-data-science-artificial-intelligence-ai-opinion/.


The Op-Ed Project. 2022. Byline Survey Report. The Op-Ed Project. https://www.theopedproject.org/byline-report.


The Op-Ed Project. 2024. The Op-Ed Project Handbook. The Op-Ed Project. https://www.theopedproject.org/resources.

title: 19  Reports
text:
20 Reports
The op-ed you drafted in Chapter 19 travels light: it reaches a commuter, a council member, or a reporter already circling the story, and gets one decision out of them before the next tab loads. The report is the opposite instrument: long, footnoted, designed to be excerpted and cited three years later in a brief you will never see, and written because some institutional reader (a program officer, a legislative staffer, a regulator’s policy advisor, an advocacy research director) needs a document they can put in a folder and hand to their general counsel. The op-ed is a knock on the door. The report is the evidence you leave behind after being let in.
This chapter treats the report as a distinct genre with its own history, political economy, and load-bearing conventions. The chapter’s artifact is a 10-to-15-page report draft, with a real executive summary, one completed section, at least one reproducible figure built from a committed notebook, and a one-paragraph cover note addressed to a named institutional reader. The starting material is the topic you pitched as an op-ed in Chapter 19; the audience shifts, but most of the argument does not.

title: 19  Reports
section: 20.1 A short political economy of the think-tank report
text:
20.1 A short political economy of the think-tank report
The modern think-tank report is a twentieth-century invention shaped by a funding ecology of foundation grants, corporate sponsorship, occasional government contracts, and a small number of large philanthropic commitments to AI and internet policy. The shops you will cite most often (AI Now, Data & Society, EPIC, CDT, Mozilla, Brookings, New America, Ada Lovelace) sit between academic research and advocacy, and each calibrates that space differently: AI Now is argumentative, Data & Society explanatory, Brookings academic with a policy preamble, Mozilla a designed magazine with citations. Read three before you write your own.
They share a structure: a one-to-two-page executive summary, findings organized by claim, methods folded in or in an appendix, recommendations aimed at a specific institutional actor, and a bibliography. Proportions matter: AI Now spends half its pages on argument and a quarter on evidence; Brookings inverts those; Mozilla spends more on design and less on methods than either. Before you draft, look at where your model report spends its pages. That is where its authors believe the argument lives.
Each report has a reader in mind who is not you: a Brookings paper on generative AI in the federal workforce is written for a senior civil servant at OMB; a Data & Society primer on worker surveillance for a program officer at a labor-focused foundation; an Access Now report for a UN Special Rapporteur’s staff. Reports that read as if written for a general audience are almost without exception worse reports.

title: 19  Reports
section: 20.2 Reports that inform and reports that mobilize
text:
20.2 Reports that inform and reports that mobilize
There is a useful distinction most authors do not draw explicitly: reports whose job is to inform versus reports whose job is to mobilize. An informing report surveys a field, names the actors, and leaves the reader better oriented. A mobilizing report builds to a demand: a rule the regulator should write, a line-item the legislator should add, a disclosure the company should make. Both are legitimate, and they are not interchangeable. A mobilizing report that reads as merely informing will be ignored by those who could have acted on it; an informing report that reads as mobilizing will be discounted by those who trusted it as a survey.
You can usually tell the difference by reading the recommendations. An informing report hedges (“policymakers should consider a range of approaches”); a mobilizing report is specific (“the Federal Trade Commission should open a Section 6(b) study into the five named vendors within 180 days”). If your recommendations could be photocopied into a different report on a different topic without changing meaning, you have written a report that will not cause anything to happen. That may be the correct choice. Be sure it is a choice.
Non-US exemplars clarify the range. Access Now’s #KeepItOn report is mobilizing, giving UN human rights mechanisms and national-level litigants a shared evidentiary record. Derechos Digitales (Santiago) publishes reports that work as regional advocacy, academic reference, and inputs to Inter-American Court proceedings. Paradigm Initiative (Lagos) publishes Londa to equip African civil society with country-by-country baselines (Paradigm Initiative 2024). The political economy differs: less foundation money, more distributed authorship, tighter integration with litigation and legislative work.

title: 19  Reports
section: 20.3 The executive summary is the report
text:
20.3 The executive summary is the report
Most readers will read exactly three things: the title, the executive summary, and the recommendations. Some will glance at the figures. Almost none will read the methods. This is not a failure of the readers: a foundation program officer reads fifteen reports a week, a legislative staffer thirty. They are triaging, and the executive summary is where the triage happens.
The implication is direct. The executive summary is not a summary of the report; it is the argument of the report, compressed to one and a half pages, with the evidence pointers inline. It must stand alone: if a reader forwards only the executive summary to their general counsel, the general counsel should be able to decide whether to recommend a meeting. The recommendations belong in the executive summary, not saved for a climactic reveal. If they are not in the executive summary, they are invisible.
Headings are the report’s second skeleton. A skimming reader reads section headings and figure captions, and if the headings read as a coherent outline, the reader will trust the document enough to read further. “Findings” is a weak heading; “Three ways the scoring system diverges from its stated criteria” is a strong one. Write headings as if they were the nut grafs of the sections they introduce.






TipThe Missing Manual



Most readers will read the executive summary, the headings, and the charts. Write those three layers as if they were the whole report. The executive summary is not a summary; it is the argument compressed to one and a half pages, with evidence pointers inline. If recommendations are not in the executive summary, they are invisible. Test this before sending: print the executive summary, the table of contents, and the figure captions, and hand them to a colleague who has not seen the report. If they can tell you what you found, what you want done, and who should do it, the three-layer skeleton works. If they cannot, your argument lives in places no one will read.

title: 19  Reports
section: 20.4 Methods transparency as a credibility move
text:
20.4 Methods transparency as a credibility move
A report is not a peer-reviewed article and does not need to be. What it needs is methods transparency a skeptical reader can audit in an afternoon: a one-page description of how you obtained your data, when, from which endpoint, and with which parameters; a link to a public code repository with the scripts that produced each figure; a datasheet-style note for each dataset you relied on, following the template proposed by Gebru and colleagues (Gebru et al. 2021); and a clear statement of what the analysis cannot support. The last item separates reports people cite from reports people weaponize.
Methods transparency connects to the accountability infrastructure of Chapter 13 and the authority-and-remedy framework of Chapter 9. A report whose claims can be reproduced against public data by a third party survives contact with a hostile reader; a report whose claims rest on private datasets, undocumented pipelines, or one-off notebooks lasts exactly as long as no one asks. You have already learned how to produce the first kind; the report is where you show that you did.






NoteIn the Public Interest



Oversight requires documentation that can travel, and reports are the documentation that travels. The op-ed in Chapter 19 gave a reader a reason to care; the report gives an institution something it can act on, cite, attach to a brief, and hand to counsel. This is authority and remedy in the sense of Chapter 9: a report without methods transparency gives a reader no grounds to trust it, and a report without specific recommendations gives no actor a path to act. The reproducibility discipline of Chapter 13 (documented notebooks, committed code, pinned dependencies, datasheets) is not academic ornament; it is the load-bearing infrastructure that lets your findings travel from your laptop to a rulemaking record to a brief to a ruling without degrading at each step. The credibility of the field depends on that infrastructure holding.

title: 19  Reports
text:
20.5 The translation gap
You already have the findings and the evidence. Writing a report is overwhelmingly the work of translating those findings into terms an institutional reader can act on, and that translation has three moves first-time authors consistently underestimate.
First, naming the actor. “Someone should regulate this” is not a recommendation; it is a complaint. The recommendation is: the Colorado Department of Regulatory Agencies should open an investigation under C.R.S. section X into the vendor’s scoring practices; the legislative staffer should draft language for the next session’s HB-25-XXXX. Name the body, the authority, and the calendar.
Second, naming the remedy. An institutional reader cannot act on “transparency”; they can act on a specific disclosure requirement, a specific records release, or a specific audit provision added to a procurement contract. Your recommendation should be copy-pasteable into a draft rule or draft bill. If it is not, it is not yet a recommendation.
Third, naming the trade-off. Every institutional reader has someone in the next room whose job is to poke holes in the recommendation. Your report is stronger if it names the most plausible objection, treats it seriously, and explains why the recommendation still stands. Reports that pretend their recommendations are free lunches get dismissed by the people who know the lunches are not free.

title: 19  Reports
text:
20.6 Quarto as a report stack
Most of the technical apparatus is what you already know. A report is a Quarto project with its own YAML header (not the book’s), a main report.qmd, one or more figures.qmd partials, a references.bib, and a directory of committed notebooks that generated each figure. Quarto renders the same source to HTML, PDF (for program officers who print everything), and, via Pandoc, .docx (for the policy staffer who runs Track Changes on your recommendations).
A minimal project layout:
report/
  _quarto.yml
  report.qmd
  figures.qmd
  references.bib
  notebooks/
    fig_01_referral_rates.ipynb
    fig_02_geographic.ipynb
  data/
    hmis_extract_2024q3.csv
    datasheet.md
  README.md
A minimal _quarto.yml:
project:
  type: default
  output-dir: _output

title: "Scoring disparities in Boulder County's homelessness response system"
author:
  - name: "Your Name"
    affiliation: "INFO 46XX, University of Colorado Boulder"
date: "2026-05-15"

format:
  html: {toc: true, embed-resources: true, theme: cosmo}
  pdf: {documentclass: scrartcl, toc: true, number-sections: true}
  docx: {reference-doc: templates/report-template.docx}

bibliography: references.bib
csl: https://www.zotero.org/styles/chicago-author-date
execute: {echo: false, warning: false}
A minimal report.qmd with an executive-summary callout and an included figures partial:
# Scoring disparities in Boulder County's homelessness response system

::: {.callout-important title="Executive summary"}
This report documents three ways the vendor's scoring system used by
Boulder County's Coordinated Entry process diverges from its stated
criteria. Using HMIS records from July 2023 through June 2024, we find
that households with recent out-of-state addresses are referred to
shelter beds at 23 percent, compared to 58 percent for in-state
households with more than a year of residency (@fig-referral-rates).
We recommend that the Boulder City Council pause the December 2026
contract renewal vote, commission an external audit, and release the
vendor's scoring logic under CORA.
:::

## Three ways the scoring system diverges from its stated criteria

{{< include figures.qmd >}}

## Methods {#sec-methods}

The HMIS extract was obtained through a CORA request (BC-2024-0912).
The pipeline is committed at
`https://github.com/yourname/boulder-coord-entry-audit`; the
datasheet follows the template of @gebru2021.

## Recommendations

1. Pause the December 2026 contract-renewal vote.
2. Commission an external audit with a disaggregation specification
   modeled on @sec-ch-07-oversight.
3. Release the vendor's scoring logic under CORA.

## References {.unnumbered}
The figures.qmd partial loads and labels reproducible charts produced by notebooks in notebooks/:
#| label: fig-referral-rates
#| fig-cap: "Shelter referral rate by residency group, Boulder County
#|   Coordinated Entry, July 2023 to June 2024. Notebook:
#|   notebooks/fig_01_referral_rates.ipynb"

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv("data/referral_rates_by_group.csv")
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.5, 3.8))
ax.barh(df["group"], df["referral_rate"])
ax.set_xlabel("Referral rate")
ax.set_xlim(0, 1)
fig.tight_layout()
To render as HTML, PDF, and .docx in one pass: quarto render report.qmd --to html,pdf,docx. The .docx output is the one the policy staffer runs Track Changes on; build for it from day one, since retrofitting a Word-compatible template at the last minute is a Friday-afternoon surprise you can avoid.

title: 19  Reports
text:
20.7 Institutional audiences, named
The report is not written for “policymakers” any more than the op-ed was written for “the public.” It is written for specific humans in specific roles. Four audiences recur.
Foundation program officers fund the work that follows your report. They read for whether the argument fits the portfolio, whether you are the right grantee, and whether the recommendations are actionable. Time budget: ten minutes. First read: executive summary and recommendations.
Legislative staffers draft the bills and committee questions. They read for statutory language, a named committee with jurisdiction, and a witness list. Time budget: under ten minutes. First read: executive summary and whatever sidebar most resembles a witness list.
Regulatory policy advisors work inside agencies considering a rulemaking or responding to a petition. They read for whether your report supplies evidence that would survive a challenge in the rulemaking record. Their time budget is longer (they will read the methods) but their standard is stricter. They are auditing you.
Advocacy organization research directors are the readers most likely to build on your work, reading for fit with their litigation, campaign, or next report, and whether you are a potential collaborator. Their time budget is the longest, and they are most likely to ask for your data.
Identify which of these four your report is primarily for before you draft. Write the executive summary and recommendations to that reader. You cannot serve all four as the primary reader.

title: 19  Reports
text:
20.8 Reports across the lineages
The report does not belong to data science. It is a shared form across the lineages of Part III: an accounting report (Chapter 13) documents trust through procedural rigor, a planning report (Chapter 14) documents a decision through stakeholder mapping, and a public-interest technology report (Chapter 16) documents a deployment through user research. When your report borrows from all three, say so. A report visibly self-aware about its genre reads as adult.

title: 19  Reports
text:
20.9 Exercises
Exercise 18.1 (Structural analysis, US). Pick a recent AI Now or Data & Society report (2023 or later). Map its structure: how many pages are executive summary, findings, methods, recommendations, appendices? Where does the argument live? Who is the implied institutional reader, and what evidence tells you? Write 400 words. Save as exercises/ch18_structural_analysis_us.md. You may use McGuinness and Schank (2021) as a framing reference for what “public interest technology” reports are trying to accomplish and how.
Exercise 18.2 (Structural analysis, non-US). Do the same for a recent report from Access Now, Derechos Digitales, or Paradigm Initiative. Pay particular attention to where the report assumes a reader who is not the US federal government. Compare to your US report from Exercise 18.1 in one paragraph at the end: what conventions differ, and what does that tell you about the political economy of each report’s audience? Save as exercises/ch18_structural_analysis_nonus.md.
Exercise 18.3 (Executive summary, outline, one full section). Take the op-ed topic you drafted in Chapter 19 and expand it into a report. Produce three deliverables: (a) a one-and-a-half-page executive summary that includes the recommendations inline; (b) a full section-by-section outline with one-sentence nut grafs for each section; (c) one section (three to five pages) written in full, with figures inline and citations against references.bib. Save as report/report.qmd in your class repository. Follow the YAML-and-layout conventions in this chapter. This is the core of your Part V artifact. See Gray and Bounegru (2021) on writing for policy audiences.
Exercise 18.4 (Reproducible figure with a committed notebook). Produce at least one figure for your report from a notebook committed to the repository at report/notebooks/. The notebook must: load data from a documented path, document every filter or transformation with a comment, and render the figure to report/figures/. Add a datasheet-style provenance note (one page, following Gebru et al. (2021)) for the dataset the notebook uses. Commit the notebook, the datasheet, and the figure. The figure caption in figures.qmd should cite the notebook by filename. Tie this explicitly to the reproducibility discipline you built in Chapter 13.
Exercise 18.5 (Cover notes to three named humans). Identify three humans (by role, and by name if you can) who would need to read your report for it to matter. At least one must be a foundation program officer or an advocacy research director; at least one must be a legislative staffer or a regulatory policy advisor. For each, draft a one-paragraph cover note tailored to that reader. The cover notes should differ substantively: a program officer’s note points to the portfolio fit and the follow-on work; a staffer’s note points to the pending bill or hearing; a regulator’s note points to the open docket or petition. Save all three as report/cover_notes.md. This draws directly on the stakeholder-mapping logic of Chapter 16 and Chapter 14.
Exercises 18.3, 18.4, and 18.5 together produce the Part V artifact: a report draft with an executive summary, one completed section, at least one reproducible figure built from a committed notebook with a datasheet-style provenance note, and three cover notes addressed to named institutional readers. When you have all four components in your class repository, tag the commit ch18-artifact-v1. The artifact is the chapter.

title: 19  Reports
text:
20.10 Looking ahead
Chapter 19 (Chapter 21) takes the genre into the academic and grantmaking ecosystems. A report documents what you found; a research proposal argues for what you will find if someone funds you. The conventions overlap and diverge in ways that catch first-time authors off guard: the specific-aims page borrows from the executive summary, but the theory of change runs the opposite direction. The work you did on the report’s recommendations has already produced most of the proposal’s significance section. From there, Chapter 22 turns your recommendations into a five-minute spoken statement, and Chapter 24 gives your report and its data a permanent address.

title: 19  Reports
text:
20.11 Further Reading and Resources

AI Now Institute, annual reports, https://ainowinstitute.org/publications. The canonical example of the mobilizing report in the US technology-policy ecosystem; read at least one cover to cover before drafting your own.
Data & Society Research Institute, reports, https://datasociety.net/library/. A wider range of report types (primers, case studies, ethnographies). Pay attention to how each is structured for its implied reader.
Access Now, #KeepItOn annual internet shutdowns report, https://www.accessnow.org/campaign/keepiton/. Canonical non-US advocacy report; note the integration with UN human rights mechanisms.
Mozilla Foundation, Internet Health Report, https://internethealthreport.org. Design-forward, magazine-style report that rewards reading for layout and voice.
Paradigm Initiative, Londa report on digital rights in Africa (Paradigm Initiative 2024), https://paradigmhq.org/londa. Distributed-authorship model with country-level reporting; the opposite of the single-institute think-tank report.
Quarto project and book documentation, https://quarto.org/docs/projects/quarto-projects.html and https://quarto.org/docs/books/. The authoritative reference for multi-format rendering; the book you are reading is built with it.
Pandoc’s Word output documentation, https://pandoc.org/MANUAL.html#option--reference-doc. The two-page guide that fixes ninety percent of .docx formatting surprises, especially how to use a reference document for headings and styles.
Tara Dawson McGuinness and Hana Schank, Power to the Public (McGuinness and Schank 2021), for the political economy of public-interest technology reports and the foundations that fund them.
The Data Journalism Handbook 2 (Gray and Bounegru 2021), particularly the chapters on report-length investigative projects; the conventions travel.
Timnit Gebru and colleagues, “Datasheets for datasets” (Gebru et al. 2021). The provenance-note template you should adapt for every dataset your report relies on.
Keegan (2026) (Keegan 2026), on the six elements of the installed base and why reports are the genre in which they most often travel.







Gebru, Timnit, Jamie Morgenstern, Briana Vecchione, et al. 2021. “Datasheets for Datasets.” Communications of the ACM 64 (12): 86–92.


Gray, Jonathan, and Liliana Bounegru, eds. 2021. The Data Journalism Handbook: Towards a Critical Data Practice. Amsterdam University Press.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.


McGuinness, Tara Dawson, and Hana Schank. 2021. Power to the Public: The Promise of Public Interest Technology. Princeton University Press.


Paradigm Initiative. 2024. Londa: Digital Rights and Inclusion in Africa Report. Paradigm Initiative. https://paradigmhq.org/londa.

title: 20  Research Proposals
text:
21 Research Proposals
The op-ed in Chapter 19 argued for a thing that already exists; the report in Chapter 20 documented a thing that had already happened. The research proposal is stranger than either. It argues, in the present tense, for the significance of work that does not yet exist, using evidence you cannot yet produce, on behalf of findings you cannot yet guarantee, to readers who will decide whether the work should exist at all. You are asking a funder to believe in a future that is currently a document.
The hard part is not the methods, which you already have. It is translating a public-interest data question into a shape a funder can recognize, and translating your own working life into a budget that a program officer can defend to a board. By the end of the chapter you will have produced four artifacts: a one-page specific-aims document, a simple budget with a narrative justification, a data management plan (DMP), and a simulated Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol. None is a submitted grant. All are the documents a submitted grant is made of.

title: 20  Research Proposals
text:
21.1 The grant-proposal genre landscape
The funders you might realistically approach divide into four ecologies, each with its own vocabulary and its own review cycle.
Federal science and humanities agencies include NSF, NIH, IMLS, and NEH. They publish detailed proposal guides (NSF’s PAPPG runs several hundred pages (National Science Foundation 2024)), use peer-review panels, require formal budgets with indirect-cost recovery, and expect data management plans and broader-impacts sections. As of 2025, they are volatile ground: appropriations uncertainty, agency restructuring at IMLS, and shifting priorities mean a proposal submitted under one quarter’s rules may be reviewed under the next’s (Institute of Museum and Library Services 2024; National Endowment for the Humanities 2024; National Institutes of Health 2024).
National private foundations include Knight, MacArthur, Ford, and Robert Wood Johnson, plus a long tail of smaller outfits funding public-interest technology work. They run on program cycles, usually expect a letter of inquiry before a full proposal, and keep review standards less public than NSF’s; you are writing for a program officer who is writing for a board (John S. and James L. Knight Foundation 2024).
Regional and community foundations include the Gates Family Foundation in Colorado, the Boettcher Foundation, the Denver Foundation, and analogues in every state (Gates Family Foundation 2024; Boettcher Foundation 2024). Grants run smaller (often $10,000 to $75,000), cycles are shorter, and priorities are locally specific. For a first public-interest research proposal, they are often the right target.
University seed grants are the quietest category and frequently the most accessible. A research development office will fund a $5,000 to $25,000 pilot with a two-page application. A pilot result is the strongest possible leverage for the next proposal up the chain.
Behind all four ecologies sits the Foundation Directory Online (Candid 2024), which maps private funders and their giving patterns. Spend an afternoon with it before you start writing.

title: 20  Research Proposals
section: 21.2 The political economy of what gets funded
text:
21.2 The political economy of what gets funded
The gap between the money available for public-interest research and the number of projects that could usefully spend it is large and does not close. Foundation endowments run on five-percent payout rules; federal budgets are set by political appropriations. The median program officer manages a portfolio in which most of the projects they would like to fund will not be funded.
Two implications follow. Specificity beats scope: a proposal promising to solve algorithmic discrimination in public-benefits systems will lose to one promising to audit the adverse-action notices issued by one county’s SNAP system over eighteen months, because the second is something a program officer can defend to a skeptical board. And if your specific aims are not legible in ninety seconds, your proposal will not get the second read.
Political economy also shapes what counts as “research.” A community-engaged audit whose primary output is a community report is harder to fund through NSF than through a regional foundation. A refusal specification whose output is a decision not to build a dataset is harder to fund than either. Part of the proposal-writing work is finding the funder for whom your project is recognizable.

title: 20  Research Proposals
section: 21.3 The specific-aims page as sales pitch
text:
21.3 The specific-aims page as sales pitch
The specific-aims page is the single most important page of any research proposal. It is one page, the first thing a reviewer reads and often the last. For most foundation reviewers, it is the only thing they will read closely before forming an opinion that is then difficult to move. The report’s executive summary from Chapter 20 is its closest sibling, but runs the opposite direction: the executive summary distills work already done; the specific-aims page promises work not yet done and argues that it is worth paying for.
A conventional specific-aims page has four moves. First, a significance paragraph naming the public-interest problem in the vocabulary the funder uses. Second, a gap paragraph naming what is not yet known, or audited, or refused. Third, two or three numbered aims, each with a one-sentence hypothesis or deliverable. Fourth, an impact paragraph naming the actors who will act on the results.
First-time authors miss the first move most often. The significance paragraph is not a general statement about why data matters; it is a claim written in the exact vocabulary of the funder’s open call. If the NSF program says “trustworthy AI,” your first paragraph says “trustworthy AI.” If the Knight Foundation call says “local news ecosystems,” your first paragraph says “local news ecosystems.” This is not cynicism. It is translation.






TipThe Missing Manual



The specific-aims page is not a summary. It is a sales pitch, written in the vocabulary of the field you are selling to, by a person who has read the funder’s last three years of awards and knows what rhymes with them. Rewrite it five times. Rewrite it once in the voice of the program officer’s most recent annual-report letter. Rewrite it once as if you were the skeptic on the review panel. Show each draft to a colleague who does not work on your topic and ask them what you are proposing to do and why a funder should care. If they cannot tell you, the page is not done. And: the budget narrative matters more than the budget numbers. A plausible story about personnel time wins over a precise calculation that assumes the work will take half as long as it will actually take. Reviewers have seen the half-time calculation. They are not impressed by it.

title: 20  Research Proposals
text:
21.4 Budgets and justifications
A proposal budget has three components. Personnel covers salary and fringe for the people doing the work (PI, co-investigators, graduate students, hourly undergraduates, a contracted community liaison). Direct costs cover everything else accounted for line by line (software licenses, compute credits, travel, honoraria, transcription, modest equipment). Indirect costs, or facilities and administration (F&A), are overhead the institution charges to keep the lights on. Federal indirect rates at research universities run from roughly 25 to 65 percent of modified total direct costs; foundations often cap indirects at 10 to 15 percent, and some refuse them entirely. Know the rate before you start.
A simple one-year, $50,000 project budget, at a regional foundation that caps indirects at 15 percent, might look like this:
import pandas as pd

budget = pd.DataFrame([
    {"category": "Personnel", "item": "PI (0.10 FTE, summer)", "amount": 14000},
    {"category": "Personnel", "item": "Graduate RA (0.25 FTE, 9 mo)", "amount": 15000},
    {"category": "Personnel", "item": "Fringe benefits (22%)", "amount": 6380},
    {"category": "Direct", "item": "Community-liaison honorarium", "amount": 3000},
    {"category": "Direct", "item": "Participant honoraria (10 x $75)", "amount": 750},
    {"category": "Direct", "item": "Compute and storage (AWS / Zenodo)", "amount": 1200},
    {"category": "Direct", "item": "Travel (2 site visits)", "amount": 1500},
    {"category": "Direct", "item": "Publication and open-access fees", "amount": 1650},
    {"category": "Indirect", "item": "F&A at 15% of MTDC", "amount": 6520},
])

totals = budget.groupby("category")["amount"].sum()
print(totals)
# => Direct        8100
#    Indirect      6520
#    Personnel    35380
#    Name: amount, dtype: int64
print("Total:", budget["amount"].sum())
# => Total: 50000
The numbers are not the hard part; the narrative justification is. Every line needs a sentence or two in a companion document saying what the money pays for and why that level of effort is necessary. The graduate RA line is not “0.25 FTE for nine months at the university rate.” It is “25 percent effort over nine academic months to conduct the FOIA log coding, build the reproducible pipeline described in Aim 2, and co-author the final community report.” A reviewer should come away knowing what each person will actually do on each Tuesday afternoon.
Program officers read the budget narrative before they read the budget. If the narrative is vague, they assume the numbers are vague. Write the narrative first and fit the numbers to it.

title: 20  Research Proposals
text:
21.5 The data management plan
Federal funders require a data management plan (DMP) in every proposal, and foundations increasingly ask for one (California Digital Library 2024). The DMP is not a formality: it tells the funder, in two pages, what data your project will produce, where it will live, who can access it, for how long, and under what conditions. The DMPTool, operated by the California Digital Library, provides templates for most major funders (California Digital Library 2024).
A serviceable DMP covers six sections.
Types of data. What you will collect or generate. Be specific. “Administrative data from the Colorado Department of Human Services under a data use agreement” is specific. “Public records” is not.
Formats and standards. CSV, Parquet, GeoJSON, JSON-LD, whatever applies. If you follow a community standard (HUD’s HMIS data dictionary, or the FAIR Guiding Principles (Wilkinson et al. 2016)), name it.
Storage during the project. University-managed storage under a DUA, encrypted at rest, with access restricted to named project personnel.
Retention and destruction. How long you keep the data, and what you do with it at the end. A state-agency DUA typically requires destruction of identifiable data within ninety days of project close.
Sharing. What you will make public, and where. A de-identified derived dataset on Zenodo (CERN 2024) with a CC-BY license and a DOI; a code repository on GitHub linked from the dataset record; a datasheet following Gebru and colleagues (Gebru et al. 2021). If something will not be shared, say why.
Preservation. Where the shared outputs live after you stop paying attention. Zenodo and the Internet Archive provide preservation commitments (Chapter 24 treats these at length); a university institutional repository is another option. A GitHub repo you maintain is not a preservation plan.
The DMP is where you operationalize the installed base for your own project: linkability in formats-and-standards, interpretability in the datasheet, continuity in preservation, safe scrutiny in sharing. Draft with those in mind and the document reads as more than compliance.

title: 20  Research Proposals
section: 21.6 The IRB protocol as a sibling genre
text:
21.6 The IRB protocol as a sibling genre
The IRB protocol sits beside the proposal but is not part of it. A proposal is a budget document; an IRB protocol is a risk document arguing the work, if funded, will not harm the humans it touches. Different office, different reader, different conventions.
The framing texts are the Belmont Report (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1978) and the revised Common Rule (US Department of Health and Human Services 2018). Belmont’s three principles (respect for persons, beneficence, justice) structure every IRB form you will fill out. Your local IRB (at CU Boulder, the Office of Research Integrity (University of Colorado Boulder Office of Research Integrity 2024)) operationalizes them through a protocol template covering study population, recruitment, consent, data handling, risks, benefits, and conflict-of-interest disclosures.
Public-interest data science frequently works at the edge of the IRB’s jurisdiction. An audit of administrative data under a DUA may be “not human subjects research.” A community survey unambiguously requires full review. A scrape of public social-media posts lands in a contested middle ground whose answer depends on your institution, your IRB chair, and the year. When in doubt, ask. An IRB that tells you a project is exempt is a better friend than an IRB that finds out about your project in the press.
The simulated protocol you will draft here is a student-level exercise in thinking through consent, risk, and benefit for a project that is probably exempt but worth describing as though it were not.

title: 20  Research Proposals
section: 21.7 Community-engaged research as a proposal-design problem
text:
21.7 Community-engaged research as a proposal-design problem
If the work involves a community partner (a neighborhood association, a legal-aid clinic, a union local, a tribal nation’s data office), the proposal has a different shape. The partner is a collaborator, not a subject; their time is an in-kind contribution or a subcontracted line item; their name belongs on the proposal; their data governance sets the terms.
Costanza-Chock’s design-justice framing (Costanza-Chock 2020) and the CARE Principles for Indigenous data governance (Carroll et al. 2020) are the two sources most worth reading before you draft a community-engaged proposal. They change the budget (honoraria become line items), the DMP (data-sovereignty provisions become load-bearing), and the aims (the community’s question is Aim 1).

title: 20  Research Proposals
text:
21.8 Broader impacts, taken seriously
NSF requires a broader-impacts section; most private foundations require something analogous. First-time authors treat these as boilerplate: we will mentor undergraduates, publish open access, present at a conference. Reviewers read past this text without registering it.
The strong version asks who will be better off if the project succeeds, in a way that would not have happened otherwise. It names community partners, open datasets, reproducible code, training opportunities for students who would not otherwise have had them, and downstream uses the research makes possible. It ties to the ownership question from Chapter 10: whose problem is this, and who gets to act on the answer? And it anticipates the sustainability question from Chapter 16: when the grant money is gone, does the infrastructure continue, or evaporate with your last invoice?






NoteIn the Public Interest



The broader-impacts section is where “public interest” becomes concrete and accountable. Do not treat it as boilerplate. Tie it explicitly to ownership as you built it in Chapter 10: the community whose data you are using is a partner with a stake in the outcomes, not a population to be studied. Tie it explicitly to sustainability as you interrogated it in Chapter 16: a funded pilot that dies when the grant ends has not advanced the installed base; it has displaced the attention of staff and community members who will be asked to engage the next funded pilot with less trust. A broader-impacts section that names the partners, names the deliverables they will own at the end, and names the maintenance plan that survives the grant is a broader-impacts section a reviewer will remember. A section that promises mentorship and open-access publication without naming who is mentored, by whom, or where the open-access version will live is a section that reads as filler.

title: 20  Research Proposals
text:
21.9 Exercises
Exercise 19.1 (Structural reading). Find a successful NSF proposal in your subfield. Many NSF awards publish a public abstract and, under FOIA, a redacted version of the full proposal; several repositories (including NSF’s own Awards Search) make this accessible. Diagram the specific-aims structure: where does significance appear, where do the aims appear, where does the impact paragraph sit, and how does the language of the aims echo the language of the program solicitation? Write 400 words. Save as exercises/ch19_nsf_aims_diagram.md.
Exercise 19.2 (Specific-aims page). Draft a one-page specific-aims document for a one-year, $50,000 research proposal that extends your Part II, III, or IV work (the audit, the refusal specification, the FOIA-tracked investigation, the stakeholder-mapped visualization, whichever is yours). Target a plausible regional funder: the Gates Family Foundation, the Boettcher Foundation, a relevant university seed-grant program. Read three of their recent awards before you write. Use the four-move structure (significance, gap, aims, impact) from this chapter. Rewrite five times. Save the fifth draft as proposal/specific_aims.md. This is the first of the chapter’s four artifacts.
Exercise 19.3 (Budget and justification). Produce a simple budget spreadsheet and a one-page narrative justification for the project in Exercise 19.2. Use either the pandas example in this chapter or a plain CSV in proposal/budget.csv. Include personnel (with FTE and fringe), direct costs, and indirect costs at the funder’s actual cap. Write the narrative as if you were the PI explaining the numbers to a skeptical program officer. Save the narrative as proposal/budget_justification.md. This is the second artifact.
Exercise 19.4 (Data management plan). Draft a two-page DMP for the project using the DMPTool template for your target funder (California Digital Library 2024). Cover all six sections: types of data, formats and standards, storage during the project, retention and destruction, sharing, and preservation. Name the datasheet standard (Gebru et al. 2021) and the preservation host (Zenodo, Internet Archive, or institutional repository) explicitly. Save as proposal/dmp.md. This is the third artifact.
Exercise 19.5 (Simulated IRB protocol). Draft a simulated IRB protocol for the project, covering consent, risk, data handling, benefits, and conflict-of-interest disclosure. Use your university’s IRB template if you can find it; otherwise use the simulated template your instructor provides. Ground the consent and risk sections in the Belmont Report’s three principles (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1978) and the revised Common Rule (US Department of Health and Human Services 2018). If the project is probably exempt, say so, and explain your reasoning. This is a learning exercise, not a submission. Save as proposal/irb_protocol.md. This is the fourth artifact.
Exercises 19.2, 19.3, 19.4, and 19.5 together produce the Part V artifact for this chapter: a specific-aims page, a budget with narrative justification, a data management plan, and a simulated IRB protocol. When all four files are committed, tag the commit ch19-artifact-v1. The artifact is the chapter (Keegan 2026).

title: 20  Research Proposals
text:
21.10 Looking ahead
Chapter 20 (Chapter 22) turns the argument you just made in writing into one you can make out loud in five minutes in front of a legislative committee or a regulatory hearing. The specific-aims page has most of the content you will need; testimony will make you cut it to a tenth of its length and speak it to a room. Chapter 22 (Chapter 24) gives the data your funded project produces a permanent address.

title: 20  Research Proposals
text:
21.11 Further Reading and Resources

NSF Proposal and Award Policies and Procedures Guide (PAPPG) (National Science Foundation 2024), https://www.nsf.gov/publications/pub_summ.jsp?ods_key=pappg. The authoritative US federal reference. Skim once cover to cover; reread the specific-aims and broader-impacts sections before every submission.
DMPTool, California Digital Library (California Digital Library 2024), https://dmptool.org. The simplest way to draft a data management plan that fits the conventions of most major funders.
The Belmont Report (National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research 1978), https://www.hhs.gov/ohrp/regulations-and-policy/belmont-report/index.html. The framing text for every IRB protocol you will ever write. It is short. Read it.
NIH Office of Extramural Research (National Institutes of Health 2024), https://grants.nih.gov/. The NIH’s grants portal; its policy guide is a useful counterpoint to NSF’s PAPPG for projects adjacent to public health.
Knight Foundation grants page (John S. and James L. Knight Foundation 2024), https://knightfoundation.org/grants/. Representative of the national private-foundation proposal ecology for journalism and civic-technology work.
Foundation Directory Online (Candid 2024), via most research-library subscriptions. The map of the private-foundation landscape. Spend an afternoon with it before you start writing.
Gates Family Foundation (Gates Family Foundation 2024) and Boettcher Foundation (Boettcher Foundation 2024) grant pages. Representative Colorado regional foundations; the closest live targets for the specific-aims page you will draft in Exercise 19.2.
Goldsmith and Kleiman on proposal writing for public-interest technology projects (Goldsmith and Kleiman 2017); Robinson on grassroots grant-writing for small-budget public-interest projects (Robinson 2017). Two very different books, both useful.
Revised Common Rule, 45 CFR 46 (US Department of Health and Human Services 2018), https://www.hhs.gov/ohrp/regulations-and-policy/regulations/45-cfr-46/. The federal regulations behind every IRB protocol at a covered institution. Skim the subpart A definitions once before you draft Exercise 19.5.
Your university’s research development office and its IRB template. For Colorado Boulder readers, the Office of Research Integrity (University of Colorado Boulder Office of Research Integrity 2024). The single most useful local resource, and the one most students discover too late.

National Commission for the Protection of Human Subjects of Biomedical and Behavioral Research. 1978. The Belmont Report: Ethical Principles and Guidelines for the Protection of Human Subjects of Research. US Department of Health, Education, and Welfare. https://www.hhs.gov/ohrp/regulations-and-policy/belmont-report/index.html.

National Science Foundation. 2024. Proposal and Award Policies and Procedures Guide (PAPPG). NSF. https://www.nsf.gov/publications/pub_summ.jsp?ods_key=pappg.
