title: 21  Testimony
text:
22 Testimony
A hearing room is an unusual venue for data science. The lights are fluorescent. The chairs are bolted to the floor. Your laptop is closed because the committee clerk asked you to close it. Somewhere behind the dais, a staffer who drafted the bill is watching the witness table with an expression you cannot quite read. A red light blinks above the microphone when your five minutes begin. A yellow light warns you at the four-minute mark. You will not get the extra forty-five seconds you wish for.
Testimony is a genre. Like the op-ed in Chapter 19 and the report in Chapter 20, it has conventions you can learn, editors who enforce them, and a specific theory of what counts as contribution. Unlike those genres, it is also a performance, delivered in public, recorded to a transcript, and preserved as part of the legislative record. A well-argued op-ed persuades at scale. A well-argued report becomes institutional evidence. A well-delivered piece of testimony becomes voice-in-the-record: a durable, searchable, cite-able mark that a particular person said a particular thing to a particular body on a particular day. That is a different kind of artifact than the other two, and the genre’s rules follow from that difference.
This chapter treats testimony as a public-record argument. You will write a five-minute oral statement, a longer written addendum for the permanent record, and a set of Q&A responses for hostile questions you can anticipate. The running example is a mock hearing before a Colorado state legislative committee considering an AI transparency or data-broker bill; many state legislatures have been debating variations of this material in the mid-2020s, which makes the scenario both plausible and useful for practice. The mechanics transfer: US Congressional hearings, state committees, European parliamentary inquiries, and UN Special Rapporteur submissions all share a common structure, even if the clocks and procedures differ.

title: 21  Testimony
section: 22.1 What testimony is and is not
text:
22.1 What testimony is and is not
An op-ed is persuasion at scale. You do not know your reader; the outlet is the introduction. A report is institutional evidence. You do know your institutional sponsor, and the sponsor’s imprint carries you. Testimony is neither. It is voice. A committee invites you, specifically, because of who you are and what you can say that someone else cannot. The invitation is political work. Witness selection signals which perspectives a committee wants on the record, and by implication, which it does not. The person who invited you expects you to sharpen a particular argument. The person across the aisle expects you to flinch when a hostile question arrives. Both expectations are correct, and neither is a reason to refuse.
Testimony has four features that distinguish it from the other Part V genres:

Voice. You speak in the first person. You name your affiliations, your relevant experience, and the claim you are there to make. An op-ed can be abstracted from the author; testimony cannot.
Brevity. Five minutes is a US Senate and House convention. State committees vary from two to seven. The European Parliament gives longer slots to invited witnesses and shorter ones to public submissions. The UN Special Rapporteur system takes written submissions of several pages but rarely oral testimony from outside the staff. Whatever the clock, it is shorter than you want.
A specific ask. Testimony that does not end in a specific legislative or regulatory request is not testimony. It is a lecture. Committees do not need lectures. They need someone to say, on the record, “vote yes on the amendment in line 42,” or “strike the preemption clause,” or “the definition of automated decision system in Section 2 should include the following four elements.”
Q&A. After the five minutes, members of the committee ask questions. They are not always in good faith. You must answer in complete sentences, without notes, under time pressure. The Q&A is where testimony is won or lost.

Frances Haugen’s October 2021 appearance before the Senate Commerce Subcommittee on Consumer Protection is a useful genre case study (Haugen 2021). Read the transcript twice. The first time, notice how much of it is not about Facebook. It is about Haugen. She names her training as a product manager, the specific team she worked on, the internal research she saw, and what her security clearance was. The personal voice is load-bearing. Without it, the claim that Facebook knew about Instagram’s effects on teen girls becomes an abstract assertion. With it, the claim is a witness account. The second time you read, mark every sentence that contains a specific ask. There are fewer than you expect, and they are not buried. She wants Section 230 reform, SEC whistleblower protections, and independent research access. The asks are concrete enough that a staffer could draft an amendment from them that afternoon.
Contrast that performance with a congressional oversight hearing where the witness is a CEO who has been subpoenaed. The CEO has been prepped by counsel to say as little as possible. The voice is still personal, but the function is defensive: the record the hearing produces is a set of non-answers a future litigant can use in a deposition. Haugen’s testimony is voice-in-the-record as contribution. The defensive CEO hearing is voice-in-the-record as preservation. Both are recognizable genre performances. You will be writing the first kind, but it helps to know the second exists, because the committee members you face will have sat through many more of the second than the first.






NoteIn the Public Interest



Oversight, as Chapter 7 defines it, requires testimony that can become part of the record. A brilliant argument made privately to a staffer is not testimony. It may be more effective, politically, than a public statement, but it leaves no durable mark. The installed base of public-interest work (Chapter 9) depends on a paper trail that future researchers, journalists, and litigants can cite. Written testimony is that paper trail. When you testify in a state committee and your written submission enters the record, you have created a legally preserved document (Chapter 12) that someone six years from now can cite in a brief, a FOIA request, or a dissertation. Chapter 22 will take up the archival deposit that makes sure your version survives even if the committee’s website does not.

title: 21  Testimony
text:
22.2 The scenario
For the rest of the chapter, imagine a real bill. Colorado SB-XX (substitute any pending number you prefer) would require state agencies to disclose when they use an automated decision system to adjudicate a benefit, permit, or license, and would give affected individuals a right to a human review. You have been invited by the bill’s sponsor to testify as an outside expert. You are not a lobbyist. You are a data scientist at a university or a nonprofit who has studied automated decision systems in public-benefits contexts. The committee is the Senate Judiciary Committee. The hearing is at 1:30 p.m. on a Thursday. You are the fourth witness. The chair is sympathetic to the bill but skeptical of anything that sounds like overreach. The ranking minority member is hostile to the bill and will ask a pointed question.
You have been told your oral statement cannot exceed five minutes. You may submit a written statement of any reasonable length. The written statement is what enters the permanent record.

title: 21  Testimony
section: 22.3 A pandas detour that earns one sentence
text:
22.3 A pandas detour that earns one sentence
Before you write either document, you need a concrete fact. Testimony that relies on vibes does not survive Q&A. The fact does not need to be novel; it needs to be yours, defensible, and citable. Here is a small analysis that produces the kind of sentence worth reading aloud.
import pandas as pd

# Hypothetical Boulder Housing Choice Voucher processing log
# Fields: applicant_id, surname, application_date, approval_date
df = pd.read_csv("hcv_processing_2023.csv", parse_dates=["application_date", "approval_date"])

df["days_to_approval"] = (df["approval_date"] - df["application_date"]).dt.days

# A very rough demographic proxy: Spanish-surname list from US Census
spanish_surnames = pd.read_csv("census_spanish_surnames.csv")["surname"].str.upper().tolist()
df["spanish_surname"] = df["surname"].str.upper().isin(spanish_surnames)

overall_median = df["days_to_approval"].median()
spanish_median = df.loc[df["spanish_surname"], "days_to_approval"].median()
gap = spanish_median - overall_median

print(f"Overall median: {overall_median} days")
print(f"Spanish-surname median: {spanish_median} days")
print(f"Gap: {gap} days")
# => Overall median: 47 days
# => Spanish-surname median: 78 days
# => Gap: 31 days
That analysis produces a single sentence you can say out loud: “Our audit found that Boulder’s Housing Choice Voucher approval time for Spanish-surname applicants was 31 days longer than the median, and that gap is larger than the federally recommended response window.” The sentence is short. It is sourced. It is falsifiable. A hostile member of the committee can ask you to submit the code, and you can submit the code. The written addendum will include a footnote with the repository URL and the commit hash. The oral statement will name the fact once and move on.
This is, incidentally, the clearest answer to the question “why should I learn pandas if my goal is public-interest policy work.” Because a single well-sourced sentence in the hearing record is worth more than a thousand words of unsupported assertion, and pandas is how you produce the sentence.

title: 21  Testimony
text:
22.4 The five-minute oral statement
Here is the structure that works. It is not the only one, but it is the one you should start from and deviate deliberately.
[0:00-0:30]  Who you are, one sentence of credentialing,
             one sentence naming the bill and your position.
[0:30-1:30]  A concrete case or finding. Name a person,
             a place, a number. Make the abstraction visible.
[1:30-3:00]  The core argument. Why the current situation
             fails the public interest. Two or three supporting
             beats, not more.
[3:00-4:00]  What the bill does well and what it should fix.
             Specific language where possible.
[4:00-4:45]  Your ask in one sentence, repeated twice if needed.
[4:45-5:00]  Thank the chair. Stop talking.
The opening is the hardest to write and the most important to read aloud. Your first sentence should fit on one breath. “Chair Gonzales, Ranking Member Liston, members of the committee, thank you for the opportunity to testify today on Senate Bill XX” is fine, but only if you then immediately say something specific. Do not spend your first minute reading your CV. You will be cut off.






TipThe Missing Manual



Two things every state-legislative handbook fails to mention. First, the five minutes start when the chair says “you may begin,” not when you sit down at the witness table. The hearing clock is not sympathetic to your throat-clearing. Practice your opening sentence out loud, time it, and cut it in half if you need to. Second, the written statement becomes the permanent record; the oral statement does not unless C-SPAN or the state cable channel happens to record it and happens to keep the recording. Treat the written statement as the testimony. Treat the oral statement as the advertisement that makes the committee want to read the written one.

title: 21  Testimony
section: 22.5 The written addendum for the record
text:
22.5 The written addendum for the record
The written submission is what a staffer will send to the state archivist, what a future researcher will find in LexisNexis, what a journalist will quote next year, and what a lawyer will attach to a brief the year after that. Two to four pages, single-spaced, with footnotes. The oral statement says the thing; the written statement shows the work.
A structure that works for most committees:

Identification. Name, affiliation, relevant experience, the bill under consideration, and your position in one or two sentences.
Summary of the problem. Two or three paragraphs. The evidence you have that is relevant to the bill. Cite your sources.
The audit finding (or analogous empirical contribution). The fact you are there to put on the record. A figure or table if it clarifies. A link to the underlying data and code.
Analysis of the bill. Section by section. Where the bill’s definitions are sound. Where they are too narrow, too broad, or unclear. Proposed amendment language where you have it.
Response to likely objections. The committee has a record of this debate. Name the most common objection and respond to it in a paragraph. You are saving the chair the trouble.
The ask. Explicit. A vote. An amendment. A floor statement. An interim study. Something the committee can actually do.
Contact information and availability. You are offering to follow up in writing.

The written statement is not a draft law review article. It is closer to a legal brief: footnoted, argumentative, and ruthlessly targeted at the decision the committee is about to make.

title: 21  Testimony
text:
22.6 Anticipating Q&A
After your five minutes, the chair will thank you. Then the questions begin. Some members will lob softballs to give you another minute on the clock; others will try to discredit you. A three-sentence answer is almost always the right length. Sentence one: direct response to the question. Sentence two: the evidence or reasoning. Sentence three: the connection to your ask or a clean exit.
Here are five question types you can expect at an AI transparency hearing, with a template answer structure for each.
The “are you a lobbyist” question. “Professor, who funded the study you cited?” Disclose the funding source. Name any relevant grants or affiliations. Connect back to why the finding stands regardless.
The “are you overreaching” question. “Wouldn’t your proposal shut down every automated system in state government?” Acknowledge the concern. Cite the bill’s scope language or your proposed amendment. Name a specific carve-out that addresses the concern.
The “are you the right expert” question. “You’re a data scientist, not a lawyer. How do you know this is the right policy?” Acknowledge the genuine limit. Name what your expertise does bring. Name the collaborators or sources whose legal expertise you are drawing on.
The “isn’t this already handled” question. “Don’t existing due-process protections cover this?” Name the specific gap. Cite a case or a dataset showing the gap. Explain why the bill closes it.
The “will this work” question. “How will agencies actually comply without massive new staffing?” Name a comparable jurisdiction that has implemented something similar. Cite the implementation cost data if you have it. Concede any open question honestly.
The worst thing you can do is answer a question you were not asked. Senators and state legislators can tell the difference, and they will use the pivot against you. If you do not know the answer, say so and offer to follow up in writing. The follow-up letter becomes part of the record.

title: 21  Testimony
text:
22.7 Practice delivery and timing
Record yourself delivering the statement. Time it. Almost every first draft runs long. The first cut is usually an adjective you did not know you had written. The second cut is an entire paragraph you liked. The third cut is the transition sentence you kept because it sounded smart. After three cuts, you are under five minutes.
A note on reading versus speaking. Senior lobbyists speak without notes. Most witnesses read from a printed statement. Either is fine. What is not fine is looking down at your laptop throughout. Print the statement. Mark the breaths in pencil. Underline the one or two phrases you must deliver cleanly.

title: 21  Testimony
text:
22.8 Closing and what comes next
Testimony is a small, unusual genre within public-interest data science. Most students will testify once or twice in their careers; a few will testify regularly. For both groups, the habit of producing the paper trail matters more than the live performance. The written statement, the audit code, the dataset, and the follow-up letter all become searchable, citable artifacts in a legislative record that outlasts the hearing.
Chapter 21 takes up public comments on federal rulemakings: a related genre with a different clock (30 to 60 days instead of five minutes), a different venue (Regulations.gov instead of a hearing room), and a different kind of reader (a docket officer at an agency, not a senator). Many of the same habits transfer. You will still need a specific ask, a concrete finding, and a written record that survives.

title: 21  Testimony
text:
22.9 Exercises
Exercise 20.1 (Guided). Read Frances Haugen’s October 2021 Senate Commerce testimony (the transcript is widely available through the committee’s archive). Mark every passage where she uses personal voice, every concrete empirical claim, and every specific legislative ask. Count each category. Write 200 words on what the ratio tells you about the genre.
Exercise 20.2 (Core artifact). Write a five-minute oral statement (~750 words) for the mock Colorado hearing scenario described in this chapter, or substitute a real pending bill in your state. Include the introductory sentence, one concrete finding from an analysis you actually run (see the pandas example in this chapter), and a specific legislative ask. Save it as artifacts/ch20_oral_statement.md.
Exercise 20.3 (Core artifact). Write a two-to-four-page written addendum for the permanent record. Follow the seven-part structure above. Include at least three footnotes with real sources, at least one table or figure from your analysis, and proposed amendment language for one section of the bill. Save it as artifacts/ch20_written_statement.pdf.
Exercise 20.4 (Core artifact). Draft three-sentence answers to five anticipated hostile questions, using the five question types in this chapter as a starting set. Save them as artifacts/ch20_qa_prep.md. Read the answers aloud and confirm that each fits in under twenty seconds.
Exercise 20.5 (Open-ended). Record yourself delivering the oral statement. Time it. If it runs long, cut it down. Once it fits, upload the written version (Exercise 20.3) to the course’s Internet Archive collection with a meaningful title, a clear abstract, and at least three topical tags. Submit the archive.org item URL. This previews the archival deposit work of Chapter 24, where you will learn the full metadata stack.

title: 21  Testimony
text:
22.10 Further Reading and Resources

Frances Haugen, testimony before the US Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation, Subcommittee on Consumer Protection, Product Safety, and Data Security, October 5, 2021. The canonical example for this chapter (Haugen 2021). Watch the full hearing if you can; the Q&A is as instructive as the prepared statement.
Joy Buolamwini, testimony before the US House Committee on Oversight and Reform on facial recognition technology, May 2019. Paired with the Gender Shades research (Buolamwini and Gebru 2018), it is a model of how an audit becomes a hearing statement. TODO: verify canonical transcript URL.
Inioluwa Deborah Raji, various congressional and state-legislative appearances on AI auditing and accountability, 2020 onward. TODO: confirm specific hearing transcripts; her FAccT work (Raji et al. 2020) is the technical backbone.
Safiya Umoja Noble, Senate Judiciary Committee comments on algorithmic amplification and platform accountability. TODO: verify specific hearing and date; her book (Noble 2018) is the long form.
Congress.gov hearings archive: https://www.congress.gov/committees. Browse recent Commerce, Judiciary, and Energy and Commerce hearings for current AI and data-broker testimony.
Senate.gov testimony archive: https://www.senate.gov/committees/hearings_meetings.htm. Committee-by-committee listings of upcoming and archived hearings.
UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights, submissions to Special Rapporteurs: https://www.ohchr.org/en/special-procedures-human-rights-council. A different form of testimony, written and international, with its own conventions.
Common Cause, “How to testify at a hearing”: https://www.commoncause.org/. A plain-English guide for first-time witnesses. TODO: pin to current URL.
Public Citizen’s advocacy resources: https://www.citizen.org/. Includes sample testimony and witness-preparation materials.
Keegan (2026), Public interest data infrastructuring (Keegan 2026), on why voice-in-the-record is part of the installed base rather than a rhetorical flourish.







Buolamwini, Joy, and Timnit Gebru. 2018. “Gender Shades: Intersectional Accuracy Disparities in Commercial Gender Classification.” Proceedings of the 1st Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency, 77–91.


Haugen, Frances. 2021. Testimony Before the United States Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation, Subcommittee on Consumer Protection, Product Safety, and Data Security. US Senate. https://www.commerce.senate.gov/2021/10/protecting-kids-online-testimony-from-a-facebook-whistleblower.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.


Noble, Safiya Umoja. 2018. Algorithms of Oppression: How Search Engines Reinforce Racism. NYU Press.


Raji, Inioluwa Deborah, Andrew Smart, Rebecca N. White, et al. 2020. “Closing the AI Accountability Gap: Defining an End-to-End Framework for Internal Algorithmic Auditing.” Proceedings of FAccT 2020, 33–44.

title: 22  Public Comments
text:
23 Public Comments
Federal rulemaking is where many of the country’s most consequential data-policy fights are decided, and almost nobody you know has written a comment on one. The op-ed in Chapter 19 reaches a general reader. The report in Chapter 20 reaches a funder. The testimony in Chapter 22 reaches a committee that invited you in advance. A public comment on a proposed rule reaches the federal agency drafting the rule, on the agency’s schedule, in a form the agency is legally required to read, without anyone inviting you first. The door is open. Most of the profession walks past it.
This chapter treats the public comment as a genre. You will read a Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM), find the specific questions the agency is asking, draft a substantive comment that cites the NPRM’s section numbers, and (with instructor approval) submit it through regulations.gov. The running example is one of the mid-2020s’ large algorithmic-accountability dockets: the Federal Trade Commission’s Commercial Surveillance and Data Security ANPRM, or the National Institute of Standards and Technology’s AI Risk Management Framework comment cycles. Both produced thousands of pages of public comment, much of it substantive, all of it now part of the administrative record. Pick either, or find a current docket that overlaps your project; a TODO note later explains how.

title: 22  Public Comments
text:
23.1 What a public comment is
The Administrative Procedure Act of 1946 is the legal scaffolding under which most federal regulation happens. Its most-cited provision for our purposes is 5 U.S.C. § 553, “Rule making,” which requires that when an agency proposes a substantive rule, it must publish notice in the Federal Register, give the public an opportunity to participate “through submission of written data, views, or arguments,” and consider what it receives before issuing a final rule (US Congress 1946). That sentence is short, but the obligations it creates are load-bearing. An agency that skips the public comment step, or that ignores comments raising a “significant” issue, risks having its rule vacated in court as “arbitrary and capricious.” The comment period is the mechanism by which federal rules are made legally durable.
The mechanics are standardized. An agency publishes an NPRM in the Federal Register. The NPRM names a docket number, describes the proposed rule, explains the agency’s legal authority, summarizes the evidence, and almost always asks the public a series of specific questions. It announces a comment period, typically 30 to 90 days. During that window, anyone, US citizen or not, may submit comments through regulations.gov or directly to the agency. At the close, the agency reviews comments, addresses significant issues in the preamble, and publishes the final rule.
Three features make comments worth your attention. They are written, which suits data scientists. They are on the agency’s clock, not yours. And the substantive ones become part of the administrative record: the bundle of documents a reviewing court will examine if the rule is later challenged. A comment you wrote in 2024 may be cited in a 2029 opinion. That is an unusual half-life for a weekend’s work.

title: 22  Public Comments
section: 23.2 Why data scientists underuse this genre
text:
23.2 Why data scientists underuse this genre
Comments are lower-friction than testimony and higher-leverage than op-eds, and the profession submits shockingly few. Three reasons recur.
First, nobody invited you. A congressional committee selects its witnesses; a newspaper commissions its op-eds. Rulemaking is the opposite: the agency publishes the NPRM, and the comment box sits waiting for whoever shows up. The lack of an invitation is the point. It is also, for a profession trained on peer review, psychologically disorienting.
Second, the form is unfamiliar. Agencies do not publish style guides for comments the way newspapers publish pitch guidelines. You have to figure out on your own that a substantive comment cites the NPRM’s section numbers, attaches supporting evidence, and ends with specific proposed changes. The conventions exist; they are just not written down where a student would find them.
Third, the outcome is uncertain. You submit, and the agency either adopts your suggestion, partially adopts it, ignores it, or rejects it in a paragraph of the final rule’s preamble. For a profession that likes metrics, a genre whose principal effect is cumulative can feel unrewarding. It is, nonetheless, how most federal regulation is shaped.

title: 22  Public Comments
text:
23.3 State and local equivalents
Federal rulemaking is the best-documented system, but not the only one. Colorado’s state rulemaking process runs through the Secretary of State’s office, which publishes the Colorado Register and maintains a rules-in-process portal [TODO: confirm canonical URL]. Colorado attorney general opinions, Public Utilities Commission dockets, and Department of Regulatory Agencies rulemakings all follow an analogous notice-and-comment rhythm. City governments run their own processes: Boulder City Council hearings accept written comment before meetings, Denver zoning amendments go through formal periods, Boulder Valley School District policy revisions allow written comment before the board votes. The mechanics differ. The genre is the same: a written, time-bounded, substantive argument submitted into a governmental record.
If federal rulemaking feels too abstract for your project, a state or city comment is a reasonable first artifact. The drafting discipline transfers, and the local audience is often more responsive.

title: 22  Public Comments
text:
23.4 The substantive comment standard
Agencies distinguish, informally but consistently, between substantive and form-letter comments. A substantive comment does at least three things: it identifies itself as responding to specific provisions of the NPRM (by section and paragraph), it offers evidence or analysis the agency did not already have, and it proposes specific changes to the rule. A form letter repeats, verbatim, text that a campaign asked supporters to paste into the comment box. Both are counted, but differently. The administrative record treats a thousand identical form letters as evidence of public sentiment; it treats one substantive comment as potentially dispositive evidence the agency must address in the final rule. Courts reviewing a rule for arbitrariness will look for whether the agency engaged with substantive comments. They will not expect the agency to engage, paragraph by paragraph, with a form-letter campaign (Congressional Research Service 2023).
This is good news for the public-interest data scientist. You cannot out-organize a thousand-member advocacy group at volume. You can produce one comment the agency is legally obliged to take seriously, which is a much better return on your weekend.






TipThe Missing Manual



“Comment flooding” is what happens when a campaign tool posts the same text a thousand times under a thousand names. Agencies treat identical or near-identical comments as one comment with a thousand signatories, not a thousand substantive arguments. This cuts both ways: your one carefully written comment is not drowned out, but if your comment looks like a flood entry (no section citations, no evidence, no specifics), it is processed accordingly regardless of your credentials. Separately, the regulations.gov API has changed endpoints and authentication mechanisms several times across the past decade. Before writing code against it, open https://open.gsa.gov/api/regulationsgov/ and confirm the current version matches what this chapter describes. TODO for students: verify the documented API at the start of the semester and note divergences in your exercise writeup.

title: 22  Public Comments
text:
23.5 The regulations.gov API
Regulations.gov is the shared federal portal where most agencies post dockets and collect comments. It exposes a REST API through api.data.gov, which requires a free API key you request by email (US General Services Administration 2024). The three endpoints you will use most are the document search (/v4/documents), the docket retrieval (/v4/dockets/{id}), and the comment submission endpoint (which has had different names across API versions; verify before writing against it).
The documentation has shifted. Older tutorials point at /api/v3/documents or at regulations.gov’s internal API, neither of which is the current canonical entry point. Treat the GSA open API documentation as authoritative and any code sample older than a year as provisional.
An illustrative search for open dockets matching a keyword:
# TODO: verify endpoint and auth against https://open.gsa.gov/api/regulationsgov/
# before running. The API has changed at least twice since 2020.

import os
import requests

API_KEY = os.environ["REGULATIONS_GOV_API_KEY"]
BASE = "https://api.regulations.gov/v4"

HEADERS = {
    "X-Api-Key": API_KEY,
    "Accept": "application/vnd.api+json",
    "User-Agent": "PublicInterestDataScience/0.1 (your-email@example.edu)",
}

def search_dockets(keyword, page_size=25):
    params = {
        "filter[searchTerm]": keyword,
        "filter[documentType]": "Proposed Rule",
        "page[size]": page_size,
        "sort": "-postedDate",
    }
    r = requests.get(f"{BASE}/documents", headers=HEADERS,
                     params=params, timeout=30)
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()["data"]

hits = search_dockets("algorithmic accountability")
for h in hits[:5]:
    attrs = h["attributes"]
    print(attrs["docketId"], attrs["title"][:70],
          attrs.get("commentEndDate", "no end date"))
# => FTC-2022-0053 Trade Regulation Rule on Commercial Surveillance ...
# => NIST-2023-0009 AI Risk Management Framework ...
The payload uses the JSON:API convention, nesting attributes under attributes and relationships under relationships. Read it once with json.dumps(..., indent=2) before writing parsing code against it.

title: 22  Public Comments
text:
23.6 Fetching and reading an NPRM
The NPRM is usually a long PDF with a parallel HTML page. The Federal Register publishes a structured XML version easier to parse than the PDF; the regulations.gov API exposes it via the document’s fileFormats attribute.
def fetch_document(document_id):
    r = requests.get(f"{BASE}/documents/{document_id}",
                     headers=HEADERS, timeout=30)
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()["data"]

doc = fetch_document("FTC-2022-0053-0001")
formats = doc["attributes"].get("fileFormats", [])
An NPRM has a predictable structure: preamble, background on statutory authority, alternatives analysis, proposed regulatory text, and almost always a numbered list of questions the agency is asking the public to address. Those numbered questions are the most valuable part of the document for a commenter. If the agency asked a question and you answered it with evidence, your comment is prima facie substantive.
A routine to extract question paragraphs from the HTML version:
# TODO: the Federal Register's HTML structure varies by document;
# adjust selectors after inspecting the specific docket.

from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
import re

def extract_questions(html_text):
    soup = BeautifulSoup(html_text, "html.parser")
    questions = []
    for p in soup.find_all(["p", "li"]):
        text = p.get_text(" ", strip=True)
        if re.search(r"\?\s*$", text) and len(text) > 40:
            questions.append(text)
    return questions

# Expect roughly 20-100 explicit questions in a substantive NPRM.
# The FTC commercial surveillance ANPRM asked 95 numbered questions.
Ninety-five questions is a lot. You are not obliged to answer all of them. You are asking which two or three overlap your evidence base, and answering those with the best argument you can muster.

title: 22  Public Comments
text:
23.7 Tracking the docket over time
A rule you are following will accumulate documents over months: the NPRM, comment-period extensions, supplemental notices, industry responses, occasionally a second NPRM. The SQLite tracker from Chapter 12 adapts here with a small extension: add a rulemakings table and a comments table alongside the CORA schema.
import sqlite3
from pathlib import Path

DB_PATH = Path("rulemaking_tracker.sqlite")

SCHEMA = """
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS rulemakings (
    docket_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    agency TEXT NOT NULL,
    title TEXT NOT NULL,
    posted_date TEXT,
    comment_end_date TEXT,
    status TEXT NOT NULL
        CHECK (status IN ('open','closed','final','withdrawn')),
    notes TEXT
);
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS comments (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    docket_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES rulemakings(docket_id),
    submitter TEXT,
    date_submitted TEXT,
    substantive INTEGER,   -- 0 or 1
    url TEXT,
    notes TEXT
);
"""

with sqlite3.connect(DB_PATH) as conn:
    conn.executescript(SCHEMA)
Reuse the insert-and-query patterns from Chapter 10. A weekly cron that polls the docket, inserts new documents, and flags rules with comment_end_date within 14 days will stop you missing windows. Missing the window is the single most common failure mode for first-time commenters.

title: 22  Public Comments
section: 23.8 The shape of a substantive comment
text:
23.8 The shape of a substantive comment
A comment has a header, an introduction, a body organized by the sections of the NPRM it addresses, and a conclusion with specific proposed changes. The first page looks like this, roughly:
[Your name, affiliation, and contact information]
[Date]

Re: Comment on [Agency] [Docket Number]
    [Full title of the NPRM]
    [Federal Register citation, e.g., 87 Fed. Reg. 51,273]

Introduction

I submit this comment on the [Agency]'s notice of proposed rulemaking
("NPRM") concerning [topic]. I am a [researcher, data scientist,
student] whose work focuses on [area]. I write in my individual
capacity; the views expressed are my own and not those of my employer.
The introduction establishes standing, the sections you intend to address, and a one-paragraph summary of your position. The body then addresses specific questions:
Response to Question 12 ("How should the Commission define 'automated
decision system' for purposes of this rule?")

The Commission's proposed definition in Section 2(a)(4) excludes
systems that are "advisory" rather than dispositive. This exclusion is
too broad. [Evidence paragraph citing a public audit, a peer-reviewed
study, or a dataset.] I propose the following revised text:

  (a)(4) "Automated decision system" means any software, including
  machine-learning models, that is used to make or materially support
  a decision affecting a legally protected interest, whether the
  decision is final or advisory.
Three things that paragraph does: names the question, cites the section, proposes concrete redlined text. Agency staff can draft a revised section from that paragraph without interpolation. That is the standard to aim for.
The conclusion restates the proposed changes as a short numbered list and provides contact information for followup. Attach evidence as exhibits. A comment with four exhibits (an audit report, a dataset card, a letter of support, a bibliography) reads as materially different from one with none.

title: 22  Public Comments
text:
23.9 Coalition comments and individual voice
Many of the most influential comments on the mid-2020s AI rulemakings were coalition submissions: ACLU with EFF and Upturn; the Leadership Conference on Civil and Human Rights with a dozen signatories; academic working groups coordinated through the AI Now Institute or the Center for Democracy & Technology. A coalition comment has institutional weight and authorial dilution. An individual comment has the opposite profile: thinner institutional authority, sharper voice.
Neither dominates. If your argument rests on a technical audit you conducted, your individual signature is load-bearing and you should sign it yourself. If your argument matches thirty other civil-society groups’ arguments, your signature adds to a coalition letter. A heuristic: if what you are saying has not been said in this exact form by any current coalition letter, submit it under your own name.






NoteIn the Public Interest



A public comment becomes part of the administrative record the moment the agency accepts it. That record is not an archive of opinions; it is the evidentiary base on which future litigation depends. When a rule is challenged in the D.C. Circuit, the court reviews the record for whether the agency adequately considered “significant” comments. A substantive comment you submit in 2026 may be cited in a 2030 opinion vacating a rule for failure to respond. This is oversight as Chapter 9 defined it: the authority-and-remedy pair wired into the legal architecture of Chapter 12. Your comment is not a speech act but a durable artifact that gives courts, journalists, and scholars a reason to look at what the agency did and did not consider. Write it so it reads well out of context six years from now, because that is when it will be read.

title: 22  Public Comments
section: 23.10 A note on timing and the regulatory calendar
text:
23.10 A note on timing and the regulatory calendar
The comment period is non-negotiable. A comment filed after the window closes is, as a legal matter, absent from the record. Watch the close date. Watch the Federal Register for extension notices (agencies sometimes extend after pressure, occasionally by weeks). Watch for supplemental NPRMs that reopen comment on a narrower issue. ProPublica and the Administrative Conference of the United States maintain rulemaking trackers that surface the high-impact dockets without you scanning the Federal Register daily.
If you are drafting for a live federal docket, verify the docket is still open before you start. TODO for students: confirm your chosen docket has not closed, been withdrawn, or moved to final rule. The FTC commercial surveillance docket and the NIST AI RMF cycles cited here are from the 2022-2024 period and may no longer be open; your instructor will help you pick a current replacement if so.

title: 22  Public Comments
text:
23.11 Exercises
Exercise 21.1 (Guided). Register for a regulations.gov API key through api.data.gov. Use the search endpoint to find three currently-open dockets relevant to your project’s topic. Record the docket ID, agency, title, and comment end date for each in a short table. Paste the table into your exercise writeup.
Exercise 21.2 (Guided). Pick one of the three dockets from Exercise 21.1 and read the NPRM carefully. Extract every numbered question the agency asks the public. List them in a document, grouped by the section of the NPRM they appear in. Write one sentence per question describing what kind of evidence would be responsive to it. Expect somewhere between 20 and 100 questions; if you find fewer than 10, your extraction is probably incomplete.
Exercise 21.3 (Analytic, Part V artifact). Draft a substantive comment that answers at least two of the questions you identified in Exercise 21.2. Your comment must: (1) identify itself by docket number and NPRM section numbers; (2) cite at least one external source beyond the NPRM, such as a peer-reviewed paper, a public audit, or an agency dataset; (3) propose concrete revised regulatory text; and (4) fall between 800 and 2,500 words. Save the draft to exercises/ch21_comment.md. This is your Part V artifact for this chapter.
Exercise 21.4 (Analytic). Retrieve three comments already submitted to your chosen docket. Classify each as (a) substantive, (b) form-letter, or (c) off-topic, using this chapter’s substantive-comment criteria. Write 150 words per classification justifying your judgment with quotations from the comment.
Exercise 21.5 (Open-ended, optional). With explicit instructor approval, submit your Exercise 21.3 comment through regulations.gov. Archive the submission confirmation (comment tracking number, date, acknowledgment email) in your exercise folder. Record the URL at which your comment appears on the docket once posted; agencies typically take 1-5 business days. Add the comment to your rulemaking_tracker.sqlite database. This exercise is optional because a submitted comment enters the permanent regulatory record under your name.

title: 22  Public Comments
text:
23.12 Looking ahead
You now have, once Exercise 21.3 is drafted, the fifth of six Part V artifacts: a substantive comment on a live rulemaking, cited to section numbers, with concrete proposed text. Chapter 24 closes Part V and the book. The op-ed, the report, the proposal, the testimony, and the comment are durable only if someone can find them after your employer’s website is reorganized, your university decommissions your faculty page, and the agency moves its docket to an archival system nobody remembers how to search. Chapter 22 teaches you to deposit each artifact into archival infrastructure (the Internet Archive and Zenodo) with resolvable identifiers, metadata, and a plan for the next decade. It is the final step in turning a portfolio of work into a public record.

title: 22  Public Comments
text:
23.13 Further Reading and Resources

Regulations.gov main portal: https://www.regulations.gov/. The shared federal docket system. Browse open dockets by agency and close date.
Federal Register’s “How to comment effectively” guide: https://www.federalregister.gov/uploads/2011/01/the_publics_guide.pdf. TODO: verify current URL; this is the widely-cited public participation handbook. A short and practical primer on drafting substantive comments.
Congressional Research Service, A Brief Overview of Rulemaking and Judicial Review, CRS Report R41546 (Congressional Research Service 2023). The best short orientation to how notice-and-comment rulemaking produces legally durable federal regulation and where comments fit in the administrative record.
Regulations.gov API documentation at the GSA open data portal: https://open.gsa.gov/api/regulationsgov/ (US General Services Administration 2024). The canonical developer documentation. Verify against the version posted at the start of your semester.
The Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs (OIRA) at OMB: https://www.reginfo.gov/public/. OIRA reviews “significant” federal rules before publication. Its public dashboard shows which rules are currently under review and where the bottlenecks are.
ProPublica’s rulemaking tracker: https://projects.propublica.org/represent/. TODO: verify canonical URL for the current rulemaking tracker. ProPublica and the Administrative Conference of the United States maintain running indices of high-profile rulemakings.
FTC Commercial Surveillance ANPRM docket, FTC-2022-0053: https://www.regulations.gov/docket/FTC-2022-0053. An exemplar docket for algorithmic-accountability commenters. Read two or three of the substantive academic comments filed; they are a usable template for your own.
NIST AI Risk Management Framework comments: https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework. A second exemplar docket, useful for students whose evidence base leans toward technical standards rather than enforcement.
Administrative Procedure Act, 5 U.S.C. § 553 (US Congress 1946). The two paragraphs of statute every commenter should have read at least once.







Congressional Research Service. 2023. A Brief Overview of Rulemaking and Judicial Review. CRS Report R41546. https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R41546.


US Congress. 1946. Administrative Procedure Act, 5 u.s.c. § 553. United States Code. https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/5/553.


US General Services Administration. 2024. Regulations.gov API Documentation. Api.data.gov. https://open.gsa.gov/api/regulationsgov/.

title: 23  Archival Deposits
text:
24 Archival Deposits
Every other chapter in Part V has addressed an audience that already exists. The op-ed (Chapter 19) argues to a reader holding tomorrow’s paper. The report (Chapter 20) answers a funder in the room. The proposal (Chapter 21) speaks to reviewers convened this fiscal year. The testimony (Chapter 22) addresses a committee that invited you. The public comment (Chapter 23) reaches an agency on a docket open for sixty days. Each of those publics was reachable because each is alive.
An archival deposit is different. You are contributing to a conversation with a public that does not yet exist. A graduate student in 2048 reading old EPA Toxics Release Inventory data to reconstruct an environmental-justice claim. A city staffer in 2035 looking for Colorado groundwater trends that USGS stopped publishing in 2026. A journalist in 2060 writing a retrospective on federal climate portals reorganized during this decade. None of those readers can be pitched to, invited, or surveyed. They will find what you deposited if, and only if, it is still there.
This is the final chapter, and the genre it teaches is the one in which openness, oversight, and ownership (Chapter 8, Chapter 9, Chapter 10) come to rest in a single physical-digital object: a bitstream, a checksum, a resolvable identifier, a provenance record, held by an institution committed to keeping them. Everything the book has built points here. The datasheet from Chapter 6, the cleaned dataset from Chapter 8, the tiered-access matrix from Chapter 10, the category audit from Chapter 18, and the five Part V artifacts can all be archived as one collection. You finish the chapter with that portfolio, deposited and citable.

title: 23  Archival Deposits
section: 24.1 Archives and climate, archives and memory
text:
24.1 Archives and climate, archives and memory
It is tempting to treat archival deposit as a technical afterthought: you did the work, now you put it somewhere. The archival profession has been saying for a decade that this is wrong. Tansey (2015) argues that climate change is an archival problem because the floods, fires, and storms that threaten records are the events that make those records most urgently needed. Mazurczyk and colleagues (2018) extend the argument quantitatively, mapping American archives against projected sea-level rise and showing how much institutional memory sits in places the country’s institutions are not planning to protect. Blanke (2024) takes it digital: the Anthropocene is also a problem for server rooms, for migrations across storage media, and for the economics of keeping bits online for a century.
Bluntly: the federal agencies whose datasets document the climate crisis are the same agencies whose data-retention budgets are most exposed to it. An EPA dataset is at risk from two directions. The weather can take the building. A single administration can take the dataset. The archive hedges both.
Ghaddar (2016) and Robinson-Sweet (2018) describe a second horizon: truth-and-reconciliation futures in which publics will want to examine what present governments recorded, classified, suppressed, or destroyed. What you deposit, and what you decline to deposit, is part of what a later public can say about us. Someone will go looking. Either your work is there or it is not.

title: 23  Archival Deposits
text:
24.2 The institutional landscape
A “durable” archive is not a synonym for “a server you rent.” Durability is an institutional property: a mandate to preserve, a budget funded across political cycles, a succession plan, and federated backup so the loss of one building does not destroy the collection. A handful of institutions meet that standard. Several more claim to and do not.
The Internet Archive operates the Wayback Machine and a general items repository (Internet Archive 2024). It accepts almost anything, mints durable URLs, and exposes a Python client. Its legal status has been repeatedly challenged in court, a sign of how much it matters.
Zenodo, operated by CERN, accepts datasets, code, and papers up to 50GB, mints a Digital Object Identifier (DOI) on publication, and preserves what it holds on CERN’s long-term infrastructure (CERN 2024). A Zenodo DOI is the shortest path from a student laptop to a citable identifier.
Figshare and OSF play similar roles; OSF integrates tightly with research-project workflows (Center for Open Science 2024). ICPSR at the University of Michigan is the oldest social-science data archive and the standard for survey data and restricted-use materials (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research 2024). Dryad is the preferred repository for the life sciences (Dryad 2024). Dataverse is a federated platform at dozens of universities; DASH at NCAR is the domain archive for atmospheric data.
Software Heritage archives source code at scale (Software Heritage 2024). LOCKSS (“Lots of Copies Keep Stuff Safe”) is the federated-preservation backbone used by libraries (Stanford University Libraries 2024). Perma.cc captures web pages for legal citation (Harvard Library Innovation Lab 2024). EDGI coordinates volunteer archiving of federal environmental data at risk of removal (Environmental Data and Governance Initiative 2024).
Notice what is not on this list. GitHub is not a preservation institution; its retention guarantees are a product roadmap, not a mandate. Hugging Face is the same. Google Drive, Dropbox, S3, and personal websites are the same. They can be links in a preservation chain; they cannot be the last link. If the only copy of your dataset lives on a platform whose survival depends on next quarter’s earnings, it is not archived. It is stored.

title: 23  Archival Deposits
text:
24.3 Federated archiving as political strategy
That list is also a political arrangement. LOCKSS’s founding claim is in its name: safety comes from redundancy across independent custodians (Stanford University Libraries 2024). When the EPA’s climate pages were reorganized in 2017, EDGI’s volunteer “data rescue” events moved copies of at-risk datasets into Internet Archive items before the changes took effect. No single institution was going to do that; a federation did.
An archival deposit is therefore political as well as technical. Depositing the same dataset in two independent repositories, in two jurisdictions, hedges against any one failing, being captured, or being compelled. The exercise below asks you to deposit to both the Internet Archive and Zenodo. That redundancy is not theater; it is the minimum viable federation.

title: 23  Archival Deposits
section: 24.4 The running example: EPA Toxics Release Inventory
text:
24.4 The running example: EPA Toxics Release Inventory
The Toxics Release Inventory (TRI), mandated by the Emergency Planning and Community Right-to-Know Act of 1986, collects annual self-reports from industrial facilities on releases of listed toxic chemicals to air, water, and land (US Environmental Protection Agency 2024). It is one of the most-cited environmental-justice datasets in the United States and has run continuously since 1987. Like every federal environmental dataset, it is subject to threshold changes, definitional revisions, and occasional quiet removal of historical files when schemas are updated.
Pick a TRI reporting year’s facility-level file (the 2022 Basic Data File is small enough for a laptop), download it, keep the original bytes. The workflow below transfers to any at-risk federal environmental dataset: NOAA Climate Data Online, USGS groundwater, EPA AirData, NOAA GHCN-D.

title: 23  Archival Deposits
text:
24.5 Preservation-appropriate formats
Before any upload, convert your artifacts into formats still readable in fifty years. This is the highest-leverage decision in the workflow. The Library of Congress’s “Sustainability of Digital Formats” registry rates formats on openness, adoption, transparency, and self-documentation (Library of Congress 2024). The short version:

Tabular data: CSV (UTF-8, RFC 4180) as primary; Parquet as companion for large tables. Not Excel; its binary format is semi-proprietary and its date handling is notorious.
Documentation: plain text or Markdown. Not Word. A .docx opened in 2045 will render in ways neither you nor your reader can predict.
Images: TIFF or PNG. Not JPEG; every re-save degrades it.
Geospatial: GeoJSON or GeoPackage primary; Shapefile as companion (its 10-character field-name limit truncates columns).
Code: plain-text source files, not notebooks, with an HTML export attached. A .py is more likely to parse in 2050 than an .ipynb.

You will work harder than you want on this step. Do it anyway. Format choice is the variable most strongly associated with whether your deposit is legible in thirty years.

title: 23  Archival Deposits
text:
24.6 Using the Internet Archive client
The internetarchive Python client and its ia command-line companion are the fastest way to deposit (Internet Archive 2024). Run ia configure once to store your credentials, then upload. The item identifier is a short unique string that becomes part of the URL (https://archive.org/details/<identifier>). Identifiers are forever; choose one that describes the content.
from internetarchive import upload, get_item

metadata = {
    "title": "EPA Toxics Release Inventory, 2022 Basic Data File (mirror)",
    "creator": "Your Name, University of Colorado Boulder",
    "date": "2026-04-23",
    "subject": ["environment", "toxics", "EPA", "TRI",
                "environmental justice"],
    "language": "eng",
    "licenseurl": "https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/mark/1.0/",
    "description": (
        "Mirror of the US EPA Toxics Release Inventory 2022 Basic "
        "Data File, retrieved from epa.gov on 2026-04-20. See the "
        "attached preservation plan."
    ),
    "mediatype": "data",
    "collection": "opensource",
}

files = ["tri_2022_us.csv", "tri_2022_us.parquet",
         "datasheet.md", "README.md", "LICENSE.txt",
         "preservation_plan.md"]

upload("epa-tri-2022-basic-mirror", files=files, metadata=metadata)
# => [<Response [200]>, <Response [200]>, ...] on success

# Verify the deposit. Compare server SHA-1 to what you computed locally.
item = get_item("epa-tri-2022-basic-mirror")
for f in item.files:
    print(f["name"], f["size"], f.get("sha1"))
HTTP 200 does not guarantee the files are actually there. Fetch the item back and confirm each file has non-zero size and a matching SHA-1. That verification is what most tutorials skip.






TipThe Missing Manual



The internetarchive client returns success codes on uploads that silently failed: the item is created, the files are listed with size 0, and the HTTP layer looks fine. Always verify by fetching the item back with ia metadata <item_id> or get_item(id) and comparing the server’s SHA-1 to yours. Separately, Zenodo DOIs take up to an hour to resolve after minting; do not panic when a fresh DOI returns “not found” ten seconds after publishing. The other silent killer is format choice. The difference between a file that survives fifty years and one that does not is usually not storage redundancy but format: CSV beats Excel, TIFF beats JPEG, Markdown beats Word, plain source beats a bundled notebook. Storage is easy. Legibility is hard.

title: 23  Archival Deposits
text:
24.7 Minting a DOI with Zenodo
Zenodo’s REST API issues a DOI on publication, which is what makes it the right second archive in the federated pair (CERN 2024). Request a personal access token from zenodo.org/account/settings/applications/, scope it to deposit:write and deposit:actions, and treat it like a password.
import os, requests

ZENODO = "https://zenodo.org/api"
PARAMS = {"access_token": os.environ["ZENODO_TOKEN"]}
HEADERS = {"Content-Type": "application/json"}

# 1. Create an empty deposition.
r = requests.post(f"{ZENODO}/deposit/depositions",
                  params=PARAMS, headers=HEADERS, json={})
r.raise_for_status()
dep = r.json()

# 2. Upload files into the deposition bucket.
for path in ["tri_2022_us.csv", "tri_2022_us.parquet",
             "datasheet.md", "README.md", "preservation_plan.md"]:
    with open(path, "rb") as fh:
        requests.put(f"{dep['links']['bucket']}/{os.path.basename(path)}",
                     data=fh, params=PARAMS).raise_for_status()

# 3. Attach Dublin Core metadata.
meta = {"metadata": {
    "title": "EPA Toxics Release Inventory, 2022 Basic Data File (mirror)",
    "upload_type": "dataset",
    "description": "Mirror of the US EPA TRI 2022 Basic Data File...",
    "creators": [{"name": "Your Name",
                  "affiliation": "University of Colorado Boulder"}],
    "keywords": ["environment", "toxics", "EPA", "TRI",
                 "environmental justice", "at-risk data"],
    "license": "cc-zero", "language": "eng",
    "related_identifiers": [
        {"identifier": "https://archive.org/details/epa-tri-2022-basic-mirror",
         "relation": "isIdenticalTo", "resource_type": "dataset"},
        {"identifier": "https://www.epa.gov/toxics-release-inventory-tri-program",
         "relation": "isDerivedFrom", "resource_type": "other"},
    ],
}}
requests.put(f"{ZENODO}/deposit/depositions/{dep['id']}",
             params=PARAMS, headers=HEADERS, json=meta).raise_for_status()

# 4. Publish. This mints the DOI and makes the record immutable.
r = requests.post(
    f"{ZENODO}/deposit/depositions/{dep['id']}/actions/publish",
    params=PARAMS)
print(r.json()["doi"])  # => 10.5281/zenodo.XXXXXXX
The related_identifiers field is where federation becomes legible. You are telling Zenodo that its record is identical to the Internet Archive item and derived from the EPA source. A future user landing on any of the three can follow the chain to the others. Before going live, test against sandbox.zenodo.org: the API is identical, the DOIs are not real, the mistakes are free.

title: 23  Archival Deposits
text:
24.8 A preservation plan
A deposit without a preservation plan is a bet that someone else will figure out what you meant. A plan makes the bet explicit by answering four questions.
Retention. How long do you intend the deposit to remain accessible, and under what conditions would it be withdrawn? “Indefinite, absent legal compulsion” is acceptable. So is “Ten years, then re-evaluate.” Specify something.
Migration. What happens when the format becomes unreadable? Parquet in 2026 will not be Parquet in 2056. State whether migration is the depositor’s responsibility (usually yes for the first generation), the repository’s (sometimes yes for major archives), or unresolved. Name a review interval; five years is reasonable.
Custodianship. Who is responsible when you move, change institutions, or leave the work? Karasti and Blomberg (2018) call this “maintenance across decades,” and their infrastructure studies (Chapter 7) is the backbone of the answer. Designate a successor, an institution, or a community. “My GitHub account” is not an answer. “The University of Colorado Boulder libraries’ digital preservation program, with EDGI as federated redundancy” is.
Access and refusal. Tie back to the tiered-access matrix from Chapter 10. Open access or tiered (DUA, embargo windows)? Refusal conditions from Chapter 18 under which portions should be withdrawn? Make the rules legible before a future dispute requires them.
A plan that answers those four questions, committed alongside the dataset in the same deposit, is a preservation plan and a governance document. Someone will need it.






NoteIn the Public Interest



The public interest includes people who do not yet exist. That is not a metaphor. The graduate student in 2048, the council staffer in 2035, the journalist in 2060: they are the public your deposit serves, and the archive is how you speak to them. This is the chapter where the book’s three values come to rest. Openness (Chapter 8) lets future readers find the bits, read them, and cite them. Oversight (Chapter 9) lets them hold present institutions accountable using records the institutions did not destroy. Ownership (Chapter 10) binds the deposit to a custodianship chain that does not evaporate with a funding cycle. The installed base (Chapter 7) was never only about the present; it was always about whether the future could read us.

title: 23  Archival Deposits
text:
24.9 Weaving the book together
The previous twenty-one chapters each contribute a piece of the portfolio. The datasheet from Chapter 6 is the provenance document. The cleaning pipeline from Chapter 8 produced the CSV and Parquet versions. The tiered-access matrix from Chapter 10 sits inside the preservation plan. The tests from Chapter 15 evidence that the pipeline still runs. The sustainability reflection from Chapter 16 is why you chose a federated pair. The category audit from Chapter 18 sits in the README as a note about what the dataset does and does not enumerate.
The five Part V artifacts cluster around the deposit. The op-ed argues the dataset matters. The report is the analysis a future reader will consult. The proposal is the forward-looking plan. The testimony is the record of saying so in public. The public comment is the administrative record. All five, alongside the dataset, archive as one collection. Exercise 22.4 asks you to build it.

title: 23  Archival Deposits
text:
24.10 Verifying and citing
A deposit that cannot be cited is a backup. A deposit that can be cited is a contribution. After publication, resolve the DOI and confirm it lands. Paste the Internet Archive URL into a fresh logged-out session and confirm you can download the files. Compute their SHA-256 and compare to what you deposited. Then write the citation in the form your future readers will paste:

Your Name (2026). EPA Toxics Release Inventory, 2022 Basic Data File (mirror) [Dataset]. Zenodo. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.XXXXXXX. Also at Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/epa-tri-2022-basic-mirror.

That citation, on your CV and project pages, is the shortest route from “I did the work” to “here is the permanent link.” It is the minimum unit of public-interest infrastructure Keegan (2026) describes. Write it.

title: 23  Archival Deposits
text:
24.11 Exercises
Exercise 22.1 (Guided). Install the internetarchive client and register at archive.org. Pick an at-risk federal environmental dataset (EPA TRI 2022, a NOAA Climate Data Online file, or a USGS groundwater extract). Download it, convert to CSV and Parquet, write a one-page datasheet following Chapter 6, and deposit the bundle as an Internet Archive item with full Dublin Core metadata. Verify with ia metadata <item_id> that every file has non-zero size and a matching SHA-1. Submit the item URL and verification output.
Exercise 22.2 (Guided). Create a Zenodo account, request an access token, and test the deposit flow against sandbox.zenodo.org before going live. Then deposit to production Zenodo with a related_identifiers entry pointing back to your Internet Archive item. Verify the DOI resolves (allow up to an hour). Submit the DOI, the resolved URL, and a note on how long resolution took.
Exercise 22.3 (Reflective). Write a two-to-three-page preservation plan for the dataset. Address retention, migration, custodianship, and access and refusal (tying back to Chapter 10 and Chapter 18). Commit it into the deposit as preservation_plan.md and update the metadata to reference it.
Exercise 22.4 (Portfolio synthesis). Archive your op-ed (Chapter 19), report (Chapter 20), proposal (Chapter 21), testimony (Chapter 22), and public comment (Chapter 23) as a coherent collection alongside your dataset deposit. Write a collection-level README introducing the project, summarizing each artifact in two to three sentences, giving full citations with DOIs or permanent URLs, and explaining how the artifacts relate. Deposit to both the Internet Archive and Zenodo. This is your end-of-book portfolio.
Exercise 22.5 (Open-ended). Propose a public-interest data infrastructure that should exist in 2035 but does not. A federated archive of state benefits-administration audit logs, a repository of municipal algorithmic impact assessments, a climate-resilient mirror of at-risk federal datasets: anything you have wanted to see while working through this book. Three to five pages. Address what it is, what publics it serves, what the first five years of building it look like year by year, what an archival-deposit strategy for its outputs entails, and who bears the maintenance labor across decades. You are not committing to build it; you are demonstrating that you can imagine one and describe what imagining it seriously requires.

title: 23  Archival Deposits
text:
24.12 A coda
The book began with three line plots of Wikipedia pageviews and a claim that “public interest” is operational rather than a slogan. It ends with a dataset, a DOI, a preservation plan, and five other artifacts that describe a project you can point to. That is the difference between reading about the practice and entering it.
What you have built, if you did the exercises honestly, is a working theory of yourself as a public-interest data scientist, tested against a named dataset, a real docket, a real funder, a real rulemaking, carrying a citation chain. You will change your mind about parts of it; the practice assumes you will. But you now have a first version, committed to the record, that a future version of you can read and argue with.
There is more work. The federal datasets keep disappearing. The dockets keep opening and closing. The benefits algorithms keep being procured. Someone has to keep doing this, and the book’s argument from page one is that “someone” includes you. Not alone, not as a hero, but as a practitioner inside a federation, in conversation with journalists, lawyers, accountants, planners, and engineers, with the critics who sharpen the practice and the refusers who bound it. Do the work. Deposit the artifacts. Cite the chain. Invite the next person in. That is what the installed base needs, and what the next public is counting on.

title: 23  Archival Deposits
text:
24.13 Further Reading and Resources

Internet Archive, Python library documentation: https://archive.org/developers/internetarchive/. The canonical reference for the client used throughout this chapter.
Zenodo, REST API documentation: https://developers.zenodo.org. Authoritative for the deposit flow; test in the sandbox at https://sandbox.zenodo.org before going live.
ICPSR, Guide to social science data preparation and archiving: https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/files/deposit/dataprep.pdf. Sixty years of social-science data stewardship, distilled.
Open Science Framework, OSF help documentation: https://help.osf.io/. The project-scaffolded alternative to Zenodo.
Software Heritage: https://www.softwareheritage.org/. The Internet Archive of source code; if your deposit includes code, cite its SWHID alongside the DOI.
LOCKSS, Program documentation: https://www.lockss.org/. The federated-preservation backbone the chapter’s federation claim leans on.
Environmental Data and Governance Initiative: https://envirodatagov.org/. Volunteer-driven federal environmental archiving; join a data-rescue event.
Digital Preservation Coalition, Digital Preservation Handbook: https://www.dpconline.org/handbook. The practitioner reference for everything this chapter only sketches.
Library of Congress, Sustainability of Digital Formats: https://www.loc.gov/preservation/digital/formats/. The registry that tells you, for any format, how scared to be about using it.
Perma.cc: https://perma.cc/. For preserving web citations in a form that will resolve when the original URL rots.







Blanke, Tobias. 2024. “Digital Archives and the Anthropocene.” Archival Science.


Center for Open Science. 2024. Open Science Framework Documentation. Center for Open Science. https://help.osf.io/.


CERN. 2024. Zenodo REST API Documentation. CERN. https://developers.zenodo.org.


Dryad. 2024. Dryad: Publish and Preserve Your Data. Dryad Digital Repository. https://datadryad.org/.


Environmental Data and Governance Initiative. 2024. EDGI: Archiving Environmental Data and Documenting Changes to Federal Websites. EDGI. https://envirodatagov.org/.


Ghaddar, J. J. 2016. “The Spectre in the Archive: Truth, Reconciliation, and Indigenous Archival Memory.” Archivaria 82: 3–26.


Harvard Library Innovation Lab. 2024. Perma.cc: Websites Change. Perma Links Don’t. Harvard Law School. https://perma.cc/.


Internet Archive. 2024. Internet Archive Python Library Documentation. Internet Archive. https://archive.org/developers/internetarchive/.


Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research. 2024. ICPSR: Advancing Research Through Data. University of Michigan. https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/.


Karasti, Helena, and Jeanette Blomberg. 2018. “Studying Infrastructuring Ethnographically.” Computer Supported Cooperative Work 27: 233–65.


Keegan, Brian C. 2026. “Public Interest Data Infrastructuring.” Under Review.


Library of Congress. 2024. Sustainability of Digital Formats: Planning for Library of Congress Collections. Library of Congress. https://www.loc.gov/preservation/digital/formats/.


Mazurczyk, Tara, Nathan Piekielek, Eira Tansey, and Benjamin Goldman. 2018. “American Archives and Climate Change: Risks and Adaptation.” Climate Risk Management 20: 111–25.


Robinson-Sweet, Anna. 2018. “Truth and Reconciliation: Archivists as Reparations Activists.” The American Archivist 81 (1): 23–37.


Software Heritage. 2024. Software Heritage: The Universal Archive of Source Code. Inria and UNESCO. https://www.softwareheritage.org/.


Stanford University Libraries. 2024. LOCKSS: Lots of Copies Keep Stuff Safe. Stanford University. https://www.lockss.org/.


Tansey, Eira. 2015. “Archival Adaptation to Climate Change.” Sustainability: Science, Practice, and Policy 11 (2): 45–56.


US Environmental Protection Agency. 2024. Toxics Release Inventory (TRI) Program. US Environmental Protection Agency. https://www.epa.gov/toxics-release-inventory-tri-program.

title: Appendix A — Appendix A: AI Assistance Disclosure
text:
A.1 What was AI-assisted
Draft zero of this book was produced with substantial assistance from Claude, an assistant made by Anthropic. The assistant generated first-pass prose for all twenty-two chapters and the index, following a detailed editorial prompt written by the author. The author then reviewed, revised, and (in many places) rewrote the output. The book’s framework, chapter scope, running examples, and exercise design originate with the author. The prose does not.
This disclosure exists because a book about public interest data science should not hide how it was made. The same principle that asks a model card to document training data asks an author to document drafting practice.
crumbs: A  Appendix A: AI Assistance Disclosure

title: Appendix A — Appendix A: AI Assistance Disclosure
text:
A.2 What was not AI-assisted
The bibliography’s selection reflects the author’s reading, not the assistant’s. The running examples (Boulder and Denver municipal records, a synthetic worker-log fixture, a class-wide FOIA tracker, the regulations.gov walkthrough) are authorial choices grounded in the companion course. The “Missing Manual” and “In the Public Interest” callout convention is the author’s. The Part V genre structure, and particularly its commitment to producing submittable artifacts, is the author’s pedagogical argument.
The assistant was asked not to invent citations. Where a suggested reference could not be verified, the bibliography contains a TODO: flag rather than a fabricated entry. Readers who find a citation that cannot be located should open an issue.
crumbs: A  Appendix A: AI Assistance Disclosure

title: Appendix A — Appendix A: AI Assistance Disclosure
text:
A.3 How to verify

The prompt used to generate draft zero is preserved in the repository’s issue history under the label prompt-archive.
Each chapter’s first commit is the AI-generated draft; subsequent commits are human revisions. Use git log --follow ch-NN-slug.qmd to inspect a chapter’s editorial history.
Chapter-by-chapter revision notes are maintained in claude.md and in pull-request discussions.
crumbs: A  Appendix A: AI Assistance Disclosure

title: Appendix A — Appendix A: AI Assistance Disclosure
text:
A.4 Why disclose
Because readers deserve to know. Because other authors deciding whether to use similar tools need honest reports of what worked and what did not. Because a book that argues for documentation of automated systems must document its own use of one. And because the professional lineages this book draws on (journalism, law, accounting, planning, engineering) all insist on provenance: whose work, whose hand, whose judgment.
crumbs: A  Appendix A: AI Assistance Disclosure

title: Appendix A — Appendix A: AI Assistance Disclosure
text:
A.5 Versioning
This disclosure was last updated on the date of the most recent commit to this file. See git log appendix-ai-disclosure.qmd for the authoritative version history.
crumbs: A  Appendix A: AI Assistance Disclosure

title: Appendix B — Appendix B: Further Resources
text:
B.1 Cross-cutting tools
This appendix collects external resources for readers who want to go further. Each chapter has its own curated “Further Reading and Resources” section; those lists are the place to start. This appendix aggregates the cross-cutting tools, communities, and training venues that appear in more than one chapter, plus a few that did not fit anywhere.

title: Appendix B — Appendix B: Further Resources
text:
Datasette (datasette.io). Simon Willison’s toolkit for publishing SQLite databases as inspectable web applications. Used in Chapters 6, 10, 11, and 22.
Quarto (quarto.org). The rendering framework this book is built with. Used implicitly throughout and explicitly in Chapter 18.
The Wayback Machine (archive.org/web). The Internet Archive’s web capture service. Used in Chapters 4 and 22.
regulations.gov (regulations.gov). The federal rulemaking docket. Central to Chapter 21.
DMPTool (dmptool.org). Templates for data management plans. Central to Chapter 19.
Zenodo (zenodo.org). CERN’s general-purpose research archive. Central to Chapter 22.
fairlearn (fairlearn.org). Python toolkit for disaggregated performance evaluation. Central to Chapters 7 and 11.
geopandas (geopandas.org). Geographic data for pandas users. Central to Chapter 12.

title: Appendix B — Appendix B: Further Resources
text:
B.2 Communities and networks

Public Interest Technology University Network (PIT-UN)
Lines of Code for America and local Code for X brigades
OpenCorporates and the Sunlight Foundation’s archived work
The Markup and ProPublica (newsroom exemplars)
Data {&} Society, AI Now Institute, EPIC, Upturn
Access Now, Derechos Digitales, Paradigm Initiative (non-US counterparts)
Te Mana Raraunga (Māori Data Sovereignty Network)
United States Indigenous Data Sovereignty Network (USIDSN)

title: Appendix B — Appendix B: Further Resources
text:
B.3 Training venues

IRE and NICAR. Investigative Reporters and Editors / National Institute for Computer-Assisted Reporting.
ICPSR Summer Program. Social-science data training at the University of Michigan.
Data Science for Social Good. Summer fellowships hosted at various universities.
FAccT and CSCW. Academic venues where much of this book’s source material lands.

title: Appendix B — Appendix B: Further Resources
section: B.4 Critical readings you did not meet in this book
text:
B.4 Critical readings you did not meet in this book
This book’s framework focuses on records, openness, oversight, and ownership. Readers who want a sharper critique of that framing should start with:

Simone Browne, Dark Matters: On the Surveillance of Blackness (2015)
Ruha Benjamin, Race After Technology (2019)
Safiya Umoja Noble, Algorithms of Oppression (2018)
Sasha Costanza-Chock, Design Justice (2020)
Audra Simpson, Mohawk Interruptus (2014) on refusal as political stance

These texts do not fit neatly into the chapter arc because they do not accept its premises. That is the point of reading them.

title: Appendix B — Appendix B: Further Resources
text:
B.5 When things change
External links break. Tools deprecate. APIs change. Report a broken link or a deprecated tool by opening an issue against the book’s repository. Maintenance of this appendix is the point, not an afterthought.
